subject: Physics
question_html:
Define electric flux. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity?
A point charge {tex}q{/tex} is kept at a distance of {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} directly above the centre of a square of side d. Use Gauss' law to obtain the expression for the electric flux through the square.
If the point charge is now moved to a point at a distance '{tex}d{/tex}' from the centre of the square and the side of the square is doubled, explain how the electric flux through the square will be affected.
question_text: Define electric flux. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity? A point charge {tex}q{/tex} is kept at a distance of {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} directly above the centre of a square of side d. Use Gauss' law to obtain the expression for the electric flux through the square. If the point charge is now moved to a point at a distance ' {tex}d{/tex} ' from the centre of the square and the side of the square is doubled, explain how the electric flux through the square will be affected.
explanation: Electric flux: The electric flux may be defined as the number of electric lines of force crossing through a surface normal to the surface. It can be measured as the surface integral of the electric field over that surface, i.e. {tex}\phi=\int_{s} \overline{E} \cdot d \overline{s}{/tex} Electric flux {tex}\phi{/tex} is a scalar quantity. Now to calculate the electric flux passing through the square of side d, draw a cube of side d such that it completely encloses the charge q. Now by using Gauss's law. Total flux through the all the six surfaces of a cube is given as {tex}\phi_{\text { total }}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { total charge enclosed }}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \phi_{\text { square }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}=\frac{q}{6 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} Hence the flux through the square of side d with charge q at a distance d/2 directly above the head is q/6ϵ o . If a charge is now moved to the distance d from the center of square and side of the square is doubled, then electric flux remains unchanged because electric flux in a closed surface depends only on the amount of charge contained inside the closed surface and is independent of the distance of charge.
solution: Electric flux: The electric flux may be defined as the number of electric lines of force crossing through a surface normal to the surface. It can be measured as the surface integral of the electric field over that surface, i.e. {tex}\phi=\int_{s} \overline{E} \cdot d \overline{s}{/tex} Electric flux {tex}\phi{/tex} is a scalar quantity. Now to calculate the electric flux passing through the square of side d, draw a cube of side d such that it completely encloses the charge q. Now by using Gauss's law. Total flux through the all the six surfaces of a cube is given as {tex}\phi_{\text { total }}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { total charge enclosed }}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \phi_{\text { square }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}=\frac{q}{6 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} Hence the flux through the square of side d with charge q at a distance d/2 directly above the head is q/6ϵ o . If a charge is now moved to the distance d from the center of square and side of the square is doubled, then electric flux remains unchanged because electric flux in a closed surface depends only on the amount of charge contained inside the closed surface and is independent of the distance of charge.
solution_html:
Electric flux: The electric flux may be defined as the number of electric lines of force crossing through a surface normal to the surface. It can be measured as the surface integral of the electric field over that surface, i.e.
{tex}\phi=\int_{s} \overline{E} \cdot d \overline{s}{/tex}
Electric flux {tex}\phi{/tex} is a scalar quantity.
Now to calculate the electric flux passing through the square of side d, draw a cube of side d such that it completely encloses the charge q. Now by using Gauss's law.

Total flux through the all the six surfaces of a cube is given as
{tex}\phi_{\text { total }}{/tex}= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { total charge enclosed }}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \phi_{\text { square }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}=\frac{q}{6 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
Hence the flux through the square of side d with charge q at a distance d/2 directly above the head is q/6ϵo.
If a charge is now moved to the distance d from the center of square and side of the square is doubled, then electric flux remains unchanged because electric flux in a closed surface depends only on the amount of charge contained inside the closed surface and is independent of the distance of charge.

question_html: Two infinitely long straight wires '1' and '2' are placed d distance apart, parallel to each other, as shown in the figure. They are uniformly charged having charge densities {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}-\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} respectively. Locate the position of the point from wire '1' at which the net electric field is zero and identify the region in which it lies.
question_text: Two infinitely long straight wires '1' and '2' are placed d distance apart, parallel to each other, as shown in the figure. They are uniformly charged having charge densities {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}-\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} respectively. Locate the position of the point from wire '1' at which the net electric field is zero and identify the region in which it lies.
explanation: Electric field due to wire 1 and wire 2 at point P {tex}{E}_{1} =\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {x}}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{2} =\frac{\lambda / 2}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}({x}+{d})}{/tex} At P, Net electric field is zero {tex}{E}_{1} ={E}_{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {x}} =\frac{\lambda}{2 \times 2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}({x}+{d})}{/tex} {tex}{x} =-2 {~d}{/tex} Negative sign indicates that point lies in the region C. At a distance 2 d from wire 1 electric field is zero.
solution: Electric field due to wire 1 and wire 2 at point P {tex}{E}_{1} =\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {x}}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{2} =\frac{\lambda / 2}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}({x}+{d})}{/tex} At P, Net electric field is zero {tex}{E}_{1} ={E}_{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {x}} =\frac{\lambda}{2 \times 2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}({x}+{d})}{/tex} {tex}{x} =-2 {~d}{/tex} Negative sign indicates that point lies in the region C. At a distance 2 d from wire 1 electric field is zero.
solution_html:
Electric field due to wire 1 and wire 2 at point P {tex}{E}_{1} =\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {x}}{/tex}
{tex}{E}_{2} =\frac{\lambda / 2}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}({x}+{d})}{/tex}
At P, Net electric field is zero
{tex}{E}_{1} ={E}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {x}} =\frac{\lambda}{2 \times 2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}({x}+{d})}{/tex}
{tex}{x} =-2 {~d}{/tex}
Negative sign indicates that point lies in the region C.
At a distance 2 d from wire 1 electric field is zero.

question_html:
1. Obtain an expression for the electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} due to a dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} at a point on its equatorial plane and specify its direction. Hence, find the value of electric field:
    1. at the centre of the dipole ({tex}{r}=0{/tex}), and
    2. at a point {tex}{r} \gg {a}{/tex},
      where 2 a is the length of the dipole.
  2. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=(10 x+5) \hat{i} N / C{/tex} exists in a region in which a cube of side L is kept as shown in the figure. Here x and {tex}L{/tex} are in metres. Calculate the net flux through the cube.
question_text: Obtain an expression for the electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} due to a dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} at a point on its equatorial plane and specify its direction. Hence, find the value of electric field: at the centre of the dipole ( {tex}{r}=0{/tex} ), and at a point {tex}{r} \gg {a}{/tex} , where 2 a is the length of the dipole. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=(10 x+5) \hat{i} N / C{/tex} exists in a region in which a cube of side L is kept as shown in the figure. Here x and {tex}L{/tex} are in metres. Calculate the net flux through the cube.
explanation: The magnitudes of the electric field due to two charges {tex}+q{/tex} and {tex}-q{/tex} are {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} The total electric field {tex}\vec{E}=-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos \theta \hat{p}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} Direction of electric field is opposite to dipole moment {tex}(\vec{p}){/tex} At centre of dipole, {tex}{r}=0{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a^{3}}{/tex} At a point {tex}{r} \gg {a}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}= (10 x+5) \hat{i} {~N} / {C}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{L}=\int \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} {tex}=-E_{L}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=-5 L^{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{R}= E_{R}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}= (10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text {net }}= \phi_{L}+\phi_{R}{/tex} {tex}= -5 L^{2}+(10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex} {tex}= 10 L^{3} {Nm}^{2} / {C}{/tex}
solution: The magnitudes of the electric field due to two charges {tex}+q{/tex} and {tex}-q{/tex} are {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} The total electric field {tex}\vec{E}=-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos \theta \hat{p}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} Direction of electric field is opposite to dipole moment {tex}(\vec{p}){/tex} At centre of dipole, {tex}{r}=0{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a^{3}}{/tex} At a point {tex}{r} \gg {a}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}= (10 x+5) \hat{i} {~N} / {C}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{L}=\int \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} {tex}=-E_{L}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=-5 L^{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{R}= E_{R}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}= (10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text {net }}= \phi_{L}+\phi_{R}{/tex} {tex}= -5 L^{2}+(10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex} {tex}= 10 L^{3} {Nm}^{2} / {C}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The magnitudes of the electric field due to two charges {tex}+q{/tex} and {tex}-q{/tex} are
    {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    The total electric field
    {tex}\vec{E}=-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos \theta \hat{p}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
    Direction of electric field is opposite to dipole moment {tex}(\vec{p}){/tex}
    1. At centre of dipole, {tex}{r}=0{/tex}
      {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a^{3}}{/tex}
    2. At a point {tex}{r} \gg {a}{/tex}
      {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\vec{E}= (10 x+5) \hat{i} {~N} / {C}{/tex}
    {tex}\phi_{L}=\int \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex}
    {tex}=-E_{L}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=-5 L^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\phi_{R}= E_{R}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}= (10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\phi_{\text {net }}= \phi_{L}+\phi_{R}{/tex}
    {tex}= -5 L^{2}+(10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}= 10 L^{3} {Nm}^{2} / {C}{/tex}

question_html: Define electric field. Write its SI unit. A point charge {tex}q=25 \mu {C}{/tex} is located at the point (0, 0) in x-y plane. Find (i) the magnitude of electric field at point ({tex}3 {~cm}, 4 {~cm}{/tex}), and (ii) the angle the field makes with the x-axis.
question_text: Define electric field. Write its SI unit. A point charge {tex}q=25 \mu {C}{/tex} is located at the point (0, 0) in x-y plane. Find (i) the magnitude of electric field at point ( {tex}3 {~cm}, 4 {~cm}{/tex} ), and (ii) the angle the field makes with the x-axis.
explanation: Electric field: Electric field due to a point charge in space may be defined as the force that a unit positive charge would experience if placed at that point. SI unit of electric field is {tex}{NC}^{-1}{/tex} or {tex}{Vm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex} |\vec{E}| =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2} {/tex} {tex} =9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{\left(5 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex} |\vec{E}| =9 \times 10^7 {NC}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{4}{3} {/tex} {tex} \theta =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} \approx 53^{\circ} {/tex}
solution: Electric field: Electric field due to a point charge in space may be defined as the force that a unit positive charge would experience if placed at that point. SI unit of electric field is {tex}{NC}^{-1}{/tex} or {tex}{Vm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex} |\vec{E}| =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2} {/tex} {tex} =9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{\left(5 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex} |\vec{E}| =9 \times 10^7 {NC}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{4}{3} {/tex} {tex} \theta =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} \approx 53^{\circ} {/tex}
solution_html:
• Electric field:
    Electric field due to a point charge in space may be defined as the force that a unit positive charge would experience if placed at that point.
  • SI unit of electric field is {tex}{NC}^{-1}{/tex} or {tex}{Vm}^{-1}{/tex}
  1. {tex} |\vec{E}| =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2} {/tex}
    {tex} =9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{\left(5 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2} {/tex}
    {tex} |\vec{E}| =9 \times 10^7 {NC}^{-1} {/tex}
  2. {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{4}{3} {/tex}
    {tex} \theta =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} \approx 53^{\circ} {/tex}

Find (i) the magnitude of the force exerted on {tex}q_{2}{/tex} by {tex}q_{1}{/tex} , and (ii) the angle that the force makes with the x -axis.
explanation: {tex} \left|\vec{F}_{21}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} =720 {~N} {/tex} The angle that the force makes with the {tex}x{/tex} -axis is {tex} \tan \theta=\frac{4}{3} {/tex} {tex} \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex}
solution: {tex} \left|\vec{F}_{21}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} =720 {~N} {/tex} The angle that the force makes with the {tex}x{/tex} -axis is {tex} \tan \theta=\frac{4}{3} {/tex} {tex} \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} \left|\vec{F}_{21}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex}
    {tex} =720 {~N} {/tex}
  2. The angle that the force makes with the {tex}x{/tex}-axis is
    {tex} \tan \theta=\frac{4}{3} {/tex}
    {tex} \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex}

A small spherical shell {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} has point charges {tex}{q}_{1}=-3 \mu {C}, {q}_{2}=-2 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}{q}_{3}=9 \mu {C}{/tex} inside it. This shell is enclosed by another big spherical shell {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} . A point charge Q is placed in between the two surfaces {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} . If the electric flux through the surface {tex}S_{2}{/tex} is four times the flux through surface {tex}S_{1}{/tex} , find charge {tex}Q{/tex} .
explanation: Open Surface - A surface which does not enclose a volume. Closed Surface - A surface which does enclose a volume. Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines crossing an area normally. Significance of Gaussian Surface:- It helps in finding the electric field in a simpler way. Reason:- Because any electric field line from the charge which enters the surface at one point will exit at another, resulting in a net zero flux. Total charge enclosed by {tex}{S}_1=(-3-2+9) \mu C=4 \mu C{/tex} Total charge enclosed by {tex}{S}_2={Q}+4 \mu C{/tex} {tex} \phi_{s_2}=4 \phi_{s_1} {/tex} {tex} \frac{{Q}+4 \mu {C}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}=4\left(\frac{4 \mu {C}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}\right) {/tex} {tex} {Q}=12 \mu {C} {/tex}
solution: Open Surface - A surface which does not enclose a volume. Closed Surface - A surface which does enclose a volume. Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines crossing an area normally. Significance of Gaussian Surface:- It helps in finding the electric field in a simpler way. Reason:- Because any electric field line from the charge which enters the surface at one point will exit at another, resulting in a net zero flux. Total charge enclosed by {tex}{S}_1=(-3-2+9) \mu C=4 \mu C{/tex} Total charge enclosed by {tex}{S}_2={Q}+4 \mu C{/tex} {tex} \phi_{s_2}=4 \phi_{s_1} {/tex} {tex} \frac{{Q}+4 \mu {C}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}=4\left(\frac{4 \mu {C}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}\right) {/tex} {tex} {Q}=12 \mu {C} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Open Surface - A surface which does not enclose a volume.
    Closed Surface - A surface which does enclose a volume.
  2. Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines crossing an area normally.
    Significance of Gaussian Surface:- It helps in finding the electric field in a simpler way.
    Reason:- Because any electric field line from the charge which enters the surface at one point will exit at another, resulting in a net zero flux.
  3. Total charge enclosed by {tex}{S}_1=(-3-2+9) \mu C=4 \mu C{/tex}
    Total charge enclosed by {tex}{S}_2={Q}+4 \mu C{/tex}
    {tex} \phi_{s_2}=4 \phi_{s_1} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{{Q}+4 \mu {C}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}=4\left(\frac{4 \mu {C}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} {Q}=12 \mu {C} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A small conducting sphere A of radius r charged to a potential V, is enclosed by a spherical conducting shell B of radius R. If A and {tex}B{/tex} are connected by a thin wire, calculate the final potential on sphere {tex}A{/tex} and shell {tex}B{/tex}.
  2. Write two characteristics of equipotential surfaces. A uniform electric field of {tex}50 {NC}^{-1}{/tex} is set up in a region along {tex}+x{/tex} axis. If the potential at the origin (0,0) is 220 V, find the potential at a point ({tex}4 {~m}, 3 {~m}{/tex}).
question_text: A small conducting sphere A of radius r charged to a potential V, is enclosed by a spherical conducting shell B of radius R. If A and {tex}B{/tex} are connected by a thin wire, calculate the final potential on sphere {tex}A{/tex} and shell {tex}B{/tex} . Write two characteristics of equipotential surfaces. A uniform electric field of {tex}50 {NC}^{-1}{/tex} is set up in a region along {tex}+x{/tex} axis. If the potential at the origin (0,0) is 220 V, find the potential at a point ( {tex}4 {~m}, 3 {~m}{/tex} ).
explanation: Potential on sphere {tex}{A}={V}=\frac{{Q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}} {r}}{/tex} Charge on sphere {tex}{A}=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {rV}{/tex} The charge is transferred to shell {tex}B{/tex} . Potential on shell {tex}{B}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {rV}}{{R}}{/tex} Potential on shell {tex}{B}=\frac{r V}{R}{/tex} Potential on sphere {tex}{A}={/tex} Potential on shell B Characteristics of equipotential surfaces:- Potential at all points on the surface is same. Equipotential surface is normal to the direction of the electric field. The work done in moving a charge on an equipotential surface is zero. {tex} V_0-V=E d=50 \times 4 {/tex} {tex} V_0-V=200 V {/tex} {tex} V=220 V-200 V {/tex} {tex} V=20 V {/tex}
solution: Potential on sphere {tex}{A}={V}=\frac{{Q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}} {r}}{/tex} Charge on sphere {tex}{A}=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {rV}{/tex} The charge is transferred to shell {tex}B{/tex} . Potential on shell {tex}{B}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {rV}}{{R}}{/tex} Potential on shell {tex}{B}=\frac{r V}{R}{/tex} Potential on sphere {tex}{A}={/tex} Potential on shell B Characteristics of equipotential surfaces:- Potential at all points on the surface is same. Equipotential surface is normal to the direction of the electric field. The work done in moving a charge on an equipotential surface is zero. {tex} V_0-V=E d=50 \times 4 {/tex} {tex} V_0-V=200 V {/tex} {tex} V=220 V-200 V {/tex} {tex} V=20 V {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Potential on sphere {tex}{A}={V}=\frac{{Q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}} {r}}{/tex}
    Charge on sphere {tex}{A}=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {rV}{/tex}
    The charge is transferred to shell {tex}B{/tex}.
    Potential on shell {tex}{B}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {rV}}{{R}}{/tex}
    Potential on shell {tex}{B}=\frac{r V}{R}{/tex}
    Potential on sphere {tex}{A}={/tex} Potential on shell B
  2. Characteristics of equipotential surfaces:-
    • Potential at all points on the surface is same.
    • Equipotential surface is normal to the direction of the electric field.
    • The work done in moving a charge on an equipotential surface is zero.
      {tex} V_0-V=E d=50 \times 4 {/tex}
      {tex} V_0-V=200 V {/tex}
      {tex} V=220 V-200 V {/tex}
      {tex} V=20 V {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write Coulomb's law of electrostatics in vector form. Apply it to determine the electric field at a point due to a system of point charges.
  2. {tex}{ABC}{/tex} is an equilateral triangle of side {tex}l{/tex}. Two point charges {tex}+2 \mu {C}{/tex} each, are located at points B and C. Find the sign and magnitude of the point charge {tex}q{/tex} to be kept at the midpoint {tex}M{/tex} of the side BC, so that the net electric field at point A becomes zero.
question_text: Write Coulomb's law of electrostatics in vector form. Apply it to determine the electric field at a point due to a system of point charges. {tex}{ABC}{/tex} is an equilateral triangle of side {tex}l{/tex} . Two point charges {tex}+2 \mu {C}{/tex} each, are located at points B and C. Find the sign and magnitude of the point charge {tex}q{/tex} to be kept at the midpoint {tex}M{/tex} of the side BC, so that the net electric field at point A becomes zero.
explanation: {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{21}^2} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} Alternative: {tex} \vec{F}_{12}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{12}^2} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} OR {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat {r}{/tex} For determining electric field due to a system of point charges {tex} \vec{E}_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1}{r_{1 p}^2} \hat{r}_{1 p} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_2}{r_{2 p}^2} \hat{r}_{2 p} {/tex} and so on By superposition principle {tex} \vec{E} =\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2+\ldots+\vec{E}_n {/tex} {tex} \vec{E} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \sum\limits_{i=1}^n \frac{q_i}{r_{i p}^2} \widehat{r}_{i p} {/tex} From the figure {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A B}\right|= \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{r^2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} {/tex} {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A C}\right| =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} {/tex} {tex} \because\left|\vec{E}_{A B}\right| =\left|\vec{E}_{A C}\right| {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resultant of {tex}\vec{E}_{A B} \& \vec{E}_{A C}{/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_{B C}=\vec{E}_{A B}+\vec{E}_{A C} {/tex} {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{B C}\right|=2 E_{A B} \cos 30^{\circ} {/tex} {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{B C}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {/tex} {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A M}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{\left(\frac{l \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Net electric field at A is zero. {tex} \therefore E_{A M}=-E_{B C} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{\left(\frac{l \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}=-\frac{2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {/tex} {tex} q=\frac{-3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \times 10^{-6} {C} \text { or } \frac{-3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mu {C} {/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{21}^2} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} Alternative: {tex} \vec{F}_{12}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{12}^2} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} OR {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat {r}{/tex} For determining electric field due to a system of point charges {tex} \vec{E}_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1}{r_{1 p}^2} \hat{r}_{1 p} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_2}{r_{2 p}^2} \hat{r}_{2 p} {/tex} and so on By superposition principle {tex} \vec{E} =\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2+\ldots+\vec{E}_n {/tex} {tex} \vec{E} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \sum\limits_{i=1}^n \frac{q_i}{r_{i p}^2} \widehat{r}_{i p} {/tex} From the figure {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A B}\right|= \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{r^2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} {/tex} {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A C}\right| =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} {/tex} {tex} \because\left|\vec{E}_{A B}\right| =\left|\vec{E}_{A C}\right| {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resultant of {tex}\vec{E}_{A B} \& \vec{E}_{A C}{/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_{B C}=\vec{E}_{A B}+\vec{E}_{A C} {/tex} {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{B C}\right|=2 E_{A B} \cos 30^{\circ} {/tex} {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{B C}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {/tex} {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A M}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{\left(\frac{l \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Net electric field at A is zero. {tex} \therefore E_{A M}=-E_{B C} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{\left(\frac{l \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}=-\frac{2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {/tex} {tex} q=\frac{-3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \times 10^{-6} {C} \text { or } \frac{-3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mu {C} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{21}^2} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex}
    Alternative: {tex} \vec{F}_{12}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{12}^2} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex}
    OR {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat {r}{/tex}
    For determining electric field due to a system of point charges

    {tex} \vec{E}_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1}{r_{1 p}^2} \hat{r}_{1 p} {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{E}_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_2}{r_{2 p}^2} \hat{r}_{2 p} {/tex}
    and so on
    By superposition principle
    {tex} \vec{E} =\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2+\ldots+\vec{E}_n {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{E} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \sum\limits_{i=1}^n \frac{q_i}{r_{i p}^2} \widehat{r}_{i p} {/tex}

  2. From the figure
    {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A B}\right|= \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{r^2} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} {/tex}
    {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A C}\right| =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} {/tex}
    {tex} \because\left|\vec{E}_{A B}\right| =\left|\vec{E}_{A C}\right| {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resultant of {tex}\vec{E}_{A B} \& \vec{E}_{A C}{/tex}
    {tex} \vec{E}_{B C}=\vec{E}_{A B}+\vec{E}_{A C} {/tex}
    {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{B C}\right|=2 E_{A B} \cos 30^{\circ} {/tex}
    {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{B C}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {/tex}
    {tex} \left|\vec{E}_{A M}\right|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{\left(\frac{l \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} {/tex}
    {tex}\because{/tex} Net electric field at A is zero.
    {tex} \therefore E_{A M}=-E_{B C} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{\left(\frac{l \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}=-\frac{2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{l^2} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {/tex}
    {tex} q=\frac{-3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \times 10^{-6} {C} \text { or } \frac{-3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mu {C} {/tex}

question_html:
An electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} given by:
{tex}\vec{E} =100 \hat{i} \frac{N}{C} \text { for } x \gt 0{/tex}
{tex}=-100 \hat{i} \frac{N}{C} \text { for } x \lt 0{/tex}
exists in a region. A right circular cylinder of length 10 cm and radius 2 cm, is placed in the region such that its axis coincides with x -axis and its two faces are at {tex}x=-5 {~cm}{/tex} and {tex}x=5 {~cm}{/tex}. Calculate:

  1. the net outward flux through the cylinder, and
  2. the net charge inside the cylinder.
question_text: An electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} given by: {tex}\vec{E} =100 \hat{i} \frac{N}{C} \text { for } x \gt 0{/tex} {tex}=-100 \hat{i} \frac{N}{C} \text { for } x \lt 0{/tex} exists in a region. A right circular cylinder of length 10 cm and radius 2 cm, is placed in the region such that its axis coincides with x -axis and its two faces are at {tex}x=-5 {~cm}{/tex} and {tex}x=5 {~cm}{/tex} . Calculate: the net outward flux through the cylinder, and the net charge inside the cylinder.
explanation: {tex} \phi_L =\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta S} {/tex} {tex} =100(-\hat{i}) \cdot \Delta S(-\hat{i}) {/tex} {tex} =100 \times \pi\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =4 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \phi_R =100(\hat{i}) \cdot \Delta S(\hat{i}) {/tex} {tex} =100 \times \pi\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =4 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \phi_{\text {total }} =\phi_{{L}}+\phi_{{R}} {/tex} {tex} =8 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} =25.12 \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \text { Charge } {/tex} {tex} q =\varepsilon_0 \phi_{\text {total }} {/tex} {tex} =25.12 \times 10^{-2} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} {/tex} {tex} =0.22 \times 10^{-11} {C} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \phi_L =\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta S} {/tex} {tex} =100(-\hat{i}) \cdot \Delta S(-\hat{i}) {/tex} {tex} =100 \times \pi\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =4 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \phi_R =100(\hat{i}) \cdot \Delta S(\hat{i}) {/tex} {tex} =100 \times \pi\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =4 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \phi_{\text {total }} =\phi_{{L}}+\phi_{{R}} {/tex} {tex} =8 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} =25.12 \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \text { Charge } {/tex} {tex} q =\varepsilon_0 \phi_{\text {total }} {/tex} {tex} =25.12 \times 10^{-2} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} {/tex} {tex} =0.22 \times 10^{-11} {C} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \phi_L =\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta S} {/tex}
{tex} =100(-\hat{i}) \cdot \Delta S(-\hat{i}) {/tex}
{tex} =100 \times \pi\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 {/tex}
{tex} =4 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex}
{tex} \phi_R =100(\hat{i}) \cdot \Delta S(\hat{i}) {/tex}
{tex} =100 \times \pi\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 {/tex}
{tex} =4 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex}
{tex} \phi_{\text {total }} =\phi_{{L}}+\phi_{{R}} {/tex}
{tex} =8 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex}
{tex} =25.12 \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Charge } {/tex}
{tex} q =\varepsilon_0 \phi_{\text {total }} {/tex}
{tex} =25.12 \times 10^{-2} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} {/tex}
{tex} =0.22 \times 10^{-11} {C} {/tex}

A cube of side 0.1 m is placed, as shown in the figure, in a region where electric field {tex}\vec{E}=500 \times \hat{i}{/tex} exists. Here {tex}x{/tex} is in meters and {tex}E{/tex} in {tex}N^{-1}{/tex}. Calculate:

question_text: A cube of side 0.1 m is placed, as shown in the figure, in a region where electric field {tex}\vec{E}=500 \times \hat{i}{/tex} exists. Here {tex}x{/tex} is in meters and {tex}E{/tex} in {tex}N^{-1}{/tex} . Calculate: the flux passing through the cube, and the charge within the cube.
explanation: {tex} \varphi_{{L}}=\overrightarrow{E_L} \cdot \vec{A}=-[500 \times 0.1] \times\left[(0.1)^2\right]=-0.5 {~N} {~m}^2 {C}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \varphi_{{R}}=\overrightarrow{E_R} \cdot \vec{A}=[500 \times 0.2] \times\left[(0.1)^2\right]=1 {~N} {~m}^2 {C}^{-1} {/tex} Net flux {tex}=\varphi_{{L}}+\varphi_{{R}}=0.5 {~N} {~m}^2 {C}^{-1}{/tex} flux, {tex} \varphi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o} {/tex} charge, {tex} {q}=\varphi \times \varepsilon_{\circ} {/tex} {tex} =0.5 \varepsilon_0 {/tex} {tex} =4.4 \times 10^{-12} {C} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \varphi_{{L}}=\overrightarrow{E_L} \cdot \vec{A}=-[500 \times 0.1] \times\left[(0.1)^2\right]=-0.5 {~N} {~m}^2 {C}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \varphi_{{R}}=\overrightarrow{E_R} \cdot \vec{A}=[500 \times 0.2] \times\left[(0.1)^2\right]=1 {~N} {~m}^2 {C}^{-1} {/tex} Net flux {tex}=\varphi_{{L}}+\varphi_{{R}}=0.5 {~N} {~m}^2 {C}^{-1}{/tex} flux, {tex} \varphi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o} {/tex} charge, {tex} {q}=\varphi \times \varepsilon_{\circ} {/tex} {tex} =0.5 \varepsilon_0 {/tex} {tex} =4.4 \times 10^{-12} {C} {/tex}

question_html: A charged sphere of radius r has surface charge density. The electric field on its surface is E. If the radius of the sphere is doubled, keeping charge density the same, the ratio of the electric field on the old sphere to that on the new sphere will be:
question_text: A charged sphere of radius r has surface charge density. The electric field on its surface is E. If the radius of the sphere is doubled, keeping charge density the same, the ratio of the electric field on the old sphere to that on the new sphere will be:

options.explanation: Given that the electric field E on the surface of a charged sphere is determined by the surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} , the formula is: {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} When the radius of the sphere is doubled, but the surface charge density remains the same, the electric field on the surface of the sphere does not change, because it still depends solely on {tex}\sigma{/tex} and not on the radius. Therefore, for both the original sphere and the new sphere: {tex} E_{\text {old }} \downarrow \nabla_{\text {new }}=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} Thus, the ratio of the electric field on the old sphere to that on the new sphere is: {tex} \frac{E_{\text {old }}}{E_{\text {new }}}=1{/tex}

explanation: Given that the electric field E on the surface of a charged sphere is determined by the surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} , the formula is: {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} When the radius of the sphere is doubled, but the surface charge density remains the same, the electric field on the surface of the sphere does not change, because it still depends solely on {tex}\sigma{/tex} and not on the radius. Therefore, for both the original sphere and the new sphere: {tex} E_{\text {old }} \downarrow \nabla_{\text {new }}=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} Thus, the ratio of the electric field on the old sphere to that on the new sphere is: {tex} \frac{E_{\text {old }}}{E_{\text {new }}}=1{/tex}

question_html: Three small charged spheres X, Y and Z carrying charges + q, q and + q respectively are placed equidistant from each other, as shown in the figure. The spheres Y and Z are held in place. Initially X is also held in place, but is otherwise free to move. When X is released, the path followed by it will be:
question_text: Three small charged spheres X, Y and Z carrying charges + q, q and + q respectively are placed equidistant from each other, as shown in the figure. The spheres Y and Z are held in place. Initially X is also held in place, but is otherwise free to move. When X is released, the path followed by it will be:

options.explanation: Given that the charges on spheres X, Y, and Z are all +q+q+q, and spheres Y and Z are fixed, the path followed by sphere X when released will be influenced by the electrostatic forces exerted by Y and Z. Since both Y and Z exert repulsive forces on X, sphere X will be pushed away from both Y and Z. The net force acting on X will be at an angle, leading to a path that curves away from the line joining Y and Z.

explanation: Given that the charges on spheres X, Y, and Z are all +q+q+q, and spheres Y and Z are fixed, the path followed by sphere X when released will be influenced by the electrostatic forces exerted by Y and Z. Since both Y and Z exert repulsive forces on X, sphere X will be pushed away from both Y and Z. The net force acting on X will be at an angle, leading to a path that curves away from the line joining Y and Z.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A thin spherical shell of radius R has a uniform surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}. Using Gauss' law, deduce an expression for electric field (i) outside and (ii) inside the shell.
  2. Two long straight thin wires AB and CD have linear charge densities {tex}10 \mu {C} / {m}{/tex} and {tex}-20 \mu {C} / {m}{/tex}, respectively. They are kept parallel to each other at a distance 1 m. Find magnitude and direction of the net electric field at a point midway between them.
question_text: A thin spherical shell of radius R has a uniform surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} . Using Gauss' law, deduce an expression for electric field (i) outside and (ii) inside the shell. Two long straight thin wires AB and CD have linear charge densities {tex}10 \mu {C} / {m}{/tex} and {tex}-20 \mu {C} / {m}{/tex} , respectively. They are kept parallel to each other at a distance 1 m. Find magnitude and direction of the net electric field at a point midway between them.
explanation: Electric Field outside the shell Electric flux through Gaussian surface {tex}\Phi={E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}{/tex} Charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface {tex}{Q}=\sigma \times 4 \pi {R}^{2}{/tex} Using Gauss' law: {tex}\int \vec{{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{ds}}=\frac{{Q}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}=\frac{\left(\sigma 4 \pi {R}^{2}\right)}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {E}=\frac{\sigma {R}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} {r}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}=\frac{\sigma {R}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} {r}^{2}} \hat{{r}}{/tex} Field inside the shell Electric flux through Gaussian surface {tex}\Phi={E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2} (\because {r} \lt {R}){/tex} Charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface {tex}{Q}=0{/tex} By Gauss' Law {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}{E}=0{/tex} (Note: Award full credit of this part if a student writes directly {tex}{E}=0{/tex} , mentioning as there is no charge enclosed by Gaussian surface) Electric field due to a long straight charged wire of linear charged density {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}{/tex} Net electric field at the mid-point {tex}{E}_{\text {net }}={E}_{1}+{E}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda_{1}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}+\frac{\lambda_{2}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{\text {net }}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}\left[\lambda_{1}+\lambda_{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 9 \times 10^{9}}{0.5}[10+20] \times 10^{-6}{/tex} {tex}=1.08 \times 10^{6} {NC}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{\text {net }}{/tex} is directed towards CD.
solution: Electric Field outside the shell Electric flux through Gaussian surface {tex}\Phi={E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}{/tex} Charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface {tex}{Q}=\sigma \times 4 \pi {R}^{2}{/tex} Using Gauss' law: {tex}\int \vec{{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{ds}}=\frac{{Q}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}=\frac{\left(\sigma 4 \pi {R}^{2}\right)}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {E}=\frac{\sigma {R}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} {r}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}=\frac{\sigma {R}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} {r}^{2}} \hat{{r}}{/tex} Field inside the shell Electric flux through Gaussian surface {tex}\Phi={E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2} (\because {r} \lt {R}){/tex} Charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface {tex}{Q}=0{/tex} By Gauss' Law {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}{E}=0{/tex} (Note: Award full credit of this part if a student writes directly {tex}{E}=0{/tex} , mentioning as there is no charge enclosed by Gaussian surface) Electric field due to a long straight charged wire of linear charged density {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}{/tex} Net electric field at the mid-point {tex}{E}_{\text {net }}={E}_{1}+{E}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda_{1}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}+\frac{\lambda_{2}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{\text {net }}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}\left[\lambda_{1}+\lambda_{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 9 \times 10^{9}}{0.5}[10+20] \times 10^{-6}{/tex} {tex}=1.08 \times 10^{6} {NC}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{\text {net }}{/tex} is directed towards CD.
solution_html:
1. Electric Field outside the shell

      Electric flux through Gaussian surface
      {tex}\Phi={E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}{/tex}
      Charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface
      {tex}{Q}=\sigma \times 4 \pi {R}^{2}{/tex}
      Using Gauss' law: {tex}\int \vec{{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{ds}}=\frac{{Q}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
      {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}=\frac{\left(\sigma 4 \pi {R}^{2}\right)}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore {E}=\frac{\sigma {R}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} {r}^{2}}{/tex}
      {tex}\vec{{E}}=\frac{\sigma {R}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} {r}^{2}} \hat{{r}}{/tex}
    2. Field inside the shell

      Electric flux through Gaussian surface
      {tex}\Phi={E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2} (\because {r} \lt {R}){/tex}
      Charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface
      {tex}{Q}=0{/tex}
      By Gauss' Law
      {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}=0{/tex}
      i.e. {tex}{E}=0{/tex}
      (Note: Award full credit of this part if a student writes directly {tex}{E}=0{/tex}, mentioning as there is no charge enclosed by Gaussian surface)
  2. Electric field due to a long straight charged wire of linear charged density {tex}\lambda{/tex}
    {tex}{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}{/tex}

    Net electric field at the mid-point
    {tex}{E}_{\text {net }}={E}_{1}+{E}_{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\lambda_{1}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}+\frac{\lambda_{2}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}{/tex}
    {tex}{E}_{\text {net }}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}}\left[\lambda_{1}+\lambda_{2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 \times 9 \times 10^{9}}{0.5}[10+20] \times 10^{-6}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.08 \times 10^{6} {NC}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{{E}}_{\text {net }}{/tex} is directed towards CD.

question_html:
1. State Gauss's Law in electrostatics. Apply this to obtain the electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} at a point near a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet.
  2. Two long straight wires 1 and 2 are kept as shown in the figure. The linear charge density of the two wires are {tex}\lambda_1=10 \mu {C} / {m}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2=-20 \mu {C} / {m}{/tex}. Find the net force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} experienced by an electron held at point {tex}P{/tex}.
question_text: State Gauss's Law in electrostatics. Apply this to obtain the electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} at a point near a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet. Two long straight wires 1 and 2 are kept as shown in the figure. The linear charge density of the two wires are {tex}\lambda_1=10 \mu {C} / {m}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2=-20 \mu {C} / {m}{/tex} . Find the net force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} experienced by an electron held at point {tex}P{/tex} .
explanation: The surface integral of electric field over a closed surface is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the total charge enclosed by the surface. {tex} \oint \vec{E} . {d} \overrightarrow{{~S}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} (Gaussian surface can be cylindrical also) As seen from figure, only two faces 1 and 2 will contribute to the flux. Flux {tex}\vec{E} . {ds}{/tex} through both the surfaces is equal and add up. The charge enclosed by surface is {tex}\sigma A{/tex} , where {tex}\sigma{/tex} is surface charge density According to Gauss's theorem {tex} 2 E A=\sigma A / \varepsilon_0 {/tex} {tex} E=\sigma / 2 \varepsilon_0 {/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0} \hat{n} {/tex} where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is unit vector directed normally out of the plane {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} \hat{r}{/tex} According to question {tex} \vec{E}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)}(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} E_2(\text { at point } {P})=\frac{\lambda_2}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}=\frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(20 \times 10^{-2}\right)}(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} E_{\text {net }}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{0.1}+\frac{2}{0.2}\right)(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} =3.6 \times 10^6(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} \vec{F}_{\text {net }}=q \times \vec{E}_{\text {net }} {/tex} {tex} \vec{F}=-1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3.6 \times 10^6(-\hat{j}) {N} {/tex} {tex} =5.76 \times 10^{-13} {~N}(\hat{j}) {/tex}
solution: The surface integral of electric field over a closed surface is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the total charge enclosed by the surface. {tex} \oint \vec{E} . {d} \overrightarrow{{~S}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} (Gaussian surface can be cylindrical also) As seen from figure, only two faces 1 and 2 will contribute to the flux. Flux {tex}\vec{E} . {ds}{/tex} through both the surfaces is equal and add up. The charge enclosed by surface is {tex}\sigma A{/tex} , where {tex}\sigma{/tex} is surface charge density According to Gauss's theorem {tex} 2 E A=\sigma A / \varepsilon_0 {/tex} {tex} E=\sigma / 2 \varepsilon_0 {/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0} \hat{n} {/tex} where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is unit vector directed normally out of the plane {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} \hat{r}{/tex} According to question {tex} \vec{E}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)}(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} E_2(\text { at point } {P})=\frac{\lambda_2}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}=\frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(20 \times 10^{-2}\right)}(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} E_{\text {net }}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{0.1}+\frac{2}{0.2}\right)(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} =3.6 \times 10^6(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} \vec{F}_{\text {net }}=q \times \vec{E}_{\text {net }} {/tex} {tex} \vec{F}=-1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3.6 \times 10^6(-\hat{j}) {N} {/tex} {tex} =5.76 \times 10^{-13} {~N}(\hat{j}) {/tex}
solution_html:
1. The surface integral of electric field over a closed surface is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the total charge enclosed by the surface. {tex} \oint \vec{E} . {d} \overrightarrow{{~S}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}

    (Gaussian surface can be cylindrical also)
    As seen from figure, only two faces 1 and 2 will contribute to the flux. Flux {tex}\vec{E} . {ds}{/tex} through both the surfaces is equal and add up.
    The charge enclosed by surface is {tex}\sigma A{/tex}, where {tex}\sigma{/tex} is surface charge density According to Gauss's theorem
    {tex} 2 E A=\sigma A / \varepsilon_0 {/tex}
    {tex} E=\sigma / 2 \varepsilon_0 {/tex}
    {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0} \hat{n} {/tex} where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is unit vector directed normally out of the plane
  2. {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} \hat{r}{/tex}
    According to question
    {tex} \vec{E}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)}(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex}
    {tex} E_2(\text { at point } {P})=\frac{\lambda_2}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2} {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{E}=\frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(20 \times 10^{-2}\right)}(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex}
    {tex} E_{\text {net }}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{0.1}+\frac{2}{0.2}\right)(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex}
    {tex} =3.6 \times 10^6(-\hat{j}) {N} / {C} {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{F}_{\text {net }}=q \times \vec{E}_{\text {net }} {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{F}=-1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3.6 \times 10^6(-\hat{j}) {N} {/tex}
    {tex} =5.76 \times 10^{-13} {~N}(\hat{j}) {/tex}

question_html:
1. Using Gauss's law, show that the electric field {tex}\vec{{E}}{/tex} at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is given by {tex}\vec{{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{n}}{/tex} where symbols have their usual meanings.
  2. Electric field {tex}\vec{{E}}{/tex} in a region is given by
    {tex}\vec{{E}}=\left(5 x^{2}+2\right) \hat{{i}}{/tex}
    where E is in N/C and {tex}x{/tex} is in meters.
    A cube of side 10 cm is placed in the region as shown in figure.

    Calculate (1) the electric flux through the cube, and (2) the net charge enclosed by the cube.
question_text: Using Gauss's law, show that the electric field {tex}\vec{{E}}{/tex} at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is given by {tex}\vec{{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{n}}{/tex} where symbols have their usual meanings. Electric field {tex}\vec{{E}}{/tex} in a region is given by {tex}\vec{{E}}=\left(5 x^{2}+2\right) \hat{{i}}{/tex} where E is in N/C and {tex}x{/tex} is in meters. A cube of side 10 cm is placed in the region as shown in figure. Calculate (1) the electric flux through the cube, and (2) the net charge enclosed by the cube.
explanation: {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}=\int\limits_{1} \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}+\int\limits_{2} \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}{/tex} {tex}=2 {EA}{/tex} From Gauss's law {tex}\oint \vec{{E}} \cdot {~d} \vec{{s}} =\frac{{q}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}2 {EA} =\frac{\sigma {A}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Vectorially {tex}\vec{{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{n}}{/tex} Electric field is normally outward of the sheet. (1) Electric flux through the cube {tex}\phi =\phi_{{L}}+\phi_{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\phi =\int \overrightarrow{{E}}_{{L}} \cdot {~d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}+\int \overrightarrow{{E}}_{{R}} \cdot {~d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}{/tex} {tex}=-2 \times 100 \times 10^{-4}+\left[5 \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}+2\right]{/tex} {tex} \times 100 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}\phi =5 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}^{2} {C}^{-1}{/tex} (2) {tex}\phi =\frac{{q}_{{en}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{q}_{{en}} =\phi \cdot \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}=5 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}{/tex} {tex}=4.43 \times 10^{-15} {C}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}=\int\limits_{1} \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}+\int\limits_{2} \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}{/tex} {tex}=2 {EA}{/tex} From Gauss's law {tex}\oint \vec{{E}} \cdot {~d} \vec{{s}} =\frac{{q}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}2 {EA} =\frac{\sigma {A}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Vectorially {tex}\vec{{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{n}}{/tex} Electric field is normally outward of the sheet. (1) Electric flux through the cube {tex}\phi =\phi_{{L}}+\phi_{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\phi =\int \overrightarrow{{E}}_{{L}} \cdot {~d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}+\int \overrightarrow{{E}}_{{R}} \cdot {~d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}{/tex} {tex}=-2 \times 100 \times 10^{-4}+\left[5 \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}+2\right]{/tex} {tex} \times 100 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}\phi =5 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}^{2} {C}^{-1}{/tex} (2) {tex}\phi =\frac{{q}_{{en}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{q}_{{en}} =\phi \cdot \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}=5 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}{/tex} {tex}=4.43 \times 10^{-15} {C}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}=\int\limits_{1} \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}+\int\limits_{2} \overrightarrow{{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{{~s}}{/tex}
    {tex}=2 {EA}{/tex}
    From Gauss's law
    {tex}\oint \vec{{E}} \cdot {~d} \vec{{s}} =\frac{{q}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}2 {EA} =\frac{\sigma {A}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Vectorially {tex}\vec{{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{n}}{/tex}
    Electric field is normally outward of the sheet.
  2. (1) Electric flux through the cube
    {tex}\phi =\phi_{{L}}+\phi_{{R}}{/tex}
    {tex}\phi =\int \overrightarrow{{E}}_{{L}} \cdot {~d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}+\int \overrightarrow{{E}}_{{R}} \cdot {~d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}{/tex}
    {tex}=-2 \times 100 \times 10^{-4}+\left[5 \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}+2\right]{/tex} {tex} \times 100 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
    {tex}\phi =5 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}^{2} {C}^{-1}{/tex}
    (2) {tex}\phi =\frac{{q}_{{en}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}{q}_{{en}} =\phi \cdot \varepsilon_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}=5 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}{/tex}
    {tex}=4.43 \times 10^{-15} {C}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Define the term 'electric flux' and write its dimensions.
  2. A plane surface, in shape of a square of side 1 cm is placed in an electric field {tex}\vec{E}=\left(100 \frac{N}{C}\right) \hat{i}{/tex} such that the unit vector normal to the surface is given by {tex}\hat{{n}}=0.8 \hat{{i}}+0.6 \hat{{k}}{/tex}. Find the electric flux through the surface.
question_text: Define the term 'electric flux' and write its dimensions. A plane surface, in shape of a square of side 1 cm is placed in an electric field {tex}\vec{E}=\left(100 \frac{N}{C}\right) \hat{i}{/tex} such that the unit vector normal to the surface is given by {tex}\hat{{n}}=0.8 \hat{{i}}+0.6 \hat{{k}}{/tex} . Find the electric flux through the surface.
explanation: It is the measure of the total number of electric field lines passing through a surface normally. Alternatively Surface integral of electric field over a surface. Alternatively {tex}\phi_{{E}}=\vec{{E}} \cdot \vec{{A}}{/tex} {tex}\left[{ML}^{3} {~T}^{-3} {~A}^{-1}\right]{/tex} {tex}\phi_{{E}}=\vec{{E}} \cdot \vec{{A}}{/tex} {tex}=(100 \hat{{i}}) \cdot\left(10^{-4} \hat{{n}}\right){/tex} {tex}=(100 \hat{{i}}) \cdot(0 \cdot 8 \hat{{i}}+0 \cdot 6 \hat{{k}}) 10^{-4} {/tex} {tex}=8 \times 10^{-3} {Nm}^{2} {C}^{-1} {/tex}
solution: It is the measure of the total number of electric field lines passing through a surface normally. Alternatively Surface integral of electric field over a surface. Alternatively {tex}\phi_{{E}}=\vec{{E}} \cdot \vec{{A}}{/tex} {tex}\left[{ML}^{3} {~T}^{-3} {~A}^{-1}\right]{/tex} {tex}\phi_{{E}}=\vec{{E}} \cdot \vec{{A}}{/tex} {tex}=(100 \hat{{i}}) \cdot\left(10^{-4} \hat{{n}}\right){/tex} {tex}=(100 \hat{{i}}) \cdot(0 \cdot 8 \hat{{i}}+0 \cdot 6 \hat{{k}}) 10^{-4} {/tex} {tex}=8 \times 10^{-3} {Nm}^{2} {C}^{-1} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. It is the measure of the total number of electric field lines passing through a surface normally.
    Alternatively
    Surface integral of electric field over a surface.
    Alternatively
    {tex}\phi_{{E}}=\vec{{E}} \cdot \vec{{A}}{/tex}
    {tex}\left[{ML}^{3} {~T}^{-3} {~A}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
  2. {tex}\phi_{{E}}=\vec{{E}} \cdot \vec{{A}}{/tex}
    {tex}=(100 \hat{{i}}) \cdot\left(10^{-4} \hat{{n}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=(100 \hat{{i}}) \cdot(0 \cdot 8 \hat{{i}}+0 \cdot 6 \hat{{k}}) 10^{-4} {/tex}
    {tex}=8 \times 10^{-3} {Nm}^{2} {C}^{-1} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Four point charges of {tex}1 \mu {C},-2 \mu {C}, 1 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}-2 \mu {C}{/tex} are placed at the corners {tex}{A}, {B}, {C}{/tex} and D respectively, of a square of side 30 cm . Find the net force acting on a charge of {tex}4 \mu {C}{/tex} placed at the centre of the square.
question_text: Four point charges of {tex}1 \mu {C},-2 \mu {C}, 1 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}-2 \mu {C}{/tex} are placed at the corners {tex}{A}, {B}, {C}{/tex} and D respectively, of a square of side 30 cm . Find the net force acting on a charge of {tex}4 \mu {C}{/tex} placed at the centre of the square.
explanation: {tex}{OA}={OB}={OC}={OD}={r}{/tex} Net force on charge {tex}4 \mu {C}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}=\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OA}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OB}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OA}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OA}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}}=0{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OB}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OB}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}}=0{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}=0{/tex}
solution: {tex}{OA}={OB}={OC}={OD}={r}{/tex} Net force on charge {tex}4 \mu {C}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}=\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OA}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OB}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OA}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OA}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}}=0{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OB}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OB}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}}=0{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{OA}={OB}={OC}={OD}={r}{/tex}
Net force on charge {tex}4 \mu {C}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}=\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OA}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OB}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OA}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OA}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OC}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OB}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{~F}}_{{OB}}+\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{OD}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{~F}}=0{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two large plane sheets {tex}P_{1}{/tex} and {tex}P_{2}{/tex} having charge densities {tex}+\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-3 \sigma{/tex} respectively are arranged parallel to each other as shown in the figure. Find the net electric field ({tex}\vec{{E}}{/tex}) at points {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C.
question_text: Two large plane sheets {tex}P_{1}{/tex} and {tex}P_{2}{/tex} having charge densities {tex}+\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-3 \sigma{/tex} respectively are arranged parallel to each other as shown in the figure. Find the net electric field ( {tex}\vec{{E}}{/tex} ) at points {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C.
explanation: Electric field at {tex}{A}\left(\overrightarrow{{E}}_{{A}}\right){/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{{A}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}})+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(\hat{{i}}){/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{{A}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(\hat{{i}}){/tex} Electric field at {tex}B\left(\vec{E}_{B}\right){/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{{B}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}{/tex} Electric field at {tex}{C}\left(\vec{{E}}_{{C}}\right){/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{{c}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}}){/tex}
solution: Electric field at {tex}{A}\left(\overrightarrow{{E}}_{{A}}\right){/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{{A}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}})+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(\hat{{i}}){/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{{A}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(\hat{{i}}){/tex} Electric field at {tex}B\left(\vec{E}_{B}\right){/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{{B}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}{/tex} Electric field at {tex}{C}\left(\vec{{E}}_{{C}}\right){/tex} {tex}\vec{{E}}_{{c}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}}){/tex}
solution_html:
Electric field at {tex}{A}\left(\overrightarrow{{E}}_{{A}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\vec{{E}}_{{A}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}})+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(\hat{{i}}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{{E}}_{{A}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(\hat{{i}}){/tex}
Electric field at {tex}B\left(\vec{E}_{B}\right){/tex}
{tex}\vec{{E}}_{{B}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}{/tex}
Electric field at {tex}{C}\left(\vec{{E}}_{{C}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\vec{{E}}_{{c}} =\vec{{E}}_{1}+\vec{{E}}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{{i}}+\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(-\hat{{i}}){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Dipoles, whether electric or magnetic, are characterised by their dipole moments, which are vector quantities. Two equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance constitute an electric dipole, while a current carrying loop behaves as a magnetic dipole. Electric dipoles create electric fields around them. Electric dipoles experience a torque when placed in an external electric field. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)

  1. Two identical electric dipoles, each consisting of charges -q and +q separated by distance d, are arranged in {tex}{x}-{y}{/tex} plane such that their negative charges lie at the origin O and positive charges lie at points {tex}({d}, 0){/tex} and {tex}(0, {~d}){/tex} respectively. The net dipole moment of the system is:
    (A) {tex}-q d(\hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex}
    (B) {tex}q d(\hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex}
    (C) {tex}\operatorname{qd}(\hat{i}-\hat{j}){/tex}
    (D) {tex}\operatorname{qd}(\hat{j}-\hat{i}){/tex}
  2. {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} are magnitudes of electric field due to a dipole, consisting of charges -q and +q separated by distance 2a, at points {tex}{r}(\gg {a}){/tex} (1) on its axis, and (2) on equatorial plane, respectively. Then {tex}\left(\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}\right){/tex} is:
    (A) {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
    (B) {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    (C) 2
    (D) 4
  3. An electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}5.0 \times 10^{-8} {Cm}{/tex} is placed in a region where an electric field of magnitude {tex}1.0 \times 10^{3} {~N} / {C}{/tex} acts at a given instant. At that instant the electric field {tex}\vec{{E}}{/tex} is inclined at an angle of {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} to dipole moment {tex}\vec{{P}}{/tex}. The magnitude of torque acting on the dipole, at that instant is:
    (A) {tex}2.5 \times 10^{-5} {Nm}{/tex}
    (B) {tex}5.0 \times 10^{-5} {Nm}{/tex}
    (C) {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}{/tex}
    (D) {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-6} {Nm}{/tex}
    1. An electron is revolving with speed v around the proton in a hydrogen atom, in a circular orbit of radius {tex}r{/tex}. The magnitude of magnetic dipole moment of the electron is:
      (A) 4 evr
      (B) 2 evr
      (C) {tex}\frac{1}{2} {evr}{/tex}
      (D) {tex}\frac{1}{4} {evr}{/tex}

      OR

    2. A square loop of side 5.0 cm carries a current of 2.0 A. The magnitude of magnetic dipole moment associated with the loop is:
      (A) {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-3} {Am}^{2}{/tex}
      (B) {tex}5.0 \times 10^{-3} {Am}^{2}{/tex}
      (C) {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-2} {Am}^{2}{/tex}
      (D) {tex}5.0 \times 10^{-2} {Am}^{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two identical dipoles are arranged in x-y plane as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and the direction of net electric field at the origin O.
question_text: Two identical dipoles are arranged in x-y plane as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and the direction of net electric field at the origin O.
explanation: Dipole moment due to dipole BA is {tex}\vec{p_1}{/tex} & dipole moment due to dipole DC is {tex}\vec{p_2}{/tex} . Electric field {tex}\vec{E_1}{/tex} due to {tex}\vec{p_1}{/tex} is along OB. Electric field {tex}\vec{E_2}{/tex} due to {tex}\vec{p_2}{/tex} is along OD. Magnitude of resultant Electric field {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{E_{n e t}}\right|=2 \sqrt{2} E{/tex} Since {tex}\left|\vec {E_1}\right|=\left|\vec {E_2}\right|=E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{a^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}_{\text {net }}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \sqrt{2} q}{a^2}{/tex} Direction of {tex}\vec{E_{\text {net }}}{/tex} is 225 o to x-axis.
solution: Dipole moment due to dipole BA is {tex}\vec{p_1}{/tex} & dipole moment due to dipole DC is {tex}\vec{p_2}{/tex} . Electric field {tex}\vec{E_1}{/tex} due to {tex}\vec{p_1}{/tex} is along OB. Electric field {tex}\vec{E_2}{/tex} due to {tex}\vec{p_2}{/tex} is along OD. Magnitude of resultant Electric field {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{E_{n e t}}\right|=2 \sqrt{2} E{/tex} Since {tex}\left|\vec {E_1}\right|=\left|\vec {E_2}\right|=E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{a^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}_{\text {net }}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \sqrt{2} q}{a^2}{/tex} Direction of {tex}\vec{E_{\text {net }}}{/tex} is 225 o to x-axis.
solution_html:
Dipole moment due to dipole BA is {tex}\vec{p_1}{/tex} & dipole moment due to dipole DC is {tex}\vec{p_2}{/tex}.
Electric field {tex}\vec{E_1}{/tex} due to {tex}\vec{p_1}{/tex} is along OB.
Electric field {tex}\vec{E_2}{/tex} due to {tex}\vec{p_2}{/tex} is along OD.
Magnitude of resultant Electric field
{tex}\left|\overrightarrow{E_{n e t}}\right|=2 \sqrt{2} E{/tex}
Since {tex}\left|\vec {E_1}\right|=\left|\vec {E_2}\right|=E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{a^2}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{E}_{\text {net }}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 \sqrt{2} q}{a^2}{/tex}
Direction of {tex}\vec{E_{\text {net }}}{/tex} is 225o to x-axis.

subject: Physics
question_html: Use Gauss's law to obtain the expression for electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane thin sheet.
question_text: Use Gauss's law to obtain the expression for electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane thin sheet.
explanation: Derivation- Net flux through the Gaussian surface {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2EA By Gauss's law {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2EA where, q = {tex}\sigma{/tex} A {tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2EA {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2E {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}{/tex}
solution: Derivation- Net flux through the Gaussian surface {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2EA By Gauss's law {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2EA where, q = {tex}\sigma{/tex} A {tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2EA {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2E {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}{/tex}
solution_html:
Derivation-
Net flux through the Gaussian surface
{tex}\phi{/tex} = 2EA
By Gauss's law {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2EA
where, q = {tex}\sigma{/tex} A
{tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2EA
{tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} = 2E {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}{/tex}

explanation: Flux through the Gaussian surface = flux through the curved cylindrical part of the surface = E {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl The surface includes charges equal to {tex}\lambda{/tex} l, Gauss‟s law then gives E {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl = {tex}\frac{\lambda l}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}{/tex}
solution: Flux through the Gaussian surface = flux through the curved cylindrical part of the surface = E {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl The surface includes charges equal to {tex}\lambda{/tex} l, Gauss‟s law then gives E {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl = {tex}\frac{\lambda l}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}{/tex}
solution_html:
Flux through the Gaussian surface
= flux through the curved cylindrical part of the surface
= E {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rl
The surface includes charges equal to {tex}\lambda{/tex}l, Gauss‟s law then gives
E {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rl = {tex}\frac{\lambda l}{\epsilon_0}{/tex}
{tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define electric flux and write its SI unit.
  2. Use Gauss' law to law to obtain the expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet.
  3. A cube of side L is kept in space, as shown in the figure. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=(A x+B) \hat{i} \frac{N}{C}{/tex} exists in the region. Find the net charge enclosed by the cube.
question_text: Define electric flux and write its SI unit. Use Gauss' law to law to obtain the expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet. A cube of side L is kept in space, as shown in the figure. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=(A x+B) \hat{i} \frac{N}{C}{/tex} exists in the region. Find the net charge enclosed by the cube.
explanation: {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \vec{A}{/tex} Electric flux is the number of electric field lines passing through an area normally. S.I. unit of electric flux Nm 2 /C or V-m. Consider a uniformly charged sheet, Pass a Gaussian surface through it , From Gauss’s law:- {tex}\phi=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d A}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} 2EA = {tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Flux through the left surface {tex}\phi_L{/tex} = Eds cos 180 o = -Eds = - BL 2 Flux through the right surface {tex}\phi_R{/tex} = Eds cos 0 o = Eds (AL + B)L 2 = AL 3 + BL 2 Net flux = {tex}A L^3=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow q=A L^3 \epsilon_0{/tex}
solution: {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \vec{A}{/tex} Electric flux is the number of electric field lines passing through an area normally. S.I. unit of electric flux Nm 2 /C or V-m. Consider a uniformly charged sheet, Pass a Gaussian surface through it , From Gauss’s law:- {tex}\phi=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d A}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} 2EA = {tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Flux through the left surface {tex}\phi_L{/tex} = Eds cos 180 o = -Eds = - BL 2 Flux through the right surface {tex}\phi_R{/tex} = Eds cos 0 o = Eds (AL + B)L 2 = AL 3 + BL 2 Net flux = {tex}A L^3=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow q=A L^3 \epsilon_0{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \vec{A}{/tex}
    Electric flux is the number of electric field lines passing through an area normally.
    S.I. unit of electric flux Nm2/C or V-m.

  2. Consider a uniformly charged sheet, Pass a Gaussian surface through it , From Gauss’s law:- {tex}\phi=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d A}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    2EA = {tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
  3. Flux through the left surface {tex}\phi_L{/tex} = Eds cos 180o = -Eds
    = - BL2
    Flux through the right surface {tex}\phi_R{/tex} = Eds cos 0o = Eds
    (AL + B)L2 = AL3 + BL2
    Net flux = {tex}A L^3=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow q=A L^3 \epsilon_0{/tex}

question_html:
The inward and the outward electric flux through a Gaussian surface are 2{tex}\phi{/tex} and {tex}\phi{/tex} respectively.

  1. What is the net charge enclosed by the surface?
  2. If the net outward flux through the surface were zero, can it be concluded that there were no charges inside the surface? Justify your answer.
question_text: The inward and the outward electric flux through a Gaussian surface are 2 {tex}\phi{/tex} and {tex}\phi{/tex} respectively. What is the net charge enclosed by the surface? If the net outward flux through the surface were zero, can it be concluded that there were no charges inside the surface? Justify your answer.
explanation: Net outward flux = -2 {tex}\phi{/tex} + {tex}\phi{/tex} = - {tex}\phi{/tex} Charge enclosed = {tex}-\phi \varepsilon_o{/tex} No There may be charges (positive and negative) and net charge is zero.
solution: Net outward flux = -2 {tex}\phi{/tex} + {tex}\phi{/tex} = - {tex}\phi{/tex} Charge enclosed = {tex}-\phi \varepsilon_o{/tex} No There may be charges (positive and negative) and net charge is zero.
solution_html:

    = -{tex}\phi{/tex}
    Charge enclosed = {tex}-\phi \varepsilon_o{/tex}
  2. No
    There may be charges (positive and negative) and net charge is zero.

question_html: A sphere S1 of radius r1 enclosing a charge Q1 is surrounded by another concentric sphere S2 of radius r2 (r2 > r1). If there is a charge -Q2 in the space between S1 and S2, find the ratio of electric flux through S1 and S2.
question_text: A sphere S 1 of radius r 1 enclosing a charge Q 1 is surrounded by another concentric sphere S 2 of radius r 2 (r 2 > r 1 ). If there is a charge -Q 2 in the space between S 1 and S 2 , find the ratio of electric flux through S 1 and S 2 .
explanation: {tex}\phi_1=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}\phi_2=\frac{Q_1-Q_2}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\phi_1}{\phi_2}=\frac{Q_1}{Q_1-Q_2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\phi_1=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}\phi_2=\frac{Q_1-Q_2}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\phi_1}{\phi_2}=\frac{Q_1}{Q_1-Q_2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\phi_1=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex}
{tex}\phi_2=\frac{Q_1-Q_2}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\phi_1}{\phi_2}=\frac{Q_1}{Q_1-Q_2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Use Gauss' law to obtain an expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long thin straight wire with uniform linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
  2. An infinitely long positively charged straight wire has a linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}. An electron is revolving in a circle with a constant speed {tex}v{/tex} such that the wire passes through the centre, and is perpendicular to the plane, of the circle. Find the kinetic energy of the electron in terms of magnitudes of its charge and linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} on the wire.
  3. Draw a graph of kinetic energy as a function of linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
question_text: Use Gauss' law to obtain an expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long thin straight wire with uniform linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} . An infinitely long positively charged straight wire has a linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} . An electron is revolving in a circle with a constant speed {tex}v{/tex} such that the wire passes through the centre, and is perpendicular to the plane, of the circle. Find the kinetic energy of the electron in terms of magnitudes of its charge and linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} on the wire. Draw a graph of kinetic energy as a function of linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
explanation: Flux through the Gaussian surface {tex}\emptyset=E .2 \pi r l{/tex} According to Gauss’s law {tex}E .2 \pi \mathrm{rl}=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\because q=\lambda l{/tex} electric field = {tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \in_0 r}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}{/tex} {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=e E{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Kinetic energy K = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} eEr {tex}=\frac{1}{2} e \frac{\lambda \cdot r}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}=\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}{/tex} Kinetic energy K = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore K \propto \lambda{/tex} kinetic energy as a function of linear charged debnsity
solution: Flux through the Gaussian surface {tex}\emptyset=E .2 \pi r l{/tex} According to Gauss’s law {tex}E .2 \pi \mathrm{rl}=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\because q=\lambda l{/tex} electric field = {tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \in_0 r}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}{/tex} {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=e E{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Kinetic energy K = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} eEr {tex}=\frac{1}{2} e \frac{\lambda \cdot r}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}=\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}{/tex} Kinetic energy K = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore K \propto \lambda{/tex} kinetic energy as a function of linear charged debnsity
solution_html:
1. Flux through the Gaussian surface
    {tex}\emptyset=E .2 \pi r l{/tex}
    According to Gauss’s law
    {tex}E .2 \pi \mathrm{rl}=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\because q=\lambda l{/tex} electric field =
    {tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \in_0 r}{/tex}
  2. {tex}E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=e E{/tex}

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Kinetic energy K = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv2
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} eEr
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} e \frac{\lambda \cdot r}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r}=\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}{/tex}
  3. Kinetic energy K = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore K \propto \lambda{/tex} kinetic energy as a function of linear charged debnsity
