question_html:
A uniform electric field is represented as {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=\left(3 \times 10^3 \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{C}}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}{/tex}. Find the electric flux of this field through a square of side 10 cm when the:

  1. plane of the square is parallel to y-z plane, and
  2. the normal to plane of the square makes an angle of 60o with the x-axis.
question_text: A uniform electric field is represented as {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=\left(3 \times 10^3 \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{C}}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}{/tex} . Find the electric flux of this field through a square of side 10 cm when the: plane of the square is parallel to y-z plane, and the normal to plane of the square makes an angle of 60 o with the x-axis.
explanation: {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot A \hat{i}=E A{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ) = 30 NC -1 m 2 {tex}\phi{/tex} = EA cos 60 o = 15 NC -1 m 2
solution: {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot A \hat{i}=E A{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ) = 30 NC -1 m 2 {tex}\phi{/tex} = EA cos 60 o = 15 NC -1 m 2
solution_html:
1. {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot A \hat{i}=E A{/tex}
    = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} (100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)
    = 30 NC-1m2
  2. {tex}\phi{/tex} = EA cos 60o
    = 15 NC-1m2

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Coulomb's law in electrostatics and write it in vector form, for two charges.
  2. 'Gauss's law is based on the inverse-square dependence on distance contained in the Coulomb's law.' Explain.
  3. Two charges A (charge q) and B (charge 2q) are located at points (0, 0) and (a, a) respectively. Let {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} be the unit vectors along x-axis and y-axis respectively. Find the force exerted by A on B, in terms of {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{j}{/tex}.
question_text: State Coulomb's law in electrostatics and write it in vector form, for two charges. 'Gauss's law is based on the inverse-square dependence on distance contained in the Coulomb's law.' Explain. Two charges A (charge q) and B (charge 2q) are located at points (0, 0) and (a, a) respectively. Let {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} be the unit vectors along x-axis and y-axis respectively. Find the force exerted by A on B, in terms of {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} .
explanation: Force between two point charges varies inversely with the square of distance between the charges and is directly proportional to the product of magnitude of the two charges and acts along the line joining the two charges. {tex}\vec{F_{12}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{12}{ }^2} \widehat{r_{12}}{/tex} Where {tex}\vec{r_{12}}{/tex} is a vector from charge q 2 to charge q 1 . In derivation of Gauss’s law, flux is calculated using Coulomb’s law and surface area. Here coulomb’s law involves {tex}\frac{1}{r^2}{/tex} factor and surface area involves r 2 factor. When product is taken, the two factors cancel out and flux becomes independent of r. {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{A B}=a \hat{i}+a \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{r}=|\vec{A B}|=\sqrt{a^2+a^2}=\sqrt{2} a{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \times \frac{q \times 2 q}{(\sqrt{2} a)^2} \times \frac{(a \hat{i}+a \widehat{j})}{\sqrt{2} a}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \times \frac{2 q^2}{2 a^2} \times \frac{(\hat{i}+\hat{j})}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \times \frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2} a^2} \times(\hat{i}+\widehat{j}){/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{q^2}{4 \sqrt{2} \pi \in_o a^2}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex} {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{q^2}{a^2}{/tex}
solution: Force between two point charges varies inversely with the square of distance between the charges and is directly proportional to the product of magnitude of the two charges and acts along the line joining the two charges. {tex}\vec{F_{12}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{12}{ }^2} \widehat{r_{12}}{/tex} Where {tex}\vec{r_{12}}{/tex} is a vector from charge q 2 to charge q 1 . In derivation of Gauss’s law, flux is calculated using Coulomb’s law and surface area. Here coulomb’s law involves {tex}\frac{1}{r^2}{/tex} factor and surface area involves r 2 factor. When product is taken, the two factors cancel out and flux becomes independent of r. {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{A B}=a \hat{i}+a \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{r}=|\vec{A B}|=\sqrt{a^2+a^2}=\sqrt{2} a{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \times \frac{q \times 2 q}{(\sqrt{2} a)^2} \times \frac{(a \hat{i}+a \widehat{j})}{\sqrt{2} a}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \times \frac{2 q^2}{2 a^2} \times \frac{(\hat{i}+\hat{j})}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \times \frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2} a^2} \times(\hat{i}+\widehat{j}){/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{q^2}{4 \sqrt{2} \pi \in_o a^2}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex} {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{q^2}{a^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Force between two point charges varies inversely with the square of distance between the charges and is directly proportional to the product of magnitude of the two charges and acts along the line joining the two charges.
    {tex}\vec{F_{12}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{12}{ }^2} \widehat{r_{12}}{/tex}
    Where {tex}\vec{r_{12}}{/tex} is a vector from charge q2 to charge q1.
  2. In derivation of Gauss’s law, flux is calculated using Coulomb’s law and surface area. Here coulomb’s law involves {tex}\frac{1}{r^2}{/tex} factor and surface area involves r2 factor. When product is taken, the two factors cancel out and flux becomes independent of r.

  3. {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{A B}=a \hat{i}+a \hat{j}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{r}=|\vec{A B}|=\sqrt{a^2+a^2}=\sqrt{2} a{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \times \frac{q \times 2 q}{(\sqrt{2} a)^2} \times \frac{(a \hat{i}+a \widehat{j})}{\sqrt{2} a}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \times \frac{2 q^2}{2 a^2} \times \frac{(\hat{i}+\hat{j})}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \times \frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2} a^2} \times(\hat{i}+\widehat{j}){/tex}
    {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{q^2}{4 \sqrt{2} \pi \in_o a^2}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex}
    {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{q^2}{a^2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two small conducting balls A and B of radius r1 and r2 have charges q1 and q2 respectively. They are connected by a wire. Obtain the expression for charges on A and B, in equilibrium.
question_text: Two small conducting balls A and B of radius r 1 and r 2 have charges q 1 and q 2 respectively. They are connected by a wire. Obtain the expression for charges on A and B, in equilibrium.
explanation: According to law of conservation of charge q i = q f q 1 + q 2 = {tex}q_1^{\prime}+q_2^{\prime}{/tex} = Q When two balls are connected with wire V 1 = V 2 {tex}\frac{k q_1^{\prime}}{r_1}=\frac{k q_2^{\prime}}{r_2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{q_1^{\prime}}{r_1}=\frac{q_2^{\prime}}{r_2}{/tex} {tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=q_2^{\prime} r_1{/tex} {tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=\left(Q-q_1^{\prime}\right) r_1{/tex} {tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=Q r_1-q_1^{\prime} r_1{/tex} {tex}q_1^{\prime}{/tex} (r 1 + r 2 ) = Qr 1 {tex}q_1^{\prime}=\frac{Q r_1}{r_1+r_2}=\frac{\left(q_1+q_2\right) r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} {tex}q_2^{\prime}=Q-q_1^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}=Q-\frac{Q r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{Q r_2}{r_1+r_2}=\frac{\left(q_1+q_2\right) r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
solution: According to law of conservation of charge q i = q f q 1 + q 2 = {tex}q_1^{\prime}+q_2^{\prime}{/tex} = Q When two balls are connected with wire V 1 = V 2 {tex}\frac{k q_1^{\prime}}{r_1}=\frac{k q_2^{\prime}}{r_2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{q_1^{\prime}}{r_1}=\frac{q_2^{\prime}}{r_2}{/tex} {tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=q_2^{\prime} r_1{/tex} {tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=\left(Q-q_1^{\prime}\right) r_1{/tex} {tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=Q r_1-q_1^{\prime} r_1{/tex} {tex}q_1^{\prime}{/tex} (r 1 + r 2 ) = Qr 1 {tex}q_1^{\prime}=\frac{Q r_1}{r_1+r_2}=\frac{\left(q_1+q_2\right) r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} {tex}q_2^{\prime}=Q-q_1^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}=Q-\frac{Q r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{Q r_2}{r_1+r_2}=\frac{\left(q_1+q_2\right) r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to law of conservation of charge
qi = qf
q1 + q2 = {tex}q_1^{\prime}+q_2^{\prime}{/tex} = Q
When two balls are connected with wire
V1 = V2
{tex}\frac{k q_1^{\prime}}{r_1}=\frac{k q_2^{\prime}}{r_2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{q_1^{\prime}}{r_1}=\frac{q_2^{\prime}}{r_2}{/tex}
{tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=q_2^{\prime} r_1{/tex}
{tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=\left(Q-q_1^{\prime}\right) r_1{/tex}
{tex}q_1^{\prime} r_2=Q r_1-q_1^{\prime} r_1{/tex}
{tex}q_1^{\prime}{/tex} (r1 + r2) = Qr1
{tex}q_1^{\prime}=\frac{Q r_1}{r_1+r_2}=\frac{\left(q_1+q_2\right) r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
{tex}q_2^{\prime}=Q-q_1^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}=Q-\frac{Q r_1}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{Q r_2}{r_1+r_2}=\frac{\left(q_1+q_2\right) r_2}{r_1+r_2}{/tex}

question_html: Which one of the following is not a scalar quantity?
question_text: Which one of the following is not a scalar quantity?

options.explanation: Electric field is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction, whereas the others (voltage, resistivity, and power) are scalar quantities.

explanation: Electric field is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction, whereas the others (voltage, resistivity, and power) are scalar quantities.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Consider two identical point charges located at points (0, 0) and (a, 0).
    1. Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric field is zero?
    2. Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric potential is zero?
      Justify your answers for each case.
  2. State the significance of negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system of charges.
    Three charges are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle ABC of side 2.0 m as shown in figure. Calculate the electric potential energy of the system of three charges.
question_text: Consider two identical point charges located at points (0, 0) and (a, 0). Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric field is zero? Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric potential is zero? Justify your answers for each case. State the significance of negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system of charges. Three charges are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle ABC of side 2.0 m as shown in figure. Calculate the electric potential energy of the system of three charges.
explanation: Yes, electric field is zero at mid point. Electric field being a vector quantity, its resultant is zero. No, potential cannot be zero on line joining the charges. Electric potential being a scalar quantity, the net potential due to two identical charges cannot be zero. Negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system signifies that the system has attractive forces. {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \times \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex} {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0}\left[\frac{q_A q_B}{r}+\frac{q_B q_c}{r}+\frac{q_c q_A}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{2}[-16-8+8] \times 10^{-12}{/tex} = -7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 J
solution: Yes, electric field is zero at mid point. Electric field being a vector quantity, its resultant is zero. No, potential cannot be zero on line joining the charges. Electric potential being a scalar quantity, the net potential due to two identical charges cannot be zero. Negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system signifies that the system has attractive forces. {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \times \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex} {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0}\left[\frac{q_A q_B}{r}+\frac{q_B q_c}{r}+\frac{q_c q_A}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{2}[-16-8+8] \times 10^{-12}{/tex} = -7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 J
solution_html:
1. Yes, electric field is zero at mid point.
      Electric field being a vector quantity, its resultant is zero.
    2. No, potential cannot be zero on line joining the charges.
      Electric potential being a scalar quantity, the net potential due to two identical charges cannot be zero.
  2. Negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system signifies that the system has attractive forces.

    {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \times \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex}
    {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0}\left[\frac{q_A q_B}{r}+\frac{q_B q_c}{r}+\frac{q_c q_A}{r}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{2}[-16-8+8] \times 10^{-12}{/tex}
    = -7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 J

subject: Physics
question_html: Two charged conducting spheres of radii a and b are connected to each other by a wire. Find the ratio of the electric fields at their surfaces.
question_text: Two charged conducting spheres of radii a and b are connected to each other by a wire. Find the ratio of the electric fields at their surfaces.
explanation: Let a be the radius of a sphere {tex}{A}, {Q}_{{A}}{/tex} be the charge on the sphere and {tex}{C}_{{A}}{/tex} be the capacitance of that sphere. Let b be the radius of a sphere {tex}B, Q_B{/tex} be the charge on the sphere, and {tex}C_B{/tex} be the capacitance of that sphere. Since the two spheres are connected by a wire, their potential {tex}({V}){/tex} will become equal. Let {tex}{E}_{{A}}{/tex} be the electric field of sphere A and {tex}{E}_{{B}}{/tex} by the electric field of sphere B. Then, {tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\left(\frac{Q_A}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 a^2}\right) \times\left(\frac{b^2 4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{Q_B}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\frac{Q_A}{Q_B} \times \frac{b^2}{a^2}{/tex} ...(i) However, {tex}\frac{Q_A}{Q_B}=\frac{C_A V}{C_B V}{/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}\frac{C_A}{C_B}=\frac{a}{b}{/tex} ...(iii) Putting the values of (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get, {tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex} Therefore, the required ratio of electric fields at the surface of the spheres is {tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex} .
solution: Let a be the radius of a sphere {tex}{A}, {Q}_{{A}}{/tex} be the charge on the sphere and {tex}{C}_{{A}}{/tex} be the capacitance of that sphere. Let b be the radius of a sphere {tex}B, Q_B{/tex} be the charge on the sphere, and {tex}C_B{/tex} be the capacitance of that sphere. Since the two spheres are connected by a wire, their potential {tex}({V}){/tex} will become equal. Let {tex}{E}_{{A}}{/tex} be the electric field of sphere A and {tex}{E}_{{B}}{/tex} by the electric field of sphere B. Then, {tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\left(\frac{Q_A}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 a^2}\right) \times\left(\frac{b^2 4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{Q_B}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\frac{Q_A}{Q_B} \times \frac{b^2}{a^2}{/tex} ...(i) However, {tex}\frac{Q_A}{Q_B}=\frac{C_A V}{C_B V}{/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}\frac{C_A}{C_B}=\frac{a}{b}{/tex} ...(iii) Putting the values of (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get, {tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex} Therefore, the required ratio of electric fields at the surface of the spheres is {tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let a be the radius of a sphere {tex}{A}, {Q}_{{A}}{/tex} be the charge on the sphere and {tex}{C}_{{A}}{/tex} be the capacitance of that sphere. Let b be the radius of a sphere {tex}B, Q_B{/tex} be the charge on the sphere, and {tex}C_B{/tex} be the capacitance of that sphere. Since the two spheres are connected by a wire, their potential {tex}({V}){/tex} will become equal. Let {tex}{E}_{{A}}{/tex} be the electric field of sphere A and {tex}{E}_{{B}}{/tex} by the electric field of sphere B. Then,
{tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\left(\frac{Q_A}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 a^2}\right) \times\left(\frac{b^2 4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{Q_B}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\frac{Q_A}{Q_B} \times \frac{b^2}{a^2}{/tex} ...(i)
However, {tex}\frac{Q_A}{Q_B}=\frac{C_A V}{C_B V}{/tex}  ...(ii)
and {tex}\frac{C_A}{C_B}=\frac{a}{b}{/tex}  ...(iii)
Putting the values of (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get,
{tex} \frac{E_A}{E_B}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
Therefore, the required ratio of electric fields at the surface of the spheres is {tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex}.

If they are brought in contact and then separated to a distance of {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}{/tex}, what is the new force between them in terms of F?
question_text: A uniformly charged large plane sheet has charge density {tex}\sigma=\left(\frac{1}{18 \pi}\right) \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2{/tex} . Find the electric field at point A which is 50 cm from the sheet. Consider a straight line with three points P, Q and R, placed 50 cm from the charged sheet on the right side as shown in the figure. At which of these points, does the magnitude of the electric field due to the sheet remain the same as that at point A and why? Two small identical conducting spheres carrying charge 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and -20 {tex}\mu{/tex} C when separated by a distance of r, experience a force F each. If they are brought in contact and then separated to a distance of {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}{/tex} , what is the new force between them in terms of F?
explanation: Electric field due to a uniformly charged sheet E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \in_o}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 N /C outward At point Q For finite plane sheet, electric field is uniform in the middle. At the edges it will be curved. q' 1 = q' 2 = {tex}\frac{10+(-20)}{2}{/tex} = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C F' = {tex}\frac{F}{2}{/tex}
solution: Electric field due to a uniformly charged sheet E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \in_o}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 N /C outward At point Q For finite plane sheet, electric field is uniform in the middle. At the edges it will be curved. q' 1 = q' 2 = {tex}\frac{10+(-20)}{2}{/tex} = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C F' = {tex}\frac{F}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Electric field due to a uniformly charged sheet
    E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \in_o}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex}10-6 N /C outward
    At point Q For finite plane sheet, electric field is uniform in the middle. At the edges it will be curved.
  2. q'1 = q'2 = {tex}\frac{10+(-20)}{2}{/tex} = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex}C
    F' = {tex}\frac{F}{2}{/tex}

question_html: If the electric flux entering and leaving a closed surface in air are {tex}\phi_1{/tex} and {tex}\phi_2{/tex} respectively, the net electric charge enclosed within the surface is ________.
question_text: If the electric flux entering and leaving a closed surface in air are {tex}\phi_1{/tex} and {tex}\phi_2{/tex} respectively, the net electric charge enclosed within the surface is ________.
explanation: {tex}\in_{0}\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right){/tex} The flux entering an enclosed surface is taken as negative and the flux leaving the surface is taken as positive, by convention. Therefore the net flux leaving the enclosed surface = {tex}\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} the charge enclosed in the surface by Gauss's law is q = {tex}\in_{0}\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right){/tex}
solution: {tex}\in_{0}\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right){/tex} The flux entering an enclosed surface is taken as negative and the flux leaving the surface is taken as positive, by convention. Therefore the net flux leaving the enclosed surface = {tex}\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} the charge enclosed in the surface by Gauss's law is q = {tex}\in_{0}\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\in_{0}\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right){/tex} 
The flux entering an enclosed surface is taken as negative and the flux leaving the surface is taken as positive, by convention. Therefore the net flux leaving the enclosed surface = {tex}\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the charge enclosed in the surface by Gauss's law is q = {tex}\in_{0}\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: What will be the ratio of the surface charge density of the inner surface to that of the outer surface of a hollow conducting sphere if a point charge is placed at the centre of the hollow conducting sphere having internal radius r and outer radius 2r?
question_text: What will be the ratio of the surface charge density of the inner surface to that of the outer surface of a hollow conducting sphere if a point charge is placed at the centre of the hollow conducting sphere having internal radius r and outer radius 2r ?
explanation: If + q charge is placed at the centre of the hollow sphere, charge on inner surface will be -q. So, charge density at the inner surface = {tex}\frac {-q}{4\pi r^2}{/tex} Charge on outer surface will be +q. So, charge density at the outer surface = {tex}\frac {q}{4 \pi(2r)^2}{/tex} . So, the required ration = {tex}\frac {[ \frac {-q}{4\pi r^2}]}{ [ \frac {q}{4 \pi(2r)^2]}}{/tex} = -4 : 1
solution: If + q charge is placed at the centre of the hollow sphere, charge on inner surface will be -q. So, charge density at the inner surface = {tex}\frac {-q}{4\pi r^2}{/tex} Charge on outer surface will be +q. So, charge density at the outer surface = {tex}\frac {q}{4 \pi(2r)^2}{/tex} . So, the required ration = {tex}\frac {[ \frac {-q}{4\pi r^2}]}{ [ \frac {q}{4 \pi(2r)^2]}}{/tex} = -4 : 1
solution_html:
If + q charge is placed at the centre of the hollow sphere, charge on inner surface will be -q. So, charge density at the inner surface ={tex}\frac {-q}{4\pi r^2}{/tex}
Charge on outer surface will be +q. So, charge density at the outer surface = {tex}\frac {q}{4 \pi(2r)^2}{/tex}.
So, the required ration = {tex}\frac {[ \frac {-q}{4\pi r^2}]}{ [ \frac {q}{4 \pi(2r)^2]}}{/tex}
= -4 : 1

subject: Physics
question_html:
A hollow conducting sphere of inner radius r1 and outer radius r2 has a charge Q on its surface. A point charge -q is also placed at the centre of the sphere.

  1. What is the surface charge density on the
    1. inner and 
    2. outer surface of the sphere?
  2. Use Gauss' law of electrostatics to obtain the expression for the electric field at a point lying outside the sphere.
question_text: A hollow conducting sphere of inner radius r 1 and outer radius r 2 has a charge Q on its surface. A point charge -q is also placed at the centre of the sphere. What is the surface charge density on the inner and outer surface of the sphere? Use Gauss' law of electrostatics to obtain the expression for the electric field at a point lying outside the sphere.
explanation: Charge placed at the centre of the hollow sphere is -q. Hence, a charge of magnitude +q will be induced to the inner surface. Therefore, total charge on the inner surface of the shell is +q. Surface charge density at the inner surface, {tex}\sigma_{\text {inner }}=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { Inner surface area }}=\frac{+q}{4 \pi r_1^2}{/tex} Electric field at a point lying outside the sphere at a distance rfrom the centre of the sphere: Applying Gauss' theorem Flux = {tex}\phi=\frac{\text { Charge enclosed }}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} Or, E {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{Q-q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{Q-q}{4 \pi r^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
solution: Charge placed at the centre of the hollow sphere is -q. Hence, a charge of magnitude +q will be induced to the inner surface. Therefore, total charge on the inner surface of the shell is +q. Surface charge density at the inner surface, {tex}\sigma_{\text {inner }}=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { Inner surface area }}=\frac{+q}{4 \pi r_1^2}{/tex} Electric field at a point lying outside the sphere at a distance rfrom the centre of the sphere: Applying Gauss' theorem Flux = {tex}\phi=\frac{\text { Charge enclosed }}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} Or, E {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{Q-q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{Q-q}{4 \pi r^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Charge placed at the centre of the hollow sphere is -q. Hence, a charge of magnitude +q will be induced to the inner surface. Therefore, total charge on the inner surface of the shell is +q. Surface charge density at the inner surface, 
    {tex}\sigma_{\text {inner }}=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { Inner surface area }}=\frac{+q}{4 \pi r_1^2}{/tex}
  2. Electric field at a point lying outside the sphere at a distance rfrom the centre of the sphere: Applying Gauss' theorem
    Flux = {tex}\phi=\frac{\text { Charge enclosed }}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex}
    Or, E {tex}\times{/tex} 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = {tex}\frac{Q-q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{Q-q}{4 \pi r^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Using Gauss law, derive expression for electric field due to a spherical shell of uniform charge distribution {tex}\sigma{/tex} and radius R at a point lying at a distance x from the centre of shell, such that
    1. 0 < x < R, and
    2. x > R.
  2. An electric field is uniform and acts along + x direction in the region of positive x. It is also uniform with the same magnitude but acts in - x direction in the region of negative x. The value of the field is E = 200 N/C for x > 0 and E = -200 N/C for x < 0. A right circular cylinder of length 20 cm and radius 5 cm has its centre at the origin and its axis along the x-axis so that one flat face is at x = + 10 cm and the other is at x = -10 cm.
    Find:
    1. The net outward flux through the cylinder.
    2. The net charge present inside the cylinder.
question_text: Using Gauss law, derive expression for electric field due to a spherical shell of uniform charge distribution {tex}\sigma{/tex} and radius R at a point lying at a distance x from the centre of shell, such that 0 < x < R, and x > R. An electric field is uniform and acts along + x direction in the region of positive x. It is also uniform with the same magnitude but acts in - x direction in the region of negative x. The value of the field is E = 200 N/C for x > 0 and E = -200 N/C for x < 0. A right circular cylinder of length 20 cm and radius 5 cm has its centre at the origin and its axis along the x-axis so that one flat face is at x = + 10 cm and the other is at x = -10 cm. Find: The net outward flux through the cylinder. The net charge present inside the cylinder.
explanation: electric field due to a spherical shell of uniform charge distribution {tex}\sigma{/tex} and radius R at a point lying at a distance x from the centre of shell, 0 < x < R As x lies inside the shell, the charge enclosed by the gaussian surface is zero q = 0 Flux through the Gaussian surface {tex}\phi=E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} Applying Gauss's theorem, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} E = 0 x > R The total charge q inside the gaussian surface is the charge on the shell of radius R and area {tex}4 \pi R^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} q = {tex}4 \pi R^{2} \sigma{/tex} Flux through gaussian surface {tex}\phi=E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} Applying gauss theorem, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}E \times 4 \pi x^{2}=\frac{4 \pi R^{2} \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\sigma R^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} x^{2}}{/tex} Outward flux through left face is {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} = {tex}-200 \times \pi(0.05)^{2}(-\hat{i}) \hat{i}{/tex} = +1.57 Nm 2 C -1 Inside flux through left face is {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} {tex}200 \times \pi(0.05)^{2}(-\hat{i}) \hat{i}{/tex} = +1.57Nm 2 C -1 Net outward flux through the cylinder {tex}\phi{/tex} = 1.57 + 1.57 + 0 = 3.14 Nm 2 C -1 The net charge within the cylinder can be found by using Gauss’s law which gives q = {tex}\in_o \phi{/tex} = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C = 2.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 C
solution: electric field due to a spherical shell of uniform charge distribution {tex}\sigma{/tex} and radius R at a point lying at a distance x from the centre of shell, 0 < x < R As x lies inside the shell, the charge enclosed by the gaussian surface is zero q = 0 Flux through the Gaussian surface {tex}\phi=E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} Applying Gauss's theorem, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} E = 0 x > R The total charge q inside the gaussian surface is the charge on the shell of radius R and area {tex}4 \pi R^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} q = {tex}4 \pi R^{2} \sigma{/tex} Flux through gaussian surface {tex}\phi=E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} Applying gauss theorem, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}E \times 4 \pi x^{2}=\frac{4 \pi R^{2} \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\sigma R^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} x^{2}}{/tex} Outward flux through left face is {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} = {tex}-200 \times \pi(0.05)^{2}(-\hat{i}) \hat{i}{/tex} = +1.57 Nm 2 C -1 Inside flux through left face is {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} {tex}200 \times \pi(0.05)^{2}(-\hat{i}) \hat{i}{/tex} = +1.57Nm 2 C -1 Net outward flux through the cylinder {tex}\phi{/tex} = 1.57 + 1.57 + 0 = 3.14 Nm 2 C -1 The net charge within the cylinder can be found by using Gauss’s law which gives q = {tex}\in_o \phi{/tex} = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C = 2.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 C
solution_html:
1. electric field due to a spherical shell of uniform charge distribution {tex}\sigma{/tex} and radius R at a point lying at a distance x from the centre of shell,
    1. 0 < x < R
      As x lies inside the shell, the charge enclosed by the gaussian surface is zero
      q = 0
      Flux through the Gaussian surface
      {tex}\phi=E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} 
      Applying Gauss's theorem,
      {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} 
      {tex}E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} = 0 
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} E = 0
    2. x > R
      The total charge q inside the gaussian surface is the charge on the shell of radius R and area {tex}4 \pi R^{2}{/tex} 
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} q = {tex}4 \pi R^{2} \sigma{/tex} 
      Flux through gaussian surface 
      {tex}\phi=E \times 4 \pi x^{2}{/tex} 
      Applying gauss theorem,
      {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} 
      {tex}E \times 4 \pi x^{2}=\frac{4 \pi R^{2} \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} 
      E = {tex}\frac{\sigma R^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} x^{2}}{/tex} 

    1. Outward flux through left face is 
      {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex}
      = {tex}-200 \times \pi(0.05)^{2}(-\hat{i}) \hat{i}{/tex}
      = +1.57 Nm2 C-1 
      Inside flux through left face is 
      {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} 
      {tex}200 \times \pi(0.05)^{2}(-\hat{i}) \hat{i}{/tex}
      = +1.57Nm2C-1 
      Net outward flux through the cylinder
      {tex}\phi{/tex} = 1.57 + 1.57 + 0 = 3.14 Nm2 C-1 
    2. The net charge within the cylinder can be found by using Gauss’s law which gives
      q = {tex}\in_o \phi{/tex}
      = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 C
      = 2.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 C

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Use Gauss’s law to show that due to a uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R, the electric field at any point situated outside the shell at a distance r from its centre is equal to the electric field at the same point, when the entire charge on the shell were concentrated at its centre. Also plot the graph showing the variation of electric field with r, for r {tex}\le{/tex} R and r {tex}\ge{/tex} R.
  2. Two-point charges of + 1 {tex}\mu{/tex}C and + 4 {tex}\mu{/tex}C are kept 30 cm apart. How far from the + 1 {tex}\mu{/tex}C charge on the line joining the two charges, will the net electric field be zero?
question_text: Use Gauss’s law to show that due to a uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R, the electric field at any point situated outside the shell at a distance r from its centre is equal to the electric field at the same point, when the entire charge on the shell were concentrated at its centre. Also plot the graph showing the variation of electric field with r, for r {tex}\le{/tex} R and r {tex}\ge{/tex} R. Two-point charges of + 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and + 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} C are kept 30 cm apart. How far from the + 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C charge on the line joining the two charges, will the net electric field be zero?
explanation: Let Surface charge density of spherical shell = {tex}\lambda{/tex} Radius = R, Consider Spherical Gaussian surface of radius = r from Gauss Law {tex}\phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}_ \mathrm{in}}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda \times 4 \pi r^{2}}{\in_0}{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{4 \pi \lambda \mathrm{R}^{2}}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} ...(i) Also, {tex}\phi=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{E}\left(4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\right){/tex} ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) {tex}\mathrm{E}\left(4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\right)=\frac{4 \pi \lambda \mathrm{R}^{2}}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\lambda \mathrm{R}^{2}}{\in_0 \mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{\theta}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda \mathrm{R}^{2}=\frac{\theta}{4 \pi}{/tex} E= {tex}\theta\over 4\pi\epsilon _0 r^2{/tex} ......(iv) hence, From equation (iv) Electric field only depend on charge enclosed and location of point. Therefore charge with either on canter or surface enclose charge by Gaussian surface (r > R) will not change {tex}\therefore{/tex} Hence it is proved. Case(1) If r < R this graph shows the variation of electric field with radius r Charge enclose, q in = 0 E = 0 Case(2) If r > R E = {tex}\frac{\theta}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} Given that, First point charge = +1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C Second point charge = +4 {tex}\mu{/tex} C Distance = 30 cm Let us consider the net electric field zero at x distance from first charge. Using formula of electric field E x = E 30-x {tex}\frac{k q_1}{r^{2}}=\frac{k q_2}{r^{\prime 2}}{/tex} Put the value into the formula {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{x^{2}}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{(30-x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{(30-x)^{2}}{x^{2}}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{30-x}{x}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{1}}{/tex} , 30 - x = 2x x = 10 cm

question_html:
1. An infinitely long thin straight wire has a uniform linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}. Obtain the expression for the electric field (E) at a point lying at a distance x from the wire, using Gauss' law.
  2. Show graphically the variation of this electric field E as a function of distance x from the wire.
question_text: An infinitely long thin straight wire has a uniform linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Obtain the expression for the electric field (E) at a point lying at a distance x from the wire, using Gauss' law. Show graphically the variation of this electric field E as a function of distance x from the wire.
explanation: Electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire having uniform linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} : x = Distance of the point P from the wire where the electric field is to be evaluated E = Electric field at the point P A Gaussian cylinder of length I, radius x is considered. An infinitesimally small area dS on the Gaussian surface is considered. The electric field is the same at all points on the curved surface of the cylinder and directed radially outward. So, E and dS are along the same direction. The total electric flux ( {tex}\phi{/tex} ) through the curved surface = {tex}\int E d S \cos \theta{/tex} Since,E and dS are along the same direction, So {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o So, {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(2 {tex}\pi{/tex} xl) The net charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface is q = {tex}\lambda{/tex} l {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss law, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} q{/tex} = E(2x {tex}\pi{/tex} l) {tex}\therefore{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi x \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
solution: Electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire having uniform linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} : x = Distance of the point P from the wire where the electric field is to be evaluated E = Electric field at the point P A Gaussian cylinder of length I, radius x is considered. An infinitesimally small area dS on the Gaussian surface is considered. The electric field is the same at all points on the curved surface of the cylinder and directed radially outward. So, E and dS are along the same direction. The total electric flux ( {tex}\phi{/tex} ) through the curved surface = {tex}\int E d S \cos \theta{/tex} Since,E and dS are along the same direction, So {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o So, {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(2 {tex}\pi{/tex} xl) The net charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface is q = {tex}\lambda{/tex} l {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss law, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} q{/tex} = E(2x {tex}\pi{/tex} l) {tex}\therefore{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi x \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire having uniform linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}: 
    x = Distance of the point P from the wire where the electric field is to be evaluated
    E = Electric field at the point P
    A Gaussian cylinder of length I, radius x is considered.
    An infinitesimally small area dS on the Gaussian surface is considered.
    The electric field is the same at all points on the curved surface of the cylinder and directed radially outward. So, E and dS are along the same direction.
    The total electric flux ({tex}\phi{/tex}) through the curved surface = {tex}\int E d S \cos \theta{/tex}
    Since,E and dS are along the same direction, So {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o
    So, {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(2 {tex}\pi{/tex}xl)
    The net charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface is q = {tex}\lambda{/tex}l
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss law,
    {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} q{/tex} = E(2x{tex}\pi{/tex}l)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi x \varepsilon_0}{/tex}

question_html: If y = (log x)x + xlogx, then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
question_text: If y = (log x) x + x logx , then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
explanation: Let u = (log x) x , and v = x logx {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dv }{dx}{/tex} u = (log x) x log u = log[(log x) x ] log u = x log(log x ) ...(i) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(x){/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} log (log x) + x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} [log(log x)] {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = u[1 {tex}\times{/tex} log (log x) + x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (log x)] {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x {tex}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{x}{\log x} \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x {tex}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x {tex}\left[\frac{\{\log (\log x) \times \log x\}+1}{\log x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x-1 [1 + {tex}\{\log (\log x) \times \log x\}{/tex} ] ...(ii) {tex}\frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} [(log x) 2 ] {tex}\frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2(log x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (log x) {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2v(log x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2x logx {tex}\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2x log x-1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} logx ...(iii) From (i) (ii) and (iii) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x-1 [1 + {log (logx) {tex}\times{/tex} log x] + 2x log x-1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} log x
solution: Let u = (log x) x , and v = x logx {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dv }{dx}{/tex} u = (log x) x log u = log[(log x) x ] log u = x log(log x ) ...(i) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(x){/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} log (log x) + x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} [log(log x)] {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = u[1 {tex}\times{/tex} log (log x) + x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (log x)] {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x {tex}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{x}{\log x} \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x {tex}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x {tex}\left[\frac{\{\log (\log x) \times \log x\}+1}{\log x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x-1 [1 + {tex}\{\log (\log x) \times \log x\}{/tex} ] ...(ii) {tex}\frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} [(log x) 2 ] {tex}\frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2(log x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (log x) {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2v(log x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2x logx {tex}\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2x log x-1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} logx ...(iii) From (i) (ii) and (iii) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (log x) x-1 [1 + {log (logx) {tex}\times{/tex} log x] + 2x log x-1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} log x
solution_html:
Let u = (log x)x, and v = xlogx
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dv }{dx}{/tex}
u = (log x)x
log u = log[(log x)x]
log u = x log(logx) ...(i)
Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(x){/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}log (log x) + x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex}[log(log x)]
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = u[1 {tex}\times{/tex} log (log x) + x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}{/tex}(log x)]
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)x{tex}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{x}{\log x} \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)x {tex}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)x {tex}\left[\frac{\{\log (\log x) \times \log x\}+1}{\log x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)x-1[1 + {tex}\{\log (\log x) \times \log x\}{/tex}] ...(ii)
{tex}\frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex}[(log x)2]
{tex}\frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2(log x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex}(log x)
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2v(log x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2xlogx {tex}\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = 2xlog x-1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} logx ...(iii)
From (i) (ii) and (iii)
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)x-1[1 + {log (logx) {tex}\times{/tex} log x] + 2xlog x-1 {tex}\cdot{/tex} log x

question_html:
1. Write two important characteristics of equipotential surfaces.
  2. A thin circular ring of radius r is charged uniformly so that its linear charge density becomes {tex}\lambda{/tex}. Derive an expression for the electric field at a point P at a distance x from it along the axis of the ring. Hence, prove that at large distances (x >> r), the ring behaves as a point charge.
question_text: Write two important characteristics of equipotential surfaces. A thin circular ring of radius r is charged uniformly so that its linear charge density becomes {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Derive an expression for the electric field at a point P at a distance x from it along the axis of the ring. Hence, prove that at large distances (x >> r), the ring behaves as a point charge.
explanation: Important characteristics of equipotential surfaces are Potential has the same value at all points on the surface. Electric field is normal to the equipotential surface at all points Electric field due to any elemental (point) charge dq, at point P. = dE = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)}{/tex} This is directed along AP Its component along the axis OP of the ring is = dE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{dE} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+r^2}}{/tex} The component, perpendicular to the axis gets cancelled by the elemental electric field due to another elemental charge symmetrically located on the other side of the axis. Hence total electric field E = {tex}\int \mathrm{dE} \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \int \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+r^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}} \int \lambda \mathrm{d} l{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x \lambda}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}} \times 2 \pi r{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}}{/tex} Where Q = {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = total charge on the ring This field is directed along the axis. When x is much larger than r, we have E = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{x^2}{/tex} This corresponds to the expression for the electric field due to a point charge. Thus at large distances the ring behaves like a point charge.
solution: Important characteristics of equipotential surfaces are Potential has the same value at all points on the surface. Electric field is normal to the equipotential surface at all points Electric field due to any elemental (point) charge dq, at point P. = dE = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)}{/tex} This is directed along AP Its component along the axis OP of the ring is = dE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{dE} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+r^2}}{/tex} The component, perpendicular to the axis gets cancelled by the elemental electric field due to another elemental charge symmetrically located on the other side of the axis. Hence total electric field E = {tex}\int \mathrm{dE} \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \int \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+r^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}} \int \lambda \mathrm{d} l{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x \lambda}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}} \times 2 \pi r{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}}{/tex} Where Q = {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = total charge on the ring This field is directed along the axis. When x is much larger than r, we have E = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{x^2}{/tex} This corresponds to the expression for the electric field due to a point charge. Thus at large distances the ring behaves like a point charge.
solution_html:
1. Important characteristics of equipotential surfaces are
    1. Potential has the same value at all points on the surface.
    2. Electric field is normal to the equipotential surface at all points

  2. Electric field due to any elemental (point) charge dq, at point P.
    = dE = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)}{/tex}
    This is directed along AP
    Its component along the axis OP of the ring is
    = dE cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{dE} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+r^2}}{/tex}
    The component, perpendicular to the axis gets cancelled by the elemental electric field due to another elemental charge symmetrically located on the other side of the axis.
    Hence total electric field
    E = {tex}\int \mathrm{dE} \cos \theta{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \int \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+r^2}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}} \int \lambda \mathrm{d} l{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x \lambda}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}} \times 2 \pi r{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2+r^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}}{/tex}
    Where Q = {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = total charge on the ring
    This field is directed along the axis.
    When x is much larger than r, we have
    E = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{x}{\left(x^2\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{x^2}{/tex}
    This corresponds to the expression for the electric field due to a point charge. Thus at large distances the ring behaves like a point charge.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two electric field lines cannot cross each other. Also, they cannot form closed loops. Give reasons.
  2. A particle of charge 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C and mass 1.6 g is moving with a velocity 4 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} ms-1. At t = 0 the particle enters in a region having an electric field {tex}\vec {E}{/tex} (in NC-1) = 80 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + 60 {tex}\hat {j}{/tex}. Find the velocity of the particle at t = 5 s.
question_text: Two electric field lines cannot cross each other. Also, they cannot form closed loops. Give reasons. A particle of charge 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and mass 1.6 g is moving with a velocity 4 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} ms -1 . At t = 0 the particle enters in a region having an electric field {tex}\vec {E}{/tex} (in NC -1 ) = 80 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + 60 {tex}\hat {j}{/tex} . Find the velocity of the particle at t = 5 s.
explanation: Two electric field lines never cross each other because if they do so, we can draw two tangents at the same point, it means there will be two directions of electric field at the same point of intersection which is not possible. Since the electric field lines start from positive charge and terminate at the negative charge hence closed loops are not possible. a = {tex}\frac{q E}{m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}(80 \hat{i}+60 j)}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec {v}{/tex} = {tex}\vec {u}{/tex} + {tex}\vec {a}t{/tex} v = 4 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + (10 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + 7.5 j) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 v = 4.5 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + 0.375 j
solution: Two electric field lines never cross each other because if they do so, we can draw two tangents at the same point, it means there will be two directions of electric field at the same point of intersection which is not possible. Since the electric field lines start from positive charge and terminate at the negative charge hence closed loops are not possible. a = {tex}\frac{q E}{m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}(80 \hat{i}+60 j)}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec {v}{/tex} = {tex}\vec {u}{/tex} + {tex}\vec {a}t{/tex} v = 4 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + (10 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + 7.5 j) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 v = 4.5 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + 0.375 j
solution_html:
1. Two electric field lines never cross each other because if they do so, we can draw two tangents at the same point, it means there will be two directions of electric field at the same point of intersection which is not possible.
    2. Since the electric field lines start from positive charge and terminate at the negative charge hence closed loops are not possible.
  2. a = {tex}\frac{q E}{m}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}(80 \hat{i}+60 j)}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec {v}{/tex} = {tex}\vec {u}{/tex} + {tex}\vec {a}t{/tex}
    v = 4{tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + (10{tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + 7.5 j){tex}\times{/tex}10-2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
    v = 4.5{tex}\hat {i}{/tex} + 0.375 j

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two point charges + Q1 and - Q2 are placed r distance apart. Obtain the expression for the amount of work done to place a third charge Q3 at the midpoint of the line joining the two charges.
  2. At what distance from charge + Q1 on the line joining the two charges (in terms of Q1, Q2 and r) will this work done be zero.
question_text: Two point charges + Q 1 and - Q 2 are placed r distance apart. Obtain the expression for the amount of work done to place a third charge Q 3 at the midpoint of the line joining the two charges. At what distance from charge + Q 1 on the line joining the two charges (in terms of Q 1 , Q 2 and r) will this work done be zero.
explanation: Work done in placing the charge Q 3 at the mid point of line joining the charges = Q 3 {tex}\times{/tex} net potential at the mid point = {tex}\mathrm{Q}_3\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{Q}_2}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 \mathrm{Q}_3}{r}\left[\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right]{/tex} Let the required distance be x, to the right of Q1. We then have net potential at this point = zero {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{x}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_2}{(x-r)}\right]{/tex} = 0 (x - r)Q 1 = Q 2 x x = {tex}\frac{r}{\left(\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right)} \mathrm{Q}_1{/tex}
solution: Work done in placing the charge Q 3 at the mid point of line joining the charges = Q 3 {tex}\times{/tex} net potential at the mid point = {tex}\mathrm{Q}_3\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{Q}_2}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 \mathrm{Q}_3}{r}\left[\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right]{/tex} Let the required distance be x, to the right of Q1. We then have net potential at this point = zero {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{x}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_2}{(x-r)}\right]{/tex} = 0 (x - r)Q 1 = Q 2 x x = {tex}\frac{r}{\left(\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right)} \mathrm{Q}_1{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Work done in placing the charge Q3 at the mid point of line joining the charges
    = Q3{tex}\times{/tex}net potential at the mid point
    = {tex}\mathrm{Q}_3\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{Q}_2}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 \mathrm{Q}_3}{r}\left[\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right]{/tex}
  2. Let the required distance be x, to the right of Q1. We then have net potential at this point = zero
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{x}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_2}{(x-r)}\right]{/tex} = 0
    (x - r)Q1 = Q2x
    x = {tex}\frac{r}{\left(\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right)} \mathrm{Q}_1{/tex}

question_html:
1. State Gauss’s law on electrostatics and derive an expression for the electric field due to a long straight thin uniformly charged wire (linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}) at a point lying at a distance r from the wire.
  2. The magnitude of electric field (in NC-1) in a region varies with the distance r(in m) as
    E = 10 r + 5
    By how much does the electric potential increase in moving from point at r = 1 m to a point at r = 10 m.
question_text: State Gauss’s law on electrostatics and derive an expression for the electric field due to a long straight thin uniformly charged wire (linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) at a point lying at a distance r from the wire. The magnitude of electric field (in NC -1 ) in a region varies with the distance r(in m) as E = 10 r + 5 By how much does the electric potential increase in moving from point at r = 1 m to a point at r = 10 m.
explanation: Gauss Theorem: The net outward electric flux through a closed surface is equal to 1/ε o times the net charge enclosed within the surface i.e., {tex}\oint_S \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \Sigma q{/tex} Electric field due to infinitely long, thin and uniformly charged straight wire: Electric Flux through the cylindrical surface {tex}\oint_S \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_1}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_1+\int_{S_2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_2+\int_{S_3} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_3{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_1}{/tex} EdS 1 cos 0 o + {tex}\int_{S_2}{/tex} EdS 2 cos 90 o + {tex}\int_{S_3}{/tex} EdS 3 cos 90 o = {tex}\int_S{/tex} EdS 1 + 0 + 0 = E {tex}\int{/tex} dS 1 (since electric field E is the same at each point of curved surface) = E {tex}2 \pi r l{/tex} (since area of curved surface = {tex}2 \pi r l{/tex} ) As {tex}\lambda{/tex} is charge per unit length and length of cylinder is l, therefore, charge enclosed by assumed surface = ( {tex}\lambda \text l{/tex} ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss's theorem {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times{/tex} charge enclosed {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E .2 \pi r l=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}(\lambda l){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Electric feild, E = {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dV = {tex}\int \text E{/tex} dr {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int {\text dV}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} (10r + 5)dr {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = [5r² + 5r] putting limits r = 1 to r = 10m so, V = 5(10) 2 + 5(10) - (5.1 2 + 5.1) = 500 + 50 - 10 = 540 Volts hence electric potential increases 540 volts in moving from r = 1m to a point at r = 10m.
solution: Gauss Theorem: The net outward electric flux through a closed surface is equal to 1/ε o times the net charge enclosed within the surface i.e., {tex}\oint_S \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \Sigma q{/tex} Electric field due to infinitely long, thin and uniformly charged straight wire: Electric Flux through the cylindrical surface {tex}\oint_S \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_1}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_1+\int_{S_2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_2+\int_{S_3} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_3{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_1}{/tex} EdS 1 cos 0 o + {tex}\int_{S_2}{/tex} EdS 2 cos 90 o + {tex}\int_{S_3}{/tex} EdS 3 cos 90 o = {tex}\int_S{/tex} EdS 1 + 0 + 0 = E {tex}\int{/tex} dS 1 (since electric field E is the same at each point of curved surface) = E {tex}2 \pi r l{/tex} (since area of curved surface = {tex}2 \pi r l{/tex} ) As {tex}\lambda{/tex} is charge per unit length and length of cylinder is l, therefore, charge enclosed by assumed surface = ( {tex}\lambda \text l{/tex} ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss's theorem {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times{/tex} charge enclosed {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E .2 \pi r l=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}(\lambda l){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Electric feild, E = {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dV = {tex}\int \text E{/tex} dr {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int {\text dV}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} (10r + 5)dr {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = [5r² + 5r] putting limits r = 1 to r = 10m so, V = 5(10) 2 + 5(10) - (5.1 2 + 5.1) = 500 + 50 - 10 = 540 Volts hence electric potential increases 540 volts in moving from r = 1m to a point at r = 10m.
solution_html:
1. Gauss Theorem: The net outward electric flux through a closed surface is equal to 1/εo times the net charge enclosed within the surface i.e., {tex}\oint_S \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \Sigma q{/tex} Electric field due to infinitely long, thin and uniformly charged straight wire:

    Electric Flux through the cylindrical surface
    {tex}\oint_S \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_1}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_1+\int_{S_2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_2+\int_{S_3} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}_3{/tex}
    = {tex}\int_{S_1}{/tex} EdS1 cos 0o + {tex}\int_{S_2}{/tex} EdS2 cos 90o + {tex}\int_{S_3}{/tex} EdS3 cos 90o
    = {tex}\int_S{/tex} EdS1 + 0 + 0
    = E {tex}\int{/tex} dS1 (since electric field E is the same at each point of curved surface)
    = E {tex}2 \pi r l{/tex} (since area of curved surface = {tex}2 \pi r l{/tex})
    As {tex}\lambda{/tex} is charge per unit length and length of cylinder is l, therefore, charge enclosed by assumed surface = ({tex}\lambda \text l{/tex})
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss's theorem
    {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times{/tex} charge enclosed
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E .2 \pi r l=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}(\lambda l){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}

  2. Electric feild, E = {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dV = {tex}\int \text E{/tex} dr
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int {\text dV}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex}(10r + 5)dr
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = [5r² + 5r]
    putting limits r = 1 to r = 10m
    so, V = 5(10)2 + 5(10) - (5.12 + 5.1)
    = 500 + 50 - 10
    = 540 Volts
    hence electric potential increases 540 volts in moving from r = 1m to a point at r = 10m.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Find an expression for the electric field strength at a distant point situated

  1. on the axis
  2. along the equatorial line of an electric dipole.
question_text: Find an expression for the electric field strength at a distant point situated on the axis along the equatorial line of an electric dipole.
explanation: Consider an electric dipole AB. The charges -q and +q of dipole are situated at A and B respectively as shown in the figure. The separation between the charges is 2a. Electric dipole moment, p = q {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2a The direction of dipole moment is from -q to +q. At axial or end-on position: Consider a point P on the axis of dipole at a distance r from mid-point O of electric dipole. The distance of point P from charge +q at B is BP = r - a and distance of point P from charge -q at A is, AP = r + a. Let E 1 and E 2 be the electric field strengths at point P due to charges +q and -q respectively. We know that the direction of electric field due to a point charge is away from positive charge and towards the negative charge. Therefore, {tex}E_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r-a)^2}{/tex} (from B to P) and {tex}E_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r+a)^2}{/tex} (from P to A) Clearly the directions of electric field strengths {tex}\overrightarrow{ E }_1{/tex} are {tex}\overrightarrow{ E }_2{/tex} along the same line but opposite to each other and {tex}E_1>E_2{/tex} because positive charge is nearer. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The resultant electric field due to electric dipole has magnitude equal to the difference of {tex}E_1{/tex} and {tex}E_2{/tex} direction from B to P i.e. {tex}E=E_1-E_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r+a)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{(r+a)^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{(r+a)^2-(r-a)^2}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{4 r a}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2(q 2 a) r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}{/tex} But q {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2l = p (electric dipole moment) {tex}\therefore E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}{/tex} ...(i) If the dipole is infinitely small and point P is far away from the dipole, then r > > 1, therefore equation (i) may be expressed as {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p r}{r^4} {/tex} or {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p}{r^3}{/tex} ...(ii) This is the expression for the electric field strength at axial position due to a short electric dipole. At a point of equatorial line: Consider a point P on broad side on the position of dipole formed of charges +q and -q at separation 2a. The distance of point P from mid point (O) of electric dipole is r. Let {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} be the electric field strengths due to charges +q and - q of electric dipole. From fig. AP = BP {tex}=\sqrt{r^2+a^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{E} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2+a^2}{/tex} along B to P {tex}\vec{E}_2 =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2+a^2}{/tex} along P to A Clearly {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} are equal in magnitude i.e. {tex}\left|\vec{E}_1\right|=\left|\vec{E}_2\right| or E_1=E_2{/tex} To find the resultant of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} , we resolve them into rectangular components. Component of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} parallel to AB = E 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} , in the direction to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} Component of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} perpendicular to AB = E 1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} along OP Component of {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} parallel to AB = E 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} in the direction {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} Component of {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} perpendicular to AB = E 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} along PO Clearly, components of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} perpendicular to AB: E 1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and E 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} being equal and opposite cancel each other, while the components of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} parallel to AB: E 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and E 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} , being in the same direction add up and give the resultant electric field whose direction is parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resultant electric field at P is E = E 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + E 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} But E 1 = E 2 {tex}= \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} From the figure, cos {tex}\theta=\frac{O B}{P B}=\frac{l}{\sqrt{r^2+a^2}}=\frac{l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{1}{ 2}}{/tex} E = 2E 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)} \cdot \frac{l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 q l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex} But q {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2l = p = electric dipole moment ...(iii) {tex}\therefore E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex} If dipole is infinitesimal and point P is far away, we have a < < r, so l 2 may be neglected as compared to r 2 and so equation (iii) gives {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{\left(r^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^3}{/tex} i.e., electric field strength due to a short dipole at broadside on position {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^3}{/tex} in the direction parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} ...(iv) Its direction is parallel to the axis of dipole from positive to negative charge.
solution: Consider an electric dipole AB. The charges -q and +q of dipole are situated at A and B respectively as shown in the figure. The separation between the charges is 2a. Electric dipole moment, p = q {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2a The direction of dipole moment is from -q to +q. At axial or end-on position: Consider a point P on the axis of dipole at a distance r from mid-point O of electric dipole. The distance of point P from charge +q at B is BP = r - a and distance of point P from charge -q at A is, AP = r + a. Let E 1 and E 2 be the electric field strengths at point P due to charges +q and -q respectively. We know that the direction of electric field due to a point charge is away from positive charge and towards the negative charge. Therefore, {tex}E_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r-a)^2}{/tex} (from B to P) and {tex}E_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r+a)^2}{/tex} (from P to A) Clearly the directions of electric field strengths {tex}\overrightarrow{ E }_1{/tex} are {tex}\overrightarrow{ E }_2{/tex} along the same line but opposite to each other and {tex}E_1>E_2{/tex} because positive charge is nearer. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The resultant electric field due to electric dipole has magnitude equal to the difference of {tex}E_1{/tex} and {tex}E_2{/tex} direction from B to P i.e. {tex}E=E_1-E_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r+a)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{(r+a)^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{(r+a)^2-(r-a)^2}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{4 r a}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2(q 2 a) r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}{/tex} But q {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2l = p (electric dipole moment) {tex}\therefore E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}{/tex} ...(i) If the dipole is infinitely small and point P is far away from the dipole, then r > > 1, therefore equation (i) may be expressed as {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p r}{r^4} {/tex} or {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p}{r^3}{/tex} ...(ii) This is the expression for the electric field strength at axial position due to a short electric dipole. At a point of equatorial line: Consider a point P on broad side on the position of dipole formed of charges +q and -q at separation 2a. The distance of point P from mid point (O) of electric dipole is r. Let {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} be the electric field strengths due to charges +q and - q of electric dipole. From fig. AP = BP {tex}=\sqrt{r^2+a^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{E} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2+a^2}{/tex} along B to P {tex}\vec{E}_2 =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2+a^2}{/tex} along P to A Clearly {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} are equal in magnitude i.e. {tex}\left|\vec{E}_1\right|=\left|\vec{E}_2\right| or E_1=E_2{/tex} To find the resultant of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} , we resolve them into rectangular components. Component of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} parallel to AB = E 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} , in the direction to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} Component of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} perpendicular to AB = E 1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} along OP Component of {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} parallel to AB = E 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} in the direction {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} Component of {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} perpendicular to AB = E 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} along PO Clearly, components of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} perpendicular to AB: E 1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and E 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} being equal and opposite cancel each other, while the components of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} parallel to AB: E 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and E 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} , being in the same direction add up and give the resultant electric field whose direction is parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resultant electric field at P is E = E 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + E 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} But E 1 = E 2 {tex}= \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} From the figure, cos {tex}\theta=\frac{O B}{P B}=\frac{l}{\sqrt{r^2+a^2}}=\frac{l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{1}{ 2}}{/tex} E = 2E 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)} \cdot \frac{l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 q l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex} But q {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2l = p = electric dipole moment ...(iii) {tex}\therefore E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex} If dipole is infinitesimal and point P is far away, we have a < < r, so l 2 may be neglected as compared to r 2 and so equation (iii) gives {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{\left(r^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^3}{/tex} i.e., electric field strength due to a short dipole at broadside on position {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^3}{/tex} in the direction parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} ...(iv) Its direction is parallel to the axis of dipole from positive to negative charge.
solution_html:
Consider an electric dipole AB. The charges -q and +q of dipole are situated at A and B respectively as shown in the figure. The separation between the charges is 2a.
Electric dipole moment, p = q{tex}\cdot{/tex}2a
The direction of dipole moment is from -q to +q.

  1. At axial or end-on position: Consider a point P on the axis of dipole at a distance r from mid-point O of electric dipole.
    The distance of point P from charge +q at B is BP = r - a and distance of point P from charge -q at A is, AP = r + a.
    Let E1 and E2 be the electric field strengths at point P due to charges +q and -q respectively. We know that the direction of electric field due to a point charge is away from positive charge and towards the negative charge. Therefore,
    {tex}E_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r-a)^2}{/tex} (from B to P) and {tex}E_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r+a)^2}{/tex} (from P to A)
    Clearly the directions of electric field strengths {tex}\overrightarrow{ E }_1{/tex} are {tex}\overrightarrow{ E }_2{/tex} along the same line but opposite to each other and {tex}E_1>E_2{/tex} because positive charge is nearer.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The resultant electric field due to electric dipole has magnitude equal to the difference of {tex}E_1{/tex} and {tex}E_2{/tex} direction from B to P i.e.
    {tex}E=E_1-E_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{(r+a)^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{(r+a)^2}\right]{/tex}{tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{(r+a)^2-(r-a)^2}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}\right]{/tex}

    {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{4 r a}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2(q 2 a) r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}{/tex}
    But q{tex}\cdot{/tex}2l = p (electric dipole moment)
    {tex}\therefore E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}{/tex} ...(i)
    If the dipole is infinitely small and point P is far away from the dipole, then r > > 1, therefore equation (i) may be expressed as
    {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p r}{r^4} {/tex} or {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 p}{r^3}{/tex} ...(ii)
    This is the expression for the electric field strength at axial position due to a short electric dipole.

  2. At a point of equatorial line: Consider a point P on broad side on the position of dipole formed of charges +q and -q at separation 2a. The distance of point P from mid point (O) of electric dipole is r. Let {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} be the electric field strengths due to charges +q and - q of electric dipole.
    From fig. AP = BP {tex}=\sqrt{r^2+a^2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \vec{E} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2+a^2}{/tex} along B to P
    {tex}\vec{E}_2 =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2+a^2}{/tex} along P to A

    Clearly {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} are equal in magnitude i.e. {tex}\left|\vec{E}_1\right|=\left|\vec{E}_2\right| or E_1=E_2{/tex}
    To find the resultant of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex}, we resolve them into rectangular components.
    Component of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} parallel to AB = E1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, in the direction to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex}
    Component of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} perpendicular to AB = E1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} along OP
    Component of {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} parallel to AB = E2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} in the direction {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex}
    Component of {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} perpendicular to AB = E2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} along PO
    Clearly, components of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} perpendicular to AB: E1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and E2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} being equal and opposite cancel each other, while the components of {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} parallel to AB: E1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    and E2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, being in the same direction add up and give the resultant electric field whose direction is parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex}.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resultant electric field at P is E = E1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + E2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    But E1 = E2 {tex}= \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex}
    From the figure, cos {tex}\theta=\frac{O B}{P B}=\frac{l}{\sqrt{r^2+a^2}}=\frac{l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{1}{ 2}}{/tex}
    E = 2E1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)} \cdot \frac{l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 q l}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex}
    But q{tex}\cdot{/tex}2l = p = electric dipole moment ...(iii)
    {tex}\therefore E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex}
    If dipole is infinitesimal and point P is far away, we have a < < r, so l2 may be neglected as compared to r2 and so equation (iii) gives
    {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{\left(r^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^3}{/tex}
    i.e., electric field strength due to a short dipole at broadside on position
    {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^3}{/tex} in the direction parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} ...(iv)
    Its direction is parallel to the axis of dipole from positive to negative charge.
