question_html:
1. Use Gauss’ law to derive the expression for the electric field {tex}(\vec{E}){/tex} due to a straight uniformly charged infinite line of charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}c/m.
  2. Draw a graph to show the variation of E with perpendicular distance r from the line of charge.
  3. Find the work done in bringing a charge q from perpendicular distance r1 to r2 (r2 > r1).
question_text: Use Gauss’ law to derive the expression for the electric field {tex}(\vec{E}){/tex} due to a straight uniformly charged infinite line of charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} c/m. Draw a graph to show the variation of E with perpendicular distance r from the line of charge. Find the work done in bringing a charge q from perpendicular distance r 1 to r 2 (r 2 > r 1 ).
explanation: To calculate the electric field, imagine a cylindrical Gaussian surface, since the field is everywhere radial, flux through two ends of the cylindrical Gaussian surface is zero. At cylindrical part of the surface electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is normal to the surface at every point and its magnitude is constant. Therefore flux through the Gaussian surface. = Flux through the curved cylindrical part of the surface. {tex}=E \times 2 \pi r l{/tex} .... (i) Applying Gauss's Law Flux {tex}\phi=\frac{q_{\text {enclored}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Total charge enclosed = Linear charge density {tex}\times{/tex} l {tex}=\lambda l{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi=\frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} .... (ii) Using Equation (i) & (ii) {tex}E \times 2 \pi r l=\frac{\pi l}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} In vector notation {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \hat{n}{/tex} (where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the line charge) The required graph is as shown: Work done in moving the charge 'q'. Through a small displacement 'dr' {tex}d W=\vec{F} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} {tex}d W=q \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} =qEdr cos0 {tex}d W=q \times \frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} d r{/tex} Work done in moving the given charge from r 1 to r 2 (r 2 > r 1 ) {tex}W = \int\limits_{{r_1}}^{{r_2}} {dW} \int {\int\limits_{{r_1}}^{{r_2}} {\frac{{\lambda qdr}}{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}} } {/tex} {tex}W=\frac{\lambda q}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{e} r_{2}-\log _{e} r_{1}\right]{/tex} {tex}W=\frac{\lambda q}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{e} \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right]{/tex}
solution: To calculate the electric field, imagine a cylindrical Gaussian surface, since the field is everywhere radial, flux through two ends of the cylindrical Gaussian surface is zero. At cylindrical part of the surface electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is normal to the surface at every point and its magnitude is constant. Therefore flux through the Gaussian surface. = Flux through the curved cylindrical part of the surface. {tex}=E \times 2 \pi r l{/tex} .... (i) Applying Gauss's Law Flux {tex}\phi=\frac{q_{\text {enclored}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Total charge enclosed = Linear charge density {tex}\times{/tex} l {tex}=\lambda l{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi=\frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} .... (ii) Using Equation (i) & (ii) {tex}E \times 2 \pi r l=\frac{\pi l}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} In vector notation {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \hat{n}{/tex} (where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the line charge) The required graph is as shown: Work done in moving the charge 'q'. Through a small displacement 'dr' {tex}d W=\vec{F} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} {tex}d W=q \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} =qEdr cos0 {tex}d W=q \times \frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} d r{/tex} Work done in moving the given charge from r 1 to r 2 (r 2 > r 1 ) {tex}W = \int\limits_{{r_1}}^{{r_2}} {dW} \int {\int\limits_{{r_1}}^{{r_2}} {\frac{{\lambda qdr}}{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}} } {/tex} {tex}W=\frac{\lambda q}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{e} r_{2}-\log _{e} r_{1}\right]{/tex} {tex}W=\frac{\lambda q}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{e} \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
1. To calculate the electric field, imagine a cylindrical Gaussian surface, since the field is everywhere radial, flux through two ends of the cylindrical Gaussian surface is zero.
    At cylindrical part of the surface electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is normal to the surface at every point and its magnitude is constant.
    Therefore flux through the Gaussian surface.
    = Flux through the curved cylindrical part of the surface.
    {tex}=E \times 2 \pi r l{/tex} .... (i)
    Applying Gauss's Law
    Flux {tex}\phi=\frac{q_{\text {enclored}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Total charge enclosed
    = Linear charge density {tex}\times{/tex} l
    {tex}=\lambda l{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \phi=\frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} .... (ii)
    Using Equation (i) & (ii)
    {tex}E \times 2 \pi r l=\frac{\pi l}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex}
    In vector notation
    {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \hat{n}{/tex}
    (where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the line charge)
  2. The required graph is as shown:
  3. Work done in moving the charge 'q'. Through a small displacement 'dr'
    {tex}d W=\vec{F} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex}
    {tex}d W=q \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex}
    =qEdr cos0
    {tex}d W=q \times \frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} d r{/tex}
    Work done in moving the given charge from r1 to r2(r2 > r1)
    {tex}W = \int\limits_{{r_1}}^{{r_2}} {dW} \int {\int\limits_{{r_1}}^{{r_2}} {\frac{{\lambda qdr}}{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}} } {/tex}
    {tex}W=\frac{\lambda q}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{e} r_{2}-\log _{e} r_{1}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}W=\frac{\lambda q}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\log _{e} \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right]{/tex}

question_html:
Two large charged plane sheets of charge densities {tex}\sigma{/tex} and –2{tex}\sigma{/tex} C/m2 are arranged vertically with a separation of d between them. Deduce expressions for the electric field at points

  1. to the left of the first sheet,
  2. to the right of the second sheet, and
  3. between the two sheets.
question_text: Two large charged plane sheets of charge densities {tex}\sigma{/tex} and –2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} C/m 2 are arranged vertically with a separation of d between them. Deduce expressions for the electric field at points to the left of the first sheet, to the right of the second sheet, and between the two sheets.
explanation: Consider two infinite plane sheets A and B separated by a distance d having surface charge densities {tex}+\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-2\sigma{/tex} respectively as shown in the figure. Let {tex}\overrightarrow{E_1}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{E_2}{/tex} be the electric field intensities due to sheets A and B. Magnitude of Electric Fields E 1 = {tex}\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_0}{/tex} and E 2 = {tex}\frac\sigma{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Electric field in the region left of the first sheet, E I = {tex}-\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_o}+\frac\sigma{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} E I = {tex}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} It's direction is towards right. Electric field in the region to the right of second sheet, E II = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E II = {tex}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} It's direction is towards left. Electric field between the two sheets, E III = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E III = {tex}\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Here, electric field is towards the right.
solution: Consider two infinite plane sheets A and B separated by a distance d having surface charge densities {tex}+\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-2\sigma{/tex} respectively as shown in the figure. Let {tex}\overrightarrow{E_1}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{E_2}{/tex} be the electric field intensities due to sheets A and B. Magnitude of Electric Fields E 1 = {tex}\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_0}{/tex} and E 2 = {tex}\frac\sigma{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Electric field in the region left of the first sheet, E I = {tex}-\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_o}+\frac\sigma{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} E I = {tex}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} It's direction is towards right. Electric field in the region to the right of second sheet, E II = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E II = {tex}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} It's direction is towards left. Electric field between the two sheets, E III = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E III = {tex}\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Here, electric field is towards the right.
solution_html:
Consider two infinite plane sheets A and B separated by a distance d having surface charge densities {tex}+\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-2\sigma{/tex} respectively as shown in the figure. Let {tex}\overrightarrow{E_1}{/tex}and {tex}\overrightarrow{E_2}{/tex} be the electric field intensities due to sheets A and B.
Magnitude of Electric Fields E1 = {tex}\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_0}{/tex} and E2 = {tex}\frac\sigma{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}

  1. Electric field in the region left of the first sheet,
    EI = {tex}-\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_o}+\frac\sigma{\varepsilon_o}{/tex}
    EI = {tex}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    It's direction is towards right.

  2. Electric field in the region to the right of second sheet,
    EII = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    EII = {tex}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    It's direction is towards left.

  3. Electric field between the two sheets,
    EIII = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    EIII = {tex}\frac{3 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Here, electric field is towards the right.

question_html: Five point charges, each of charge +q are placed on five vertices of a regular hexagon of side l. Find the magnitude of the resultant force on a charge -q placed at the centre of the hexagon.
question_text: Five point charges, each of charge +q are placed on five vertices of a regular hexagon of side l . Find the magnitude of the resultant force on a charge -q placed at the centre of the hexagon.
explanation: Four charges cancel the effect of each other, so net force on the charge placed at centre is due to the remaining fifth charge, i.e. Net Force, | {tex}\overline F{/tex} | = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\mathrm{q}^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Four charges cancel the effect of each other, so net force on the charge placed at centre is due to the remaining fifth charge, i.e. Net Force, | {tex}\overline F{/tex} | = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\mathrm{q}^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Four charges cancel the effect of each other, so net force on the charge placed at centre is due to the remaining fifth charge, i.e.
Net Force, |{tex}\overline F{/tex}| = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\mathrm{q}^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A simple pendulum consists of a small sphere of mass m suspended by a thread of length l. The sphere carries a positive charge q. The pendulum is placed in a uniform electric field of strength E directed vertically downwards. With what period will the pendulum oscillate if the electrostatic force acting on the sphere is less than the gravitational force? Assume that oscillations are small.
question_text: A simple pendulum consists of a small sphere of mass m suspended by a thread of length l. The sphere carries a positive charge q. The pendulum is placed in a uniform electric field of strength E directed vertically downwards. With what period will the pendulum oscillate if the electrostatic force acting on the sphere is less than the gravitational force? Assume that oscillations are small.
explanation: Upward force on bob = qE Weight of bob acting downward = mg Net downward force on bob = mg - qE {tex}\therefore{/tex} Acceleartion, {tex}a=\frac{m g-q E}{m}=\left(g-\frac{q E}{m}\right){/tex} The time period of the oscillations is, {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{a}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{(g-q E / m)}}{/tex}
solution: Upward force on bob = qE Weight of bob acting downward = mg Net downward force on bob = mg - qE {tex}\therefore{/tex} Acceleartion, {tex}a=\frac{m g-q E}{m}=\left(g-\frac{q E}{m}\right){/tex} The time period of the oscillations is, {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{a}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{(g-q E / m)}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Upward force on bob = qE
Weight of bob acting downward = mg
Net downward force on bob = mg - qE
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Acceleartion, {tex}a=\frac{m g-q E}{m}=\left(g-\frac{q E}{m}\right){/tex}
The time period of the oscillations is,  {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{a}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{(g-q E / m)}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A spherical conducting shell of inner radius r1 and outer radius r2 has a charge Q.

  1. A charge q is placed at the centre of the shell. Find out the surface charge density on the inner and outer surfaces of the shell.
  2. Is the electric field inside a cavity (with no charge) zero; independent of the fact whether the shell is spherical or not? Explain.
question_text: A spherical conducting shell of inner radius r 1 and outer radius r 2 has a charge Q. A charge q is placed at the centre of the shell. Find out the surface charge density on the inner and outer surfaces of the shell. Is the electric field inside a cavity (with no charge) zero; independent of the fact whether the shell is spherical or not? Explain.
explanation: Finding the surface charge density in the inner and the outer surface of the shell. The electric field in the cavity Diagram: The charge induced on the inner surface of the shell will be -q. The surface charge density on inner surface of the shell is {tex}\sigma_1=-\frac{q}{4 \pi r_1^2}{/tex} The surface charge density on the outer shell is {tex}\sigma_2=-\frac{Q+q}{4 \pi r_2^2}{/tex} Consider a Gaussian surface inside the shell, net flux is zero since q net = 0. According to Gauss' law, it is independent of the shape and size of shell.
solution: Finding the surface charge density in the inner and the outer surface of the shell. The electric field in the cavity Diagram: The charge induced on the inner surface of the shell will be -q. The surface charge density on inner surface of the shell is {tex}\sigma_1=-\frac{q}{4 \pi r_1^2}{/tex} The surface charge density on the outer shell is {tex}\sigma_2=-\frac{Q+q}{4 \pi r_2^2}{/tex} Consider a Gaussian surface inside the shell, net flux is zero since q net = 0. According to Gauss' law, it is independent of the shape and size of shell.
solution_html:
Finding the surface charge density in the inner and the outer surface of the shell.
The electric field in the cavity
Diagram:


  1. The charge induced on the inner surface of the shell will be -q.
    The surface charge density on inner surface of the shell is {tex}\sigma_1=-\frac{q}{4 \pi r_1^2}{/tex}
    The surface charge density on the outer shell is {tex}\sigma_2=-\frac{Q+q}{4 \pi r_2^2}{/tex}
  2. Consider a Gaussian surface inside the shell, net flux is zero since qnet = 0. According to Gauss' law, it is independent of the shape and size of shell.

subject: Physics
question_html: A metallic spherical shell has an inner radius R1 and outer radius R2. A charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell. What will be the surface charge density on the (i) inner surface, and (ii) outer surface of the shell?
question_text: A metallic spherical shell has an inner radius R 1 and outer radius R 2 . A charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell. What will be the surface charge density on the (i) inner surface, and (ii) outer surface of the shell?
explanation: Due to induction by charge +Q at centre, the inner surface acquires -Q charge and outer surface of shell will acquire +Q charge. So, Surface charge density on outside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{+Q}{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{/tex} Surface charge density on inside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-Q}{4 \pi R_{1}^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Due to induction by charge +Q at centre, the inner surface acquires -Q charge and outer surface of shell will acquire +Q charge. So, Surface charge density on outside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{+Q}{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{/tex} Surface charge density on inside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-Q}{4 \pi R_{1}^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Due to induction by charge +Q at centre, the inner surface acquires -Q charge and outer surface of shell will acquire +Q charge. So,

  1. Surface charge density on outside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{+Q}{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{/tex}
  2. Surface charge density on inside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-Q}{4 \pi R_{1}^{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two identical conducting balls A and B have charges -Q and +3Q respectively. They are brought in contact with each other and then separated by a distance d apart. Find the nature of the Coulomb force between them.
question_text: Two identical conducting balls A and B have charges -Q and +3Q respectively. They are brought in contact with each other and then separated by a distance d apart. Find the nature of the Coulomb force between them.
explanation: The nature of the coulomb force between them will be repulsive.
solution: The nature of the coulomb force between them will be repulsive.

question_html: Apply Gauss’s law to show that for a charged spherical shell, the electric field outside the shell is, as if the entire charge were concentrated at the centre.
question_text: Apply Gauss’s law to show that for a charged spherical shell, the electric field outside the shell is, as if the entire charge were concentrated at the centre.
explanation: Flux through the small section of Gaussian surface {tex}\phi=\oint \vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{ds}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi=\oint{/tex} Eds {tex}\cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\because E \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{ds}}, \theta=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi=\mathrm{E} .4 \pi R^2{/tex} ...(1) Applying Gauss's theorem {tex}\phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(2) from equations 1 and 2 {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}}{R^2}{/tex}
solution: Flux through the small section of Gaussian surface {tex}\phi=\oint \vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{ds}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi=\oint{/tex} Eds {tex}\cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\because E \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{ds}}, \theta=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi=\mathrm{E} .4 \pi R^2{/tex} ...(1) Applying Gauss's theorem {tex}\phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(2) from equations 1 and 2 {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}}{R^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Flux through the small section of Gaussian surface
{tex}\phi=\oint \vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{ds}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \phi=\oint{/tex} Eds {tex}\cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}\because E \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{ds}}, \theta=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \phi=\mathrm{E} .4 \pi R^2{/tex} ...(1)
Applying Gauss's theorem
{tex}\phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(2)
from equations 1 and 2
{tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}}{R^2}{/tex}

question_html:
In the electric field shown in Figure, the electric field lines on the left have twice the separation as that between those on the right. If the magnitude of the field at point A is 40 NC-1, calculate the force experienced by a proton placed at point A.

Also find the magnitude of electric field at the point B.
question_text: In the electric field shown in Figure, the electric field lines on the left have twice the separation as that between those on the right. If the magnitude of the field at point A is 40 NC -1 , calculate the force experienced by a proton placed at point A. Also find the magnitude of electric field at the point B.
explanation: Force on proton at point A, E = qE A = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 N Since separation between electric field lines at point B is twice as that at the point A, electric field at point B, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=40 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 20 NC -1
solution: Force on proton at point A, E = qE A = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 N Since separation between electric field lines at point B is twice as that at the point A, electric field at point B, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=40 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 20 NC -1
solution_html:
Force on proton at point A,
E = qEA = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 N
Since separation between electric field lines at point B is twice as that at the point A, electric field at point B,
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=40 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 20 NC-1

subject: Physics
question_html: Two large parallel plane sheets have uniform charge densities +{tex}\sigma{/tex} and -{tex}\sigma{/tex}. Determine the electric field (i) between the sheets, and (ii) outside the sheets.
question_text: Two large parallel plane sheets have uniform charge densities + {tex}\sigma{/tex} and - {tex}\sigma{/tex} . Determine the electric field (i) between the sheets, and (ii) outside the sheets.
explanation: Now Electric field Intensity due to a plane sheet of charge {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Here {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{+\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{-\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Electric field at Point Q (In between the sheets) {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{A}}+\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Field at the point P or R(outside the sheet). {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{A}}+\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=0{/tex}
solution: Now Electric field Intensity due to a plane sheet of charge {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Here {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{+\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{-\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Electric field at Point Q (In between the sheets) {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{A}}+\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Field at the point P or R(outside the sheet). {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{A}}+\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
Now Electric field Intensity due to a plane sheet of charge
{tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
Here {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{+\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{-\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex}

  1. Electric field at Point Q (In between the sheets)
    {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{A}}+\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
  2. Field at the point P or R(outside the sheet).
    {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}}=\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{A}}+\vec{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=0{/tex}

question_html: Why is the direction of the electric field due to a charged conducting sphere at any point perpendicular to its surface?
question_text: Why is the direction of the electric field due to a charged conducting sphere at any point perpendicular to its surface?
explanation: If electric field is not perpendicular but has a component tangential to the surface of the conductor, it will exert force on charge and make them more. It means electrostatic condition is violated.
solution: If electric field is not perpendicular but has a component tangential to the surface of the conductor, it will exert force on charge and make them more. It means electrostatic condition is violated.

subject: Physics
question_html: A simple pendulum consists of a small sphere of mass m suspended by a thread of length l. The sphere carries a positive charge q. The pendulum is placed in a uniform electric field of strength E directed vertically downwards. Find the period of oscillation of the pendulum due to the electrostatic force acting on the sphere, neglecting the effect of the gravitational force.
question_text: A simple pendulum consists of a small sphere of mass m suspended by a thread of length l. The sphere carries a positive charge q. The pendulum is placed in a uniform electric field of strength E directed vertically downwards. Find the period of oscillation of the pendulum due to the electrostatic force acting on the sphere, neglecting the effect of the gravitational force.
explanation: Derivation F r = -qEsin {tex}\varphi{/tex} (Restoring force) ma = -qEsin {tex}\varphi{/tex} when {tex}\varphi{/tex} is small ma = -qE {tex}\varphi{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{m} \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\mathrm{qE} \frac{x}{l}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\mathrm{q} \frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}} \frac{x}{l}{/tex} comparing with equation of linear SHM. {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\omega^2 x{/tex} {tex}\omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{qE}}{\mathrm{ml}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{ml}}{\mathrm{qE}}}{/tex}
solution: Derivation F r = -qEsin {tex}\varphi{/tex} (Restoring force) ma = -qEsin {tex}\varphi{/tex} when {tex}\varphi{/tex} is small ma = -qE {tex}\varphi{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{m} \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\mathrm{qE} \frac{x}{l}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\mathrm{q} \frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}} \frac{x}{l}{/tex} comparing with equation of linear SHM. {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\omega^2 x{/tex} {tex}\omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{qE}}{\mathrm{ml}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{ml}}{\mathrm{qE}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Derivation
Fr = -qEsin{tex}\varphi{/tex} (Restoring force)
ma = -qEsin{tex}\varphi{/tex}
when {tex}\varphi{/tex} is small
ma = -qE{tex}\varphi{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{m} \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\mathrm{qE} \frac{x}{l}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\mathrm{q} \frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}} \frac{x}{l}{/tex}
comparing with equation of linear SHM.
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-\omega^2 x{/tex}
{tex}\omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{qE}}{\mathrm{ml}}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{ml}}{\mathrm{qE}}}{/tex}

question_html: An electric field along the x-axis is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=100 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}{/tex} for x > 0 and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=-100 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}{/tex} for x < 0. A right circular cylinder of length 20 cm and radius 5 cm lies parallel to the x-axis, with its centre at the origin and one face at x = + 10 cm, the other face at x = - 10 cm. Calculate the net outward flux through the cylinder.
question_text: An electric field along the x-axis is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=100 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}{/tex} for x > 0 and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=-100 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}{/tex} for x < 0. A right circular cylinder of length 20 cm and radius 5 cm lies parallel to the x-axis, with its centre at the origin and one face at x = + 10 cm, the other face at x = - 10 cm. Calculate the net outward flux through the cylinder.
explanation: Net outward flux through the cylinder {tex}\vec{E} \cdot \vec{\Delta S}=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_1}+\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_2}+\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_3}{/tex} {tex}=(100+100) \pi r^2+0{/tex} (Here cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 90 o = 0 for {tex}\Delta S_3{/tex} ) {tex}=\left[200 \times 3.14 \times(0.05)^2\right] \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{C}{/tex} = 1.57 Nm 2 /C
solution: Net outward flux through the cylinder {tex}\vec{E} \cdot \vec{\Delta S}=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_1}+\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_2}+\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_3}{/tex} {tex}=(100+100) \pi r^2+0{/tex} (Here cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 90 o = 0 for {tex}\Delta S_3{/tex} ) {tex}=\left[200 \times 3.14 \times(0.05)^2\right] \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{C}{/tex} = 1.57 Nm 2 /C
solution_html:
Net outward flux through the cylinder
{tex}\vec{E} \cdot \vec{\Delta S}=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_1}+\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_2}+\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S_3}{/tex}
{tex}=(100+100) \pi r^2+0{/tex} (Here cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 90o = 0 for {tex}\Delta S_3{/tex})
{tex}=\left[200 \times 3.14 \times(0.05)^2\right] \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{C}{/tex}
= 1.57 Nm2/C

subject: Physics
question_html: Obtain the expression for the electric field at a point on the equatorial line of an electric dipole.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the electric field at a point on the equatorial line of an electric dipole.
explanation: {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}E=E_{+q} \cos \theta+E_{-q} \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=2 E_{+q} \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 q a}{4 \pi \epsilon_0\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}E=E_{+q} \cos \theta+E_{-q} \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=2 E_{+q} \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 q a}{4 \pi \epsilon_0\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}E_{+q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex}
{tex}E_{-q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex}
{tex}E=E_{+q} \cos \theta+E_{-q} \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}=2 E_{+q} \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 q a}{4 \pi \epsilon_0\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}

question_html: An electric field along the x-axis is given by {tex}\vec{E}=250 \hat{i}{/tex} N/C for x > 0 and {tex}\vec{E}=-250 \hat{i} N / C{/tex} for x < 0 . A right circular cylinder of length 24 cm and radius 5 cm lies parallel to the x-axis, with its centre at the origin and one face at x + 12 cm, the other face at x = -12 cm. question_text: An electric field along the x-axis is given by {tex}\vec{E}=250 \hat{i}{/tex} N/C for x > 0 and {tex}\vec{E}=-250 \hat{i} N / C{/tex} for x < 0 . A right circular cylinder of length 24 cm and radius 5 cm lies parallel to the x-axis, with its centre at the origin and one face at x + 12 cm, the other face at x = -12 cm. Calculate the net outward flux through the cylinder.
explanation: {tex} \text { flux }=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} Alternatively {tex}\emptyset=\int E d s \operatorname{Cos} \theta{/tex} {tex} \emptyset=\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_1}+\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_2}+\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_3}{/tex} Net outward flux {tex} =\left[250 \times \pi \times\left(\frac{5}{100}\right)^2+250 \times \pi \times\left(\frac{5}{100}\right)^2+0\right] \mathrm{Wb} {/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{5}{4} \pi\right) \mathrm{Wb} {/tex} {tex}(\cong 3.93) \mathrm{Wb} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { flux }=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} Alternatively {tex}\emptyset=\int E d s \operatorname{Cos} \theta{/tex} {tex} \emptyset=\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_1}+\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_2}+\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_3}{/tex} Net outward flux {tex} =\left[250 \times \pi \times\left(\frac{5}{100}\right)^2+250 \times \pi \times\left(\frac{5}{100}\right)^2+0\right] \mathrm{Wb} {/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{5}{4} \pi\right) \mathrm{Wb} {/tex} {tex}(\cong 3.93) \mathrm{Wb} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \text { flux }=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} 
Alternatively {tex}\emptyset=\int E d s \operatorname{Cos} \theta{/tex}
{tex} \emptyset=\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_1}+\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_2}+\int \vec{E} \cdot d \overrightarrow{s_3}{/tex}
Net outward flux
{tex} =\left[250 \times \pi \times\left(\frac{5}{100}\right)^2+250 \times \pi \times\left(\frac{5}{100}\right)^2+0\right] \mathrm{Wb} {/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{5}{4} \pi\right) \mathrm{Wb} {/tex}
{tex}(\cong 3.93) \mathrm{Wb} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Using Gauss’ law, obtain expressions for the electric field (i) inside, and (ii) outside a positively charged spherical shell.
  2. Show graphically variation of the electric field as a function of the distance r from the centre of the sphere.
  3. A square plane sheet of side 10 cm is inclined at an angle of 30o with the direction of a uniform electric field of 200 NC-1. Calculate the electric flux passing through the sheet.
question_text: Using Gauss’ law, obtain expressions for the electric field (i) inside, and (ii) outside a positively charged spherical shell. Show graphically variation of the electric field as a function of the distance r from the centre of the sphere. A square plane sheet of side 10 cm is inclined at an angle of 30 o with the direction of a uniform electric field of 200 NC -1 . Calculate the electric flux passing through the sheet.
explanation: Inside The point P is inside the spherical shell. The Gaussian surface is a sphere through P centered at ‘O’ Flux through this surface = {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2{/tex} However there is no charge enclosed by this Gaussian surface. Hence using Gauss’s Law {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = 0 Outside To calculate Electric Field {tex}\vec E{/tex} at the outside point P, we take the Gaussian surface to be a sphere of radius ‘r’ and with center O, passing through P. Electric Flux through the Gaussian surface {tex}\varphi=E \times 4 \pi r^2{/tex} Charge enclosed by this the Gaussian surface = {tex}\sigma \times 4 \pi R^2{/tex} By Gauss’s Law {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2=\frac{\sigma \times 4 \pi R^2}{\epsilon_0}=q / \epsilon_0{/tex} Where q= total charge on the spherical shell. {tex}\therefore E=\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0^{\prime}} \frac{q}{r^2} \hat{r}{/tex} Electric flux passing through the square sheet {tex}\phi=\int \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d s}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{EA} \cos \theta{/tex} =200 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 × cos 60 o = 1.0 Nm 2 /C
solution: Inside The point P is inside the spherical shell. The Gaussian surface is a sphere through P centered at ‘O’ Flux through this surface = {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2{/tex} However there is no charge enclosed by this Gaussian surface. Hence using Gauss’s Law {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = 0 Outside To calculate Electric Field {tex}\vec E{/tex} at the outside point P, we take the Gaussian surface to be a sphere of radius ‘r’ and with center O, passing through P. Electric Flux through the Gaussian surface {tex}\varphi=E \times 4 \pi r^2{/tex} Charge enclosed by this the Gaussian surface = {tex}\sigma \times 4 \pi R^2{/tex} By Gauss’s Law {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2=\frac{\sigma \times 4 \pi R^2}{\epsilon_0}=q / \epsilon_0{/tex} Where q= total charge on the spherical shell. {tex}\therefore E=\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0^{\prime}} \frac{q}{r^2} \hat{r}{/tex} Electric flux passing through the square sheet {tex}\phi=\int \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d s}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{EA} \cos \theta{/tex} =200 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 × cos 60 o = 1.0 Nm 2 /C
solution_html:
1. Inside

    The point P is inside the spherical shell. The Gaussian surface is a sphere through P centered at ‘O’
    Flux through this surface = {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2{/tex}
    However there is no charge enclosed by this Gaussian surface. Hence using Gauss’s Law
    {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = 0
    Outside

    To calculate Electric Field {tex}\vec E{/tex} at the outside point P, we take the Gaussian surface to be a sphere of radius ‘r’ and with center O, passing through P.
    Electric Flux through the Gaussian surface
    {tex}\varphi=E \times 4 \pi r^2{/tex}
    Charge enclosed by this the Gaussian surface = {tex}\sigma \times 4 \pi R^2{/tex}
    By Gauss’s Law
    {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^2=\frac{\sigma \times 4 \pi R^2}{\epsilon_0}=q / \epsilon_0{/tex}
    Where q= total charge on the spherical shell.
    {tex}\therefore E=\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0^{\prime}} \frac{q}{r^2} \hat{r}{/tex}

  3. Electric flux passing through the square sheet
    {tex}\phi=\int \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d s}{/tex}
    {tex}=\mathrm{EA} \cos \theta{/tex}
    =200 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 × cos 60o
    = 1.0 Nm2/C

solution: x = -10 cm x = 10 cm From the figure, we can see that on each face the electric field E and small area elements S are parallel. Therefore, outward flux, {tex}\phi=E \Delta S{/tex} On the left side {tex}\phi_1=E \Delta S{/tex} {tex}=100 \widehat{i} \Delta S{/tex} {tex}=+100 \Delta S{/tex} , since {tex}\Delta S=-\Delta S{/tex} {tex}=0.785 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-1}{/tex} On the side of the cylinder electric field is perpendicular to {tex}\triangle S{/tex} and hence {tex}E . \triangle S=0{/tex} . Therefore, outward flux on the side of the cylinder {tex}\phi=0.785+0.785+0=1.57 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:

From the figure, we can see that on each face the electric field E and small area elements S are parallel. Therefore, outward flux, {tex}\phi=E \Delta S{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define electric flux. Write its SI units.
  2. A spherical rubber balloon carries a charge that is uniformly distributed over its surface. As the balloon is blown up and increases in size, how does the total electric flux coming out of the surface change? Give reason.
question_text: Define electric flux. Write its SI units. A spherical rubber balloon carries a charge that is uniformly distributed over its surface. As the balloon is blown up and increases in size, how does the total electric flux coming out of the surface change? Give reason.
explanation: Electric flux is defined as the total number of electric lines of force passing normally through that area. It is given by: {tex}{\phi _E} = \overrightarrow E \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow S{/tex} SI unit of electric flux is Nm 2 C -1 . As the balloon is blown up, the total charge on the balloon surface remains unchanged, so the total electric flux coming out of its surface remains unchanged.
solution: Electric flux is defined as the total number of electric lines of force passing normally through that area. It is given by: {tex}{\phi _E} = \overrightarrow E \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow S{/tex} SI unit of electric flux is Nm 2 C -1 . As the balloon is blown up, the total charge on the balloon surface remains unchanged, so the total electric flux coming out of its surface remains unchanged.
solution_html:
1. Electric flux is defined as the total number of electric lines of force passing normally through that area. It is given by:
    {tex}{\phi _E} = \overrightarrow E \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow S{/tex}
    SI unit of electric flux is Nm2C-1.
  2. As the balloon is blown up, the total charge on the balloon surface remains unchanged, so the total electric flux coming out of its surface remains unchanged.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Four point charges Q, q, Q and q are placed at the corners of a square of side 'a' as shown in the figure.

Find the 

  1. Resultant electric force on a charge Q, and 
  2. Potential energy of this system.
question_text: Four point charges Q, q, Q and q are placed at the corners of a square of side ' a' as shown in the figure. Find the Resultant electric force on a charge Q, and Potential energy of this system.
explanation: Force on charge Q at B due to charge q {tex}\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{1 \mathrm{qQ}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{Oa}}{ }^2} \text { along } \vec {BY}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{1 \mathrm{qQ}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{Oa}}{ }^2} \text { along } \vec {BX}{/tex} Resultant force, F = {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{F}_1^2+\mathrm{F}_2^2} \text { along BO }{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2\left(\frac{1 q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0 a^2}}\right)^2}=\frac{q Q \sqrt{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_O a^2}{/tex} Force due to charge Q = F 3 = {tex}\frac{1 \quad Q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_Q\left(\sqrt{2} a^2\right)} \text { along } \vec{B O}{/tex} Total resultant force = F + F3 along {tex}\vec {BO}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1 \sqrt{2} Q q}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 a^2}+\frac{1 Q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon 2 a^2}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{1 Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{o a^2} }\left(\sqrt{2} q+\frac{Q}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} and {tex}\vec {BO}{/tex} Total potential energy of the system, U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q Q}{\sqrt{2} a}+\frac{q q}{\sqrt{2} a}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[\frac{4 Q q}{a}+\frac{Q^2}{\sqrt{2} a}+\frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2} a}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[4 \mathrm{Qq}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2+\mathrm{q}^2}{\sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex}
solution: Force on charge Q at B due to charge q {tex}\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{1 \mathrm{qQ}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{Oa}}{ }^2} \text { along } \vec {BY}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{1 \mathrm{qQ}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{Oa}}{ }^2} \text { along } \vec {BX}{/tex} Resultant force, F = {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{F}_1^2+\mathrm{F}_2^2} \text { along BO }{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2\left(\frac{1 q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0 a^2}}\right)^2}=\frac{q Q \sqrt{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_O a^2}{/tex} Force due to charge Q = F 3 = {tex}\frac{1 \quad Q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_Q\left(\sqrt{2} a^2\right)} \text { along } \vec{B O}{/tex} Total resultant force = F + F3 along {tex}\vec {BO}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1 \sqrt{2} Q q}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 a^2}+\frac{1 Q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon 2 a^2}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{1 Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{o a^2} }\left(\sqrt{2} q+\frac{Q}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} and {tex}\vec {BO}{/tex} Total potential energy of the system, U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q Q}{\sqrt{2} a}+\frac{q q}{\sqrt{2} a}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[\frac{4 Q q}{a}+\frac{Q^2}{\sqrt{2} a}+\frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2} a}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[4 \mathrm{Qq}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2+\mathrm{q}^2}{\sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Force on charge Q at B due to charge q
    {tex}\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{1 \mathrm{qQ}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{Oa}}{ }^2} \text { along } \vec {BY}{/tex}

    {tex}\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{1 \mathrm{qQ}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{Oa}}{ }^2} \text { along } \vec {BX}{/tex}
    Resultant force, F = {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{F}_1^2+\mathrm{F}_2^2} \text { along BO }{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{2\left(\frac{1 q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0 a^2}}\right)^2}=\frac{q Q \sqrt{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_O a^2}{/tex}
    Force due to charge Q = F3 = {tex}\frac{1 \quad Q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_Q\left(\sqrt{2} a^2\right)} \text { along } \vec{B O}{/tex}
    Total resultant force = F + F3 along {tex}\vec {BO}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1 \sqrt{2} Q q}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 a^2}+\frac{1 Q Q}{4 \pi \epsilon 2 a^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\left[\frac{1 Q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{o a^2} }\left(\sqrt{2} q+\frac{Q}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} and {tex}\vec {BO}{/tex}
  2. Total potential energy of the system,
    U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q q}{a}+\frac{Q Q}{\sqrt{2} a}+\frac{q q}{\sqrt{2} a}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[\frac{4 Q q}{a}+\frac{Q^2}{\sqrt{2} a}+\frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2} a}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\left[4 \mathrm{Qq}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2+\mathrm{q}^2}{\sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex}

explanation: Electric flux is associated with the electric field. The electric flux through a surface held inside an electric field represents the total number of electric lines of force crossing the surface in a direction normal to the surface.

question_html:
1. Three point charges q, – 4q and 2q are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle ABC of side {tex}l{/tex} as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the magnitude of the resultant electric force acting on the charge q.
  2. Find out the amount of the work done to separate the charges at infinite distance.
question_text: Three point charges q, – 4q and 2q are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle ABC of side {tex}l{/tex} as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the magnitude of the resultant electric force acting on the charge q. Find out the amount of the work done to separate the charges at infinite distance.
explanation: Consider the figure shown below. The forces acting on charge q at A due to charges -4q at B and 2q at C are F 1 along AB and F 2 along CA respectively. | {tex}\vec F_1{/tex} | = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(4 \mathrm{q})(\mathrm{q})}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\left(4 \mathrm{q}^{2}\right)}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex} | {tex}\vec F_2{/tex} | = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 \mathrm{q})(\mathrm{q})}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex} Thus, F 1 = 2F 2 Now angle between {tex}\vec F_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec F_2{/tex} is 120 o . Thus magnitude of the resultant force F is given by, F = {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{F}_{1}^{1}+\mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{F}_{1} \mathrm{F}_{2} \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex} F = {tex}\sqrt{\left(2 F_{2}\right)^{2}+F_{2}^{2}+4 F_{2}^{2} \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex} F = {tex}\sqrt{4 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}+\mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}-2 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}}{/tex} F = {tex}\sqrt{3 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}}{/tex} F = {tex} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex} The amount of work done to separate the charges to infinity will be equal to potential energy of the system of charges. U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [q {tex}\times{/tex} (-4q) + (q {tex}\times{/tex} 2q) + (-4q {tex}\times{/tex} 2q)] U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [-4q 2 + 2q 2 - 8q 2 ] U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [-10q 2 ] U = - {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [10q 2 ]
solution: Consider the figure shown below. The forces acting on charge q at A due to charges -4q at B and 2q at C are F 1 along AB and F 2 along CA respectively. | {tex}\vec F_1{/tex} | = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(4 \mathrm{q})(\mathrm{q})}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\left(4 \mathrm{q}^{2}\right)}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex} | {tex}\vec F_2{/tex} | = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 \mathrm{q})(\mathrm{q})}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex} Thus, F 1 = 2F 2 Now angle between {tex}\vec F_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec F_2{/tex} is 120 o . Thus magnitude of the resultant force F is given by, F = {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{F}_{1}^{1}+\mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{F}_{1} \mathrm{F}_{2} \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex} F = {tex}\sqrt{\left(2 F_{2}\right)^{2}+F_{2}^{2}+4 F_{2}^{2} \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex} F = {tex}\sqrt{4 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}+\mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}-2 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}}{/tex} F = {tex}\sqrt{3 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}}{/tex} F = {tex} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex} The amount of work done to separate the charges to infinity will be equal to potential energy of the system of charges. U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [q {tex}\times{/tex} (-4q) + (q {tex}\times{/tex} 2q) + (-4q {tex}\times{/tex} 2q)] U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [-4q 2 + 2q 2 - 8q 2 ] U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [-10q 2 ] U = - {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [10q 2 ]
solution_html:
1. Consider the figure shown below. The forces acting on charge q at A due to charges -4q at B and 2q at C are F1 along AB and F2 along CA respectively.

    | {tex}\vec F_1{/tex}| = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(4 \mathrm{q})(\mathrm{q})}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\left(4 \mathrm{q}^{2}\right)}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex}
    | {tex}\vec F_2{/tex}| = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 \mathrm{q})(\mathrm{q})}{l^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex}
    Thus, F1 = 2F2
    Now angle between {tex}\vec F_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec F_2{/tex} is 120o. Thus magnitude of the resultant force F is given by,
    F = {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{F}_{1}^{1}+\mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{F}_{1} \mathrm{F}_{2} \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex}
    F = {tex}\sqrt{\left(2 F_{2}\right)^{2}+F_{2}^{2}+4 F_{2}^{2} \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex}
    F = {tex}\sqrt{4 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}+\mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}-2 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}}{/tex}
    F = {tex}\sqrt{3 \mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}}{/tex} 
    F = {tex} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q^{2}}{l^{2}}{/tex}

  2. The amount of work done to separate the charges to infinity will be equal to potential energy of the system of charges.
    U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex}[q {tex}\times{/tex}(-4q) + (q {tex}\times{/tex} 2q) + (-4q {tex}\times{/tex} 2q)]
    U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex}[-4q2 + 2q2 - 8q2]
    U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [-10q2]
    U = -{tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} l}{/tex} [10q2]

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. An electric dipole is kept first to the left and then to the right of a negatively charged infinite plane sheet having a uniform surface charge density. The arrows p1 and p2 show the directions of its electric dipole moments in the two cases.

    Identify for each case, whether the dipole is in stable or unstable equilibrium. Justify each answer
  2. Next, the dipole is kept in a similar way (as shown), near an infinitely long straight wire having uniform negative linear charge density.

    Will the dipole be in equilibrium at these two positions? Justify your answer.
question_text: An electric dipole is kept first to the left and then to the right of a negatively charged infinite plane sheet having a uniform surface charge density. The arrows p 1 and p 2 show the directions of its electric dipole moments in the two cases. Identify for each case, whether the dipole is in stable or unstable equilibrium. Justify each answer Next, the dipole is kept in a similar way (as shown), near an infinitely long straight wire having uniform negative linear charge density. Will the dipole be in equilibrium at these two positions? Justify your answer.
explanation: p 1 : stable equilibrium p 2 : unstable equilibrium The electric field, on either side, is directed towards the negatively charged sheet and its magnitude is independent of the distance of the field point from the sheet. For position, p 1 dipole moment and electric field are parallel For position P 2 they are anti-parallel. In the case of P 1 dipole makes 180 o angle with electric field hence in the stable equilibrium whereas in case P 2 dipole makes 0 o angle with the electric field hence unstable. The dipole will not be in equilibrium in any of the two positions. The electric field due to an infinite straight charged wire is non-uniform (E {tex}\alpha \frac 1r{/tex} ). Hence, there will be a net non-zero force on the dipole in each case.
solution: p 1 : stable equilibrium p 2 : unstable equilibrium The electric field, on either side, is directed towards the negatively charged sheet and its magnitude is independent of the distance of the field point from the sheet. For position, p 1 dipole moment and electric field are parallel For position P 2 they are anti-parallel. In the case of P 1 dipole makes 180 o angle with electric field hence in the stable equilibrium whereas in case P 2 dipole makes 0 o angle with the electric field hence unstable. The dipole will not be in equilibrium in any of the two positions. The electric field due to an infinite straight charged wire is non-uniform (E {tex}\alpha \frac 1r{/tex} ). Hence, there will be a net non-zero force on the dipole in each case.
solution_html:
1. p1: stable equilibrium
    p2: unstable equilibrium
    The electric field, on either side, is directed towards the negatively charged sheet and its magnitude is independent of the distance of the field point from the sheet. For position, p1 dipole moment and electric field are parallel For position P2 they are anti-parallel. In the case of P1 dipole makes 180o angle with electric field hence in the stable equilibrium whereas in case P2 dipole makes 0o angle with the electric field hence unstable.
  2. The dipole will not be in equilibrium in any of the two positions. The electric field due to an infinite straight charged wire is non-uniform (E{tex}\alpha \frac 1r{/tex}). Hence, there will be a net non-zero force on the dipole in each case.

question_html: A uniformly charged non-conducting disc with surface charge density 10 nC/m2 has radius R = 3 cm. The magnitude of the electric field at a point P on the perpendicular bisector at a distance 2 cm from the origin of the disc is
question_text: A uniformly charged non-conducting disc with surface charge density 10 nC/m 2 has radius R = 3 cm. The magnitude of the electric field at a point P on the perpendicular bisector at a distance 2 cm from the origin of the disc is

options.explanation: Electric field at an axial point of the disc at distance r is E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\left[1-\frac{x}{\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times 2 \pi \times\left[1-\frac{x}{\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.28 {tex}\left[1-\frac{2}{\sqrt{9+4}}\right]{/tex} NC -1 = 90 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left[1-\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right]{/tex} NC -1 = 251.2 NC -1

explanation: Electric field at an axial point of the disc at distance r is E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\left[1-\frac{x}{\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times 2 \pi \times\left[1-\frac{x}{\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.28 {tex}\left[1-\frac{2}{\sqrt{9+4}}\right]{/tex} NC -1 = 90 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left[1-\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right]{/tex} NC -1 = 251.2 NC -1

question_html: A long solid non-conducting cylinder of radius R is charged such that the volume charge density is proportional to r, where r is the distance from the axis. The electric field E at a distance r(r < R) will depend on r as:
question_text: A long solid non-conducting cylinder of radius R is charged such that the volume charge density is proportional to r, where r is the distance from the axis. The electric field E at a distance r(r < R) will depend on r as:

options.explanation: {tex}\rho \propto r \Rightarrow \rho=\alpha r{/tex} dp = {tex}\rho{/tex} dV = {tex}\alpha{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} dr {tex}\times{/tex} l Using Guass's theorem, E {tex}\times2 \pi r l=\frac{\int_{0}^{r} 2 \pi \alpha l r^{2} d r}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \alpha l r^{3}}{3 \varepsilon_{0} \times 2 \pi r l}=\frac{\alpha r^{2}}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E {tex}\propto{/tex} r 2

explanation: {tex}\rho \propto r \Rightarrow \rho=\alpha r{/tex} dp = {tex}\rho{/tex} dV = {tex}\alpha{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} dr {tex}\times{/tex} l Using Guass's theorem, E {tex}\times2 \pi r l=\frac{\int_{0}^{r} 2 \pi \alpha l r^{2} d r}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \alpha l r^{3}}{3 \varepsilon_{0} \times 2 \pi r l}=\frac{\alpha r^{2}}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E {tex}\propto{/tex} r 2

subject: Physics
question_html:
The electric field components due to a charge inside the cube of side 0.1 m are as shown.
Ex = ax, where a = 100 N/C-m, Ey = 0, Ez = 0

Calculate:

  1. the flux through the cube, and
  2. the charge inside the cube.
question_text: The electric field components due to a charge inside the cube of side 0.1 m are as shown. E x = ax, where a = 100 N/C-m, E y = 0, E z = 0 Calculate: the flux through the cube, and the charge inside the cube.
explanation: i. Calculate the Flux through the Cube The total flux {tex}(\Phi){/tex} is the sum of the flux through the left face {tex}\left(\Phi_L\right){/tex} and the right face {tex}\left(\Phi_R\right){/tex} . At the Left Face ( {tex}x=0{/tex} ): {tex} E_L=\alpha(0)=0 {~N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} \Phi_L=E_L \cdot A \cdot \cos \left(180^{\circ}\right)=0 {/tex} At the Right Face {tex}(x=0.1 {~m}){/tex} : The area of the face is {tex}A=(0.1 {~m})^2=0.01 {~m}^2{/tex} . {tex} E_R=\alpha(0.1)=100 \times 0.1=10 {~N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} \Phi_R=E_R \cdot A \cdot \cos \left(0^{\circ}\right)=10 \times 0.01=0.1 {~N} \cdot {~m}^2 / {C} {/tex} Total Flux: {tex} \Phi_{\text {total }}=\Phi_L+\Phi_R=0+0.1={0 . 1} {V} \cdot {~m}\left(\text { or } {N} \cdot {~m}^2 / {C}\right) {/tex} ii. Calculate the Charge inside the Cube According to Gauss's Law: {tex} \Phi=\frac{q_{\text {enclosed }}}{\epsilon_0} {/tex} Where {tex}\epsilon_0{/tex} (permittivity of free space) is approximately {tex}8.854 \times 10^{-12} {C}^2 / {N} \cdot {m}^2{/tex} . Solving for {tex}q{/tex} : {tex} q=\Phi \cdot \epsilon_0 {/tex} {tex} q=0.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} {/tex} {tex} q={8 . 8 5 4} \times {1 0}^{-{1 3}} {C} {/tex}
solution: i. Calculate the Flux through the Cube The total flux {tex}(\Phi){/tex} is the sum of the flux through the left face {tex}\left(\Phi_L\right){/tex} and the right face {tex}\left(\Phi_R\right){/tex} . At the Left Face ( {tex}x=0{/tex} ): {tex} E_L=\alpha(0)=0 {~N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} \Phi_L=E_L \cdot A \cdot \cos \left(180^{\circ}\right)=0 {/tex} At the Right Face {tex}(x=0.1 {~m}){/tex} : The area of the face is {tex}A=(0.1 {~m})^2=0.01 {~m}^2{/tex} . {tex} E_R=\alpha(0.1)=100 \times 0.1=10 {~N} / {C} {/tex} {tex} \Phi_R=E_R \cdot A \cdot \cos \left(0^{\circ}\right)=10 \times 0.01=0.1 {~N} \cdot {~m}^2 / {C} {/tex} Total Flux: {tex} \Phi_{\text {total }}=\Phi_L+\Phi_R=0+0.1={0 . 1} {V} \cdot {~m}\left(\text { or } {N} \cdot {~m}^2 / {C}\right) {/tex} ii. Calculate the Charge inside the Cube According to Gauss's Law: {tex} \Phi=\frac{q_{\text {enclosed }}}{\epsilon_0} {/tex} Where {tex}\epsilon_0{/tex} (permittivity of free space) is approximately {tex}8.854 \times 10^{-12} {C}^2 / {N} \cdot {m}^2{/tex} . Solving for {tex}q{/tex} : {tex} q=\Phi \cdot \epsilon_0 {/tex} {tex} q=0.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} {/tex} {tex} q={8 . 8 5 4} \times {1 0}^{-{1 3}} {C} {/tex}
solution_html:
i. Calculate the Flux through the Cube

The total flux {tex}(\Phi){/tex} is the sum of the flux through the left face {tex}\left(\Phi_L\right){/tex} and the right face {tex}\left(\Phi_R\right){/tex}.
At the Left Face ( {tex}x=0{/tex} ):

{tex} E_L=\alpha(0)=0 {~N} / {C} {/tex}
{tex} \Phi_L=E_L \cdot A \cdot \cos \left(180^{\circ}\right)=0 {/tex}

At the Right Face {tex}(x=0.1 {~m}){/tex} : The area of the face is {tex}A=(0.1 {~m})^2=0.01 {~m}^2{/tex}.

{tex} E_R=\alpha(0.1)=100 \times 0.1=10 {~N} / {C} {/tex}
{tex} \Phi_R=E_R \cdot A \cdot \cos \left(0^{\circ}\right)=10 \times 0.01=0.1 {~N} \cdot {~m}^2 / {C} {/tex}

Total Flux:

{tex} \Phi_{\text {total }}=\Phi_L+\Phi_R=0+0.1={0 . 1} {V} \cdot {~m}\left(\text { or } {N} \cdot {~m}^2 / {C}\right) {/tex}

ii. Calculate the Charge inside the Cube

According to Gauss's Law:

{tex} \Phi=\frac{q_{\text {enclosed }}}{\epsilon_0} {/tex}

Where {tex}\epsilon_0{/tex} (permittivity of free space) is approximately {tex}8.854 \times 10^{-12} {C}^2 / {N} \cdot {m}^2{/tex}.
Solving for {tex}q{/tex} :

{tex} q=\Phi \cdot \epsilon_0 {/tex}
{tex} q=0.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} {/tex}
{tex} q={8 . 8 5 4} \times {1 0}^{-{1 3}} {C} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Assertion: Net electric field inside a conductor is zero.
Reason: Total positive charge equals to total negative charge in a charged conductor.
question_text: Assertion: Net electric field inside a conductor is zero. Reason: Total positive charge equals to total negative charge in a charged conductor.

question_text: An electron falls from rest through a vertical distance h in a uniform and vertically upward-directed electric field E. The direction of the electric field is now reversed, keeping its magnitude the same. A proton is allowed to fall from rest in it through the same vertical distance h. The time of fall of the electron, in comparison to the time of fall of the proton is

options.explanation: F = ma = eE {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{e E}{m}{/tex} s = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at 2 h = 0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{e E}{m}{/tex} t 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} t = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 h m}{e E}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t {tex}\propto \sqrt{m}{/tex} As electron has a smaller mass, its time of fall will be smaller than that of a proton.

question_html:
An electron falls through a distance of 1.5 cm in a uniform electric field of magnitude 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 N/C

Calculate the time it takes to fall through this distance starting from rest.

If the direction of the field is reversed (fig. b) keeping its magnitude unchanged, calculate the time taken by a proton to fall through this distance starting from rest.
question_text: An electron falls through a distance of 1.5 cm in a uniform electric field of magnitude 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 N/C Calculate the time it takes to fall through this distance starting from rest. If the direction of the field is reversed (fig. b) keeping its magnitude unchanged, calculate the time taken by a proton to fall through this distance starting from rest.
explanation: For the electron The field is upward , so the negatively charged particle, electron experiences a downward froce of magnitude eE where E is the magnitude of the electric field. The acceleration of the electron is ae = eE/m Starting from rest , the time required by the electron to fall through a distance h {tex}a=\frac{e E}{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t=\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 10^{-2} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.0 \times 10^{4}}}{/tex} = 2.92 * 10 -9 s for proton The field is downward, and the positively charged proton experiences a downward force of magnitude eE . The acceleration of the proton is {tex}t=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{4}}}{/tex} t = 1.3*10 -7
solution: For the electron The field is upward , so the negatively charged particle, electron experiences a downward froce of magnitude eE where E is the magnitude of the electric field. The acceleration of the electron is ae = eE/m Starting from rest , the time required by the electron to fall through a distance h {tex}a=\frac{e E}{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t=\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 10^{-2} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.0 \times 10^{4}}}{/tex} = 2.92 * 10 -9 s for proton The field is downward, and the positively charged proton experiences a downward force of magnitude eE . The acceleration of the proton is {tex}t=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{4}}}{/tex} t = 1.3*10 -7
solution_html:
1. For the electron
    The field is upward , so the negatively charged particle, electron experiences a downward froce of magnitude eE where E is the magnitude of the electric field. The acceleration of the electron is ae = eE/m
    Starting from rest , the time required by the electron to fall through a distance h
    {tex}a=\frac{e E}{m}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t=\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 10^{-2} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.0 \times 10^{4}}}{/tex}
    = 2.92 * 10-9s
  2. for proton
    The field is downward, and the positively charged proton experiences a downward force of magnitude eE . The acceleration of the proton is
    {tex}t=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{4}}}{/tex}
    t = 1.3*10-7

explanation: Electric Flux is the dot product of electric field and area vector. Also accept {tex}\phi=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} SI Unit: Nm 2 /C or volt-meter For a given case {tex}\phi=\phi_{1}+\phi_{2}={/tex} {tex}\left[E_{x}(a t x=2 a)-E_{x}(a t x=a)\right] a^{2}{/tex} = [a(2a) - a(a)]a 2 - aa 3 = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.1) 3 = 0.1 Mm 2 /C But {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore q=\varepsilon_{0} \phi{/tex} = 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 C = 0.8854 C
solution: Electric Flux is the dot product of electric field and area vector. Also accept {tex}\phi=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} SI Unit: Nm 2 /C or volt-meter For a given case {tex}\phi=\phi_{1}+\phi_{2}={/tex} {tex}\left[E_{x}(a t x=2 a)-E_{x}(a t x=a)\right] a^{2}{/tex} = [a(2a) - a(a)]a 2 - aa 3 = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.1) 3 = 0.1 Mm 2 /C But {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore q=\varepsilon_{0} \phi{/tex} = 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 C = 0.8854 C

{tex}\phi=\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex}
SI Unit: Nm2/C or volt-meter For a given case

= 100 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.1)3 = 0.1 Mm2/C

subject: Physics
question_html: Four point charges Q, q, Q and q are placed at the corners of a square of side a as shown in the figure. Find the resultant electric force on a charge Q.
question_text: Four point charges Q, q, Q and q are placed at the corners of a square of side a as shown in the figure. Find the resultant electric force on a charge Q.
explanation: Let us find the force on the charge Q at the point C Force due to the other charge Q {tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q^2}{(a \sqrt{2})^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 a^2}\right) \text { (along AC) }{/tex} Force due to the charge q (at B), F 2 {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along BC Force due to the charge q (at D), F 3 {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q}{a^2} \text { along } DC{/tex} Resultant of these two equal forces {tex}F_{23}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q(\sqrt{2})}{a^2} \text { (along AC) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on charge Q (at point C) F = F 1 + {tex}F_{23}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{a^2}\left[\frac{Q}{2}+\sqrt{2} q\right]{/tex} This force is directed along AC. (For the charge Q, at the point A, the force will have the same magnitude but will be directed along CA)
solution: Let us find the force on the charge Q at the point C Force due to the other charge Q {tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q^2}{(a \sqrt{2})^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 a^2}\right) \text { (along AC) }{/tex} Force due to the charge q (at B), F 2 {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along BC Force due to the charge q (at D), F 3 {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q}{a^2} \text { along } DC{/tex} Resultant of these two equal forces {tex}F_{23}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q(\sqrt{2})}{a^2} \text { (along AC) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on charge Q (at point C) F = F 1 + {tex}F_{23}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{a^2}\left[\frac{Q}{2}+\sqrt{2} q\right]{/tex} This force is directed along AC. (For the charge Q, at the point A, the force will have the same magnitude but will be directed along CA)
solution_html:
Let us find the force on the charge Q at the point C
Force due to the other charge Q
{tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q^2}{(a \sqrt{2})^2}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 a^2}\right) \text { (along AC) }{/tex}
Force due to the charge q (at B), F2
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along BC
Force due to the charge q (at D), F3
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q}{a^2} \text { along } DC{/tex}
Resultant of these two equal forces
{tex}F_{23}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q(\sqrt{2})}{a^2} \text { (along AC) }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on charge Q (at point C)
F = F1 + {tex}F_{23}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{a^2}\left[\frac{Q}{2}+\sqrt{2} q\right]{/tex}
This force is directed along AC. (For the charge Q, at the point A, the force will have the same magnitude but will be directed along CA)

subject: Physics
question_html: Three point charges q, - 4q and 2q are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle ABC of side I as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the magnitude of the resultant electric force acting on the charge q.
question_text: Three point charges q, - 4q and 2q are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle ABC of side I as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the magnitude of the resultant electric force acting on the charge q.
explanation: Force on charge q due to the charge -4q {tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{4 q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex} , along AB Force on the charge q, due to the charge 2q {tex}F_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{2 q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex} , along CA The forces F 1 and F 2 are inclined to each other at an angle of 120 o Hence, resultant electric force on charge q F {tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2+2 F_1 F_2 \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2+2 F_1 F_2 \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2-F_1 F_2}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q^2}{l^2}\right) \sqrt{16+4-8}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{3} q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex}
solution: Force on charge q due to the charge -4q {tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{4 q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex} , along AB Force on the charge q, due to the charge 2q {tex}F_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{2 q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex} , along CA The forces F 1 and F 2 are inclined to each other at an angle of 120 o Hence, resultant electric force on charge q F {tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2+2 F_1 F_2 \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2+2 F_1 F_2 \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2-F_1 F_2}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q^2}{l^2}\right) \sqrt{16+4-8}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{3} q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Force on charge q due to the charge -4q
{tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{4 q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex}, along AB
Force on the charge q, due to the charge 2q
{tex}F_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{2 q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex}, along CA
The forces F1 and F2 are inclined to each other at an angle of 120o Hence, resultant electric force on charge q

F {tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2+2 F_1 F_2 \cos \theta}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2+2 F_1 F_2 \cos 120^{\circ}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2-F_1 F_2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q^2}{l^2}\right) \sqrt{16+4-8}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{3} q^2}{l^2}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State Gauss's law in electrostatics. Use this law to derive an expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire of linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} Cm-1.
question_text: State Gauss's law in electrostatics. Use this law to derive an expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire of linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} Cm -1 .
explanation: Gauss's law in electrostatics: It states that total electric flux over the closed surface S in vacuum is {tex}\frac{1}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} times the total charge (q) contained in side S. {tex}\therefore {\phi _E} = \oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire: Let an infinitely long line charge having linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Assume a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length l coaxial with the line charge to determine its electric field at distance r. By symmetry, the electric field E has same magnitude at each point of the curved surface S 1 and is directed radially outward. So angle at surfaces between {tex}\overrightarrow {dS}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow E{/tex} is zero, and angle of {tex}{\overrightarrow {dS} _2},\,{\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} with {tex}\overrightarrow E {/tex} at S 2 and S 3 are 90°. Total flux through the cylindrical surface, {tex}\oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _1} + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _2} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} {tex} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {E\,d{S_1}} \cdot \cos 0^\circ + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {E\,d{S_2}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ{/tex} {tex} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {E\,d{S_3}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ {/tex} {tex} = E\oint {d{S_1}} = E \times 2\pi rl{/tex} Since {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the charge per unit length and l is the length of the wire, Thus, the charge enclosed {tex}q = \lambda l{/tex} According to Gaussian law, {tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} or, {tex}E \times 2\pi rl = \frac{{\lambda l}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{/tex}
solution: Gauss's law in electrostatics: It states that total electric flux over the closed surface S in vacuum is {tex}\frac{1}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} times the total charge (q) contained in side S. {tex}\therefore {\phi _E} = \oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire: Let an infinitely long line charge having linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Assume a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length l coaxial with the line charge to determine its electric field at distance r. By symmetry, the electric field E has same magnitude at each point of the curved surface S 1 and is directed radially outward. So angle at surfaces between {tex}\overrightarrow {dS}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow E{/tex} is zero, and angle of {tex}{\overrightarrow {dS} _2},\,{\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} with {tex}\overrightarrow E {/tex} at S 2 and S 3 are 90°. Total flux through the cylindrical surface, {tex}\oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _1} + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _2} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} {tex} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {E\,d{S_1}} \cdot \cos 0^\circ + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {E\,d{S_2}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ{/tex} {tex} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {E\,d{S_3}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ {/tex} {tex} = E\oint {d{S_1}} = E \times 2\pi rl{/tex} Since {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the charge per unit length and l is the length of the wire, Thus, the charge enclosed {tex}q = \lambda l{/tex} According to Gaussian law, {tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} or, {tex}E \times 2\pi rl = \frac{{\lambda l}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Gauss's law in electrostatics: It states that total electric flux over the closed surface S in vacuum is {tex}\frac{1}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} times the total charge (q) contained in side S.
{tex}\therefore {\phi _E} = \oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
Electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire:
Let an infinitely long line charge having linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}. Assume a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length l coaxial with the line charge to determine its electric field at distance r.
By symmetry, the electric field E has same magnitude at each point of the curved surface S1 and is directed radially outward. So angle at surfaces between {tex}\overrightarrow {dS}{/tex}  and {tex}\overrightarrow E{/tex} is zero, and angle of {tex}{\overrightarrow {dS} _2},\,{\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex}  with {tex}\overrightarrow E {/tex} at S2 and S3 are 90°.

Total flux through the cylindrical surface,
{tex}\oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _1} + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _2} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex}
{tex} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {E\,d{S_1}} \cdot \cos 0^\circ + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {E\,d{S_2}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ{/tex} {tex} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {E\,d{S_3}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ {/tex}
{tex} = E\oint {d{S_1}} = E \times 2\pi rl{/tex}
Since {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the charge per unit length and l is the length of the wire,
Thus, the charge enclosed
{tex}q = \lambda l{/tex}
According to Gaussian law,
{tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
or, {tex}E \times 2\pi rl = \frac{{\lambda l}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore E = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{/tex}
