question_html: Using Gauss's law in electrostatics, deduce an expression for electric field intensity due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet. If another identical sheet is placed parallel to it, show that there is no electric field in the region between the two sheets.
question_text: Using Gauss's law in electrostatics, deduce an expression for electric field intensity due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet. If another identical sheet is placed parallel to it, show that there is no electric field in the region between the two sheets.
explanation: In the figure, if a is uniform surface charge density of an infinite plane sheet with x-axis to be normal to the plane, then by symmetry, the electric field will not depend on y and z coordinates and their directions. Considering the Gaussian rectangular parallelepiped surface with cross-sectional area A, two faces 1 and 2 will contribute to flux where electric field lines result as parallel to other faces which do not contribute to total flux. It is analyzed that the unit vector which is normal to the first surface will be in the negative x-direction while the unit vector which is normal to the second surface will be in positive +x direction, so flux {tex}E . \Delta S{/tex} by the surfaces will results equal and will add up. Consider an infinite sheet of positive charge having a density {tex}\sigma{/tex} on sheet. Let P be the point on which net flux through Gaussian surface = 2EA charge enclosed by closed surface = {tex}\sigma{/tex} A As per Gauss's law, {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2 E A=\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} This shows that the electric field around an infinite plane of charge does not vary with distance from the plane. In terms of vector, {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{n}{/tex} where, {tex}\hat n{/tex} = unit vector normal to plane and going away from it {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = directed away from the plate if {tex}\sigma{/tex} is (+) positive and toward the plate if {tex}\sigma{/tex} is (-) negative. Since the two charged infinite plates have identical charges, so the electric field between two identical charged sheets will be as shown in the figure: Now the electric field due to surface 1 {tex}\vec{E}_{1}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Now the electric field due to surface 2 {tex}\vec{E}_{2}=-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Now the resultant electric field between the uniformly charged infinite plates {tex}=\vec{E}_{1}+\vec{E}_{2}, \theta=180^{\circ}{/tex} So, {tex}E=\sqrt{E_{1}^{2}+E_{2}^{2}+2 E_{1} E_{2} \cos \theta}v{/tex} = E 1 - E 2 {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}=0{/tex}
solution: In the figure, if a is uniform surface charge density of an infinite plane sheet with x-axis to be normal to the plane, then by symmetry, the electric field will not depend on y and z coordinates and their directions. Considering the Gaussian rectangular parallelepiped surface with cross-sectional area A, two faces 1 and 2 will contribute to flux where electric field lines result as parallel to other faces which do not contribute to total flux. It is analyzed that the unit vector which is normal to the first surface will be in the negative x-direction while the unit vector which is normal to the second surface will be in positive +x direction, so flux {tex}E . \Delta S{/tex} by the surfaces will results equal and will add up. Consider an infinite sheet of positive charge having a density {tex}\sigma{/tex} on sheet. Let P be the point on which net flux through Gaussian surface = 2EA charge enclosed by closed surface = {tex}\sigma{/tex} A As per Gauss's law, {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2 E A=\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} This shows that the electric field around an infinite plane of charge does not vary with distance from the plane. In terms of vector, {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{n}{/tex} where, {tex}\hat n{/tex} = unit vector normal to plane and going away from it {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = directed away from the plate if {tex}\sigma{/tex} is (+) positive and toward the plate if {tex}\sigma{/tex} is (-) negative. Since the two charged infinite plates have identical charges, so the electric field between two identical charged sheets will be as shown in the figure: Now the electric field due to surface 1 {tex}\vec{E}_{1}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Now the electric field due to surface 2 {tex}\vec{E}_{2}=-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Now the resultant electric field between the uniformly charged infinite plates {tex}=\vec{E}_{1}+\vec{E}_{2}, \theta=180^{\circ}{/tex} So, {tex}E=\sqrt{E_{1}^{2}+E_{2}^{2}+2 E_{1} E_{2} \cos \theta}v{/tex} = E 1 - E 2 {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
In the figure, if a is uniform surface charge density of an infinite plane sheet with x-axis to be normal to the plane, then by symmetry, the electric field will not depend on y and z coordinates and their directions. Considering the Gaussian rectangular parallelepiped surface with cross-sectional area A, two faces 1 and 2 will contribute to flux where electric field lines result as parallel to other faces which do not contribute to total flux.
It is analyzed that the unit vector which is normal to the first surface will be in the negative x-direction while the unit vector which is normal to the second surface will be in positive +x direction, so flux {tex}E . \Delta S{/tex}  by the surfaces will results equal and will add up. subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Use Gauss theorem to find the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinitely large plane thin sheet with surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}.
  2. An infinitely large thin plane sheet has a uniform surface charge density {tex}+\sigma{/tex}. Obtain the expression for the amount of work done in bringing a point charge q from infinity to a point, distance r, in front of the charged plane sheet.
question_text: Use Gauss theorem to find the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinitely large plane thin sheet with surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} . An infinitely large thin plane sheet has a uniform surface charge density {tex}+\sigma{/tex} . Obtain the expression for the amount of work done in bringing a point charge q from infinity to a point, distance r, in front of the charged plane sheet.
explanation: Consider an infinite thin plane sheet of positive charge with uniform charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} on both sides of the sheet. Let a point be at a distance of 'a' from the sheet at which the electric field is required. The gaussian cylinder is of area of cross section A. Electric flux crossing the gaussian surface, {tex}\phi=E \times{/tex} Area of the cross section of the gaussian cylinder. Here, electric lines of force area parallel to the curved surface area of the cylinder, the flux due to the electric field of the plane sheet of charge passes only through two circular section of the cylinder. {tex}\phi=E \times 2 A{/tex} ..... (i) According to Gauss's Theorem, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} Here, charge enclosed by the gaussian surface, {tex}q=\sigma A{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi=\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} ..... (ii) From equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}E \times 2 A=\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} The direction of electric field for positive charge is in the outward direction and perpendicular to the plane of infinite sheet. As we know, electric field to a uniformly charged infinitely large plane thin sheet with surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} is {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex} . Therefore, the amount of work done (against the electric field E) in bringing a point charge q from infinity to a point, distance r, in front of the charged plane sheet is {tex}W=\int_{0}^{r} F \cdot d r{/tex} where, F is the force experience by the charge q due to electric field E. or W = F.r Now, F = Eq {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} W = Eqr Here, {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}W=\frac{\sigma q r}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex}
solution: Consider an infinite thin plane sheet of positive charge with uniform charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} on both sides of the sheet. Let a point be at a distance of 'a' from the sheet at which the electric field is required. The gaussian cylinder is of area of cross section A. Electric flux crossing the gaussian surface, {tex}\phi=E \times{/tex} Area of the cross section of the gaussian cylinder. Here, electric lines of force area parallel to the curved surface area of the cylinder, the flux due to the electric field of the plane sheet of charge passes only through two circular section of the cylinder. {tex}\phi=E \times 2 A{/tex} ..... (i) According to Gauss's Theorem, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} Here, charge enclosed by the gaussian surface, {tex}q=\sigma A{/tex} {tex}\therefore \phi=\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} ..... (ii) From equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}E \times 2 A=\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} The direction of electric field for positive charge is in the outward direction and perpendicular to the plane of infinite sheet. As we know, electric field to a uniformly charged infinitely large plane thin sheet with surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} is {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex} . Therefore, the amount of work done (against the electric field E) in bringing a point charge q from infinity to a point, distance r, in front of the charged plane sheet is {tex}W=\int_{0}^{r} F \cdot d r{/tex} where, F is the force experience by the charge q due to electric field E. or W = F.r Now, F = Eq {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} W = Eqr Here, {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}W=\frac{\sigma q r}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Consider an infinite thin plane sheet of positive charge with uniform charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} on both sides of the sheet. Let a point be at a distance of 'a' from the sheet at which the electric field is required.
    The gaussian cylinder is of area of cross section A.
    Electric flux crossing the gaussian surface,
    {tex}\phi=E \times{/tex} Area of the cross section of the gaussian cylinder.
    Here, electric lines of force area parallel to the curved surface area of the cylinder, the flux due to the electric field of the plane sheet of charge passes only through two circular section of the cylinder.
    {tex}\phi=E \times 2 A{/tex} ..... (i)
    According to Gauss's Theorem,
    {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Here, charge enclosed by the gaussian surface,
    {tex}q=\sigma A{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \phi=\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} ..... (ii)
    From equations (i) and (ii), we get
    {tex}E \times 2 A=\frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    The direction of electric field for positive charge is in the outward direction and perpendicular to the plane of infinite sheet.
  2. As we know, electric field to a uniformly charged infinitely large plane thin sheet with surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} is {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex}. Therefore, the amount of work done (against the electric field E) in bringing a point charge q from infinity to a point, distance r, in front of the charged plane sheet is
    {tex}W=\int_{0}^{r} F \cdot d r{/tex}
    where, F is the force experience by the charge q due to electric field E.
    or W = F.r
    Now,
    F = Eq
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} W = Eqr
    Here, {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex}
    Therefore, {tex}W=\frac{\sigma q r}{2 \varepsilon_{o}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A point charge +Q is placed at the centre O of an uncharged hollow spherical conductor of inner radius a and outer radius b. Find the following :

  1. The magnitude and sign of the charge induced on the inner and outer surface of the conducting shell.
  2. The magnitude of electric field vector at a distance (i) r = {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} and (ii) r = 2b, from the centre of the shell.
question_text: A point charge +Q is placed at the centre O of an uncharged hollow spherical conductor of inner radius a and outer radius b. Find the following : The magnitude and sign of the charge induced on the inner and outer surface of the conducting shell. The magnitude of electric field vector at a distance (i) r = {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} and (ii) r = 2b, from the centre of the shell.
explanation: The charge placed at the centre of a shell is +Q. Hence, a charge of magnitude -Q will be induced to the inner surface of the shell. Therefore, the total charge on the inner surface of the shell is -Q. To make the potential same, a charge of +Q is induced on the outer surface of the shell, repelled by the +Q at the center. The electric flux is electric field times the area of the spherical surface {tex}\phi=E A=4 E \pi r^{2}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} So, the electric field outside the sphere (r > R) is seen to be identical to that of a point charge Q at the center of the sphere. {tex}E=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex} The magnitude of the electric field vector at a distance {tex}r=\frac{a}{2}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{4 Q}{a^{2}}{/tex} The magnitude of the electric field vector at a distance r = 2b {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{ Q}{4 b^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{4b^{2}}{/tex}
solution: The charge placed at the centre of a shell is +Q. Hence, a charge of magnitude -Q will be induced to the inner surface of the shell. Therefore, the total charge on the inner surface of the shell is -Q. To make the potential same, a charge of +Q is induced on the outer surface of the shell, repelled by the +Q at the center. The electric flux is electric field times the area of the spherical surface {tex}\phi=E A=4 E \pi r^{2}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} So, the electric field outside the sphere (r > R) is seen to be identical to that of a point charge Q at the center of the sphere. {tex}E=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex} The magnitude of the electric field vector at a distance {tex}r=\frac{a}{2}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{4 Q}{a^{2}}{/tex} The magnitude of the electric field vector at a distance r = 2b {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{ Q}{4 b^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{4b^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1.  The charge placed at the centre of a shell is +Q. Hence, a charge of magnitude -Q will be induced to the inner surface of the shell. Therefore, the total charge on the inner surface of the shell is -Q.
     To make the potential same, a charge of +Q is induced on the outer surface of the shell, repelled by the +Q at the center.
  2. The electric flux is electric field times the area of the spherical surface 
    {tex}\phi=E A=4 E \pi r^{2}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    So, the electric field outside the sphere (r > R) is seen to be identical to that of a point charge Q at the center of the sphere.
    {tex}E=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex}
    The magnitude of the electric field vector at a distance
    {tex}r=\frac{a}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{4 Q}{a^{2}}{/tex}
    The magnitude of the electric field vector at a distance
    r = 2b
    {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{ Q}{4 b^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{4b^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: Suppose the charge of a proton and an electron differ slightly. One of them is -e, the other is (e + {tex}\Delta{/tex}e). If the net of electrostatic force and the gravitational force between two hydrogen atoms placed at a distance d (much greater than atomic size) apart is zero, then {tex}\Delta{/tex}e is of the order of [Given mass of hydrogen = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg]
question_text: Suppose the charge of a proton and an electron differ slightly. One of them is -e, the other is (e + {tex}\Delta{/tex} e). If the net of electrostatic force and the gravitational force between two hydrogen atoms placed at a distance d (much greater than atomic size) apart is zero, then {tex}\Delta{/tex} e is of the order of [Given mass of hydrogen = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg]

options.explanation: Net charge on one H-atom = -e + e + {tex}\Delta e=\Delta e{/tex} For net force between two H-atoms to be zero, F e (repulsive) = F g (attractive) {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\Delta e^{2}}{d^{2}}=\frac{G m^{2}}{d^{2}}{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\left(\Delta e^{2}\right){/tex} = 6.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 {tex}\times{/tex} (1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 ) 2 {tex}\Delta e^{2}=\frac{6.67 \times 1.67 \times 1.67}{9} \times{/tex} 10 -74 {tex}\Delta e \approx{/tex} 10 -37

explanation: Net charge on one H-atom = -e + e + {tex}\Delta e=\Delta e{/tex} For net force between two H-atoms to be zero, F e (repulsive) = F g (attractive) {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\Delta e^{2}}{d^{2}}=\frac{G m^{2}}{d^{2}}{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\left(\Delta e^{2}\right){/tex} = 6.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 {tex}\times{/tex} (1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 ) 2 {tex}\Delta e^{2}=\frac{6.67 \times 1.67 \times 1.67}{9} \times{/tex} 10 -74 {tex}\Delta e \approx{/tex} 10 -37

question_html:
A charge Q is distributed uniformly over a metallic sphere of radius R. Obtain the expression for the electric field (E) and electric potential (V) at a point 0 < x <R.
Show or plot the variation of E and V with x for 0 < x < 2R.
question_text: A charge Q is distributed uniformly over a metallic sphere of radius R. Obtain the expression for the electric field (E) and electric potential (V) at a point 0 < x <R. Show or plot the variation of E and V with x for 0 < x < 2R.
explanation: By Gauss law {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d} s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} q = 0 in interval 0 < x < R E = 0 or {tex}E=-\frac{d V}{d r}{/tex} Hence, V = constant = {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R}{/tex} The variation of E and V with x for 0 < x < 2R.
solution: By Gauss law {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d} s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} q = 0 in interval 0 < x < R E = 0 or {tex}E=-\frac{d V}{d r}{/tex} Hence, V = constant = {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R}{/tex} The variation of E and V with x for 0 < x < 2R.
solution_html:
By Gauss law
{tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \vec{d} s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
q = 0 in interval 0 < x < R
E = 0
or {tex}E=-\frac{d V}{d r}{/tex}
Hence, V = constant = {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R}{/tex}
The variation of E and V with x for 0 < x < 2R.

question_html:
1. State Gauss Law. Using this law, obtain the expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long straight conductor of linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
  2. A wire of length L has linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} = kx, where x is measured from the end A of the wire. This wire is enclosed by Gaussian hollow surface. Find the expression for the electric flux through this Surface.
question_text: State Gauss Law. Using this law, obtain the expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long straight conductor of linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} . A wire of length L has linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} = kx, where x is measured from the end A of the wire. This wire is enclosed by Gaussian hollow surface. Find the expression for the electric flux through this Surface.
explanation: Gauss's law: The law relates the flux through any closed surface and the net charge enclosed with in the surface. The law states that the total flux of the electric field E over any closed surface is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed by the surface. {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Since The electric flux ( {tex}\phi{/tex} ) through curved surface = {tex}\oint{/tex} Edscos {tex}\theta{/tex} . {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\oint{/tex} Eds [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0; cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1] = E(2r {tex}\pi{/tex} l) [The surface area of the curved part is] since E and ds are right angles 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl to each other, the electric flux through the plane caps = 0. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total flux through the Gaussian surface, {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl). The net charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is, q = {tex}\lambda{/tex} l {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss's law,= E {tex}\frac{(2\pi rl)\lambda l}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} or E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0r}{/tex} . The direction of electric field E is radially outward, if line charge is positive and inward, if the line charge is negative. The length of wire is l and linear charge density is {tex}\lambda{/tex} = kx, so linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dQ}{dx}{/tex} Now, dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex} dx = kxdx {tex}Q=\int_{0}^{l} d q=\int_{0}^{l} k x d x=\frac{1}{2} k l^{2}{/tex} As per Gauss law, {tex}\phi=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} where, {tex}\phi{/tex} = electric flux, Q = net charge enclosed in Gaussian surface {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = permittivity of medium Hence, {tex}\phi=\frac{k l^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution: Gauss's law: The law relates the flux through any closed surface and the net charge enclosed with in the surface. The law states that the total flux of the electric field E over any closed surface is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed by the surface. {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Since The electric flux ( {tex}\phi{/tex} ) through curved surface = {tex}\oint{/tex} Edscos {tex}\theta{/tex} . {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\oint{/tex} Eds [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0; cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1] = E(2r {tex}\pi{/tex} l) [The surface area of the curved part is] since E and ds are right angles 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl to each other, the electric flux through the plane caps = 0. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total flux through the Gaussian surface, {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(2 {tex}\pi{/tex} rl). The net charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is, q = {tex}\lambda{/tex} l {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss's law,= E {tex}\frac{(2\pi rl)\lambda l}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} or E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0r}{/tex} . The direction of electric field E is radially outward, if line charge is positive and inward, if the line charge is negative. The length of wire is l and linear charge density is {tex}\lambda{/tex} = kx, so linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dQ}{dx}{/tex} Now, dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex} dx = kxdx {tex}Q=\int_{0}^{l} d q=\int_{0}^{l} k x d x=\frac{1}{2} k l^{2}{/tex} As per Gauss law, {tex}\phi=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} where, {tex}\phi{/tex} = electric flux, Q = net charge enclosed in Gaussian surface {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = permittivity of medium Hence, {tex}\phi=\frac{k l^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Gauss's law:
    The law relates the flux through any closed surface and the net charge enclosed with in the surface. The law states that the total flux of the electric field E over any closed surface is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed by the surface.
    {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}

    Since The electric flux ({tex}\phi{/tex}) through curved surface = {tex}\oint{/tex}Edscos{tex}\theta{/tex}. {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\oint{/tex}Eds [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0; cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = 1] = E(2r{tex}\pi{/tex}l) [The surface area of the curved part is] since E and ds are right angles 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rl to each other, the electric flux through the plane caps = 0.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total flux through the Gaussian surface, {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(2{tex}\pi{/tex}rl). The net charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is, q = {tex}\lambda{/tex}l {tex}\therefore{/tex} By Gauss's law,= E{tex}\frac{(2\pi rl)\lambda l}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} or E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon_0r}{/tex}. The direction of electric field E is radially outward, if line charge is positive and inward, if the line charge is negative.
  2. The length of wire is l and linear charge density is {tex}\lambda{/tex} = kx, so linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dQ}{dx}{/tex}
    Now, dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex}dx = kxdx
    {tex}Q=\int_{0}^{l} d q=\int_{0}^{l} k x d x=\frac{1}{2} k l^{2}{/tex}
    As per Gauss law,
    {tex}\phi=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    where, {tex}\phi{/tex} = electric flux, Q = net charge enclosed in Gaussian surface
    {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = permittivity of medium
    Hence, {tex}\phi=\frac{k l^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

question_html: An early model for an atom considered it to have a positively charged point nucleus of charge Ze, surrounded by a uniform density of negative charge upto a radius R. The atom as a whole is neutral. The electric field at a distance r from the nucleus is (r < R)
question_text: An early model for an atom considered it to have a positively charged point nucleus of charge Ze, surrounded by a uniform density of negative charge upto a radius R. The atom as a whole is neutral. The electric field at a distance r from the nucleus is (r < R)

options.explanation: Charge on nucleus is = +Ze total negative charge = -Ze ( {tex}\because{/tex} atoms is electrical neutral) Negative charge density, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { charge }}{\text { volume }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{Ze}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^3}{/tex} i.e., {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{3 \mathrm{Ze}}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}^3}{/tex} ...(i) Consider a Gaussian surface with radius r By Gauss's theorem {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(r) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}4 \pi r^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(ii) Charge enclosed by Gaussian surface q' = Ze + {tex}\frac{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3}{3} \rho{/tex} = Ze - Ze {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}^3}{\mathrm{R}^3}{/tex} [Using (i)] From (ii) E(r) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}^{\prime}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{r}^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Ze}-\mathrm{Ze} \frac{\mathrm{r}^3}{\mathrm{R}^3}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{r}^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Ze}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^2}-\frac{\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{R}^3}\right){/tex} .

explanation: Charge on nucleus is = +Ze total negative charge = -Ze ( {tex}\because{/tex} atoms is electrical neutral) Negative charge density, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { charge }}{\text { volume }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{Ze}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^3}{/tex} i.e., {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{3 \mathrm{Ze}}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}^3}{/tex} ...(i) Consider a Gaussian surface with radius r By Gauss's theorem {tex}\phi{/tex} = E(r) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}4 \pi r^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(ii) Charge enclosed by Gaussian surface q' = Ze + {tex}\frac{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3}{3} \rho{/tex} = Ze - Ze {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}^3}{\mathrm{R}^3}{/tex} [Using (i)] From (ii) E(r) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}^{\prime}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{r}^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Ze}-\mathrm{Ze} \frac{\mathrm{r}^3}{\mathrm{R}^3}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{r}^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Ze}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^2}-\frac{\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{R}^3}\right){/tex} .

question_html: How does the electric flux due to a point charge enclosed by a spherical Gaussian surface get affected when its radius is increased?
question_text: How does the electric flux due to a point charge enclosed by a spherical Gaussian surface get affected when its radius is increased?
explanation: We know that flux through a Gaussian surface is given by, {tex}\phi=\frac{q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} which is independent of radius. So, it is not affected on changing radius.
solution: We know that flux through a Gaussian surface is given by, {tex}\phi=\frac{q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} which is independent of radius. So, it is not affected on changing radius.
solution_html:
We know that flux through a Gaussian surface is given by,
{tex}\phi=\frac{q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex}
which is independent of radius.
So, it is not affected on changing radius.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Define electric flux. Write its SI unit.
A charge q is enclosed by a spherical surface of radius R. If the radius is reduced to half, how would the electric flux through the surface change?
question_text: Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. A charge q is enclosed by a spherical surface of radius R. If the radius is reduced to half, how would the electric flux through the surface change?
explanation: Electric flux: The total electric flux linked with a surface is equal to the total number of electric lines of force passing through the surface when the surface is held normal to the direction of the electric field. The SI unit of electric flux is Nm 2 C -1 . According to Gauss theorem, if charge q is enclosed by closed surface, therefore total electric flux linked with the closed surface is given by, {tex}\phi = \oint _ { S } \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} Flux only depend on charge and permittivity, so flux will not change by reducing the radius.
solution: Electric flux: The total electric flux linked with a surface is equal to the total number of electric lines of force passing through the surface when the surface is held normal to the direction of the electric field. The SI unit of electric flux is Nm 2 C -1 . According to Gauss theorem, if charge q is enclosed by closed surface, therefore total electric flux linked with the closed surface is given by, {tex}\phi = \oint _ { S } \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} Flux only depend on charge and permittivity, so flux will not change by reducing the radius.
solution_html:
Electric flux: The total electric flux linked with a surface is equal to the total number of electric lines of force passing through the surface when the surface is held normal to the direction of the electric field. The SI unit of electric flux is Nm2C-1.
According to Gauss theorem, if charge q is enclosed by closed surface, therefore total electric flux linked with the closed surface is given by,
{tex}\phi = \oint _ { S } \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
Flux only depend on charge and permittivity, so flux will not change by reducing the radius.

question_html:
1. Define electric flux. Write its SI unit.
  2. Using Gauss' law, prove that the electric field at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is independent of distance from it.
  3. How is the field directed if
    1. the sheet is positively charged?
    2. negatively charged?
question_text: Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. Using Gauss' law, prove that the electric field at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is independent of distance from it. How is the field directed if the sheet is positively charged? negatively charged?
explanation: Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the surface. Alternatively, surface integral of the electric field is defined as the electric flux through a closed surface {tex}\phi=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} SI unit: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} or volt.metre Outward flux through the Gaussian surface, {tex}2 \mathrm{EA}=\sigma \mathrm{A} / \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}=\sigma / 2 \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} In vector form, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{n}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the plane, away from it. Hence, electric field is independent of the distance from the sheet. For positively charged sheet, the electric field is directed away from the sheet. For negatively charged sheet, the electric field is directed towards the plane of sheet.
solution: Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the surface. Alternatively, surface integral of the electric field is defined as the electric flux through a closed surface {tex}\phi=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} SI unit: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} or volt.metre Outward flux through the Gaussian surface, {tex}2 \mathrm{EA}=\sigma \mathrm{A} / \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}=\sigma / 2 \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} In vector form, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{n}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the plane, away from it. Hence, electric field is independent of the distance from the sheet. For positively charged sheet, the electric field is directed away from the sheet. For negatively charged sheet, the electric field is directed towards the plane of sheet.
solution_html:
1. Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the surface. Alternatively, surface integral of the electric field is defined as the electric flux through a closed surface
    {tex}\phi=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex}
    SI unit: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} or volt.metre


  2. Outward flux through the Gaussian surface,
    {tex}2 \mathrm{EA}=\sigma \mathrm{A} / \varepsilon_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}=\sigma / 2 \varepsilon_{0}{/tex}
    In vector form, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{n}{/tex}
    where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the plane, away from it.
    Hence, electric field is independent of the distance from the sheet.
    1. For positively charged sheet, the electric field is directed away from the sheet.
    2. For negatively charged sheet, the electric field is directed towards the plane of sheet.

question_html: State Gauss's theorem and express it mathematically. Using it, derive an expression for the electric field intensity at a point near a thin infinite plane sheet of charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}Cm-2.
question_text: State Gauss's theorem and express it mathematically. Using it, derive an expression for the electric field intensity at a point near a thin infinite plane sheet of charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} Cm -2 .
explanation: Gauss's law states that the electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed by the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. i.e {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q_{e n}}{ \epsilon_0}{/tex} Let P be any point at a distance r from the sheet. Let the small area element E = dsn E and 'n' are perpendicular, on the surface of imagined cylinder, so electric flux is zero. E and 'nn' are parallel on the two cylindrical edges P and Q, which contributes electric flux. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric flux over the edges P and Q of the cylinder is 2 {tex}\oint{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} 2 {tex}\oint{/tex} Eds = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} ] 2E {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{q}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{S}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} [where S - area of circle] E = {tex}\frac{\pi r^2 \sigma}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} vecotrically {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0} \hat{n}{/tex}
solution: Gauss's law states that the electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed by the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. i.e {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q_{e n}}{ \epsilon_0}{/tex} Let P be any point at a distance r from the sheet. Let the small area element E = dsn E and 'n' are perpendicular, on the surface of imagined cylinder, so electric flux is zero. E and 'nn' are parallel on the two cylindrical edges P and Q, which contributes electric flux. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric flux over the edges P and Q of the cylinder is 2 {tex}\oint{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} 2 {tex}\oint{/tex} Eds = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} ] 2E {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{q}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{S}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} [where S - area of circle] E = {tex}\frac{\pi r^2 \sigma}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} vecotrically {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0} \hat{n}{/tex}
solution_html:
Gauss's law states that the electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed by the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. i.e {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q_{e n}}{ \epsilon_0}{/tex}
Let P be any point at a distance r from the sheet.
Let the small area element E = dsn
E and 'n' are perpendicular, on the surface of imagined cylinder, so electric flux is zero. E and 'nn' are parallel on the two cylindrical edges P and Q, which contributes electric flux.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric flux over the edges P and Q of the cylinder is

2 {tex}\oint{/tex}{tex}\vec{E}{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex}
2 {tex}\oint{/tex}Eds = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex}]
2E{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}{/tex}
{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{q}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{S}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} [where S - area of circle]
E = {tex}\frac{\pi r^2 \sigma}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}
E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} vecotrically {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0} \hat{n}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Using Gauss' law, deduce the expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged spherical conducting shell of radius R at a point

  1. outside the shell
  2. inside the shell

Plot a graph showing a variation of the electric field as a function of r > R and r < R (r being the distance from the centre of the shell).
question_text: Using Gauss' law, deduce the expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged spherical conducting shell of radius R at a point outside the shell inside the shell Plot a graph showing a variation of the electric field as a function of r > R and r < R (r being the distance from the centre of the shell).
explanation: Electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell: When point P lies outside the spherical shell: Suppose that we have to calculate electric field at the point P at a distance r (r > R) from its centre. Let {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} be the electric field at point P, then the electric flux through area element {tex}\vec{dS}{/tex} is given by, {tex}\Delta \phi=\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta S}{/tex} Since {tex}\vec{\Delta S}{/tex} is also along normal to the surface, {tex}\Delta{\phi}=E d S{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total electric flux through the Gaussian surface is given by. {tex}\phi=\oint E d S=E \oint d S{/tex} Now, {tex}\oint d S=4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \phi=E \times 4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} .....(i) Since the charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface is q, according to the Gauss's law, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} ....(ii) From equation (i) and (ii), we obtain {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^{2}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r^{2}}{/tex} (for r > R) When point P lies inside the spherical shell: In such a case, the Gaussian surface encloses no charge. According to Gauss's law, {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^{2}=0{/tex} i.e., E = 0 (r > R) A graph showing the variation of electric field as a function of r is shown in figure.
solution: Electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell: When point P lies outside the spherical shell: Suppose that we have to calculate electric field at the point P at a distance r (r > R) from its centre. Let {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} be the electric field at point P, then the electric flux through area element {tex}\vec{dS}{/tex} is given by, {tex}\Delta \phi=\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta S}{/tex} Since {tex}\vec{\Delta S}{/tex} is also along normal to the surface, {tex}\Delta{\phi}=E d S{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total electric flux through the Gaussian surface is given by. {tex}\phi=\oint E d S=E \oint d S{/tex} Now, {tex}\oint d S=4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \phi=E \times 4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} .....(i) Since the charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface is q, according to the Gauss's law, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} ....(ii) From equation (i) and (ii), we obtain {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^{2}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r^{2}}{/tex} (for r > R) When point P lies inside the spherical shell: In such a case, the Gaussian surface encloses no charge. According to Gauss's law, {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^{2}=0{/tex} i.e., E = 0 (r > R) A graph showing the variation of electric field as a function of r is shown in figure.
solution_html:
Electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell:

  1. When point P lies outside the spherical shell: Suppose that we have to calculate electric field at the point P at a distance r (r > R) from its centre.
    Let {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} be the electric field at point P, then the electric flux through area element {tex}\vec{dS}{/tex} is given by, 
    {tex}\Delta \phi=\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta S}{/tex}
    Since {tex}\vec{\Delta S}{/tex} is also along normal to the surface,
    {tex}\Delta{\phi}=E d S{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total electric flux through the Gaussian surface is given by.
    {tex}\phi=\oint E d S=E \oint d S{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\oint d S=4 \pi r^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \phi=E \times 4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} .....(i)
    Since the charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface is q, according to the Gauss's law,
    {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} ....(ii)
    From equation (i) and (ii), we obtain
    {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^{2}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r^{2}}{/tex} (for r > R)
  2. When point P lies inside the spherical shell: In such a case, the Gaussian surface encloses no charge. According to Gauss's law,
    {tex}E \times 4 \pi r^{2}=0{/tex}
    i.e., E = 0 (r > R)
    A graph showing the variation of electric field as a function of r is shown in figure.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two thin concentric and coplanar spherical shells, of radii a and b (b > a) carry charges, q and Q, respectively. Find the magnitude of the electric field, at a point at distance x, from their common centre for:

  1. 0 < x < a
  2. {tex}a \leq x<b{/tex}
  3. {tex}b \leq x<\infty{/tex}
question_text: Two thin concentric and coplanar spherical shells, of radii a and b (b > a) carry charges, q and Q, respectively. Find the magnitude of the electric field, at a point at distance x, from their common centre for: 0 < x < a {tex}a \leq x<b{/tex} {tex}b \leq x<\infty{/tex}
explanation: Field due to a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero at all points inside the shell. So in the given question, we have: For 0 < x < a The point lies inside both the spherical shells. Hence, E(x) = 0 For {tex}a \leq x<b{/tex} Point is outside the spherical shell of radius 'a' but inside the spherical shell of radius 'b' {tex}\therefore E(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}b \leq x<\infty{/tex} Point is outside of both the spherical shells. Total effective charge at the centre equals (Q + q) {tex}\therefore E(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot\left(\frac{q+\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex}
solution: Field due to a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero at all points inside the shell. So in the given question, we have: For 0 < x < a The point lies inside both the spherical shells. Hence, E(x) = 0 For {tex}a \leq x<b{/tex} Point is outside the spherical shell of radius 'a' but inside the spherical shell of radius 'b' {tex}\therefore E(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}b \leq x<\infty{/tex} Point is outside of both the spherical shells. Total effective charge at the centre equals (Q + q) {tex}\therefore E(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot\left(\frac{q+\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Field due to a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero at all points inside the shell. So in the given question, we have:

  1. For 0 < x < a
    The point lies inside both the spherical shells.
    Hence, E(x) = 0
  2. For {tex}a \leq x<b{/tex}
    Point is outside the spherical shell of radius 'a' but inside the spherical shell of radius 'b'
    {tex}\therefore E(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{x^{2}}{/tex}
  3. {tex}b \leq x<\infty{/tex}
    Point is outside of both the spherical shells. Total effective charge at the centre equals (Q + q)
    {tex}\therefore E(x)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot\left(\frac{q+\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A charge is distributed uniformly over a ring of radius a. Obtain an expression for the electric field intensity E at a point on the axis of the ring. Hence, show that for points at large distances from the ring, it behaves like a point charge.
question_text: A charge is distributed uniformly over a ring of radius a . Obtain an expression for the electric field intensity E at a point on the axis of the ring. Hence, show that for points at large distances from the ring, it behaves like a point charge.
explanation: From the given figure it is clear that net Electric Field at point P {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} d E \cos \theta{/tex} dE = Electric field due to a small element having charge dq {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{d q}{r^{2}}{/tex} Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} = Linear charge density {tex}=\frac{d q}{d l}{/tex} dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex} dl Hence E {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\lambda d l}{r^{2}} \times \frac{x}{r}{/tex} , where {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{x}{r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}(2 \pi a){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} , where total charge {tex}Q=\lambda \times 2 \pi a{/tex} At large distance i.e., x >> a {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}{/tex} This is the Electric field due to a point charge at distance x.
solution: From the given figure it is clear that net Electric Field at point P {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} d E \cos \theta{/tex} dE = Electric field due to a small element having charge dq {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{d q}{r^{2}}{/tex} Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} = Linear charge density {tex}=\frac{d q}{d l}{/tex} dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex} dl Hence E {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\lambda d l}{r^{2}} \times \frac{x}{r}{/tex} , where {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{x}{r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}(2 \pi a){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} , where total charge {tex}Q=\lambda \times 2 \pi a{/tex} At large distance i.e., x >> a {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}{/tex} This is the Electric field due to a point charge at distance x.
solution_html:
From the given figure it is clear that net Electric Field at point P {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} d E \cos \theta{/tex}
dE = Electric field due to a small element having charge dq
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{d q}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} = Linear charge density
{tex}=\frac{d q}{d l}{/tex}
dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex}dl
Hence E {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\lambda d l}{r^{2}} \times \frac{x}{r}{/tex}, where {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{x}{r}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\lambda x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}(2 \pi a){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex},
where total charge {tex}Q=\lambda \times 2 \pi a{/tex}
At large distance i.e., x >> a
{tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}{/tex}
This is the Electric field due to a point charge at distance x.

subject: Physics
question_html: State Gauss's law in electrostatics.
question_text: State Gauss's law in electrostatics.
explanation: Gauss's law in electrostatics: "The surface integral of electrostatic field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} produced by any source over any closed surface S enclosing a volume V in a vacuum, i.e., total electric flux over the closed surface S in a vacuum, is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} times the total charge (Q) contained inside S, i.e., the surface chosen for the surface integral is Gaussian surface. {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{E}}=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution: Gauss's law in electrostatics: "The surface integral of electrostatic field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} produced by any source over any closed surface S enclosing a volume V in a vacuum, i.e., total electric flux over the closed surface S in a vacuum, is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} times the total charge (Q) contained inside S, i.e., the surface chosen for the surface integral is Gaussian surface. {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{E}}=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Gauss's law in electrostatics: "The surface integral of electrostatic field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} produced by any source over any closed surface S enclosing a volume V in a vacuum, i.e., total electric flux over the closed surface S in a vacuum, is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}times the total charge (Q) contained inside S, i.e., the surface chosen for the surface integral is Gaussian surface.
{tex}\phi_{\mathrm{E}}=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

question_html:
Given a uniformly charged plane/sheet of surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1017 C/cm2.

  1. Find the electric field intensity at a point A, 5 mm away from the sheet on the left side.
  2. Given a straight line with three points X, Y and Z placed 50 cm away from the charged sheet on the right side. At which of these points, the field due to the sheet remains the same as that of point A and why?
question_text: Given a uniformly charged plane/sheet of surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 17 C/cm 2 . Find the electric field intensity at a point A, 5 mm away from the sheet on the left side. Given a straight line with three points X, Y and Z placed 50 cm away from the charged sheet on the right side. At which of these points, the field due to the sheet remains the same as that of point A and why?
explanation: The electric field intensity due to a sheet is given by {tex}E=\sigma/2\in{/tex} 0 . At point A, E= 2 x 10 17 /2 x 8.85 x 10 -12 = 1.12 x 10 28 N/C At point Y, because at 50 cm, the charge sheet acts as a finite sheet and thus the magnitude remains the same towards the middle region of the plan sheet.
solution: The electric field intensity due to a sheet is given by {tex}E=\sigma/2\in{/tex} 0 . At point A, E= 2 x 10 17 /2 x 8.85 x 10 -12 = 1.12 x 10 28 N/C At point Y, because at 50 cm, the charge sheet acts as a finite sheet and thus the magnitude remains the same towards the middle region of the plan sheet.
solution_html:
1. The electric field intensity due to a sheet is given by {tex}E=\sigma/2\in{/tex}0.  
    At point A, E= 2 x 1017/2  x 8.85 x 10-12 = 1.12 x  1028  N/C
  2. At point Y, because at 50 cm, the charge sheet acts as a finite sheet and thus the magnitude remains the same towards the middle region of the plan sheet.

question_html:
A charge + Q, is uniformly distributed within a sphere of radius R. Find the electric field, due to this charge distribution, at a distant point r from the centre of the sphere where :

  1. 0 < r < R
  2. r > R
question_text: A charge + Q, is uniformly distributed within a sphere of radius R. Find the electric field, due to this charge distribution, at a distant point r from the centre of the sphere where : 0 < r < R r > R
explanation: A charge +q is uniformly distributed, within a sphere of radius R. We have {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q}{r^{2}}{/tex} for a point charge Now (volume) charge density {tex}\rho=\frac{Q}{\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3} R^{3}\right)}{/tex} Charge contained within a sphere of radius r (0 < r < R ) {tex}Q'=\rho \cdot \frac{4 \pi}{3}, r^{3}=Q\left(\frac{r^{3}}{R^{3}}\right){/tex} Electric Field, {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{\prime}}{r^{2}}={/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R^{3}} \cdot r\right){/tex} For r > R Electric field = (Electric field due to a point charge Q at the centre) {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^{2}}{/tex}
solution: A charge +q is uniformly distributed, within a sphere of radius R. We have {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q}{r^{2}}{/tex} for a point charge Now (volume) charge density {tex}\rho=\frac{Q}{\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3} R^{3}\right)}{/tex} Charge contained within a sphere of radius r (0 < r < R ) {tex}Q'=\rho \cdot \frac{4 \pi}{3}, r^{3}=Q\left(\frac{r^{3}}{R^{3}}\right){/tex} Electric Field, {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{\prime}}{r^{2}}={/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R^{3}} \cdot r\right){/tex} For r > R Electric field = (Electric field due to a point charge Q at the centre) {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
A charge +q  is uniformly distributed, within a sphere of radius R. We have
{tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q}{r^{2}}{/tex} for a point charge
Now (volume) charge density 
{tex}\rho=\frac{Q}{\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3} R^{3}\right)}{/tex}

  1. Charge contained within a sphere of radius r (0 < r < R )
    {tex}Q'=\rho \cdot \frac{4 \pi}{3}, r^{3}=Q\left(\frac{r^{3}}{R^{3}}\right){/tex}
    Electric Field,
    {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{\prime}}{r^{2}}={/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R^{3}} \cdot r\right){/tex} 
  2. For r > R
    Electric field = (Electric field due to a point charge Q at the centre)
    {tex}E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^{2}}{/tex}

question_html:
A thin metallic spherical shell of radius R carries a charge Q on its surface. A point charge {tex}\frac Q2{/tex} is placed at the centre C and p another charge +2Q is placed outside the shell at A at a distance x from the centre as shown in the figure.

  1. Find the electric flux through the shell.
  2. State the law used.
  3. Find the force on the charges at the centre C of the shell and at the point A.
question_text: A thin metallic spherical shell of radius R carries a charge Q on its surface. A point charge {tex}\frac Q2{/tex} is placed at the centre C and p another charge +2Q is placed outside the shell at A at a distance x from the centre as shown in the figure. Find the electric flux through the shell. State the law used. Find the force on the charges at the centre C of the shell and at the point A.
explanation: Electric flux through a Gaussian surface, {tex}\phi=\frac{\text { Total enclosed charge }}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Net charge enclosed inside the shell, q = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric flux through the shell {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}=0{/tex} Gauss's Law: Electric flux through a Gaussian surface is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed within it. Mathematically, {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times q{/tex} We know that electric field or net charge inside the spherical conducting shell is zero. Hence, the force on charge {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} is zero. Force on charge at A, F A {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Q\left(Q+\frac{Q}{2}\right)}{x^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{3 Q^2}{x^2}{/tex}
solution: Electric flux through a Gaussian surface, {tex}\phi=\frac{\text { Total enclosed charge }}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Net charge enclosed inside the shell, q = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric flux through the shell {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}=0{/tex} Gauss's Law: Electric flux through a Gaussian surface is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed within it. Mathematically, {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times q{/tex} We know that electric field or net charge inside the spherical conducting shell is zero. Hence, the force on charge {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} is zero. Force on charge at A, F A {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Q\left(Q+\frac{Q}{2}\right)}{x^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{3 Q^2}{x^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Electric flux through a Gaussian surface, {tex}\phi=\frac{\text { Total enclosed charge }}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    Net charge enclosed inside the shell, q = 0
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric flux through the shell {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}=0{/tex}
  2. Gauss's Law: Electric flux through a Gaussian surface is {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed within it.
    Mathematically, {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times q{/tex}
  3. We know that electric field or net charge inside the spherical conducting shell is zero. Hence, the force on charge {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} is zero.
    Force on charge at A, FA {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Q\left(Q+\frac{Q}{2}\right)}{x^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{3 Q^2}{x^2}{/tex}

question_html: Two identical charged spheres suspended from a common point by two massless strings of lengths l, are initially at a distance d(d << l) apart because of their mutual repulsion. The charges begin to leak from both the spheres at a constant rate. As a result, the spheres approach each other with a velocity v. Then v varies as a function of the distance x between the spheres, as
question_text: Two identical charged spheres suspended from a common point by two massless strings of lengths l, are initially at a distance d(d << l) apart because of their mutual repulsion. The charges begin to leak from both the spheres at a constant rate. As a result, the spheres approach each other with a velocity v. Then v varies as a function of the distance x between the spheres, as

options.explanation: From {tex}\triangle{/tex} ACO of forces, {tex}\frac{F}{A C}=\frac{m g}{O C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{k q^{2}}{x^{2}(\frac x 2)}=\frac{m g}{\sqrt{l^{2}-\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 k q^{2}}{x^{3}}=\frac{m g}{l}{/tex} [ {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} << l] {tex}\therefore{/tex} q 2 = {tex}\frac{m g}{2 k l}\cdot{/tex} x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q 2 {tex}\propto{/tex} x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q {tex}\propto{/tex} x 3/2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d q}{d t} \propto \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d q}{d t} \propto \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot v{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} is constant for both spheres, so {tex}v \propto \frac{1}{x^{\frac{1}{2}}} \Rightarrow v \propto x^{-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}

explanation: From {tex}\triangle{/tex} ACO of forces, {tex}\frac{F}{A C}=\frac{m g}{O C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{k q^{2}}{x^{2}(\frac x 2)}=\frac{m g}{\sqrt{l^{2}-\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 k q^{2}}{x^{3}}=\frac{m g}{l}{/tex} [ {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} << l] {tex}\therefore{/tex} q 2 = {tex}\frac{m g}{2 k l}\cdot{/tex} x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q 2 {tex}\propto{/tex} x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q {tex}\propto{/tex} x 3/2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d q}{d t} \propto \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d q}{d t} \propto \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot v{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} is constant for both spheres, so {tex}v \propto \frac{1}{x^{\frac{1}{2}}} \Rightarrow v \propto x^{-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}

question_html: What is the flux due to electric field {tex}\mathbf { E } = 3 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { i } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} through a square of side 10cm, when it is held normal to E?
question_text: What is the flux due to electric field {tex}\mathbf { E } = 3 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { i } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} through a square of side 10cm, when it is held normal to E?
explanation: Given: {tex}\mathbf { E } = 3 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathbf { N C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Side of square (S) = 10 cm = 0.10 m Area of square (A) = (side) 2 = (0.1) 2 = {tex}1 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Hence, electric flux through the square, {tex}\phi = E \cdot A = \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \right) \times10 ^ { - 2 } \hat i = 30 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\mathbf { E } = 3 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathbf { N C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Side of square (S) = 10 cm = 0.10 m Area of square (A) = (side) 2 = (0.1) 2 = {tex}1 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Hence, electric flux through the square, {tex}\phi = E \cdot A = \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \right) \times10 ^ { - 2 } \hat i = 30 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\mathbf { E } = 3 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathbf { N C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
Side of square (S) = 10 cm = 0.10 m
Area of square (A) = (side)2 = (0.1)2 = {tex}1 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Hence, electric flux through the square,
{tex}\phi = E \cdot A = \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \right) \times10 ^ { - 2 } \hat i = 30 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. Gauss' law in electrostatics is true for any closed surface, no matter what its shape or size is. Justify this statement with the help of a suitable example.
  2. Use Gauss' law to prove that the electric field inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero.
question_text: Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. Gauss' law in electrostatics is true for any closed surface, no matter what its shape or size is. Justify this statement with the help of a suitable example. Use Gauss' law to prove that the electric field inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero.
explanation: The electric flux through an area is defined as the electric field multiplied by the area of the surface projected on a plane, perpendicular to the field. Its S.I. unit is voltmeter (V m ) or Newton metre square per coulomb (Nm 2 C -1 ). The given statement is justified because while measuring the flux, the surface area is more important than its volume on its size. Electric field inside the shell: The charge resides on the surface of a conductor. Thus, a hollow charged conductor is equivalent to a charged spherical shell. Let's consider a spherical Gaussian surface of radius (r < R). If E is the electric field inside the shell, then by symmetry electric field strength has the same magnitude E i on the Gaussian surface and is directed radially outward. Electric flux through the Gaussian surface is given by, {tex}=\int_{s} \vec{E}_{i} \cdot d \vec{S}{/tex} {tex}=\int E_{i} d S \cos 0=E_{i} .4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} Now, Gaussian surface is inside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is zero. Therefore, using Gauss's theorem, we have {tex}\int_{S} \vec{E}_{i} \cdot d \vec{S}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \times \text { charge enclosed }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E_{i} \cdot 4 \pi r^{2}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \times 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E i = 0 Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero.
solution: The electric flux through an area is defined as the electric field multiplied by the area of the surface projected on a plane, perpendicular to the field. Its S.I. unit is voltmeter (V m ) or Newton metre square per coulomb (Nm 2 C -1 ). The given statement is justified because while measuring the flux, the surface area is more important than its volume on its size. Electric field inside the shell: The charge resides on the surface of a conductor. Thus, a hollow charged conductor is equivalent to a charged spherical shell. Let's consider a spherical Gaussian surface of radius (r < R). If E is the electric field inside the shell, then by symmetry electric field strength has the same magnitude E i on the Gaussian surface and is directed radially outward. Electric flux through the Gaussian surface is given by, {tex}=\int_{s} \vec{E}_{i} \cdot d \vec{S}{/tex} {tex}=\int E_{i} d S \cos 0=E_{i} .4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} Now, Gaussian surface is inside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is zero. Therefore, using Gauss's theorem, we have {tex}\int_{S} \vec{E}_{i} \cdot d \vec{S}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \times \text { charge enclosed }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E_{i} \cdot 4 \pi r^{2}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \times 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E i = 0 Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero.
solution_html:
1. The electric flux through an area is defined as the electric field multiplied by the area of the surface projected on a plane, perpendicular to the field. Its S.I. unit is voltmeter (Vm) or Newton metre square per coulomb (Nm2 C-1). The given statement is justified because while measuring the flux, the surface area is more important than its volume on its size.
  2. Electric field inside the shell:

    The charge resides on the surface of a conductor. Thus, a hollow charged conductor is equivalent to a charged spherical shell. Let's consider a spherical Gaussian surface of radius (r < R). If E is the electric field inside the shell, then by symmetry electric field strength has the same magnitude Ei on the Gaussian surface and is directed radially outward.
    Electric flux through the Gaussian surface is given by,
    {tex}=\int_{s} \vec{E}_{i} \cdot d \vec{S}{/tex}
    {tex}=\int E_{i} d S \cos 0=E_{i} .4 \pi r^{2}{/tex}
    Now, Gaussian surface is inside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is zero.
    Therefore, using Gauss's theorem, we have
    {tex}\int_{S} \vec{E}_{i} \cdot d \vec{S}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \times \text { charge enclosed }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow E_{i} \cdot 4 \pi r^{2}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \times 0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ei = 0
    Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero.

question_html:
A thin metallic spherical shell of radius R carries a charge Q on its surface. A point charge {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} is placed at its centre C and another charge +2Q is placed outside the shell at a distance x from the centre as shown in Fig.

Find:

  1. the force on the charge at the centre of the shell and at the point A.
  2. the electric flux through the shell.
question_text: A thin metallic spherical shell of radius R carries a charge Q on its surface. A point charge {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} is placed at its centre C and another charge +2Q is placed outside the shell at a distance x from the centre as shown in Fig. Find: the force on the charge at the centre of the shell and at the point A. the electric flux through the shell.
explanation: Net force on the charge {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} placed at the centre of the shell is zero. Force on the charge 2Q kept at point A at distance r from the centre is F = E {tex}\times{/tex} 2Q = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q / 2}{r^{2}} \times{/tex} 2Q {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} The electric flux through the shell, {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{Q / 2}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution: Net force on the charge {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} placed at the centre of the shell is zero. Force on the charge 2Q kept at point A at distance r from the centre is F = E {tex}\times{/tex} 2Q = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q / 2}{r^{2}} \times{/tex} 2Q {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} The electric flux through the shell, {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{Q / 2}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Net force on the charge {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} placed at the centre of the shell is zero.
    Force on the charge 2Q kept at point A at distance r from the centre is
    F = E {tex}\times{/tex} 2Q = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q / 2}{r^{2}} \times{/tex} 2Q
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
  2. The electric flux through the shell,
    {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{Q / 2}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

question_html: Two parallel uniformly charged infinite plane sheets, 1 and 2, have charge densities +{tex}\sigma{/tex} and -2{tex}\sigma{/tex} respectively. Give the magnitude and direction of the net electric field at a point (i) in between the two sheets and (ii) outside near the sheet 1.
question_text: Two parallel uniformly charged infinite plane sheets, 1 and 2 , have charge densities + {tex}\sigma{/tex} and -2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} respectively. Give the magnitude and direction of the net electric field at a point (i) in between the two sheets and (ii) outside near the sheet 1 .
explanation: Let {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} be the electric field intensity at the point P 1 , between the plates. So, | {tex}E_{P_1}{/tex} | = | E 1 | + | E 2 | {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}+\frac{2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} (directed towards sheet 2) {tex}\vec{E}_{P_1}=\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}(-\hat{ j })=-\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \hat{ j }{/tex} Outside near the sheet '1', {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{P_2}\right|=\vec{E}_2-\vec{E}_1{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} (directed towards sheet 2) {tex}\vec{E}_{P_2}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}(-\hat{ j })=-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0} \hat{ j }{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} be the electric field intensity at the point P 1 , between the plates. So, | {tex}E_{P_1}{/tex} | = | E 1 | + | E 2 | {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}+\frac{2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} (directed towards sheet 2) {tex}\vec{E}_{P_1}=\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}(-\hat{ j })=-\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \hat{ j }{/tex} Outside near the sheet '1', {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{P_2}\right|=\vec{E}_2-\vec{E}_1{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} (directed towards sheet 2) {tex}\vec{E}_{P_2}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}(-\hat{ j })=-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0} \hat{ j }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}_2{/tex} be the electric field intensity at the point P1, between the plates. So,
    | {tex}E_{P_1}{/tex} | = | E1 | + | E2 |
    {tex}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}+\frac{2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} (directed towards sheet 2)
    {tex}\vec{E}_{P_1}=\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0}(-\hat{ j })=-\frac{3 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \hat{ j }{/tex}
  2. Outside near the sheet '1', {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{P_2}\right|=\vec{E}_2-\vec{E}_1{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} (directed towards sheet 2)
    {tex}\vec{E}_{P_2}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}(-\hat{ j })=-\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0} \hat{ j }{/tex}

question_html:
1. Define electric flux. Write its SI unit.
  2. State and explain Gauss's law. Find out the outward flux due to a point charge +q placed at the centre of a cube of side a. Why is it found to be independent of the size and shape of the surface enclosing it? Explain.
question_text: Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. State and explain Gauss's law. Find out the outward flux due to a point charge +q placed at the centre of a cube of side a . Why is it found to be independent of the size and shape of the surface enclosing it? Explain.
explanation: The total number of electric field lines crossing perpendicular to surface is called electric flux. The SI unit of electric flux is volt meters (V - m), The total electric flux across a closed surface in an electric field is equal to ( {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} ) times the total charge enclosed by it. Consider a closed surface S in an electric field. Let Q be the total charge in the volume enclosed by the surface. Q = {tex}\sum q_n{/tex} where {tex}\sum q_n{/tex} = q 1 + q 2 + q 3 + ...+ q n Then outward electric flux over entire area is {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\oint{/tex} E.ds = {tex}\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} a cube of side a enclose charge +q at its centre. Because the electric flux through the square surface is {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{6\in_0}{/tex} the square surfaces of cube are six. Hence, according to Gauss’s theorem in electrostatics, the total outward flux due to a charge +q of a cube is {tex}\phi{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}\frac{q}{6\in_0}{/tex} ) = {tex}q\in_0{/tex} The result shows that the electric flux passing through a closed surface is proportional to the charge enclosed. In addition, the result reinforces that the flux is independent of the shape and size of the closed surface.
