question_html:
1. Gauss's law in electrostatics is tijue for any closed surface, no matter what its shape or size is. Justify this statement with the help of a suitable example.
  2. Use Gauss's law to prove that the electric field inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero.
  3. Given the electric field in the region {tex}\vec{E}=2 x \hat{i}{/tex}, find the net electric flux through tne cube and the charge enclosed by it.
question_text: Gauss's law in electrostatics is tijue for any closed surface, no matter what its shape or size is. Justify this statement with the help of a suitable example. Use Gauss's law to prove that the electric field inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero. Given the electric field in the region {tex}\vec{E}=2 x \hat{i}{/tex} , find the net electric flux through tne cube and the charge enclosed by it.
explanation: According to Gauss theorem, the electric flux through a closed surface depends only on the net charge enclosed by the surface and not upon the shape or size of the surface. For any closed arbitrary shape of the surface enclosing a charge the outward flux is the same as that due to a spherical Gaussian surface enclosing the same charge. Justification: This is due to the fact that (i) Electric field is radial and (ii) The electric field E ∝ 1/R 2 Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero. {tex}=\int_S \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}_i \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathbf{S}}{/tex} {tex}=\int E_i \; d S \; \cos 0=E_i \cdot 4 \pi r^2{/tex} Now, Gaussian surface is inside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is zero. Hence, by Gauss’s theorem {tex}\int_S \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}_i} \cdot \overrightarrow{d \mathbf{S}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times{/tex} charge enclosed {tex}\Rightarrow E_i 4 \pi r^2=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times 0 \Rightarrow E_i=0{/tex} Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero. Since the electric field has only x component, for faces normal to x direction, the angle between E and {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is {tex}\pm\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Therefore, the flux is separately zero for each face of the cube except the two shaded ones. The magnitude of the electric field at the left face is E L = 0 (As x = 0 at the left face) The magnitude of the electric field at the right face is E R = 2a (As x = a at the right face) The corresponding fluxes are right face is E r = 2a (As x = a at the right face) The corresponding fluxes are {tex}\phi_L=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}=0{/tex} {tex}\phi_R=\vec{E}_R \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} {tex}=E_R \Delta S \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=E_R \Delta S \left(\because \theta=0^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi R=E R a^2{/tex} Net flux {tex}(\phi){/tex} throught the cube {tex}=\phi \mathrm{L}+\phi \mathrm{R}{/tex} = 0 + ERa 2 = ERa 2 {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2a(a) 2 = 2a 3 We can use Gauss's law to find the total charge q inside the cube. {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{q}=\phi \varepsilon_0=2 \mathrm{a}^3 \varepsilon_0{/tex}
solution: According to Gauss theorem, the electric flux through a closed surface depends only on the net charge enclosed by the surface and not upon the shape or size of the surface. For any closed arbitrary shape of the surface enclosing a charge the outward flux is the same as that due to a spherical Gaussian surface enclosing the same charge. Justification: This is due to the fact that (i) Electric field is radial and (ii) The electric field E ∝ 1/R 2 Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero. {tex}=\int_S \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}_i \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathbf{S}}{/tex} {tex}=\int E_i \; d S \; \cos 0=E_i \cdot 4 \pi r^2{/tex} Now, Gaussian surface is inside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is zero. Hence, by Gauss’s theorem {tex}\int_S \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}_i} \cdot \overrightarrow{d \mathbf{S}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times{/tex} charge enclosed {tex}\Rightarrow E_i 4 \pi r^2=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times 0 \Rightarrow E_i=0{/tex} Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero. Since the electric field has only x component, for faces normal to x direction, the angle between E and {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is {tex}\pm\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Therefore, the flux is separately zero for each face of the cube except the two shaded ones. The magnitude of the electric field at the left face is E L = 0 (As x = 0 at the left face) The magnitude of the electric field at the right face is E R = 2a (As x = a at the right face) The corresponding fluxes are right face is E r = 2a (As x = a at the right face) The corresponding fluxes are {tex}\phi_L=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}=0{/tex} {tex}\phi_R=\vec{E}_R \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} {tex}=E_R \Delta S \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=E_R \Delta S \left(\because \theta=0^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi R=E R a^2{/tex} Net flux {tex}(\phi){/tex} throught the cube {tex}=\phi \mathrm{L}+\phi \mathrm{R}{/tex} = 0 + ERa 2 = ERa 2 {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2a(a) 2 = 2a 3 We can use Gauss's law to find the total charge q inside the cube. {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{q}=\phi \varepsilon_0=2 \mathrm{a}^3 \varepsilon_0{/tex}
solution_html:
1. According to Gauss theorem, the electric flux through a closed surface depends only on the net charge enclosed by the surface and not upon the shape or size of the surface. For any closed arbitrary shape of the surface enclosing a charge the outward flux is the same as that due to a spherical Gaussian surface enclosing the same charge. Justification: This is due to the fact that (i) Electric field is radial and (ii) The electric field E ∝ 1/R2 Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero.

  2. {tex}=\int_S \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}_i \cdot d \overrightarrow{\mathbf{S}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\int E_i \; d S \; \cos 0=E_i \cdot 4 \pi r^2{/tex}
    Now, Gaussian surface is inside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is zero. Hence, by Gauss’s theorem
    {tex}\int_S \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}_i} \cdot \overrightarrow{d \mathbf{S}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times{/tex} charge enclosed
    {tex}\Rightarrow E_i 4 \pi r^2=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times 0 \Rightarrow E_i=0{/tex}
    Thus, electric field at each point inside a charged thin spherical shell is zero.
  3. Since the electric field has only x component, for faces normal to x direction, the angle between E and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is {tex}\pm\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}. Therefore, the flux is separately zero for each face of the cube except the two shaded ones.

    The magnitude of the electric field at the left face is EL = 0 (As x = 0 at the left face) The magnitude of the electric field at the right face is ER = 2a (As x = a at the right face) The corresponding fluxes are
    right face is Er = 2a
    (As x = a at the right face)
    The corresponding fluxes are
    {tex}\phi_L=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}=0{/tex}
    {tex}\phi_R=\vec{E}_R \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex}
    {tex}=E_R \Delta S \cos \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=E_R \Delta S \left(\because \theta=0^{\circ}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \phi R=E R a^2{/tex}
    Net flux {tex}(\phi){/tex} throught the cube {tex}=\phi \mathrm{L}+\phi \mathrm{R}{/tex}
    = 0 + ERa2 = ERa2
    {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2a(a)2 = 2a3
    We can use Gauss's law to find the total charge q inside the cube.
    {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{q}=\phi \varepsilon_0=2 \mathrm{a}^3 \varepsilon_0{/tex}

question_html:
A right circular cylinder of length a and radius r has its centre at the origin and its axis along the x-axis so that one face is at x = +{tex}\frac a2{/tex} and the other at x = -{tex}\frac a2{/tex}, as shown in the figure. A uniform electric field is acting parallel to the x-axis such that {tex}\vec{E}=E_0 \hat{i}{/tex} for x > 0 and = {tex}\vec{E}=-E_0 \hat{i}{/tex} for x > 0.

Find out the flux (i) through the flat faces, (ii) through the curved surface of the cylinder. What is the net outward flux through the cylinder and the net charge inside the cylinder?
question_text: A right circular cylinder of length a and radius r has its centre at the origin and its axis along the x-axis so that one face is at x = + {tex}\frac a2{/tex} and the other at x = - {tex}\frac a2{/tex} , as shown in the figure. A uniform electric field is acting parallel to the x-axis such that {tex}\vec{E}=E_0 \hat{i}{/tex} for x > 0 and = {tex}\vec{E}=-E_0 \hat{i}{/tex} for x > 0. Find out the flux (i) through the flat faces, (ii) through the curved surface of the cylinder. What is the net outward flux through the cylinder and the net charge inside the cylinder?
explanation: Flux through the flat faces (both) {tex}\phi_1=E_0 \hat{i} . \pi r^2 \hat{i}{/tex} {tex}=\left|E_0\right| \pi r^2 [\because \hat{i} \hat{i}=1]{/tex} Flux through the curved surface {tex}\phi_2 =E_0 \hat{i} \cdot(2 \pi r a) \hat{j}{/tex} = 0 {tex}[\because i \hat{j}=0]{/tex} (Field and area vector are perpendicular to each other) Net outward flux through the cylinder, {tex}\phi_{n e t} =2 \phi_1+\phi_2{/tex} {tex}=2 E_0 \pi r^2{/tex} According to Gauss's theorem, {tex}\phi_{n e t}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge inside the cylinder {tex}Q =2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2 E_0{/tex}
solution: Flux through the flat faces (both) {tex}\phi_1=E_0 \hat{i} . \pi r^2 \hat{i}{/tex} {tex}=\left|E_0\right| \pi r^2 [\because \hat{i} \hat{i}=1]{/tex} Flux through the curved surface {tex}\phi_2 =E_0 \hat{i} \cdot(2 \pi r a) \hat{j}{/tex} = 0 {tex}[\because i \hat{j}=0]{/tex} (Field and area vector are perpendicular to each other) Net outward flux through the cylinder, {tex}\phi_{n e t} =2 \phi_1+\phi_2{/tex} {tex}=2 E_0 \pi r^2{/tex} According to Gauss's theorem, {tex}\phi_{n e t}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge inside the cylinder {tex}Q =2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2 E_0{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Flux through the flat faces (both)
    {tex}\phi_1=E_0 \hat{i} . \pi r^2 \hat{i}{/tex}{tex}=\left|E_0\right| \pi r^2 [\because \hat{i} \hat{i}=1]{/tex}

  2. Flux through the curved surface
    {tex}\phi_2 =E_0 \hat{i} \cdot(2 \pi r a) \hat{j}{/tex}
    = 0 {tex}[\because i \hat{j}=0]{/tex}
    (Field and area vector are perpendicular to each other)
    Net outward flux through the cylinder,
    {tex}\phi_{n e t} =2 \phi_1+\phi_2{/tex}
    {tex}=2 E_0 \pi r^2{/tex}
    According to Gauss's theorem, {tex}\phi_{n e t}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge inside the cylinder
    {tex}Q =2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2 E_0{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. The outward electric flux due to charge +Q is independent of the shape and size of the surface which encloses it. Give two reasons to justify this statement.
  2. Two identical circular loops 1 and 2 of radius R each have linear charge densities -{tex}\lambda{/tex} and +{tex}\lambda{/tex} C/m respectively. The loops are placed coaxially with their centres R{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} distance apart. Find the magnitude and direction of the net electric field at the centre of loop 1.
question_text: The outward electric flux due to charge +Q is independent of the shape and size of the surface which encloses it. Give two reasons to justify this statement. Two identical circular loops 1 and 2 of radius R each have linear charge densities - {tex}\lambda{/tex} and + {tex}\lambda{/tex} C/m respectively. The loops are placed coaxially with their centres R {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} distance apart. Find the magnitude and direction of the net electric field at the centre of loop 1 .
explanation: In figure, A charge +Q is enclosed in side the surfaces S 1 and S 2 . For a given charge Q the same number of electric field lines emanating from the surfaces S 1 and S 2 depends on the charge Q and independent to the shape and size of the surfaces of S 1 and S 2 . From Gauss's law the net-outward electric flux through any closed surface of any shape and size is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the charge enclosed within that surface i.e., {tex}\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Electric field at the centre O 1 due to loop 1 is given by {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{ E }_1\right|{/tex} = 0 (As Z = 0) Electric field at a point outside the loop 2 on the axis passing normally through O 2 of loop 2 is. {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{ E }_2\right|=\frac{\lambda R}{2 \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z}{\left(R^2+Z^2\right)^\frac {3 }{ 2}}{/tex} Since Z = R {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda R}{2 \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{R \sqrt{3}}{\left(R^2+3 R^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}{/tex} towards right (As {tex}\lambda{/tex} is positive) So, net electric field at the centre of loop 1 {tex}\vec{E} =\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2{/tex} {tex}=0+\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}=\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}{/tex}
solution: In figure, A charge +Q is enclosed in side the surfaces S 1 and S 2 . For a given charge Q the same number of electric field lines emanating from the surfaces S 1 and S 2 depends on the charge Q and independent to the shape and size of the surfaces of S 1 and S 2 . From Gauss's law the net-outward electric flux through any closed surface of any shape and size is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the charge enclosed within that surface i.e., {tex}\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Electric field at the centre O 1 due to loop 1 is given by {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{ E }_1\right|{/tex} = 0 (As Z = 0) Electric field at a point outside the loop 2 on the axis passing normally through O 2 of loop 2 is. {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{ E }_2\right|=\frac{\lambda R}{2 \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z}{\left(R^2+Z^2\right)^\frac {3 }{ 2}}{/tex} Since Z = R {tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda R}{2 \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{R \sqrt{3}}{\left(R^2+3 R^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}{/tex} towards right (As {tex}\lambda{/tex} is positive) So, net electric field at the centre of loop 1 {tex}\vec{E} =\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2{/tex} {tex}=0+\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}=\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. In figure, A charge +Q is enclosed in side the surfaces S1 and S2.
    1. For a given charge Q the same number of electric field lines emanating from the surfaces S1 and S2 depends on the charge Q and independent to the shape and size of the surfaces of S1 and S2.
    2. From Gauss's law the net-outward electric flux through any closed surface of any shape and size is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the charge enclosed within that surface i.e., {tex}\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}


  2. Electric field at the centre O1 due to loop 1 is given by
    {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{ E }_1\right|{/tex} = 0 (As Z = 0)
    Electric field at a point outside the loop 2 on the axis passing normally through O2 of loop 2 is.
    {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{ E }_2\right|=\frac{\lambda R}{2 \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z}{\left(R^2+Z^2\right)^\frac {3 }{ 2}}{/tex}
    Since Z = R{tex} \sqrt{3}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\lambda R}{2 \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{R \sqrt{3}}{\left(R^2+3 R^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}{/tex} towards right (As {tex}\lambda{/tex} is positive)
    So, net electric field at the centre of loop 1
    {tex}\vec{E} =\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2{/tex}
    {tex}=0+\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}=\frac{\lambda \sqrt{3}}{16 \varepsilon_0 R}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State the theorem which relates the total charge enclosed within a closed surface and the electric flux passing through it. Prove it for a single point charge.
  2. An atom was earlier assumed to be a sphere of radius having a positively charged point nucleus of charge +Ze at its centre. This nucleus was believed to be surrounded by a uniform density of negative charge that made the atom neutral as a whole. Use this theorem to find the electric field of this 'atom' at a distance r(r < a) from the centre of the atom.
question_text: State the theorem which relates the total charge enclosed within a closed surface and the electric flux passing through it. Prove it for a single point charge. An atom was earlier assumed to be a sphere of radius having a positively charged point nucleus of charge +Ze at its centre. This nucleus was believed to be surrounded by a uniform density of negative charge that made the atom neutral as a whole. Use this theorem to find the electric field of this 'atom' at a distance r(r < a) from the centre of the atom.
explanation: Gauss law states that the total electric flux out of a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed divided by the permittivity. Proof: Let us consider the total flux through a sphere of radius r, which encloses a point charge g at its centre. Divide the sphere into small area elements, as shown in the figure. The flux through an area element {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{10} r^2} \hat{r} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} where we have used Coulomb's law for the electric field due to a single charge q. The unit vector {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is along the radius vector from the centre to the area element. Now, since the normal to a sphere at every point is along the radius vector at that point, the area element {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\vec{S}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} have the same direction. Therefore, {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} S Let ρ be the uniform density of negative charge. then We have, {tex}\frac{4\pi a^3\rho}{3}{/tex} = Ze {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = ( {tex}\frac{3Ze}{4\pi a^3}{/tex} ) Taking a sphere of radius r (centre at the nucleus) as the Gaussian surface, we have, E(r) {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(i) where, q is the net charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface. Now, q = (+Ze) + {tex}\left(-\rho \cdot \frac{4 \pi r^3}{3}\right){/tex} = Ze - Ze {tex}\left(\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex} = Ze {tex}\left(1-\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex} Substituting this value of a, in eq. (i) E(r) = {tex}\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r^2}-\frac{r}{a^3}\right){/tex}
solution: Gauss law states that the total electric flux out of a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed divided by the permittivity. Proof: Let us consider the total flux through a sphere of radius r, which encloses a point charge g at its centre. Divide the sphere into small area elements, as shown in the figure. The flux through an area element {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{10} r^2} \hat{r} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} where we have used Coulomb's law for the electric field due to a single charge q. The unit vector {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is along the radius vector from the centre to the area element. Now, since the normal to a sphere at every point is along the radius vector at that point, the area element {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\vec{S}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} have the same direction. Therefore, {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} S Let ρ be the uniform density of negative charge. then We have, {tex}\frac{4\pi a^3\rho}{3}{/tex} = Ze {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = ( {tex}\frac{3Ze}{4\pi a^3}{/tex} ) Taking a sphere of radius r (centre at the nucleus) as the Gaussian surface, we have, E(r) {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(i) where, q is the net charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface. Now, q = (+Ze) + {tex}\left(-\rho \cdot \frac{4 \pi r^3}{3}\right){/tex} = Ze - Ze {tex}\left(\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex} = Ze {tex}\left(1-\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex} Substituting this value of a, in eq. (i) E(r) = {tex}\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r^2}-\frac{r}{a^3}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
1. Gauss law states that the total electric flux out of a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed divided by the permittivity.
    Proof: Let us consider the total flux through a sphere of radius r, which encloses a point charge g at its centre. Divide the sphere into small area elements, as shown in the figure.

    The flux through an area element {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}{tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{10} r^2} \hat{r} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex}
    where we have used Coulomb's law for the electric field due to a single charge q. The unit vector {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is along the radius vector from the centre to the area element. Now, since the normal to a sphere at every point is along the radius vector at that point, the area element {tex}\Delta{/tex}{tex}\vec{S}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} have the same direction.
    Therefore,
    {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
  2. Let ρ be the uniform density of negative charge.
    then We have,
    {tex}\frac{4\pi a^3\rho}{3}{/tex} = Ze
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = ({tex}\frac{3Ze}{4\pi a^3}{/tex})
    Taking a sphere of radius r (centre at the nucleus) as the Gaussian surface,
    we have,
    E(r) {tex}\times{/tex} 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(i)
    where, q is the net charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface.
    Now,
    q = (+Ze) + {tex}\left(-\rho \cdot \frac{4 \pi r^3}{3}\right){/tex}
    = Ze - Ze{tex}\left(\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex}
    = Ze{tex}\left(1-\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex}
    Substituting this value of a, in eq. (i)
    E(r) = {tex}\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}{tex}\left(1-\frac{r^3}{a^3}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r^2}-\frac{r}{a^3}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
S1 and S2 are two concentric spheres enclosing charges Q and 2Q respectively as shown in Fig.

  1. What is the ratio of the electric flux through S1 and S2?
  2. How will the electric flux through the sphere S1, change, if a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S1 in place of air?
  3. How will the electric flux through sphere S1 change, if a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S2, in place of air?
question_text: S 1 and S 2 are two concentric spheres enclosing charges Q and 2Q respectively as shown in Fig. What is the ratio of the electric flux through S 1 and S 2 ? How will the electric flux through the sphere S 1 , change, if a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S 1 in place of air? How will the electric flux through sphere S 1 change, if a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S 2 , in place of air?
explanation: By Gauss's Theorem, Flux through S 1 is {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Flux through S 2 is {tex}\phi_{2}=\frac{2 Q+Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{3 Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Ratio of electric flux through S 1 and S 2 is {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{Q / \varepsilon_{0}}{3 Q / \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 1 : 3 If a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S 1 , then flux through S 1 becomes {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\oint \vec{E}^{\prime} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\oint \frac{\vec{E}}{\kappa} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} The flux through S 1 does not change with the introduction of the dielectric medium inside the sphere S 2 .
solution: By Gauss's Theorem, Flux through S 1 is {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Flux through S 2 is {tex}\phi_{2}=\frac{2 Q+Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{3 Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Ratio of electric flux through S 1 and S 2 is {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{Q / \varepsilon_{0}}{3 Q / \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 1 : 3 If a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S 1 , then flux through S 1 becomes {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\oint \vec{E}^{\prime} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\oint \frac{\vec{E}}{\kappa} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} The flux through S 1 does not change with the introduction of the dielectric medium inside the sphere S 2 .
solution_html:
1. By Gauss's Theorem,
    Flux through S1 is {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Flux through S2 is {tex}\phi_{2}=\frac{2 Q+Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{3 Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Ratio of electric flux through S1 and S2 is
    {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{Q / \varepsilon_{0}}{3 Q / \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 1 : 3
  2. If a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S1, then flux through S1 becomes
    {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\oint \vec{E}^{\prime} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\oint \frac{\vec{E}}{\kappa} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
  3. The flux through S1 does not change with the introduction of the dielectric medium inside the sphere S2.

question_html: Given a uniform electric field {tex}E = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} NC-1. Find the flux of this field through a square of 10cm on a side whose plane is parallel to the YZ-plane. What would be the flux through the same square if the plane makes an angle of 30o with the X-axis?
question_text: Given a uniform electric field {tex}E = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} NC -1 . Find the flux of this field through a square of 10cm on a side whose plane is parallel to the YZ-plane. What would be the flux through the same square if the plane makes an angle of 30 o with the X-axis?
explanation: Given, electric field intensity {tex}\mathbf { E } = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { i } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Magnitude of electric field intensity {tex}| \mathbf { E } | = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Side of square, S = 10 cm = 0.1 m Area of square, A = (0.1) 2 = 0.01 m 2 The plane of the square is parallel to the YZ-plane. Hence, the angle between the unit vector normal to the plane and electric field is zero. i.e., {tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Flux through the plane is the scalar product of the Electric field vector and Area vector. {tex}\phi = | E | \times A \cos \theta \Rightarrow \phi = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \times 0.01 \cos 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}\phi = 50 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} If the plane makes an angle of 30 o with the X-axis, then the angle between area vector and electric field will be, {tex}\theta = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Flux through the plane, {tex}\phi = | \mathbf { E } | \times A \times \cos 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \times 0.01 \times \cos 60 ^ { \circ } = 25 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Here the positive value of electric flux denotes that electric field lines are coming out of the loop.
solution: Given, electric field intensity {tex}\mathbf { E } = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { i } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Magnitude of electric field intensity {tex}| \mathbf { E } | = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Side of square, S = 10 cm = 0.1 m Area of square, A = (0.1) 2 = 0.01 m 2 The plane of the square is parallel to the YZ-plane. Hence, the angle between the unit vector normal to the plane and electric field is zero. i.e., {tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Flux through the plane is the scalar product of the Electric field vector and Area vector. {tex}\phi = | E | \times A \cos \theta \Rightarrow \phi = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \times 0.01 \cos 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}\phi = 50 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} If the plane makes an angle of 30 o with the X-axis, then the angle between area vector and electric field will be, {tex}\theta = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Flux through the plane, {tex}\phi = | \mathbf { E } | \times A \times \cos 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \times 0.01 \times \cos 60 ^ { \circ } = 25 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Here the positive value of electric flux denotes that electric field lines are coming out of the loop.
solution_html:
Given, electric field intensity
{tex}\mathbf { E } = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { i } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
Magnitude of electric field intensity
{tex}| \mathbf { E } | = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
Side of square, S = 10 cm = 0.1 m
Area of square, A = (0.1)2 = 0.01 m2
The plane of the square is parallel to the YZ-plane.
Hence, the angle between the unit vector normal to the plane and electric field is zero.
i.e., {tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Flux through the plane is the scalar product of the Electric field vector and Area vector.
{tex}\phi = | E | \times A \cos \theta \Rightarrow \phi = 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \times 0.01 \cos 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\phi = 50 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
If the plane makes an angle of 30o with the X-axis, then the angle between area vector and electric field will be, {tex}\theta = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Flux through the plane,
{tex}\phi = | \mathbf { E } | \times A \times \cos 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}= 5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \times 0.01 \times \cos 60 ^ { \circ } = 25 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
Here the positive value of electric flux denotes that electric field lines are coming out of the loop.

question_html: Given a uniform electric field {tex}\mathbf { E } = 2 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} NC-1. Find the flux of this field through a square of side 20 cm, whose plane is parallel to the YZ-plane. What would be the flux through the same square, if the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis?
question_text: Given a uniform electric field {tex}\mathbf { E } = 2 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} NC -1 . Find the flux of this field through a square of side 20 cm, whose plane is parallel to the YZ-plane. What would be the flux through the same square, if the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis?
explanation: {tex}\mathbf { E } = 2 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} NC -1 {tex}\begin{array}{l}A\;=\;4\times10^{-2}\;\hat im^2\\\end{array}{/tex} Electric flux = E.A = 80 Nm 2 C -1 If the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis, then area vector makes 60 o with x-axis, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\theta=60^o\\\end{array}{/tex} Thus, Electric flux = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E\;A\;cos\;\theta\;=\;80\;\cos\;60\;=\;40\;NC^{-1}m^2\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathbf { E } = 2 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} NC -1 {tex}\begin{array}{l}A\;=\;4\times10^{-2}\;\hat im^2\\\end{array}{/tex} Electric flux = E.A = 80 Nm 2 C -1 If the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis, then area vector makes 60 o with x-axis, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\theta=60^o\\\end{array}{/tex} Thus, Electric flux = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E\;A\;cos\;\theta\;=\;80\;\cos\;60\;=\;40\;NC^{-1}m^2\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mathbf { E } = 2 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} NC-1
{tex}\begin{array}{l}A\;=\;4\times10^{-2}\;\hat im^2\\\end{array}{/tex}
Electric flux = E.A = 80 Nm2C-1
If the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis, then area vector makes 60o with x-axis,{tex}\begin{array}{l}\theta=60^o\\\end{array}{/tex}
Thus, Electric flux = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E\;A\;cos\;\theta\;=\;80\;\cos\;60\;=\;40\;NC^{-1}m^2\\\end{array}{/tex}

question_text: Given a uniform electric field {tex}\mathbf { E } = 4 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathbf { i }{/tex} NC -1 . Find the flux of this field through a square of 5 cm on a side whose plane is parallel to the YZ-plane. What would be the flux through the same square if the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis?
explanation: {tex}\begin{array}{l}A=\;25\times10^{-4}\;\hat im^2\\\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\mathbf { E } = 4 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathbf {\hat i }{/tex} NC -1 Electric flux = E.A = 10 Nm 2 C -1 If the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis then the area vector makes 60 o with x-axis, then {tex}\begin{array}{l}\theta=60\\\end{array}{/tex} Electric flux {tex}\begin{array}{l}=\;EA\;\cos\;\theta\\\end{array}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{array}{l}25\times10^{-4}\times4\times10^3\times\cos\;60=5\;NC^{-1}\;m^2\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\begin{array}{l}A=\;25\times10^{-4}\;\hat im^2\\\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\mathbf { E } = 4 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathbf {\hat i }{/tex} NC -1 Electric flux = E.A = 10 Nm 2 C -1 If the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis then the area vector makes 60 o with x-axis, then {tex}\begin{array}{l}\theta=60\\\end{array}{/tex} Electric flux {tex}\begin{array}{l}=\;EA\;\cos\;\theta\\\end{array}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{array}{l}25\times10^{-4}\times4\times10^3\times\cos\;60=5\;NC^{-1}\;m^2\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\begin{array}{l}A=\;25\times10^{-4}\;\hat im^2\\\end{array}{/tex}
{tex}\mathbf { E } = 4 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathbf {\hat i }{/tex} NC-1
Electric flux = E.A = 10 Nm2C-1
If the plane makes an angle of 30° with the X-axis then the area vector makes 60o with x-axis, then {tex}\begin{array}{l}\theta=60\\\end{array}{/tex}
Electric flux {tex}\begin{array}{l}=\;EA\;\cos\;\theta\\\end{array}{/tex} = {tex}\begin{array}{l}25\times10^{-4}\times4\times10^3\times\cos\;60=5\;NC^{-1}\;m^2\\\end{array}{/tex}

question_html: A small metal sphere carrying a charge +Q is located at the centre of a spherical cavity in a large uncharged metallic spherical shell. Write the charges on the inner and outer surfaces of the shell. Write the expression for the electric field at the point P1.
question_text: A small metal sphere carrying a charge +Q is located at the centre of a spherical cavity in a large uncharged metallic spherical shell. Write the charges on the inner and outer surfaces of the shell. Write the expression for the electric field at the point P 1 .
explanation: According to the question, the charge on inner surface = +Q The charge on outer surface = -Q So for point P, it would appear that only charge that is producing electric field at P will only +Q The electric field at point P 1 is given by- {tex}E = Q / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, the charge on inner surface = +Q The charge on outer surface = -Q So for point P, it would appear that only charge that is producing electric field at P will only +Q The electric field at point P 1 is given by- {tex}E = Q / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: Why do the electrostatic field lines not form closed loop?
question_text: Why do the electrostatic field lines not form closed loop?
explanation: The electrostatic field lines do not form a closed loop because electric field lines can not reside under the conductor. If they form close loop like magnetic field there must be existence of another pole which is not the case with the electric charges.
solution: The electrostatic field lines do not form a closed loop because electric field lines can not reside under the conductor. If they form close loop like magnetic field there must be existence of another pole which is not the case with the electric charges.
solution_html: The electrostatic field lines do not form a closed loop because electric field lines can not reside under the conductor. If they form close loop like magnetic field there must be existence of another pole which is not the case with the electric charges.

question_html: Two equal balls having equal positive charge q coulombs are suspended by two insulating strings of equal length. What would be the effect on the force when a plastic sheet is inserted between the two charges and angle between them?
question_text: Two equal balls having equal positive charge q coulombs are suspended by two insulating strings of equal length. What would be the effect on the force when a plastic sheet is inserted between the two charges and angle between them?
explanation: According to the question, both the balls have the same charge q. Let the balls be separated by a distance r. Hence, according to Coulomb's law, if F and F' are the force of attraction between balls in the air and in medium respectively. Then, {tex}F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} In the second case, when plastic sheet is inserted, {tex}F ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi K \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore F' = F / K{/tex} where K is a dielectric constant of the material and K > 1 for insulators like plastic, hence the force is reduced when a plastic sheet is inserted between them. As the angle between them is directly proportional to repulsive force, so angle will also decrease when plastic sheet is inserted between them.
solution: According to the question, both the balls have the same charge q. Let the balls be separated by a distance r. Hence, according to Coulomb's law, if F and F' are the force of attraction between balls in the air and in medium respectively. Then, {tex}F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} In the second case, when plastic sheet is inserted, {tex}F ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi K \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore F' = F / K{/tex} where K is a dielectric constant of the material and K > 1 for insulators like plastic, hence the force is reduced when a plastic sheet is inserted between them. As the angle between them is directly proportional to repulsive force, so angle will also decrease when plastic sheet is inserted between them.
solution_html:
According to the question, both the balls have the same charge q. Let the balls be separated by a distance r. Hence, according to Coulomb's law, if F and F' are the force of attraction between balls in the air and in medium respectively.
Then, {tex}F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} 
In the second case, when plastic sheet is inserted,
{tex}F ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi K \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore F' = F / K{/tex}
where K is a dielectric constant of the material and K > 1 for insulators like plastic, hence the force is reduced when a plastic sheet is inserted between them.
As the angle between them is directly proportional to repulsive force, so angle will also decrease when plastic sheet is inserted between them.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why do the electric field lines never cross each other?
question_text: Why do the electric field lines never cross each other?
explanation: At the intersection point, if electric field lines cross each other, then there would be two directions of the electric field. So any test charge placed at that point can move in any two directions simultaneously which is not possible, so lines of forces never cross each other.
solution: At the intersection point, if electric field lines cross each other, then there would be two directions of the electric field. So any test charge placed at that point can move in any two directions simultaneously which is not possible, so lines of forces never cross each other.

question_html: An electric dipole of length 4 cm when placed with its axis making an angle of 60° with a uniform electric field, experiences a torque of {tex}4\sqrt 3{/tex} Nm. Calculate the potential energy of the dipole if it has a charge of ± 8nC.
question_text: An electric dipole of length 4 cm when placed with its axis making an angle of 60° with a uniform electric field, experiences a torque of {tex}4\sqrt 3{/tex} Nm. Calculate the potential energy of the dipole if it has a charge of ± 8nC.
explanation: Given, Length of the dipole (2a) = 4 cm {tex}= 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Angle, {tex}\theta = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau = 4 \sqrt { 3 } \mathrm { Nm }{/tex} Charge, {tex}Q = 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} We know that, Torque, {tex}\tau = pEsin\theta =Q ( 2 a ) E \sin \theta{/tex} Thus, Electric field, {tex}E = \frac { \tau } { Q ( 2 a ) \sin \theta } {/tex} {tex}E= \frac { 4 \sqrt { 3 } } { 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times \sin 60 ^ { \circ } }{/tex} {tex}E = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 10 } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Potential energy of a dipole placed in an electric field is given by - {tex}U = - p E \cos \theta = - Q ( 2 a ) E \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}U = - 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times 25 \times 10 ^ { 10 } \cos 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} U = -4 Joule So, the potential energy of the dipole will be -4 Joule and negative sign shows that interaction between electric field and dipole will be attractive in nature and work must be done to overcome this attraction.
solution: Given, Length of the dipole (2a) = 4 cm {tex}= 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Angle, {tex}\theta = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau = 4 \sqrt { 3 } \mathrm { Nm }{/tex} Charge, {tex}Q = 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} We know that, Torque, {tex}\tau = pEsin\theta =Q ( 2 a ) E \sin \theta{/tex} Thus, Electric field, {tex}E = \frac { \tau } { Q ( 2 a ) \sin \theta } {/tex} {tex}E= \frac { 4 \sqrt { 3 } } { 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times \sin 60 ^ { \circ } }{/tex} {tex}E = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 10 } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Potential energy of a dipole placed in an electric field is given by - {tex}U = - p E \cos \theta = - Q ( 2 a ) E \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}U = - 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times 25 \times 10 ^ { 10 } \cos 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} U = -4 Joule So, the potential energy of the dipole will be -4 Joule and negative sign shows that interaction between electric field and dipole will be attractive in nature and work must be done to overcome this attraction.
solution_html:
Given,
Length of the dipole (2a) = 4 cm {tex}= 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Angle, {tex}\theta = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Torque, {tex}\tau = 4 \sqrt { 3 } \mathrm { Nm }{/tex}
Charge, {tex}Q = 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}
We know that,
Torque, {tex}\tau = pEsin\theta =Q ( 2 a ) E \sin \theta{/tex}
Thus, Electric field,
{tex}E = \frac { \tau } { Q ( 2 a ) \sin \theta } {/tex}
{tex}E= \frac { 4 \sqrt { 3 } } { 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times \sin 60 ^ { \circ } }{/tex}
{tex}E = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 10 } \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Potential energy of a dipole placed in an electric field is given by -
{tex}U = - p E \cos \theta = - Q ( 2 a ) E \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}U = - 8 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 4 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times 25 \times 10 ^ { 10 } \cos 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
U = -4 Joule
So, the potential energy of the dipole will be -4 Joule and negative sign shows that interaction between electric field and dipole will be attractive in nature and work must be done to overcome this attraction.

question_html:
An electric dipole is placed in a uniform electric field E with its dipole moment p parallel to the field. Find

  1. the work done in turning the dipole till its dipole moment points in the direction opposite to E.
  2. the orientation of the dipole for which the torque acting on it becomes maximum.
question_text: An electric dipole is placed in a uniform electric field E with its dipole moment p parallel to the field. Find the work done in turning the dipole till its dipole moment points in the direction opposite to E. the orientation of the dipole for which the torque acting on it becomes maximum.
explanation: If a dipole is placed in an electric field, then in order to rotate it we have to do the work against electric field lines which can be found as: Work done in rotating the dipole, {tex}W = \int _ { \theta _ { 1 } } ^ { \theta _ { 2 } } \tau d \theta{/tex} If the dipole is turned from direction parallel to electric field to direction opposite to electric field, then angle {tex}\theta {/tex} will change from 0 to {tex}\pi {/tex} . We know that, {tex}\tau = p E \sin \theta{/tex} If {tex}\theta = \pi / 2{/tex} , then {tex}\tau {/tex} is maximum i.e. {tex}\tau = p E \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow \tau = p E{/tex} (maximum) Maximum torque will be experienced by the dipole when its dipole moment is perpendicular to electric field lines.
solution: If a dipole is placed in an electric field, then in order to rotate it we have to do the work against electric field lines which can be found as: Work done in rotating the dipole, {tex}W = \int _ { \theta _ { 1 } } ^ { \theta _ { 2 } } \tau d \theta{/tex} If the dipole is turned from direction parallel to electric field to direction opposite to electric field, then angle {tex}\theta {/tex} will change from 0 to {tex}\pi {/tex} . We know that, {tex}\tau = p E \sin \theta{/tex} If {tex}\theta = \pi / 2{/tex} , then {tex}\tau {/tex} is maximum i.e. {tex}\tau = p E \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow \tau = p E{/tex} (maximum) Maximum torque will be experienced by the dipole when its dipole moment is perpendicular to electric field lines.
solution_html:
1. If a dipole is placed in an electric field, then in order to rotate it we have to do the work against electric field lines which can be found as:
    Work done in rotating the dipole, {tex}W = \int _ { \theta _ { 1 } } ^ { \theta _ { 2 } } \tau d \theta{/tex} 
    If the dipole is turned from direction parallel to electric field to direction opposite to electric field, then angle {tex}\theta {/tex} will change from 0 to {tex}\pi {/tex}.

  2. We know that, {tex}\tau = p E \sin \theta{/tex}
    If {tex}\theta = \pi / 2{/tex} , then {tex}\tau {/tex} is maximum
    i.e. {tex}\tau = p E \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow \tau = p E{/tex} (maximum)
    Maximum torque will be experienced by the dipole when its dipole moment is perpendicular to electric field lines.

question_html: Two point charges q1 and q2 are placed at a distance d apart as shown in the figure. The electric field intensity is zero at the point P on the line joining them as shown. Write two conclusions that you can draw from this.
question_text: Two point charges q 1 and q 2 are placed at a distance d apart as shown in the figure. The electric field intensity is zero at the point P on the line joining them as shown. Write two conclusions that you can draw from this.
explanation: As the electric field intensity at point P is zero, the electric field produced by both the charges should be equal and opposite in direction which can be possible in the following manner: The two-point charges (q 1 and q 2 ) should be of opposite nature. The magnitude of charge q 1 must be greater than the magnitude of charge q 2 as distance of q 1 is greater than q 2 .
solution: As the electric field intensity at point P is zero, the electric field produced by both the charges should be equal and opposite in direction which can be possible in the following manner: The two-point charges (q 1 and q 2 ) should be of opposite nature. The magnitude of charge q 1 must be greater than the magnitude of charge q 2 as distance of q 1 is greater than q 2 .
solution_html:
As the electric field intensity at point P is zero, the electric field produced by both the charges should be equal and opposite in direction which can be possible in the following manner:

  1. The two-point charges (q1 and q2) should be of opposite nature.
  2. The magnitude of charge q1 must be greater than the magnitude of charge q2  as distance of q1 is greater than q2.

question_html: A sphere S1 of radius r1 encloses a net charge Q. If there is another concentric sphere S2 of radius r2 (r2 > r1) enclosing charge 2Q, find the ratio of the electric flux through S1 and S2. How will the electric flux through sphere S1 change if a medium of dielectric constant K is introduced in the space inside S2 in place of air?
question_text: A sphere S 1 of radius r 1 encloses a net charge Q. If there is another concentric sphere S 2 of radius r 2 (r 2 > r 1 ) enclosing charge 2Q, find the ratio of the electric flux through S 1 and S 2 . How will the electric flux through sphere S 1 change if a medium of dielectric constant K is introduced in the space inside S 2 in place of air?
explanation: According to Gauss' law electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. Flux through S 1 , {tex}\phi _ { 1 } = \frac { Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ........ (i) Flux through S 2 , {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \frac { Q + 2 Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { 3 Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ........ (ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{\phi _2}}} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} There is no change in the flux through S 1 with dielectric medium inside the sphere S 2 as number of electric field lines does not get affected with the insertion of dielectric slab.
solution: According to Gauss' law electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. Flux through S 1 , {tex}\phi _ { 1 } = \frac { Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ........ (i) Flux through S 2 , {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \frac { Q + 2 Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { 3 Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ........ (ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{\phi _2}}} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} There is no change in the flux through S 1 with dielectric medium inside the sphere S 2 as number of electric field lines does not get affected with the insertion of dielectric slab.
solution_html:
According to Gauss' law electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum.
Flux through S1, {tex}\phi _ { 1 } = \frac { Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ........ (i)
Flux through S2, {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \frac { Q + 2 Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { 3 Q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ........ (ii)
On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{\phi _2}}} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
There is no change in the flux through S1 with dielectric medium inside the sphere S2  as number of electric field lines does not get affected with the insertion of dielectric slab.

question_html: Two infinitely large plane thin parallel sheets having surface charge densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} are shown in the figure. Write the magnitudes and directions of the net fields in the regions marked II and III.
question_text: Two infinitely large plane thin parallel sheets having surface charge densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} are shown in the figure. Write the magnitudes and directions of the net fields in the regions marked II and III.
explanation: According to the figure, A and B are two thin plane parallel sheets of charge having uniform densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 }{/tex} with {tex}\sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\phi = E \times{/tex} area of the end faces of the cylinder {tex}E \times 2 A = \frac { \sigma A } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} In region II - The electric field due to the sheet of charge A having surface charge density 'σ 1 ' will be from left to right (along the positive direction) and that due to the sheet of charge B having surface charge density 'σ 2 ' will be from right to left (along the negative direction) Therefore, in region II, we have electric field opposing each other. {tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \left( - \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } - \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side) In region III - The electric fields due to both the charged sheets will be from left to right, i.e. along the positive direction. Therefore, in region III, we have electric fields supporting each other {tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } + \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side)
solution: According to the figure, A and B are two thin plane parallel sheets of charge having uniform densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 }{/tex} with {tex}\sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\phi = E \times{/tex} area of the end faces of the cylinder {tex}E \times 2 A = \frac { \sigma A } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} In region II - The electric field due to the sheet of charge A having surface charge density 'σ 1 ' will be from left to right (along the positive direction) and that due to the sheet of charge B having surface charge density 'σ 2 ' will be from right to left (along the negative direction) Therefore, in region II, we have electric field opposing each other. {tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \left( - \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } - \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side) In region III - The electric fields due to both the charged sheets will be from left to right, i.e. along the positive direction. Therefore, in region III, we have electric fields supporting each other {tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } + \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side)
solution_html:
According to the figure, A and B are two thin plane parallel sheets of charge having uniform densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 }{/tex}with {tex}\sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\phi = E \times{/tex} area of the end faces of the cylinder
{tex}E \times 2 A = \frac { \sigma A } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}

In region II -
The electric field due to the sheet of charge A having surface charge density 'σ1'  will be from left to right (along the positive direction) and that due to the sheet of charge B having surface charge density 'σ2' will be from right to left (along the negative direction) Therefore, in region II, we have electric field opposing each other.
{tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \left( - \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } - \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side)
In region III -
The electric fields due to both the charged sheets will be from left to right, i.e. along the positive direction. Therefore, in region III, we have electric fields supporting each other
{tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
{tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } + \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex}(along positive direction i.e towards right side)

question_html:
1. Deduce the expression for the torque acting on a dipole of dipole moment p in the presence of uniform electric field E.
  2. Consider two hollow concentric spheres S1 and S2 enclosing charges 2Q and 4Q respectively as shown in the figure.
    1. Find out the ratio of the electric flux through them.
    2. How will the electric flux through the sphere S1 changes, if a medium of dielectric constant {tex}\in_r{/tex} is introduced in the space inside S1 in place of air? Deduce the necessary expression?
question_text: Deduce the expression for the torque acting on a dipole of dipole moment p in the presence of uniform electric field E. Consider two hollow concentric spheres S 1 and S 2 enclosing charges 2Q and 4Q respectively as shown in the figure. Find out the ratio of the electric flux through them. How will the electric flux through the sphere S 1 changes, if a medium of dielectric constant {tex}\in_r{/tex} is introduced in the space inside S 1 in place of air? Deduce the necessary expression?
explanation: Consider an electric dipole consisting of charges -q and +q and of length 2a placed in a uniform electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the field as shown in Fig. Force on charge -q at A = - q {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} (opposite to {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} ) and force on charge +q at B = q {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} (along {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} ) Thus, electric dipole is under the action of two equal and unlike parallel forces, which give rise to a torque on the dipole. The magnitude of the torque is given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = either force {tex}\times{/tex} perpendicular distance between the two forces = q E (AN) = q E (2a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = q (2a) E sin {tex}\theta{/tex} or {tex}\tau{/tex} = p E sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Here, p = q (2a) is electric dipole moment of the electric dipole. The torque on the dipole tends to align it along the direction of the electric field. Since electric dipole moment vector {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}{/tex} is a vector from the charge -q to +q, the above equation may be expressed as {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex} Charge enclosed by sphere S 1 = 2Q Charge enclosed by sphere S 2 = 2Q + 4Q = 6Q Now, using gauss law, electric flux enclosed by sphere S 1 and S 2 is given by {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{2 Q}{\epsilon_{0}} \text { and } \phi_{2}=\frac{6 Q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} Ratio of electric flux is, {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{\frac{2 Q}{\epsilon_{o}}}{\frac{6 Q}{\epsilon_{o}}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} If a medium of dielectric constant ' {tex}\in_r{/tex} ' is introduced in the space inside S 1 in place of air, electric flux becomes {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{\phi_1}{\epsilon_{r}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}<\phi_{1}{/tex} That is, electric flux decreases.
solution: Consider an electric dipole consisting of charges -q and +q and of length 2a placed in a uniform electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the field as shown in Fig. Force on charge -q at A = - q {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} (opposite to {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} ) and force on charge +q at B = q {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} (along {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} ) Thus, electric dipole is under the action of two equal and unlike parallel forces, which give rise to a torque on the dipole. The magnitude of the torque is given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = either force {tex}\times{/tex} perpendicular distance between the two forces = q E (AN) = q E (2a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = q (2a) E sin {tex}\theta{/tex} or {tex}\tau{/tex} = p E sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Here, p = q (2a) is electric dipole moment of the electric dipole. The torque on the dipole tends to align it along the direction of the electric field. Since electric dipole moment vector {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}{/tex} is a vector from the charge -q to +q, the above equation may be expressed as {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex} Charge enclosed by sphere S 1 = 2Q Charge enclosed by sphere S 2 = 2Q + 4Q = 6Q Now, using gauss law, electric flux enclosed by sphere S 1 and S 2 is given by {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{2 Q}{\epsilon_{0}} \text { and } \phi_{2}=\frac{6 Q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} Ratio of electric flux is, {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{\frac{2 Q}{\epsilon_{o}}}{\frac{6 Q}{\epsilon_{o}}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} If a medium of dielectric constant ' {tex}\in_r{/tex} ' is introduced in the space inside S 1 in place of air, electric flux becomes {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{\phi_1}{\epsilon_{r}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}<\phi_{1}{/tex} That is, electric flux decreases.
solution_html:
1. Consider an electric dipole consisting of charges -q and +q and of length 2a placed in a uniform electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the field as shown in Fig.

    Force on charge -q at A = - q{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} (opposite to {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} )
    and force on charge +q at B = q{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} (along {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex})
    Thus, electric dipole is under the action of two equal and unlike parallel forces, which give rise to a torque on the dipole. The magnitude of the torque is given by
    {tex}\tau{/tex} = either force {tex}\times{/tex} perpendicular distance between the two forces
    = q E (AN) = q E (2a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) = q (2a) E sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
    or {tex}\tau{/tex} = p E sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
    Here, p = q (2a) is electric dipole moment of the electric dipole.
    The torque on the dipole tends to align it along the direction of the electric field.
    Since electric dipole moment vector {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}{/tex} is a vector from the charge -q to +q, the above equation may be expressed as
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex}
  2.  
    1. Charge enclosed by sphere S1 = 2Q
      Charge enclosed by sphere S2 = 2Q + 4Q = 6Q
      Now, using gauss law, electric flux enclosed by sphere S1 and S2 is given by
      {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{2 Q}{\epsilon_{0}} \text { and } \phi_{2}=\frac{6 Q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex}
      Ratio of electric flux is,
      {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{\frac{2 Q}{\epsilon_{o}}}{\frac{6 Q}{\epsilon_{o}}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
    2. If a medium of dielectric constant '{tex}\in_r{/tex}' is introduced in the space inside S1 in place of air, electric flux becomes
      {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{\phi_1}{\epsilon_{r}}{/tex}
      Therefore,
      {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}<\phi_{1}{/tex}
      That is, electric flux decreases.

dipole moment, P = q. 2l = {tex}\begin{array}{l}2\times10^{-9}\;\times10^{-2}\;=2\times10^{-11}Cm\\\end{array}{/tex} 
torque {tex}\begin{array}{l}\tau=PE\;\sin\theta=2\times10^{-11}\times E\times\sin\;60\\\end{array}{/tex}

So, {tex}\begin{array}{l}E=6\times10^{11}\;N/C\\\end{array}{/tex}
Potential energy of dipole = -PE cos{tex}\begin{array}{l}\theta\\\end{array}{/tex}{tex}\begin{array}{l}=-\;2\times10^{-11}\times6\times10^{11}\times\cos\;60=-6\;joule\\\end{array}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve. That is, a field cannot have sudden breaks. Why not?
  2. Explain why two field lines never cross each other at any point?
question_text: An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve. That is, a field cannot have sudden breaks. Why not? Explain why two field lines never cross each other at any point?
explanation: An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve because a charge experiences a continuous force when traced in an electrostatic field. The field line cannot have sudden breaks because the charge moves continuously and does not jump from one point to the other. If two field lines cross each other at a point, then electric field intensity will show two directions at that point. This is not possible. Hence, two field lines never cross each other
solution: An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve because a charge experiences a continuous force when traced in an electrostatic field. The field line cannot have sudden breaks because the charge moves continuously and does not jump from one point to the other. If two field lines cross each other at a point, then electric field intensity will show two directions at that point. This is not possible. Hence, two field lines never cross each other
solution_html:
1. An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve because a charge experiences a continuous force when traced in an electrostatic field. The field line cannot have sudden breaks because the charge moves continuously and does not jump from one point to the other.
  2. If two field lines cross each other at a point, then electric field intensity will show two directions at that point. This is not possible. Hence, two field lines never cross each other

question_html: Two equal balls having equal positive charge q coulombs are suspended by two insulating strings of equal length. What would be the effect on the force when a plastic sheet is inserted between the two?
question_text: Two equal balls having equal positive charge q coulombs are suspended by two insulating strings of equal length. What would be the effect on the force when a plastic sheet is inserted between the two?

question_html:
1. A point charge (+Q) is kept in the vicinity of an uncharged conducting plate. Sketch electric field lines between the charge and the plate. 
  2. Two infinitely large plane thin parallel sheets having surface charge densities {tex}\sigma_1{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_2{/tex} ({tex}\sigma_1{/tex} > {tex}\sigma_2{/tex}) are shown in the figure. Write the magnitudes and directions of the fields in the regions marked II and III.
question_text: A point charge (+Q) is kept in the vicinity of an uncharged conducting plate. Sketch electric field lines between the charge and the plate. Two infinitely large plane thin parallel sheets having surface charge densities {tex}\sigma_1{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_2{/tex} ( {tex}\sigma_1{/tex} > {tex}\sigma_2{/tex} ) are shown in the figure. Write the magnitudes and directions of the fields in the regions marked II and III.
explanation: For region II, the electric field due to sheet of charge A will be from left to right and due to the sheet of charge B will be from right to left thus, {tex}E_{I I}=\frac{1}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\sigma_{1}-\sigma_{2}\right){/tex} towards right from sheet A to sheet B. For region III, the electric field due to both charged sheet will be from left to right thus, {tex}E_{I I I}=\frac{1}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\sigma_{1}+\sigma_{2}\right){/tex} towards the right side away from the two sheets.
solution: For region II, the electric field due to sheet of charge A will be from left to right and due to the sheet of charge B will be from right to left thus, {tex}E_{I I}=\frac{1}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\sigma_{1}-\sigma_{2}\right){/tex} towards right from sheet A to sheet B. For region III, the electric field due to both charged sheet will be from left to right thus, {tex}E_{I I I}=\frac{1}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\sigma_{1}+\sigma_{2}\right){/tex} towards the right side away from the two sheets.
solution_html:
1.  
    1. For region II, the electric field due to sheet of charge A will be from left to right and due to the sheet of charge B will be from right to left thus,
      {tex}E_{I I}=\frac{1}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\sigma_{1}-\sigma_{2}\right){/tex}
      towards right from sheet A to sheet B.
    2. For region III, the electric field due to both charged sheet will be from left to right thus, 
      {tex}E_{I I I}=\frac{1}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\sigma_{1}+\sigma_{2}\right){/tex}
       towards the right side away from the two sheets.

question_html: Two concentric metallic spherical shells of radii R and 2R are given charge Q1 and Q2 respectively. The surface charge densities on the outer surfaces of the shells are equal. Determine the ratio Q1:Q2.
question_text: Two concentric metallic spherical shells of radii R and 2R are given charge Q 1 and Q 2 respectively. The surface charge densities on the outer surfaces of the shells are equal. Determine the ratio Q 1 :Q 2 .
explanation: Surface charge density(σ) is Charge per unit area on the surface of any conductor, {tex}\sigma = \frac { Q } { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } }{/tex} According to the question, surface charge density is constant on both the surfaces, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = constant Let Q 1 and Q 2 are two charges. Hence, Charge {tex}Q_1 = 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} .............(i) Charge {tex}Q _ { 2 } = 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} ....... (ii) On dividing Eq. (i) with Eq (ii), we get {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { Q _ { 1 } } { Q _ { 2 } } = \frac { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma } { 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } \sigma } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} So, we can conclude that in order to have uniform surface charge densities on the surfaces of spherical shell, charge Q 1 should be quarter to that of charge Q 2 .
solution: Surface charge density(σ) is Charge per unit area on the surface of any conductor, {tex}\sigma = \frac { Q } { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } }{/tex} According to the question, surface charge density is constant on both the surfaces, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = constant Let Q 1 and Q 2 are two charges. Hence, Charge {tex}Q_1 = 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} .............(i) Charge {tex}Q _ { 2 } = 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} ....... (ii) On dividing Eq. (i) with Eq (ii), we get {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { Q _ { 1 } } { Q _ { 2 } } = \frac { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma } { 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } \sigma } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} So, we can conclude that in order to have uniform surface charge densities on the surfaces of spherical shell, charge Q 1 should be quarter to that of charge Q 2 .
solution_html:
Surface charge density(σ) is Charge per unit area on the surface of any conductor, {tex}\sigma = \frac { Q } { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
According to the question, surface charge density is constant on both the surfaces,
{tex}\sigma{/tex} = constant
Let Q1 and Q2 are two charges.

Hence,
Charge {tex}Q_1 = 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} .............(i)
Charge {tex}Q _ { 2 } = 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} ....... (ii)
On dividing Eq. (i) with Eq (ii), we get
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { Q _ { 1 } } { Q _ { 2 } } = \frac { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma } { 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } \sigma } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}
So, we can conclude that in order to have uniform surface charge densities on the surfaces of spherical shell, charge Q1 should be quarter to that of charge Q2.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two charges of magnitudes -2Q and +Q are located at points (a, 0) and (4a,0), respectively. What is the electric flux due to these charges through a sphere of radius 3a with its centre at the origin?
question_text: Two charges of magnitudes -2Q and +Q are located at points (a, 0) and (4a,0), respectively. What is the electric flux due to these charges through a sphere of radius 3a with its centre at the origin?
explanation: Using Gauss's Law, Flux {tex}(\phi)= {q \over \epsilon _\circ}{/tex} where q is the total charge inside the surface. Here, q = -2Q and gaussian surface is a sphere of radius 3a and centre at origin. So, Flux {tex}(\phi)= {-2Q \over \epsilon _\circ}{/tex} Here, flux is only due to charge -2Q because number of field lines enter the sphere due to charge +Q is equal to number of field lines coming out of the sphere, so net flux is zero due to charge +Q.
solution: Using Gauss's Law, Flux {tex}(\phi)= {q \over \epsilon _\circ}{/tex} where q is the total charge inside the surface. Here, q = -2Q and gaussian surface is a sphere of radius 3a and centre at origin. So, Flux {tex}(\phi)= {-2Q \over \epsilon _\circ}{/tex} Here, flux is only due to charge -2Q because number of field lines enter the sphere due to charge +Q is equal to number of field lines coming out of the sphere, so net flux is zero due to charge +Q.
solution_html:
Using Gauss's Law,
Flux {tex}(\phi)= {q \over \epsilon _\circ}{/tex}
where q is the total charge inside the surface.
Here, q = -2Q and gaussian surface is a sphere of radius 3a and centre at origin.
So, Flux {tex}(\phi)= {-2Q \over \epsilon _\circ}{/tex}
Here, flux is only due to charge -2Q because number of field lines enter the sphere due to charge +Q is equal to number of field lines coming out of the sphere, so net flux is zero due to charge +Q.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A hollow cylindrical box of length 1 m and area of cross-section 25 cm2 is placed in a three-dimensional coordinate system as shown in the figure. The electric field in the region is given by {tex}E = 50 x \hat { i }{/tex}, where E is in NC-1 and x is in metre.
Find

  1. net flux through the cylinder.
  2. charge enclosed by the cylinder.
question_text: A hollow cylindrical box of length 1 m and area of cross-section 25 cm 2 is placed in a three-dimensional coordinate system as shown in the figure. The electric field in the region is given by {tex}E = 50 x \hat { i }{/tex} , where E is in NC -1 and x is in metre. Find net flux through the cylinder. charge enclosed by the cylinder.
explanation: Given, {tex}E = 50 x \hat i{/tex} and {tex}\Delta S = 25 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} As the electric field is only along the X-axis, so flux will pass only through the cross-section of the cylinder. Magnitude of electric field at cross-section A: {tex}E _ { A } = 50 \times 1 = 50 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} (because Distance of first face from the origin is x = 1m) Magnitude of electric field at cross-section B: {tex}E _ { B } = 50 \times 2 = 100 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} ( because Distance of second face from origin is x = 1 + 1 = 2m) The corresponding electric fluxes are {tex}\phi _ { A } = E _ { A } \cdot \Delta S = 50 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= - 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\phi _ { B } = E _ { B } \cdot \Delta S = 100 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= 0.25 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} So, the net flux through the cylinder, {tex}\phi = \phi _ { A } + \phi _ { B } =- 0.125 + 0.25 = 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Using Gauss's law, {tex}\phi = \oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \ 0.125= \frac { q } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q =8.85 \times 0.125 \times 10 ^ { - 12 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q = 1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} So, the charge enclosed by the cylinder is {tex}1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} .
solution: Given, {tex}E = 50 x \hat i{/tex} and {tex}\Delta S = 25 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} As the electric field is only along the X-axis, so flux will pass only through the cross-section of the cylinder. Magnitude of electric field at cross-section A: {tex}E _ { A } = 50 \times 1 = 50 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} (because Distance of first face from the origin is x = 1m) Magnitude of electric field at cross-section B: {tex}E _ { B } = 50 \times 2 = 100 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} ( because Distance of second face from origin is x = 1 + 1 = 2m) The corresponding electric fluxes are {tex}\phi _ { A } = E _ { A } \cdot \Delta S = 50 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= - 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\phi _ { B } = E _ { B } \cdot \Delta S = 100 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= 0.25 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} So, the net flux through the cylinder, {tex}\phi = \phi _ { A } + \phi _ { B } =- 0.125 + 0.25 = 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Using Gauss's law, {tex}\phi = \oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \ 0.125= \frac { q } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q =8.85 \times 0.125 \times 10 ^ { - 12 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q = 1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} So, the charge enclosed by the cylinder is {tex}1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given, {tex}E = 50 x \hat i{/tex}
and {tex}\Delta S = 25 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

As the electric field is only along the X-axis, so flux will pass only through the cross-section of the cylinder.
Magnitude of electric field at cross-section A:
{tex}E _ { A } = 50 \times 1 = 50 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}  (because Distance of first face from the origin is x = 1m)
Magnitude of electric field at cross-section B:
{tex}E _ { B } = 50 \times 2 = 100 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}  ( because Distance of second face from origin is x = 1 + 1 = 2m)
The corresponding electric fluxes are
{tex}\phi _ { A } = E _ { A } \cdot \Delta S = 50 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}= - 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
and  {tex}\phi _ { B } = E _ { B } \cdot \Delta S = 100 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}= 0.25 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}

  1. So, the net flux through the cylinder,
    {tex}\phi = \phi _ { A } + \phi _ { B } =- 0.125 + 0.25 = 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
  2. Using Gauss's law,
    {tex}\phi = \oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \ 0.125= \frac { q } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q =8.85 \times 0.125 \times 10 ^ { - 12 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q = 1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}
    So, the charge enclosed by the cylinder is {tex}1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}.

question_text: Two dipoles, made from charges {tex}\pm{/tex} q and {tex}\pm{/tex} Q respectively, have equal dipole moments. Give the the ratio between the 'separations' of these two pairs of charges, the angle between the dipole axes of these two dipoles.

If the distance between two charges of dipoles be I 1 and I 2 respectively, then dipole moments of dipoles will be qxl 1 and Qxl 2 (because dipole moment=q x length between charges) The ratio between the separations of two pairs of charges q, Q qxl 1 = Qxl 2 or, {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{Q}{q}{/tex} for equilibrium, the angle between dipole axes of two dipoles will be zero.
