solution_html:
As qa = Qa', we have
{tex}\frac{a}{a^{\prime}}=\frac{Q}{q}{/tex} .
If the distance between two charges of dipoles be I1 and I2 respectively, then dipole moments of dipoles will be qxl1 and Qxl2 (because dipole moment=q x length  between charges)

  1. The ratio between the separations of two pairs of charges q, Q
    qxl1 = Qxl2
    or, {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{Q}{q}{/tex}
  2.  for equilibrium, the angle between dipole axes of two dipoles will be zero.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the term electric flux. Write its S.I. unit.
question_text: Define the term electric flux . Write its S.I. unit.
explanation: The electric flux through an area is the product of the magnitude of the area and the component of the electric field vector normal to it. {tex}\phi=\bigtriangleup S(Ecos \theta){/tex} {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} Or, Electric flux is defined as the total number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the field. Or, Electric flux is the property of an electric field which may be thought of as a number of electric lines of force that passes through a given area The S.I. unit of 'electric flux' is NC -1 m 2 .
solution: The electric flux through an area is the product of the magnitude of the area and the component of the electric field vector normal to it. {tex}\phi=\bigtriangleup S(Ecos \theta){/tex} {tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex} Or, Electric flux is defined as the total number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the field. Or, Electric flux is the property of an electric field which may be thought of as a number of electric lines of force that passes through a given area The S.I. unit of 'electric flux' is NC -1 m 2 .
solution_html:
The electric flux through an area is the product of the magnitude of the area and the component of the electric field vector normal to it.
{tex}\phi=\bigtriangleup S(Ecos \theta){/tex}
{tex}\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}{/tex}
Or, Electric flux is defined as the total number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the field.
Or, Electric flux is the property of an electric field which may be thought of as a number of electric lines of force that passes through a given area 
The S.I. unit of 'electric flux' is NC-1m2.

question_html:
1. Define electric dipole moment. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity? Derive the expression for the electric field of a dipole at a point on the equatorial plane of the dipole.
  2. Draw the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole. Locate the points where the potential due to the dipole is zero.
question_text: Define electric dipole moment. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity? Derive the expression for the electric field of a dipole at a point on the equatorial plane of the dipole. Draw the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole. Locate the points where the potential due to the dipole is zero.
explanation: Electric dipole moment: The strength of an electric dipole is measured by the quantity of electric dipole moment. Its magnitude is equal to the product of the magnitude of either charge and the distance between the two charges. or Electric dipole consists of equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance. Electric dipole moment, p = q {tex}\times{/tex} 2a It is a vector quantity. In vector form, it is written as {tex}\vec{p}=q \times 2 \vec{a} \hat{p}{/tex} where the direction of {tex}2 \vec{a} \hat{p}{/tex} is from a negative charge to a positive charge. The electric field of the dipole at points on the equatorial plane: The magnitudes of the electric field due to the two charges +q and -q are given by, {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{E}-q|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex} The direction of {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right| \text { and }\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex} are shown in the figure. The components normal to the dipole axis cancel out. The components along the dipole axis add up {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total electric field {tex}\vec{E}=-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos \theta \hat{p}{/tex} [Negative sign shows that field is opposite to p] {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{-2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} ...(i) At large distances (r >>a) this reduces to {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{-2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}} \hat{p}{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{E}-q|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex} {tex}\because \quad \vec{p}=2 q a \hat{p}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \vec{E}=\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}{/tex} Equipotential surface due to electric dipole : The potential due to the dipole is zero at the line bisecting the dipole length. Equipotential surfaces are that at every point of which electric potential is the same.
solution: Electric dipole moment: The strength of an electric dipole is measured by the quantity of electric dipole moment. Its magnitude is equal to the product of the magnitude of either charge and the distance between the two charges. or Electric dipole consists of equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance. Electric dipole moment, p = q {tex}\times{/tex} 2a It is a vector quantity. In vector form, it is written as {tex}\vec{p}=q \times 2 \vec{a} \hat{p}{/tex} where the direction of {tex}2 \vec{a} \hat{p}{/tex} is from a negative charge to a positive charge. The electric field of the dipole at points on the equatorial plane: The magnitudes of the electric field due to the two charges +q and -q are given by, {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{E}-q|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex} The direction of {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right| \text { and }\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex} are shown in the figure. The components normal to the dipole axis cancel out. The components along the dipole axis add up {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total electric field {tex}\vec{E}=-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos \theta \hat{p}{/tex} [Negative sign shows that field is opposite to p] {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{-2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} ...(i) At large distances (r >>a) this reduces to {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{-2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}} \hat{p}{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{E}-q|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex} {tex}\because \quad \vec{p}=2 q a \hat{p}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \vec{E}=\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}{/tex} Equipotential surface due to electric dipole : The potential due to the dipole is zero at the line bisecting the dipole length. Equipotential surfaces are that at every point of which electric potential is the same.

1. Electric dipole moment: The strength of an electric dipole is measured by the quantity of electric dipole moment. Its magnitude is equal to the product of the magnitude of either charge and the distance between the two charges.

    The electric field of the dipole at points on the equatorial plane:

    The magnitudes of the electric field due to the two charges +q and -q are given by,
    {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}|\vec{E}-q|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex}
    The direction of {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right| \text { and }\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex} are shown in the figure. The components normal to the dipole axis cancel out. The components along the dipole axis add up
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total electric field
    {tex}\vec{E}=-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos \theta \hat{p}{/tex}
    [Negative sign shows that field is opposite to p]
    {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{-2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} ...(i)
    At large distances (r >>a)  this reduces to
    {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{-2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}} \hat{p}{/tex}
    {tex}\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}|\vec{E}-q|=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad\left|\vec{E}_{+q}\right|=\left|\vec{E}_{-q}\right|{/tex}
    {tex}\because \quad \vec{p}=2 q a \hat{p}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \vec{E}=\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}{/tex}
  2. Equipotential surface due to electric dipole :

    The potential due to the dipole is zero at the line bisecting the dipole length. Equipotential surfaces are that at every point of which electric potential is the same.

question_html:
1. An infinitely long positively charged wire has a linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} Cm-1. An electron is revolving around the wire as its centre with a constant velocity in a circular plane perpendicular to the wire. Deduce the expression for its kinetic energy.
  2. Plot a graph of the kinetic energy as a function of charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
question_text: An infinitely long positively charged wire has a linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} Cm -1 . An electron is revolving around the wire as its centre with a constant velocity in a circular plane perpendicular to the wire. Deduce the expression for its kinetic energy. Plot a graph of the kinetic energy as a function of charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
explanation: The electrostatic force exerted by the line charge on the electron provides the centripetal force for the revolution of the electron. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force exerted by electric field = Centripetal force eE = {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} Here v is the orbital velocity of the electron But E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{e \lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} or v 2 = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m}{/tex} Kinetic energy of the electron will be E k = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} As E k {tex}\propto \lambda{/tex} , the graph of kinetic energe E k vs. charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} will be straight line as shown in Fig.
solution: The electrostatic force exerted by the line charge on the electron provides the centripetal force for the revolution of the electron. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force exerted by electric field = Centripetal force eE = {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} Here v is the orbital velocity of the electron But E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{e \lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} or v 2 = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m}{/tex} Kinetic energy of the electron will be E k = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} As E k {tex}\propto \lambda{/tex} , the graph of kinetic energe E k vs. charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} will be straight line as shown in Fig.
solution_html:
1. The electrostatic force exerted by the line charge on the electron provides the centripetal force for the revolution of the electron.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force exerted by electric field = Centripetal force
    eE = {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex}
    Here v is the orbital velocity of the electron
    But E = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{e \lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} or v2 = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m}{/tex}
    Kinetic energy of the electron will be
    Ek = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv2 = {tex}\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
  2. As Ek {tex}\propto \lambda{/tex}, the graph of kinetic energe Ek vs. charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} will be straight line as shown in Fig.

question_html: Two charges of magnitudes -2Q and +Q are located at points (a, 0) and (4a, 0) respectively. What is the electric flux due to these charges through a sphere of radius 3a with the center at the origin?
question_text: Two charges of magnitudes -2Q and +Q are located at points (a, 0) and (4a, 0) respectively. What is the electric flux due to these charges through a sphere of radius 3a with the center at the origin?
explanation: It follows that the sphere of radius 3a will enclose the charge - 2Q only, which is located at the point (a, 0). The charge -Q located at the point (4a, 0) is outside the sphere. Therefore, according to Gauss' theorem, the electric flux due to the given charges through the sphere, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{-2 Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

question_html: Define dipole moment of an electric dipole. Is it a scalar quantity or a vector quantity?
question_text: Define dipole moment of an electric dipole. Is it a scalar quantity or a vector quantity?
explanation: The electric dipole moment of an electric dipole is equal to the product of its either charge and the length of the electric dipole. It is denoted by p. Its SI unit is coulomb-metre. It is a vector quantity and its direction is from a negative charge to positive charge.
solution: The electric dipole moment of an electric dipole is equal to the product of its either charge and the length of the electric dipole. It is denoted by p. Its SI unit is coulomb-metre. It is a vector quantity and its direction is from a negative charge to positive charge.
solution_html:
The electric dipole moment of an electric dipole is equal to the product of its either charge and the length of the electric dipole. It is denoted by p. Its SI unit is coulomb-metre.
It is a vector quantity and its direction is from a negative charge to positive charge.

question_html: How Electric field intensity (E) is related with distance 'r' for a point charge 'q'?
question_text: How Electric field intensity (E) is related with distance 'r' for a point charge 'q'?
explanation: The electric field produced by the charge q at a point r is given as E(r) = {tex}\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac q{r^2}\widehat r{/tex} where {tex}\widehat r{/tex} is a unit vector from the origin to the point r.
solution: The electric field produced by the charge q at a point r is given as E(r) = {tex}\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac q{r^2}\widehat r{/tex} where {tex}\widehat r{/tex} is a unit vector from the origin to the point r.
solution_html:
The electric field produced by the charge q at a point r is given as
E(r) = {tex}\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac q{r^2}\widehat r{/tex}
where {tex}\widehat r{/tex} is a unit vector from the origin to the point r.

subject: Physics
question_html:
State Gauss's law.
A thin straight infintely long conducting wire of linear charge density {tex}\lambda {/tex} is enclosed by a cylindrical surface of radius r and length l. Its axis coinciding with the length of the wire. Obtain the expression for the electric field, indicating its direction, at a point on the surface of the cylinder.
question_text: State Gauss's law. A thin straight infintely long conducting wire of linear charge density {tex}\lambda {/tex} is enclosed by a cylindrical surface of radius r and length l. Its axis coinciding with the length of the wire. Obtain the expression for the electric field, indicating its direction, at a point on the surface of the cylinder.
explanation: Gauss' law states that the total flux through a closed surface is {tex}\frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} times to the net charge enclosed by the closed surface. Mathematically, {tex}\phi _ { E } = \oint _ { s } \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} . Here, {tex}\varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} is the absolute permittivity of the free spaced, q is the total charge enclosed and E is the electric field at the area element dS. Consider a thin cylindrical Gaussian surface S with charged wire on its axis and point P on its surface. Then net electric flux through surface S is {tex}\phi = \oint_s {E \cdot ds} = \mathop {\int E dS\cos {{90}^\circ }}\limits_{Upper\,plane\,face} + \mathop {\int E dS\cos {0^ \circ }}\limits_{Curved\,surface} + \mathop {\int E dS\cos {{90}^\circ }}\limits_{Lower\,plane\,face} {/tex} {tex}\phi = 0 + E A + 0 \text { or } \phi = E \cdot 2 \pi l{/tex} But by Gauss's theorem, {tex}\phi = q / \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \lambda l / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} where, q is the charge on length l of wire enclosed by cylindrical surface S and {tex}\lambda {/tex} is uniform linear charge density of wire. {tex}\therefore \quad E \times 2 \pi l = \frac { \lambda l } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { \lambda } { 2 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} Thus, electric field of a line charge is inversely proportional to distance directed normal to the surface of charged wire.
solution: Gauss' law states that the total flux through a closed surface is {tex}\frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} times to the net charge enclosed by the closed surface. Mathematically, {tex}\phi _ { E } = \oint _ { s } \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} . Here, {tex}\varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} is the absolute permittivity of the free spaced, q is the total charge enclosed and E is the electric field at the area element dS. Consider a thin cylindrical Gaussian surface S with charged wire on its axis and point P on its surface. Then net electric flux through surface S is {tex}\phi = \oint_s {E \cdot ds} = \mathop {\int E dS\cos {{90}^\circ }}\limits_{Upper\,plane\,face} + \mathop {\int E dS\cos {0^ \circ }}\limits_{Curved\,surface} + \mathop {\int E dS\cos {{90}^\circ }}\limits_{Lower\,plane\,face} {/tex} {tex}\phi = 0 + E A + 0 \text { or } \phi = E \cdot 2 \pi l{/tex} But by Gauss's theorem, {tex}\phi = q / \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \lambda l / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} where, q is the charge on length l of wire enclosed by cylindrical surface S and {tex}\lambda {/tex} is uniform linear charge density of wire. {tex}\therefore \quad E \times 2 \pi l = \frac { \lambda l } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { \lambda } { 2 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} Thus, electric field of a line charge is inversely proportional to distance directed normal to the surface of charged wire.
solution_html:
Gauss' law states that the total flux through a closed surface is {tex}\frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} times to the net charge enclosed by the closed surface. Mathematically, {tex}\phi _ { E } = \oint _ { s } \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}.
Here, {tex}\varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} is the absolute permittivity of the free spaced, q is the total charge enclosed and E is the electric field at the area element dS.

Consider a thin cylindrical Gaussian surface S with charged wire on its axis and point P on its surface.

Then net electric flux through surface S is
{tex}\phi = \oint_s {E \cdot ds} = \mathop {\int E dS\cos {{90}^\circ }}\limits_{Upper\,plane\,face} + \mathop {\int E dS\cos {0^ \circ }}\limits_{Curved\,surface} + \mathop {\int E dS\cos {{90}^\circ }}\limits_{Lower\,plane\,face} {/tex} 
{tex}\phi = 0 + E A + 0 \text { or } \phi = E \cdot 2 \pi l{/tex}
But by Gauss's theorem, {tex}\phi = q / \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \lambda l / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} where, q is the charge on length l of wire enclosed by cylindrical surface S and {tex}\lambda {/tex} is uniform linear charge density of wire.
{tex}\therefore \quad E \times 2 \pi l = \frac { \lambda l } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { \lambda } { 2 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex}
Thus, electric field of a line charge is inversely proportional to distance directed normal to the surface of charged wire.

subject: Physics
question_html: The electric field intensity within a conductor is always zero. Why?
question_text: The electric field intensity within a conductor is always zero. Why?
explanation: The electric lines of force cannot pass through a conductor. As a result, there can be no electric field inside a conductor. Hence, the electric field intensity inside a conductor is zero.
solution: The electric lines of force cannot pass through a conductor. As a result, there can be no electric field inside a conductor. Hence, the electric field intensity inside a conductor is zero.

question_html: A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube of side I. What is the electric flux passing through two opposite faces of the cube?
question_text: A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube of side I. What is the electric flux passing through two opposite faces of the cube?
explanation: The electric flux due to the charge q through all the six faces of the cube, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Therefore, electric flux through the two opposite faces, {tex}\phi^{\prime}=\frac{2}{6} \times \phi=\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution: The electric flux due to the charge q through all the six faces of the cube, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Therefore, electric flux through the two opposite faces, {tex}\phi^{\prime}=\frac{2}{6} \times \phi=\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The electric flux due to the charge q through all the six faces of the cube,
{tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
Therefore, electric flux through the two opposite faces,
{tex}\phi^{\prime}=\frac{2}{6} \times \phi=\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{q}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

question_html:
Define electric flux. Write its SI unit.
A small metal sphere carrying charge +Q is located at the centre of a spherical cavity inside a large uncharged metallic spherical shell as shown in the figure. Use Gauss' law to find the expressions for the electric field at points P1 and P2.
question_text: Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. A small metal sphere carrying charge +Q is located at the centre of a spherical cavity inside a large uncharged metallic spherical shell as shown in the figure. Use Gauss' law to find the expressions for the electric field at points P 1 and P 2 .
explanation: Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the surface. Alternatively, it is defined as surface integral of the electric field is defined as the electric flux through a closed surface {tex}\phi=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} SI unit: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} or volt metre Let point P 1 is at a distance R from the centre O. S 1 is the Gaussian surface, then according to Gauss's theorem {tex}\oint_{s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{E} \oint_{\mathrm{s}} d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}E=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0} \times \oint_{s} d s}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R^{2}} \quad\left[\text { As } \oint_{s} d s=4 \pi R^{2}\right]{/tex} Inside the shell, the net charge is zero, so the field is also zero. Hence, the electric field at point P 2 will be zero.
solution: Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the surface. Alternatively, it is defined as surface integral of the electric field is defined as the electric flux through a closed surface {tex}\phi=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} SI unit: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} or volt metre Let point P 1 is at a distance R from the centre O. S 1 is the Gaussian surface, then according to Gauss's theorem {tex}\oint_{s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{E} \oint_{\mathrm{s}} d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}E=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0} \times \oint_{s} d s}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R^{2}} \quad\left[\text { As } \oint_{s} d s=4 \pi R^{2}\right]{/tex} Inside the shell, the net charge is zero, so the field is also zero. Hence, the electric field at point P 2 will be zero.
solution_html:
Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the surface.
Alternatively, it is defined as surface integral of the electric field is defined as the electric flux through a closed surface
{tex}\phi=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex}
SI unit: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} or volt metre
Let point P1 is at a distance R from the centre O. S1 is the Gaussian surface, then according to Gauss's theorem
{tex}\oint_{s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
or {tex}\mathrm{E} \oint_{\mathrm{s}} d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
or {tex}E=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0} \times \oint_{s} d s}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R^{2}} \quad\left[\text { As } \oint_{s} d s=4 \pi R^{2}\right]{/tex}

Inside the shell, the net charge is zero, so the field is also zero. Hence, the electric field at point P2 will be zero.

question_html:
State Gauss' law in electrostatics. A cube with each side 'a' is kept in an electric field given by {tex}E = C x \hat { i }{/tex} as shown in the figure, where C is a positive dimensional constant. Find out

  1. the electric flux through the cube.
  2. the net charge inside the cube.
question_text: State Gauss' law in electrostatics. A cube with each side 'a' is kept in an electric field given by {tex}E = C x \hat { i }{/tex} as shown in the figure, where C is a positive dimensional constant. Find out the electric flux through the cube. the net charge inside the cube.
explanation: Gauss's Law: It states that the total electric flux crossing through a closed surface is equal to times the total charge contained inside the closed surface. {tex}E = C x \hat { i }{/tex} Electric field at left face of cube = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E_1=Ca\widehat i\\\end{array}{/tex} Electric field at the right surface = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E_2=2Ca\widehat i\\\end{array}{/tex} Electric flux through left surface of cube = E 1 A cos 180 = -E 1 A = - Ca.a 2 = -Ca 3 units Electric flux through right surface of cube = E 2 A cos 0 = E 2 A = 2Ca.a 2 = 2Ca 3 units Net flux through the cube = 2Ca 3 - Ca 3 = Ca 3 units Using gauss law net flux inside the cube = {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac q{\varepsilon_0}\\\end{array}{/tex} From (i), {tex}\begin{array}{l}q\;=Ca^3\varepsilon_0\\\end{array} {/tex} This is the net charge inside the cube.
solution: Gauss's Law: It states that the total electric flux crossing through a closed surface is equal to times the total charge contained inside the closed surface. {tex}E = C x \hat { i }{/tex} Electric field at left face of cube = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E_1=Ca\widehat i\\\end{array}{/tex} Electric field at the right surface = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E_2=2Ca\widehat i\\\end{array}{/tex} Electric flux through left surface of cube = E 1 A cos 180 = -E 1 A = - Ca.a 2 = -Ca 3 units Electric flux through right surface of cube = E 2 A cos 0 = E 2 A = 2Ca.a 2 = 2Ca 3 units Net flux through the cube = 2Ca 3 - Ca 3 = Ca 3 units Using gauss law net flux inside the cube = {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac q{\varepsilon_0}\\\end{array}{/tex} From (i), {tex}\begin{array}{l}q\;=Ca^3\varepsilon_0\\\end{array} {/tex} This is the net charge inside the cube.
solution_html:
Gauss's Law: It states that the total electric flux crossing through a closed surface is equal to times the total charge contained inside the closed surface.







{tex}E = C x \hat { i }{/tex}
Electric field at left face of cube = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E_1=Ca\widehat i\\\end{array}{/tex}
Electric field at the right surface = {tex}\begin{array}{l}E_2=2Ca\widehat i\\\end{array}{/tex}
Electric flux through left surface of cube = E1A cos 180 = -E1A = - Ca.a2 = -Ca3 units
Electric flux through right surface of cube = E2A cos 0 = E2 A = 2Ca.a2 = 2Ca3 units

  1. Net flux through the cube = 2Ca3 - Ca3 = Ca3 units
  2. Using gauss law net flux inside the cube = {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac q{\varepsilon_0}\\\end{array}{/tex}
    From (i),
    {tex}\begin{array}{l}q\;=Ca^3\varepsilon_0\\\end{array} {/tex} 
    This is the net charge inside the cube.

question_html:
1. Using Gauss's law, prove that the electric field at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is independent of the distance from it.
  2. How is the field directed if
    1. the sheet is positively charged,
    2. negatively charged?
question_text: Using Gauss's law, prove that the electric field at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is independent of the distance from it. How is the field directed if the sheet is positively charged, negatively charged?
explanation: Electric field near the surface of an infinite sheet of positive charge is directed away from the sheet of charge. Consider a cylindrical gaussian surface with its axis perpendicular to the sheet of charge as shown in the figure. The net flux through the curved surface is zero as the electric field and surface are parallel to each other. Hence, {tex}\phi{/tex} curved = {tex}\int{/tex} E.dS = 0.The net flux through the upper plane surface is given by: {tex}\phi_{pu}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} E.dS = EA. Similarly, The net flux through the lower plane surface is given by: {tex}\phi_{lu}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} E.dS = EA. Net flux through the closed cylindrical surface is: {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\phi{/tex} curved + {tex}\phi_{pu}{/tex} + {tex}\phi_{lu}{/tex} = 2EA ...(i) By Gauss' Law, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii), E = {tex}\frac{\sigma 2}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Hence, the electric field at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is independent of the distance from it. If the sheet is positively charged, field is directed away from the sheet of charge. If the sheet is negatively charged, field is directed towards the sheet of charge.
solution: Electric field near the surface of an infinite sheet of positive charge is directed away from the sheet of charge. Consider a cylindrical gaussian surface with its axis perpendicular to the sheet of charge as shown in the figure. The net flux through the curved surface is zero as the electric field and surface are parallel to each other. Hence, {tex}\phi{/tex} curved = {tex}\int{/tex} E.dS = 0.The net flux through the upper plane surface is given by: {tex}\phi_{pu}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} E.dS = EA. Similarly, The net flux through the lower plane surface is given by: {tex}\phi_{lu}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} E.dS = EA. Net flux through the closed cylindrical surface is: {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\phi{/tex} curved + {tex}\phi_{pu}{/tex} + {tex}\phi_{lu}{/tex} = 2EA ...(i) By Gauss' Law, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii), E = {tex}\frac{\sigma 2}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Hence, the electric field at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is independent of the distance from it. If the sheet is positively charged, field is directed away from the sheet of charge. If the sheet is negatively charged, field is directed towards the sheet of charge.
solution_html:
1. Electric field near the surface of an infinite sheet of positive charge is directed away from the sheet of charge.
    Consider a cylindrical gaussian surface with its axis perpendicular to the sheet of charge as shown in the figure. The net flux through the curved surface is zero as the electric field and surface are parallel to each other.
    Hence, {tex}\phi{/tex}curved = {tex}\int{/tex}E.dS = 0.The net flux through the upper plane surface is given by: {tex}\phi_{pu}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex}E.dS = EA.
    Similarly, The net flux through the lower plane surface is given by: {tex}\phi_{lu}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex}E.dS = EA.
    Net flux through the closed cylindrical surface is: {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\phi{/tex}curved + {tex}\phi_{pu}{/tex} + {tex}\phi_{lu}{/tex} = 2EA ...(i)
    By Gauss' Law, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q_{enclosed}}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(ii)
    From (i) and (ii),
    E = {tex}\frac{\sigma 2}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Hence, the electric field at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is independent of the distance from it.
    1. If the sheet is positively charged, field is directed away from the sheet of charge.
    2. If the sheet is negatively charged, field is directed towards the sheet of charge.

question_html: A thin conducting spherical shell of radius R has charge +q spread uniformly over its surface. Using Gauss's law, derive an expression for an electric field at a point outside the shell. Draw a graph of electric field E(r) with distance r from the centre of the shell for {tex}0 \leqslant r \leqslant \infty{/tex}.
question_text: A thin conducting spherical shell of radius R has charge +q spread uniformly over its surface. Using Gauss's law, derive an expression for an electric field at a point outside the shell. Draw a graph of electric field E(r) with distance r from the centre of the shell for {tex}0 \leqslant r \leqslant \infty{/tex} .
explanation: Electric field intensity at any point outside a uniformly charged spherical shell: Assume a thin spherical shell of radius R with centre O. Let charge +q is uniformly distributed over the surface of the shell. Let P be any point on the Gaussian surface sphere S 1 with centre O and radius r (r > R). According to Gauss's law. {tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow d s = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow \oint\limits_E {\overrightarrow E \cdot \hat n} \,ds = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E\oint {ds} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow E \cdot 4\pi {r^2} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {E_r} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{q}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} At any point on the surface of the shell, r = R {tex}\therefore {E_R} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{q}{{{R^2}}}{/tex} If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is charge density, {tex}\therefore q = 4\pi {R^2}\sigma {/tex} {tex}\therefore {E_R} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{4\pi {R^2}\sigma }}{{{R^2}}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{E_R} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Graph: As the charge on shell reside on the outer surface, so there is no charge inside the shell, so electric field by Gauss's law will be zero. So inside shell, r < R q = 0 or {tex}\sigma = 0{/tex} {tex}E = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Therefore, E = 0 The variation of the electric field intensity E(r) with distance r from the centre for shell {tex}0 \leqslant r \leqslant \infty{/tex} is shown below.
solution: Electric field intensity at any point outside a uniformly charged spherical shell: Assume a thin spherical shell of radius R with centre O. Let charge +q is uniformly distributed over the surface of the shell. Let P be any point on the Gaussian surface sphere S 1 with centre O and radius r (r > R). According to Gauss's law. {tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow d s = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow \oint\limits_E {\overrightarrow E \cdot \hat n} \,ds = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E\oint {ds} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow E \cdot 4\pi {r^2} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {E_r} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{q}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} At any point on the surface of the shell, r = R {tex}\therefore {E_R} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{q}{{{R^2}}}{/tex} If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is charge density, {tex}\therefore q = 4\pi {R^2}\sigma {/tex} {tex}\therefore {E_R} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{4\pi {R^2}\sigma }}{{{R^2}}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{E_R} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Graph: As the charge on shell reside on the outer surface, so there is no charge inside the shell, so electric field by Gauss's law will be zero. So inside shell, r < R q = 0 or {tex}\sigma = 0{/tex} {tex}E = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Therefore, E = 0 The variation of the electric field intensity E(r) with distance r from the centre for shell {tex}0 \leqslant r \leqslant \infty{/tex} is shown below.
solution_html:
Electric field intensity at any point outside a uniformly charged spherical shell:
Assume a thin spherical shell of radius R with centre O. Let charge +q is uniformly distributed over the surface of the shell.

Let P be any point on the Gaussian surface sphere S1 with centre O and radius r (r > R). According to Gauss's law.
{tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow d s = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow \oint\limits_E {\overrightarrow E \cdot \hat n} \,ds = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore E\oint {ds} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow E \cdot 4\pi {r^2} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {E_r} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{q}{{{r^2}}}{/tex}
At any point on the surface of the shell, r = R
{tex}\therefore {E_R} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{q}{{{R^2}}}{/tex}
If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is charge density,
{tex}\therefore q = 4\pi {R^2}\sigma {/tex}
{tex}\therefore {E_R} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{4\pi {R^2}\sigma }}{{{R^2}}}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}{E_R} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
Graph: As the charge on shell reside on the outer surface, so there is no charge inside the shell, so electric field by Gauss's law will be zero.
So inside shell, r < R
q = 0 or {tex}\sigma = 0{/tex}
{tex}E = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
Therefore, E = 0
The variation of the electric field intensity E(r) with distance r from the centre for shell {tex}0 \leqslant r \leqslant \infty{/tex}  is shown below.

question_html: A thin straight infinitely long conducting wire having charge density {tex}\lambda {/tex} is enclosed by a cylindrical surface of radius r and length l, its axis coinciding with the length of the wire. Find the expression for the electric flux through the surface of the cylinder.
question_text: A thin straight infinitely long conducting wire having charge density {tex}\lambda {/tex} is enclosed by a cylindrical surface of radius r and length l, its axis coinciding with the length of the wire. Find the expression for the electric flux through the surface of the cylinder.
explanation: A thin straight conducting wire will be a uniform linear charge distribution. Let q charge be enclosed by the cylindrical surface. Gauss' law states that electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. Linear charge density, {tex}\lambda = \frac { q } { l }{/tex} {tex}q = \lambda l{/tex} ....... (i) By Gauss' theorem, {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total electric flux through the surface of cylinder {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [Gauss' theorem] {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \frac { \lambda l } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)]
solution: A thin straight conducting wire will be a uniform linear charge distribution. Let q charge be enclosed by the cylindrical surface. Gauss' law states that electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. Linear charge density, {tex}\lambda = \frac { q } { l }{/tex} {tex}q = \lambda l{/tex} ....... (i) By Gauss' theorem, {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total electric flux through the surface of cylinder {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [Gauss' theorem] {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \frac { \lambda l } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)]
solution_html:
A thin straight conducting wire will be a uniform linear charge distribution. Let q charge be enclosed by the cylindrical surface.

Gauss' law states that electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum.
Linear charge density,
{tex}\lambda = \frac { q } { l }{/tex}
{tex}q = \lambda l{/tex} ....... (i)
By Gauss' theorem,
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Total electric flux through the surface of cylinder
{tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [Gauss' theorem]
{tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \frac { \lambda l } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)]

question_html: Calculate the amount of work done in turning an electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}3 \times {10^{ - 8}}{/tex} Cm from its position of unstable equilibrium to the position of stable equilibrium in a uniform electric field of intensity 103 NC-1.
question_text: Calculate the amount of work done in turning an electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}3 \times {10^{ - 8}}{/tex} Cm from its position of unstable equilibrium to the position of stable equilibrium in a uniform electric field of intensity 10 3 NC -1 .
explanation: According to the question, for unstable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is because potential energy at this orientation is maximum {tex}\theta _ { 1 } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Finally, for stable equilibrium, {tex}\theta _ { 2 } = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Required work done can be found by calculating the difference in potential energy of the dipole in these two conditions. Thus, {tex}W = p E \left( \cos \theta _ { 1 } - \cos \theta _ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 3 \times 10 ^ { - 8 } \times 10 ^ { 3 } \left( \cos 180 ^ { \circ } - \cos 0 ^ { \circ } \right){/tex} {tex}= - 6 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } {/tex} J This work done is negative which shows that the decrease in potential energy of the dipole will be converted into work.
solution: According to the question, for unstable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is because potential energy at this orientation is maximum {tex}\theta _ { 1 } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Finally, for stable equilibrium, {tex}\theta _ { 2 } = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Required work done can be found by calculating the difference in potential energy of the dipole in these two conditions. Thus, {tex}W = p E \left( \cos \theta _ { 1 } - \cos \theta _ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 3 \times 10 ^ { - 8 } \times 10 ^ { 3 } \left( \cos 180 ^ { \circ } - \cos 0 ^ { \circ } \right){/tex} {tex}= - 6 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } {/tex} J This work done is negative which shows that the decrease in potential energy of the dipole will be converted into work.
solution_html:
According to the question, for unstable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is because potential energy at this orientation is maximum {tex}\theta _ { 1 } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Finally, for stable equilibrium, {tex}\theta _ { 2 } = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Required work done can be found by calculating the difference in potential energy of the dipole in these two conditions. Thus,
{tex}W = p E \left( \cos \theta _ { 1 } - \cos \theta _ { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= 3 \times 10 ^ { - 8 } \times 10 ^ { 3 } \left( \cos 180 ^ { \circ } - \cos 0 ^ { \circ } \right){/tex}
{tex}= - 6 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } {/tex} J
This work done is negative which shows that the decrease in potential energy of the dipole will be converted into work.

question_html: Plot a graph showing the variation of Coulomb force (F) versus 1/r2, where r is the distance between the two charges of each pair of charges (1µC, 2µC) and (1µC, -3µC). Interpret the graphs obtained.
question_text: Plot a graph showing the variation of Coulomb force (F) versus 1/r 2 , where r is the distance between the two charges of each pair of charges (1µC, 2µC) and (1µC, -3µC). Interpret the graphs obtained.
explanation: As per Coulomb's law, the force acting between two stationary point charges will be given by {tex}F = \left( \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For fixed charges q 1 and q 2 , {tex}F \propto \left( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} The slope of F versus {tex}\frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} graph depends on q 1 and q 2 . Magnitude of force is higher for the second pair as higher the product of charges more will be the force at fixed distance r according to Coulomb's law. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Slope of F versus {tex}\frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} graph corresponding to second pair (1µC,- 3µC) is greater. Higher the magnitude of product of charges q 1 and q 2 , higher the slope of the graph between F and 1/r 2 .
solution: As per Coulomb's law, the force acting between two stationary point charges will be given by {tex}F = \left( \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For fixed charges q 1 and q 2 , {tex}F \propto \left( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} The slope of F versus {tex}\frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} graph depends on q 1 and q 2 . Magnitude of force is higher for the second pair as higher the product of charges more will be the force at fixed distance r according to Coulomb's law. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Slope of F versus {tex}\frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} graph corresponding to second pair (1µC,- 3µC) is greater. Higher the magnitude of product of charges q 1 and q 2 , higher the slope of the graph between F and 1/r 2 .
solution_html:
As per Coulomb's law, the force acting between two stationary point charges will be given by
{tex}F = \left( \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
For fixed charges q1 and q2,
{tex}F \propto \left( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
The slope of F versus {tex}\frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} graph depends on q1 and q2.
Magnitude of force is higher for the second pair as higher the product of charges more will be the force at fixed distance r according to Coulomb's law.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Slope of F versus {tex}\frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} graph corresponding to second pair (1µC,- 3µC) is greater. Higher the magnitude of product of charges q1 and q2, higher the slope of the graph between F and 1/r2.

  1. a conducting sphere having negative charge on it.

question_text: Sketch the pattern of electric field lines due to a conducting sphere having negative charge on it. an electric dipole.
explanation: Electric field lines are paths taken by unit positive charge to travel. Electric field lines due to a conducting sphere are shown in figure in which electric field lines are terminating perpendicular to the surface and inside the sphere they are zero. Negative charge on sphere attracts electric field. Electric field lines due to an electric dipole are shown in figure in which electric field lines originating from +q is terminating on -q. There is only one electric filed line which is passing straight from +q to -q , remaining electric field lines they follow path which is trajectory originating from +q is terminating on -q .
solution: Electric field lines are paths taken by unit positive charge to travel. Electric field lines due to a conducting sphere are shown in figure in which electric field lines are terminating perpendicular to the surface and inside the sphere they are zero. Negative charge on sphere attracts electric field. Electric field lines due to an electric dipole are shown in figure in which electric field lines originating from +q is terminating on -q. There is only one electric filed line which is passing straight from +q to -q , remaining electric field lines they follow path which is trajectory originating from +q is terminating on -q .

1. Electric field lines are paths taken by unit positive charge to travel. Electric field lines due to a conducting sphere are shown in figure in which electric field lines are terminating perpendicular to the surface and inside the sphere they are zero. Negative charge on sphere attracts electric field.
  2. Electric field lines due to an electric dipole are shown in figure in which electric field lines originating from +q is terminating on -q. There is only one electric filed line which is passing straight from +q to -q , remaining electric field lines they follow path which is trajectory originating from +q is terminating on -q .

question_html: Name and define the SI unit of charge.
question_text: Name and define the SI unit of charge.
explanation: The SI unit of electric charge is the coulomb. One coulomb is that amount of charge which repels an equal and similar charge with a force of 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 N when placed in a vacuum at a distance of 1 metre from it.
solution: The SI unit of electric charge is the coulomb. One coulomb is that amount of charge which repels an equal and similar charge with a force of 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 N when placed in a vacuum at a distance of 1 metre from it.

question_html: In which orientation, a dipole placed in a uniform electric field is in (i) stable equilibrium (ii) unstable equilibrium?
question_text: In which orientation, a dipole placed in a uniform electric field is in (i) stable equilibrium (ii) unstable equilibrium?
explanation: For stable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is 0° i.e. it should be placed parallel to the electric field. In this case Potential energy of the dipole is minimum U = - pE For unstable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is 180°i.e. it should be placed antiparallel to the electric field. In this case potential energy of the dipole is maximum U = pE
solution: For stable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is 0° i.e. it should be placed parallel to the electric field. In this case Potential energy of the dipole is minimum U = - pE For unstable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is 180°i.e. it should be placed antiparallel to the electric field. In this case potential energy of the dipole is maximum U = pE
solution_html:
For stable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is 0° i.e. it should be placed parallel to the electric field. In this case Potential energy of the dipole is minimum
U = - pE

For unstable equilibrium, the angle between p and E is 180°i.e. it should be placed antiparallel to the electric field. In this case potential energy of the dipole is maximum
U = pE

question_html: Define dielectric constant of a medium in terms of force between two electric charges.
question_text: Define dielectric constant of a medium in terms of force between two electric charges.
explanation: Dielectric constant is defined as the ratio of force between two charges placed some distance apart in vaccum to the force between the same two charges placed at same distance apart in the given medium. Mathematically, {tex}\epsilon_r{/tex} (or K) = {tex}\frac{F_{vac}}{F_{med}}{/tex}
solution: Dielectric constant is defined as the ratio of force between two charges placed some distance apart in vaccum to the force between the same two charges placed at same distance apart in the given medium. Mathematically, {tex}\epsilon_r{/tex} (or K) = {tex}\frac{F_{vac}}{F_{med}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Dielectric constant is defined as the ratio of force between two charges placed some distance apart in vaccum to the force between the same two charges placed at same distance apart in the given medium. Mathematically,
{tex}\epsilon_r{/tex}(or K) = {tex}\frac{F_{vac}}{F_{med}}{/tex}

question_html: Using Gauss' law, obtain the expression for the electric field due to uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R at a point outside the shell. Draw a graph showing the variation of electric field with r, for r > R and r < R.
question_text: Using Gauss' law, obtain the expression for the electric field due to uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R at a point outside the shell. Draw a graph showing the variation of electric field with r, for r > R and r < R.
explanation: Considering a Gaussian surface in the form of a sphere at radius r, the electric field has the same magnitude at every point of the sphere and is directed outward. Let us consider charge +q be uniformly distributed over a spherical shell of radius R. 'E' at any point is radially outward (if charge q is positive) and has same magnitude at all points which lie at the same distance r from centre of spherical shell such that r > R. Therefore, Gaussian surface is concentric sphere of radius r such that r > R. By Gauss' theorem {tex}\oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \oint E d S \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} E and dS are along the same direction] {tex}E \cdot \oint d S = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} Magnitude of E is same at every point on Gaussian surface] {tex}E \times 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, graph is shown below. Variation of E with r for a spherical shell in which charge q is distributed over its surface which shows that electric field inside a spherical shell is zero.
solution: Considering a Gaussian surface in the form of a sphere at radius r, the electric field has the same magnitude at every point of the sphere and is directed outward. Let us consider charge +q be uniformly distributed over a spherical shell of radius R. 'E' at any point is radially outward (if charge q is positive) and has same magnitude at all points which lie at the same distance r from centre of spherical shell such that r > R. Therefore, Gaussian surface is concentric sphere of radius r such that r > R. By Gauss' theorem {tex}\oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \oint E d S \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} E and dS are along the same direction] {tex}E \cdot \oint d S = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} Magnitude of E is same at every point on Gaussian surface] {tex}E \times 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, graph is shown below. Variation of E with r for a spherical shell in which charge q is distributed over its surface which shows that electric field inside a spherical shell is zero.
solution_html:
Considering a Gaussian surface in the form of a sphere at radius r, the electric field has the same magnitude at every point of the sphere and is directed outward. Let us consider charge +q be uniformly distributed over a spherical shell of radius R.
'E' at any point is radially outward (if charge q is positive) and has same magnitude at all points which lie at the same distance r from centre of spherical shell such that r > R.
Therefore, Gaussian surface is concentric sphere of radius r such that r > R.

By Gauss' theorem
{tex}\oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \oint E d S \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
[{tex}\because {/tex} E and dS are along the same direction]
{tex}E \cdot \oint d S = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [{tex}\because {/tex} Magnitude of E is same at every point on Gaussian surface]
{tex}E \times 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, graph is shown below.

Variation of E with r for a spherical shell in which charge q is distributed over its surface which shows that electric field inside a spherical shell is zero.

question_text: Charges of magnitudes 2q and -q are located at points (a, 0, 0) and (4a, 0, 0). Find the ratio of the flux of electric field due to these charges through concentric spheres of radii 2a and 8a centered at the origin.
explanation: The locations of the charges are shown in the figure. solution_html:
The locations of the charges are shown in the figure.

The electric flux through the sphere of radius 2a,

The electric flux through the sphere of radius 8a

question_html: A small uncharged metallic sphere is positioned exactly at a point midway between two equal and opposite point charges. If the sphere is slightly displaced towards the positive charge and released, then
question_text: A small uncharged metallic sphere is positioned exactly at a point midway between two equal and opposite point charges. If the sphere is slightly displaced towards the positive charge and released, then

options.text: it will oscillate about its original position
options.html: it will oscillate about its original position

options.text: it will move further towards the positive charge
options.html: it will move further towards the positive charge
options.explanation: At the midpoint, the forces on the sphere due to the +ve and -ve charges are equal and opposite. So, net force = 0. When the sphere is slightly displayed towards the +ve charge, the force due to +ve charge is more than that due to -ve charge. So the sphere will move further towards the +ve charge.

options.text: its electric potential energy will decrease and kinetic energy will increase

options.text: its total energy remains constant but it non-zero

question_html: Two electrically charged particles, having charges of different magnitude when placed at a distance d from each other, experience a force of attraction F. These two particles are put in contact and again placed at the same distance from each other. What is the nature of the new force between them? Is the magnitude of the force of interaction between them now more or less than F?
question_text: Two electrically charged particles, having charges of different magnitude when placed at a distance d from each other, experience a force of attraction F. These two particles are put in contact and again placed at the same distance from each other. What is the nature of the new force between them? Is the magnitude of the force of interaction between them now more or less than F?
explanation: When the two particles are put in contact, they share the difference of charge identically. Hence the two particles repel, with a force less than F.
solution: When the two particles are put in contact, they share the difference of charge identically. Hence the two particles repel, with a force less than F.
solution_html: When the two particles are put in contact, they share the difference of charge identically. Hence the two particles repel, with a force less than F.

question_html: A charged particle, of charge 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C and mass 10 milligram, moving with a velocity of 1000 m/s enters a uniform electric field of strength 103 NC-1 directed perpendicular to its direction of motion. Find the velocity and displacement, of the particle after 10 s.
question_text: A charged particle, of charge 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and mass 10 milligram, moving with a velocity of 1000 m/s enters a uniform electric field of strength 10 3 NC -1 directed perpendicular to its direction of motion. Find the velocity and displacement, of the particle after 10 s.
explanation: The velocity of the particle, normal to the direction of field v x = 1000 ms -1 , is constant The velocity of the particle, along the direction of field, after 10 s, is given by v y = u y + a y t = 0 + {tex}\frac{q E_{y}}{m} t=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3} \times 10}{10 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 2000 ms -1 The net velocity after 10 s, v = {tex}\sqrt{v_{x}^{2}+v_{y}^{2}}=\sqrt{(1000)^{2}+(2000)^{2}}{/tex} = 1000 {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} ms -1 Displacement, along the x-axis, after 10 s, x = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10m = 10000 m Displacement along y-axis (in the direction of field) after 10 s, y = {tex}u_{y} t+\frac{1}{2} a_{y} t^{2}{/tex} = (0)t + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E_{y}}{m} t^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} \times{/tex} (10) 2 = 10000 m Net displacement, r = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\sqrt{(10000)^{2}+(10000)^{2}}{/tex} = 10000 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} m
solution: The velocity of the particle, normal to the direction of field v x = 1000 ms -1 , is constant The velocity of the particle, along the direction of field, after 10 s, is given by v y = u y + a y t = 0 + {tex}\frac{q E_{y}}{m} t=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3} \times 10}{10 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 2000 ms -1 The net velocity after 10 s, v = {tex}\sqrt{v_{x}^{2}+v_{y}^{2}}=\sqrt{(1000)^{2}+(2000)^{2}}{/tex} = 1000 {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} ms -1 Displacement, along the x-axis, after 10 s, x = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10m = 10000 m Displacement along y-axis (in the direction of field) after 10 s, y = {tex}u_{y} t+\frac{1}{2} a_{y} t^{2}{/tex} = (0)t + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E_{y}}{m} t^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} \times{/tex} (10) 2 = 10000 m Net displacement, r = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\sqrt{(10000)^{2}+(10000)^{2}}{/tex} = 10000 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} m
solution_html:
The velocity of the particle, normal to the direction of field
vx = 1000 ms-1, is constant
The velocity of the particle, along the direction of field, after 10 s, is given by
vy = uy + ayt
= 0 + {tex}\frac{q E_{y}}{m} t=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3} \times 10}{10 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 2000 ms-1
The net velocity after 10 s,
v = {tex}\sqrt{v_{x}^{2}+v_{y}^{2}}=\sqrt{(1000)^{2}+(2000)^{2}}{/tex} = 1000 {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} ms-1
Displacement, along the x-axis, after 10 s,
x = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10m = 10000 m
Displacement along y-axis (in the direction of field) after 10 s,
y = {tex}u_{y} t+\frac{1}{2} a_{y} t^{2}{/tex} = (0)t + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E_{y}}{m} t^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} \times{/tex} (10)2
= 10000 m
Net displacement,
r = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\sqrt{(10000)^{2}+(10000)^{2}}{/tex} = 10000 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} m

question_html: Plot a graph showing the variation of coulomb force (F) versus {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r^{2}}\right){/tex}, where r is the distance between the two charges of each pair of charges: (1 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C) and (2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, -3 {tex}\mu{/tex}C). Interpret the graphs obtained.
question_text: Plot a graph showing the variation of coulomb force (F) versus {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r^{2}}\right){/tex} , where r is the distance between the two charges of each pair of charges: (1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C) and (2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, -3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C). Interpret the graphs obtained.
explanation: As F {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} , so F versus {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} graph is a straight line for both pairs of charges. For the pair (1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C), F is repulsive, the graph OA has a +ve slope. For the pair (2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, -3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C), F is attractive, the graph OB has a -ve slope. Clearly, F attraction = 3F repulsion , the slope of OB is three times the slope of OA.

For the pair (1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C), F is repulsive, the graph OA has a +ve slope. For the pair (2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, -3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C), F is attractive, the graph OB has a -ve slope. Clearly, F attraction = 3F repulsion , the slope of OB is three times the slope of OA.
solution_html:
1. As F {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex}, so F versus {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} graph is a straight line for both pairs of charges.
  2. For the pair (1 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C), F is repulsive, the graph OA has a +ve slope. For the pair (2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, -3 {tex}\mu{/tex}C), F is attractive, the graph OB has a -ve slope.
  3. Clearly, Fattraction = 3Frepulsion, the slope of OB is three times the slope of OA.

question_html: What do you understand by quantisation of electric charges?
question_text: What do you understand by quantisation of electric charges?
explanation: The quantisation of electric charge means that the total charge (q) of a body is always an integral multiple of a basic charge (e) which is the charge on an electron. Thus q = ne, where n = 0, {tex}\pm{/tex} 1, {tex}\pm{/tex} 2, {tex}\pm{/tex} 3, .....

question_html: A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube of side I.What is the electric flux passing through each face of the cube?
question_text: A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube of side I.What is the electric flux passing through each face of the cube?
explanation: Applying Gauss' theorem, Total electric flux linked with cube, {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} As charge is at centre, therefore, electric flux will be symmetrically distributed through all 6 faces of the cube so each face will receive 1/6 of the total electric flux. Flux linked with each face of the cube = Total Flux/6 {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \phi = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \times \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { q } { 6 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
solution: Applying Gauss' theorem, Total electric flux linked with cube, {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} As charge is at centre, therefore, electric flux will be symmetrically distributed through all 6 faces of the cube so each face will receive 1/6 of the total electric flux. Flux linked with each face of the cube = Total Flux/6 {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \phi = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \times \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { q } { 6 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Applying Gauss' theorem,
Total electric flux linked with cube, {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
As charge is at centre, therefore, electric flux will be symmetrically distributed through all 6 faces of the cube so each face will receive 1/6 of the total electric flux.
Flux linked with each face of the cube = Total Flux/6 {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \phi = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \times \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { q } { 6 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}

question_html:
A spherical conducting shell of inner radius R1 and outer radius R2 has a charge Q. A charge q is placed at the centre of the shell.

  1. What is the surface charge density on the
    (a) inner surface,
    (b) outer surface of the shell?
  2. Write the expression for the electric field at a point to x > R2 from the centre of the shell.
question_text: A spherical conducting shell of inner radius R 1 and outer radius R 2 has a charge Q. A charge q is placed at the centre of the shell. What is the surface charge density on the (a) inner surface, (b) outer surface of the shell? Write the expression for the electric field at a point to x > R 2 from the centre of the shell.
explanation: a) Charge produced on inner surface due to induction = -q {tex}\therefore {/tex} Surface charge density of inner surface {tex}= \frac { - q } { 4 \pi R _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} When charge -q is induced on inner walls, then equal charge +q is produced at outer surface. b) Charge on outer surface = q + Q {tex}\therefore {/tex} Surface charge density of outer surface {tex}= \frac { q + Q } { 4 \pi R _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Electric field intensity at point P separated by a distance x (x > R 2 ) {tex}E = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { ( q + Q ) } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} along CP and away from spherical shell
solution: a) Charge produced on inner surface due to induction = -q {tex}\therefore {/tex} Surface charge density of inner surface {tex}= \frac { - q } { 4 \pi R _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} When charge -q is induced on inner walls, then equal charge +q is produced at outer surface. b) Charge on outer surface = q + Q {tex}\therefore {/tex} Surface charge density of outer surface {tex}= \frac { q + Q } { 4 \pi R _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Electric field intensity at point P separated by a distance x (x > R 2 ) {tex}E = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { ( q + Q ) } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} along CP and away from spherical shell
solution_html:
1. a) Charge produced on inner surface due to induction = -q
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Surface charge density of inner surface {tex}= \frac { - q } { 4 \pi R _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

    When charge -q is induced on inner walls, then equal charge +q is produced at outer surface.
    b) Charge on outer surface = q + Q
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Surface charge density of outer surface {tex}= \frac { q + Q } { 4 \pi R _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
     
  2. Electric field intensity at point P separated by a distance x (x > R2)
    {tex}E = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { ( q + Q ) } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} along CP and away from spherical shell

question_html: Show that the electric field at the surface of a charged conductor is given by {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { n } }{/tex}, where {tex}\sigma {/tex} is the surface charge density and {tex}\hat n{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the surface in the outward direction.
question_text: Show that the electric field at the surface of a charged conductor is given by {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { n } }{/tex} , where {tex}\sigma {/tex} is the surface charge density and {tex}\hat n{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the surface in the outward direction.
explanation: Let q charge be uniformly distributed over the spherical shell of radius r for which we have to find the electric field at the surface. Surface charge density on spherical shell is the charge per unit area. Thus, {tex}\sigma = \frac { q } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ........ (i) Electric field intensity on the surface of spherical shell assuming all charge resides in the centre is, {tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r ^ { 2 } } \hat { \mathbf { n } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} electric field acts along radially outward and along {tex}\hat n{/tex} as the charge is positive] {tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { \left( q / 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } \right) } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { n } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { n }{/tex} ......... (ii) This relation shows that for a given conductor electric field intensity(E) is directly proportional to surface charge density(σ) and perpendicular to the surface as shown in the figure.
solution: Let q charge be uniformly distributed over the spherical shell of radius r for which we have to find the electric field at the surface. Surface charge density on spherical shell is the charge per unit area. Thus, {tex}\sigma = \frac { q } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ........ (i) Electric field intensity on the surface of spherical shell assuming all charge resides in the centre is, {tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r ^ { 2 } } \hat { \mathbf { n } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} electric field acts along radially outward and along {tex}\hat n{/tex} as the charge is positive] {tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { \left( q / 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } \right) } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { n } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { n }{/tex} ......... (ii) This relation shows that for a given conductor electric field intensity(E) is directly proportional to surface charge density(σ) and perpendicular to the surface as shown in the figure.
solution_html:
Let q charge be uniformly distributed over the spherical shell of radius r for which we have to find the electric field at the surface.

Surface charge density on spherical shell is the charge per unit area. Thus,
{tex}\sigma = \frac { q } { 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ........ (i)
Electric field intensity on the surface of spherical shell assuming all charge resides in the centre is,
{tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r ^ { 2 } } \hat { \mathbf { n } }{/tex}
[{tex}\because {/tex} electric field acts along radially outward and along {tex}\hat n{/tex} as the charge is positive]
{tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { \left( q / 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } \right) } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { n } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { n }{/tex}......... (ii)
This relation shows that for a given conductor electric field intensity(E) is directly proportional to surface charge density(σ) and perpendicular to the surface as shown in the figure.
