question_html: Figure shows three point charges, +2q, -q and +3q. Two charges +2q and-q are enclosed within a surface S. What is the electric flux due to this configuration through the surface S?
question_text: Figure shows three point charges, +2q, -q and +3q. Two charges +2q and-q are enclosed within a surface S. What is the electric flux due to this configuration through the surface S?
explanation: Thus Gauss' law states that electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. Electric flux through the closed surface S is {tex}\phi _ { s } = \frac { \sum q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { + 2 q - q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \phi _ { s } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} Charge +3q is outside the closed surface S, therefore, it would not be taken into consideration in applying Gauss' theorem.
solution: Thus Gauss' law states that electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum. Electric flux through the closed surface S is {tex}\phi _ { s } = \frac { \sum q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { + 2 q - q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \phi _ { s } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} Charge +3q is outside the closed surface S, therefore, it would not be taken into consideration in applying Gauss' theorem.
solution_html:
Thus Gauss' law states that electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed inside the surface divided by permittivity of vacuum.
Electric flux through the closed surface S is
{tex}\phi _ { s } = \frac { \sum q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { + 2 q - q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \phi _ { s } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
Charge +3q is outside the closed surface S, therefore, it would not be taken into consideration in applying Gauss' theorem.

subject: Physics
question_html: A dipole is present in an electrostatic field of magnitude 106 NC-1. If the work done in rotating it from its position of stable equilibrium to its position of unstable equilibrium is 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-23 J, then find the magnitude of the dipole moment of this dipole.
question_text: A dipole is present in an electrostatic field of magnitude 10 6 NC -1 . If the work done in rotating it from its position of stable equilibrium to its position of unstable equilibrium is 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -23 J, then find the magnitude of the dipole moment of this dipole.
explanation: Given: Electric field intensity, E = 10 6 NC -1 Work done, W = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -23 Joule Work was done in rotating the dipole from a stable equilibrium position to unstable equilibrium position which increases it's potential energy. {tex}W = p E \left( \cos \theta _ { 1 } - \cos \theta _ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}W = p E \left( \cos 0 ^ { \circ } - \cos 180 ^ { \circ } \right) = 2 p E{/tex} As 2pE work was done in rotating the dipole against the electric field, magnitude of dipole moment can be found using below formula - {tex}\therefore \quad p = \frac { W } { 2 E }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 23 } } { 2 \times 10 ^ { 6 } } = 10 ^ { - 29 } \mathrm { Cm }{/tex}
solution: Given: Electric field intensity, E = 10 6 NC -1 Work done, W = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -23 Joule Work was done in rotating the dipole from a stable equilibrium position to unstable equilibrium position which increases it's potential energy. {tex}W = p E \left( \cos \theta _ { 1 } - \cos \theta _ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}W = p E \left( \cos 0 ^ { \circ } - \cos 180 ^ { \circ } \right) = 2 p E{/tex} As 2pE work was done in rotating the dipole against the electric field, magnitude of dipole moment can be found using below formula - {tex}\therefore \quad p = \frac { W } { 2 E }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 23 } } { 2 \times 10 ^ { 6 } } = 10 ^ { - 29 } \mathrm { Cm }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Electric field intensity, E = 106 NC-1
Work done, W = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-23 Joule
Work was done in rotating the dipole from a stable equilibrium position to unstable equilibrium position which increases it's potential energy.
{tex}W = p E \left( \cos \theta _ { 1 } - \cos \theta _ { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}W = p E \left( \cos 0 ^ { \circ } - \cos 180 ^ { \circ } \right) = 2 p E{/tex}
As 2pE work was done in rotating the dipole against the electric field, magnitude of dipole moment can be found using below formula - 
{tex}\therefore \quad p = \frac { W } { 2 E }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 23 } } { 2 \times 10 ^ { 6 } } = 10 ^ { - 29 } \mathrm { Cm }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A dipole with a dipole moment of magnitude p is in stable equilibrium in an electrostatic field of magnitude E. Find the work done in rotating this dipole from its stable position to unstable position?
question_text: A dipole with a dipole moment of magnitude p is in stable equilibrium in an electrostatic field of magnitude E. Find the work done in rotating this dipole from its stable position to unstable position?
explanation: For stable equilibrium, the angle between p and E, {tex}\theta _ { 1 } = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} So potential energy in stable equilibrium, U 1 = - pE For unstable equilibrium, {tex}\theta _ { 2 } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} So potential energy in unstable equilibrium, U 2 = pE We know that from work energy theorem, Work done in rotating the dipole from angle {tex}{\theta _1}{/tex} to {tex}{\theta _2}{/tex} , {tex}W = -\Delta \ U{/tex} = U 2 - U 1 = pE - (-pE) = 2pE
solution: For stable equilibrium, the angle between p and E, {tex}\theta _ { 1 } = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} So potential energy in stable equilibrium, U 1 = - pE For unstable equilibrium, {tex}\theta _ { 2 } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} So potential energy in unstable equilibrium, U 2 = pE We know that from work energy theorem, Work done in rotating the dipole from angle {tex}{\theta _1}{/tex} to {tex}{\theta _2}{/tex} , {tex}W = -\Delta \ U{/tex} = U 2 - U 1 = pE - (-pE) = 2pE
solution_html:
For stable equilibrium, the angle between p and E,
{tex}\theta _ { 1 } = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
So potential energy in stable equilibrium, U1 = - pE
For unstable equilibrium, {tex}\theta _ { 2 } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
So potential energy in unstable equilibrium, U2 = pE
We know that from work energy theorem,
Work done in rotating the dipole from angle {tex}{\theta _1}{/tex} to {tex}{\theta _2}{/tex},
{tex}W = -\Delta \ U{/tex}
= U2 - U1 = pE - (-pE) = 2pE

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two point charges having equal charges separated by 1 metre distance experience a force of 8 N. What will be the force experienced by them, if they are held in water at the same distance? 
(Given, Kwater = 80).
question_text: Two point charges having equal charges separated by 1 metre distance experience a force of 8 N. What will be the force experienced by them, if they are held in water at the same distance? (Given, K water = 80).
explanation: When two point charges system is moved from air to another medium(in this case it is water) keeping other variable (e.g. distance, the magnitude of charge) unchanged, so, the only factor which may affect the interacting force is a dielectric constant of medium(K). Force acting between two point charges can be calculated using Coulomb law is {tex}F = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } K } \frac { { q _ { 1 } } q _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } \quad F \propto \frac { 1 } { K }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { F _ { \mathrm { air } } } { F _ { \mathrm { medium } } } = K \Rightarrow \frac { 8 } { F _ { \mathrm { water } } } = 80{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad F _ { \text { water } } = \frac { 8 } { 80 } \Rightarrow F _ { \text { water } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \mathrm { N }{/tex} So the force between two point charges will be 0.1 N instead of 8 N when system is moved from air to water.
solution: When two point charges system is moved from air to another medium(in this case it is water) keeping other variable (e.g. distance, the magnitude of charge) unchanged, so, the only factor which may affect the interacting force is a dielectric constant of medium(K). Force acting between two point charges can be calculated using Coulomb law is {tex}F = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } K } \frac { { q _ { 1 } } q _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } \quad F \propto \frac { 1 } { K }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { F _ { \mathrm { air } } } { F _ { \mathrm { medium } } } = K \Rightarrow \frac { 8 } { F _ { \mathrm { water } } } = 80{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad F _ { \text { water } } = \frac { 8 } { 80 } \Rightarrow F _ { \text { water } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \mathrm { N }{/tex} So the force between two point charges will be 0.1 N instead of 8 N when system is moved from air to water.
solution_html:
When two point charges system is moved from air to another medium(in this case it is water) keeping other variable (e.g. distance, the magnitude of charge) unchanged, so, the only factor which may affect the interacting force is a dielectric constant of medium(K).
Force acting between two point charges can be calculated using Coulomb law is
{tex}F = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } K } \frac { { q _ { 1 } } q _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } \quad F \propto \frac { 1 } { K }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { F _ { \mathrm { air } } } { F _ { \mathrm { medium } } } = K \Rightarrow \frac { 8 } { F _ { \mathrm { water } } } = 80{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad F _ { \text { water } } = \frac { 8 } { 80 } \Rightarrow F _ { \text { water } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \mathrm { N }{/tex}
So the force between two point charges will be 0.1 N instead of 8 N when system is moved from air to water.

question_html:
Point out whether the following statement is right or wrong:
Statement - The mutual forces between two charges do not get affected by the presence of other charges.
question_text: Point out whether the following statement is right or wrong: Statement - The mutual forces between two charges do not get affected by the presence of other charges.
explanation: The statement is right because the mutual force acting between two point charges is proportional to the product of the magnitude of charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them i.e. independent of the other charges present in the surrounding.
solution: The statement is right because the mutual force acting between two point charges is proportional to the product of the magnitude of charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them i.e. independent of the other charges present in the surrounding.

question_html: A metallic sphere is placed in a uniform electric field as shown in the figure. Which path is followed by electric field lines and why?
question_text: A metallic sphere is placed in a uniform electric field as shown in the figure. Which path is followed by electric field lines and why?
explanation: Electric field lines will follow path "d" because electric field intensity inside the conductor will be zero, therefore, no electric field lines of force exist inside the sphere. Also electric field lines are always perpendicular to the surface of the conductor, that surface of the conductor must be an equipotential surface.
solution: Electric field lines will follow path "d" because electric field intensity inside the conductor will be zero, therefore, no electric field lines of force exist inside the sphere. Also electric field lines are always perpendicular to the surface of the conductor, that surface of the conductor must be an equipotential surface.
solution_html: Electric field lines will follow path "d" because electric field intensity inside the conductor will be zero, therefore, no electric field lines of force exist inside the sphere. Also electric field lines are always perpendicular to the surface of the conductor, that surface of the conductor must be an equipotential surface.

question_html:
A spherical conducting shell of inner radius r1 and outer radius r2 has a charge Q. A charge q is placed at the centre of the shell.

  1. What is the surface charge density on the (a) inner surface, (b) outer surface of the shell?
  2. Write the expression for the electric field at a point x > r2 from the centre of the spherical shell.
question_text: A spherical conducting shell of inner radius r 1 and outer radius r 2 has a charge Q. A charge q is placed at the centre of the shell. What is the surface charge density on the (a) inner surface, (b) outer surface of the shell? Write the expression for the electric field at a point x > r 2 from the centre of the spherical shell.
explanation: (a) Surface charge density on the inner surface of the shell, {tex}\sigma_{\text {inner}}=\frac{q}{4 \pi r_{1}^{2}}{/tex} (b) Surface charge density on the outer surface of the shell, {tex}\sigma_{\text {outer}}=\frac{\mathbf{Q}+q}{4 \pi r_{2}^{2}}{/tex} Electric field at a point x > r 2 from the centre of the shell, E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q+q}{x^{2}}{/tex}
solution: (a) Surface charge density on the inner surface of the shell, {tex}\sigma_{\text {inner}}=\frac{q}{4 \pi r_{1}^{2}}{/tex} (b) Surface charge density on the outer surface of the shell, {tex}\sigma_{\text {outer}}=\frac{\mathbf{Q}+q}{4 \pi r_{2}^{2}}{/tex} Electric field at a point x > r 2 from the centre of the shell, E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q+q}{x^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. (a) Surface charge density on the inner surface of the shell,
    {tex}\sigma_{\text {inner}}=\frac{q}{4 \pi r_{1}^{2}}{/tex}
    (b) Surface charge density on the outer surface of the shell,
    {tex}\sigma_{\text {outer}}=\frac{\mathbf{Q}+q}{4 \pi r_{2}^{2}}{/tex}
  2. Electric field at a point x > r2 from the centre of the shell,
    E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q+q}{x^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: Does the torque exerted on a dipole in a non-uniform field depend on the orientation of the dipole with respect to the field?
question_text: Does the torque exerted on a dipole in a non-uniform field depend on the orientation of the dipole with respect to the field?
explanation: Yes, In a non-uniform electric field, the field vector {tex}\vec{E}(\vec{r}){/tex} changes from point to point, either in magnitude or in direction or both. Therefore, the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}(\vec{r}){/tex} for a dipole located at {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} changes with the change in orientation of the dipole with respect to the field.
solution: Yes, In a non-uniform electric field, the field vector {tex}\vec{E}(\vec{r}){/tex} changes from point to point, either in magnitude or in direction or both. Therefore, the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}(\vec{r}){/tex} for a dipole located at {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} changes with the change in orientation of the dipole with respect to the field.
solution_html: Yes, In a non-uniform electric field, the field vector {tex}\vec{E}(\vec{r}){/tex} changes from point to point, either in magnitude or in direction or both. Therefore, the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}(\vec{r}){/tex} for a dipole located at {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} changes with the change in orientation of the dipole with respect to the field.

question_text: Write an expression for the flux {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} , of the electric field {tex}\overrightarrow {E}{/tex} through an area element {tex}\overrightarrow {\Delta S}{/tex} .
explanation: If an elementary surface area {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is held inside the electric field E and E n is the component of electric field normal to the surface area {tex}\Delta{/tex} S, then electric flux through the surface, {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = E n {tex}\Delta{/tex} S In vector notation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta \mathrm{S}}{/tex}
solution: If an elementary surface area {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is held inside the electric field E and E n is the component of electric field normal to the surface area {tex}\Delta{/tex} S, then electric flux through the surface, {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = E n {tex}\Delta{/tex} S In vector notation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta \mathrm{S}}{/tex}

question_html: If the radius of the Gaussian surface enclosing a charge is halved, how does the electric flux through the Gaussian surface change?
question_text: If the radius of the Gaussian surface enclosing a charge is halved, how does the electric flux through the Gaussian surface change?
explanation: Electric flux {tex}{\phi _E}{/tex} is given by {tex}{\phi _E} = \oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \frac{Q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} where Q is total charge inside the closed surface. {tex}\therefore{/tex} On changing the radius of sphere, the electric flux through the Gaussian surface remains same as the net charge enclosed within the surface is same.
solution: Electric flux {tex}{\phi _E}{/tex} is given by {tex}{\phi _E} = \oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \frac{Q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} where Q is total charge inside the closed surface. {tex}\therefore{/tex} On changing the radius of sphere, the electric flux through the Gaussian surface remains same as the net charge enclosed within the surface is same.
solution_html:
Electric flux {tex}{\phi _E}{/tex} is given by
{tex}{\phi _E} = \oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \frac{Q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
where Q is total charge inside the closed surface.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} On changing the radius of sphere, the electric flux through the Gaussian surface remains same as the net charge enclosed within the surface is same.

question_html: A sphere S1 of radius r1 encloses a charge Q. If there is another concentric sphere S2 of radius r2 (r2 > r1) and there are no additional charges between S1 and S2, find the ratio of electric flux through S1 and S2.
question_text: A sphere S 1 of radius r 1 encloses a charge Q. If there is another concentric sphere S 2 of radius r 2 (r 2 > r 1 ) and there are no additional charges between S 1 and S 2 , find the ratio of electric flux through S 1 and S 2 .
explanation: According to Gauss' theorem, total flux passing through a closed surface, {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \oint \mathbf { E } d \mathbf { S }{/tex} As, S 1 and S 2 are concentric spheres of radii r 1 and r 2 respectively, then we have {tex}\phi _ { 1 } = q _ { 1 } / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ...................... (i) {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = q _ { 2 } / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ...................... (ii) {tex}\therefore {/tex} On dividing Eq. (i) with Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { \ { \phi } _ { 1 } } { \phi _ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { Q } { Q } = 1{/tex} Hence, {tex}\phi _ { 1 } : \phi _ { 2 } = 1 : 1{/tex}
solution: According to Gauss' theorem, total flux passing through a closed surface, {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \oint \mathbf { E } d \mathbf { S }{/tex} As, S 1 and S 2 are concentric spheres of radii r 1 and r 2 respectively, then we have {tex}\phi _ { 1 } = q _ { 1 } / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ...................... (i) {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = q _ { 2 } / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ...................... (ii) {tex}\therefore {/tex} On dividing Eq. (i) with Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { \ { \phi } _ { 1 } } { \phi _ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { Q } { Q } = 1{/tex} Hence, {tex}\phi _ { 1 } : \phi _ { 2 } = 1 : 1{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Gauss' theorem, total flux passing through a closed surface, {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \oint \mathbf { E } d \mathbf { S }{/tex}
As, S1 and S2 are concentric spheres of radii r1 and r2 respectively, then we have
{tex}\phi _ { 1 } = q _ { 1 } / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ...................... (i)
{tex}\phi _ { 2 } = q _ { 2 } / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ...................... (ii)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} On dividing Eq. (i) with Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\frac { \ { \phi } _ { 1 } } { \phi _ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { Q } { Q } = 1{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\phi _ { 1 } : \phi _ { 2 } = 1 : 1{/tex}

question_html:
A uniformly charged conducting sphere of 2.4 m diameter has a surface charge density of 80.0 {tex}\mu{/tex}Cm-2.

  1. Find the charge on the sphere.
  2. What is the total electric flux leaving the surface of the sphere?
question_text: A uniformly charged conducting sphere of 2.4 m diameter has a surface charge density of 80.0 {tex}\mu{/tex} Cm -2 . Find the charge on the sphere. What is the total electric flux leaving the surface of the sphere?
explanation: Radius of sphere, {tex}R = \frac { d } { 2 } = \frac { 2.4 } { 2 } = 1.2 \mathrm { m }{/tex} Surface charge density, {tex}\sigma = 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { Cm } ^ { - 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \sigma = \frac { q } { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } {/tex} or {tex}q = 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} Charge on the sphere is given by (q) {tex}q = 4 \times 3.14 \times (1.2 ) ^ { 2 } \times 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { }{/tex} {tex}q = 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} Gauss’s law states that the net flux of an electric field in a closed surface is directly proportional to the enclosed electric charge. Electric flux is given by, {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\phi = \frac { 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } {/tex} = {tex} 1.63 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} Nm 2 C -1
solution: Radius of sphere, {tex}R = \frac { d } { 2 } = \frac { 2.4 } { 2 } = 1.2 \mathrm { m }{/tex} Surface charge density, {tex}\sigma = 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { Cm } ^ { - 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \sigma = \frac { q } { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } {/tex} or {tex}q = 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} Charge on the sphere is given by (q) {tex}q = 4 \times 3.14 \times (1.2 ) ^ { 2 } \times 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { }{/tex} {tex}q = 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} Gauss’s law states that the net flux of an electric field in a closed surface is directly proportional to the enclosed electric charge. Electric flux is given by, {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\phi = \frac { 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } {/tex} = {tex} 1.63 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} Nm 2 C -1
solution_html:
1. Radius of sphere, {tex}R = \frac { d } { 2 } = \frac { 2.4 } { 2 } = 1.2 \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    Surface charge density, {tex}\sigma = 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { Cm } ^ { - 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \sigma = \frac { q } { 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } {/tex} or {tex}q = 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex}
    Charge on the sphere is given by (q)
    {tex}q = 4 \times 3.14 \times (1.2 ) ^ { 2 } \times 80 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { }{/tex}
    {tex}q = 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}
  2. Gauss’s law states that the net flux of an electric field in a closed surface is directly proportional to the enclosed electric charge.
    Electric flux is given by, {tex}\phi = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\phi = \frac { 1.447 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } {/tex} = {tex} 1.63 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} Nm2C-1

question_html: A point charge causes an electric flux {tex}- 3 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \mathrm { N } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} to pass through a spherical Gaussian surface. Calculate the value of the point charge. If the radius of the Gaussian surface is doubled, how much flux would pass through the surface?
question_text: A point charge causes an electric flux {tex}- 3 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \mathrm { N } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} to pass through a spherical Gaussian surface. Calculate the value of the point charge. If the radius of the Gaussian surface is doubled, how much flux would pass through the surface?
explanation: According to Gauss's law, the net flux of an electric field through a surface divided by the enclosed charge is equal to a constant. Thus, {tex}\phi = \frac q{\epsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}q = \phi \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} {tex}q = \left( - 3 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \right) \times 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 }{/tex} {tex}= - 26.55 \times 10 ^ { - 26 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} If the radius of Gaussian surface is doubled, then electric flux passing through the surface remains unchanged because it depends only on charge enclosed by the surface and is independent of its size.
solution: According to Gauss's law, the net flux of an electric field through a surface divided by the enclosed charge is equal to a constant. Thus, {tex}\phi = \frac q{\epsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}q = \phi \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} {tex}q = \left( - 3 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \right) \times 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 }{/tex} {tex}= - 26.55 \times 10 ^ { - 26 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} If the radius of Gaussian surface is doubled, then electric flux passing through the surface remains unchanged because it depends only on charge enclosed by the surface and is independent of its size.
solution_html:
According to Gauss's law, the net flux of an electric field through a surface divided by the enclosed charge is equal to a constant.
Thus, {tex}\phi = \frac q{\epsilon_o}{/tex}
{tex}q = \phi \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}q = \left( - 3 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \right) \times 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 }{/tex}
{tex}= - 26.55 \times 10 ^ { - 26 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}
If the radius of Gaussian surface is doubled, then electric flux passing through the surface remains unchanged because it depends only on charge enclosed by the surface and is independent of its size.

question_html: Use Gauss' law to derive the expression for the electric field between two uniformly charge parallel sheets with surface charge densities {tex}\sigma{/tex} and {tex}- \sigma{/tex}, respectively.
question_text: Use Gauss' law to derive the expression for the electric field between two uniformly charge parallel sheets with surface charge densities {tex}\sigma{/tex} and {tex}- \sigma{/tex} , respectively.
explanation: Let us consider two uniform large parallel sheets carrying charge densities {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} and {tex}- \sigma{/tex} respectively, separated by a small distance 'l' from each other. One plate contains positive charge and other plate contains negative charge. Electric field lines travels from positive charged plate to negative charged plate. By Gauss' law, it can be proved that electric field intensity due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is given by {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ....... (i) The electric field is directed normally outward from the plane sheet if nature of charge on sheet is positive and normally inward if charge is negative in nature. Let {tex}\hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} represents unit vector directed from positive plate to negative plate. Now, Electric Field Intensity (EFI) at any point P between the two plates is given by {tex}\mathbf { E } _ { 1 } = + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} [due to positive plate] {tex}\mathbf { E } _ { 2 } = + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} [due to negative plate] {tex}\therefore {/tex} Electric field intensity at point P is given by {tex}\mathbf { E } = \mathbf { E } _ { 1 } + \mathbf { E } _ { 2 } = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } } + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } } \Rightarrow \mathbf { E } = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} Thus, a uniform electric field is produced between the two infinite parallel plane sheet of charge which is directed from positive plate to negative plate.
solution: Let us consider two uniform large parallel sheets carrying charge densities {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} and {tex}- \sigma{/tex} respectively, separated by a small distance 'l' from each other. One plate contains positive charge and other plate contains negative charge. Electric field lines travels from positive charged plate to negative charged plate. By Gauss' law, it can be proved that electric field intensity due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is given by {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ....... (i) The electric field is directed normally outward from the plane sheet if nature of charge on sheet is positive and normally inward if charge is negative in nature. Let {tex}\hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} represents unit vector directed from positive plate to negative plate. Now, Electric Field Intensity (EFI) at any point P between the two plates is given by {tex}\mathbf { E } _ { 1 } = + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} [due to positive plate] {tex}\mathbf { E } _ { 2 } = + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} [due to negative plate] {tex}\therefore {/tex} Electric field intensity at point P is given by {tex}\mathbf { E } = \mathbf { E } _ { 1 } + \mathbf { E } _ { 2 } = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } } + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } } \Rightarrow \mathbf { E } = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} Thus, a uniform electric field is produced between the two infinite parallel plane sheet of charge which is directed from positive plate to negative plate.
solution_html:
Let us consider two uniform large parallel sheets carrying charge densities {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} and {tex}- \sigma{/tex} respectively, separated by a small distance 'l'  from each other. One plate contains positive charge and other plate contains negative charge. Electric field lines travels from positive charged plate to negative charged plate.
By Gauss' law, it can be proved that electric field intensity due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet  is given by 
{tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} ....... (i)
The electric field is directed normally outward from the plane sheet if nature of charge on sheet is positive and normally inward if charge is negative in nature.

Let {tex}\hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} represents unit vector directed from positive plate to negative plate.
Now, Electric Field Intensity (EFI) at any point P between the two plates is given by
{tex}\mathbf { E } _ { 1 } = + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} [due to positive plate]
{tex}\mathbf { E } _ { 2 } = + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex} [due to negative plate]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Electric field intensity at point P is given by
{tex}\mathbf { E } = \mathbf { E } _ { 1 } + \mathbf { E } _ { 2 } = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } } + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } } \Rightarrow \mathbf { E } = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \hat { \mathbf { r } }{/tex}
Thus, a uniform electric field is produced between the two infinite parallel plane sheet of charge which is directed from positive plate to negative plate.

question_html: The sum of two point charges is 7µC. They repel each other with a force of 1N when kept 30 cm apart in free space. Calculate the value of each charge.
question_text: The sum of two point charges is 7µC. They repel each other with a force of 1N when kept 30 cm apart in free space. Calculate the value of each charge.
explanation: Let us assume that one of two charges is x {tex}\mu{/tex} C. Therefore, other charge will be (7 - x) {tex}\mu{/tex} C. If they repel each other, then both charges will be of the same nature and repulsion between them can be calculated by Coulomb's law. Thus, {tex}F = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} F = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex} \frac { \left( x \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \right) ( 7 - x ) \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { ( 0.3 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, F = 1N Thus, 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9-12 x(7 - x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10 = x(7 - x) {tex}\therefore{/tex} x 2 - 7x + 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (x - 5) = 0 x = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C or 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C Therefore, charges are 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C. They can also be -2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and will result in the same amount of repulsive force.
solution: Let us assume that one of two charges is x {tex}\mu{/tex} C. Therefore, other charge will be (7 - x) {tex}\mu{/tex} C. If they repel each other, then both charges will be of the same nature and repulsion between them can be calculated by Coulomb's law. Thus, {tex}F = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} F = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex} \frac { \left( x \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \right) ( 7 - x ) \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { ( 0.3 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, F = 1N Thus, 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9-12 x(7 - x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10 = x(7 - x) {tex}\therefore{/tex} x 2 - 7x + 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (x - 5) = 0 x = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C or 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C Therefore, charges are 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C. They can also be -2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and will result in the same amount of repulsive force.
solution_html:
Let us assume that one of two charges is x {tex}\mu{/tex}C. Therefore, other charge will be (7 - x) {tex}\mu{/tex}C.
If they repel each other, then both charges will be of the same nature and repulsion between them can be calculated by Coulomb's law.
Thus, {tex}F = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
F = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex} \frac { \left( x \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \right) ( 7 - x ) \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { ( 0.3 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, F = 1N
Thus,
9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109-12x(7 - x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10 = x(7 - x) 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 - 7x + 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (x - 5) = 0
x = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C or 5{tex}\mu{/tex}C
Therefore, charges are 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C and 5{tex}\mu{/tex}C. They can also be -2{tex}\mu{/tex}C and -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C and will result in the same amount of repulsive force.

question_html:
Figure shows two large metal plates P1 and P2 tightly held against each other and placed between two equal and unlike point charges perpendicular to the line joining them.

  1. What will happen to the plates when they are released?
  2. Draw the pattern of the electric field lines for the system.
question_text: Figure shows two large metal plates P 1 and P 2 tightly held against each other and placed between two equal and unlike point charges perpendicular to the line joining them. What will happen to the plates when they are released? Draw the pattern of the electric field lines for the system.
explanation: By electrostatic induction, charge induces on the plates and opposite nature of charge appears on the surface facing each other. Therefore, they start attracting each other. Field lines must be perpendicular to the plates. Also, equispaced field lines exist between two plates as the electric field between them is uniform. When plates are released, they will tend to move away from one another; plate P 1 moving towards +Q and P 2 towards -Q due to attraction. The pattern of the electric field lines for the system is shown below:
solution: By electrostatic induction, charge induces on the plates and opposite nature of charge appears on the surface facing each other. Therefore, they start attracting each other. Field lines must be perpendicular to the plates. Also, equispaced field lines exist between two plates as the electric field between them is uniform. When plates are released, they will tend to move away from one another; plate P 1 moving towards +Q and P 2 towards -Q due to attraction. The pattern of the electric field lines for the system is shown below:

1. By electrostatic induction, charge induces on the plates and opposite nature of charge appears on the surface facing each other. Therefore, they start attracting each other.
  2. Field lines must be perpendicular to the plates. Also, equispaced field lines exist between two plates as the electric field between them is uniform.

question_html: State Gauss' law in electrostatics. Using this law, derive an expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet.
question_text: State Gauss' law in electrostatics. Using this law, derive an expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet.
explanation: Gauss Theorem: The net outward electric flux through a closed surface is equal to {tex}\frac1{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed within the surface. {tex}\oint\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS\;}=\frac1{\varepsilon_0}\sum_{}q{/tex} Electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet: Let electric charge be uniformly distributed over the surface of a thin, non-conducting infinite sheet. Consider a cylindrical Gaussian surface having two plane faces A and B lying on the opposite sides and parallel to the charged sheet and the cylindrical surface perpendicular to the sheet as shown in figure. By symmetry, the electric field strength at every point on the flat surface is the same and its direction is normal outwards at the points on the two plane surfaces and parallel to the curved surface. Total electric flux, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\oint E.dS=\int_{S_1}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_1}+\int_{S_2}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_2}+\int_{S_3}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_3}\\\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\oint E.dS=\int_{S_1}E.dS_1\cos\;0+\int_{S_2}E\;dS_2\cos\;0+\int_{S_3}EdS_3\cos90{/tex} {tex}\oint E . d S=E \int_{S_{1}} d S_{1}+E \int_{S_{2}} d S_{2}+0{/tex} {tex}\oint E . d S=2 E \int_{S_{1}} d S_{1}=2 E a{/tex} ...... (i) If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is charge per unit area of sheet and 'a' is the intersecting area, the charge enclosed by Gaussian surface = σa According to Gauss’s theorem Total electric flux {tex}=\oint E . d S=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} q{/tex} {tex}\oint E . d S=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \sigma a{/tex} ..... (ii) From (i) and (ii) we have, {tex}E=\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Thus electric field strength due to an infinite flat sheet of charge is independent of the distance of the point and is directed normally away from the charge.
solution: Gauss Theorem: The net outward electric flux through a closed surface is equal to {tex}\frac1{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed within the surface. {tex}\oint\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS\;}=\frac1{\varepsilon_0}\sum_{}q{/tex} Electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet: Let electric charge be uniformly distributed over the surface of a thin, non-conducting infinite sheet. Consider a cylindrical Gaussian surface having two plane faces A and B lying on the opposite sides and parallel to the charged sheet and the cylindrical surface perpendicular to the sheet as shown in figure. By symmetry, the electric field strength at every point on the flat surface is the same and its direction is normal outwards at the points on the two plane surfaces and parallel to the curved surface. Total electric flux, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\oint E.dS=\int_{S_1}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_1}+\int_{S_2}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_2}+\int_{S_3}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_3}\\\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\oint E.dS=\int_{S_1}E.dS_1\cos\;0+\int_{S_2}E\;dS_2\cos\;0+\int_{S_3}EdS_3\cos90{/tex} {tex}\oint E . d S=E \int_{S_{1}} d S_{1}+E \int_{S_{2}} d S_{2}+0{/tex} {tex}\oint E . d S=2 E \int_{S_{1}} d S_{1}=2 E a{/tex} ...... (i) If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is charge per unit area of sheet and 'a' is the intersecting area, the charge enclosed by Gaussian surface = σa According to Gauss’s theorem Total electric flux {tex}=\oint E . d S=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} q{/tex} {tex}\oint E . d S=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \sigma a{/tex} ..... (ii) From (i) and (ii) we have, {tex}E=\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_0}{/tex} Thus electric field strength due to an infinite flat sheet of charge is independent of the distance of the point and is directed normally away from the charge.
solution_html:
Gauss Theorem: The net outward electric flux through a closed surface is equal to {tex}\frac1{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the net charge enclosed within the surface.
{tex}\oint\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS\;}=\frac1{\varepsilon_0}\sum_{}q{/tex}
Electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet:
Let electric charge be uniformly distributed over the surface of a thin, non-conducting infinite sheet. Consider a cylindrical Gaussian surface having two plane faces A and B lying on the opposite sides and parallel to the charged sheet and the cylindrical surface perpendicular to the sheet as shown in figure.
By symmetry, the electric field strength at every point on the flat surface is the same and its direction is normal outwards at the points on the two plane surfaces and parallel to the curved surface.






Total electric flux,
{tex}\begin{array}{l}\oint E.dS=\int_{S_1}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_1}+\int_{S_2}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_2}+\int_{S_3}\overrightarrow E.\overrightarrow{dS_3}\\\end{array}{/tex}
{tex}\oint E.dS=\int_{S_1}E.dS_1\cos\;0+\int_{S_2}E\;dS_2\cos\;0+\int_{S_3}EdS_3\cos90{/tex}
{tex}\oint E . d S=E \int_{S_{1}} d S_{1}+E \int_{S_{2}} d S_{2}+0{/tex}
{tex}\oint E . d S=2 E \int_{S_{1}} d S_{1}=2 E a{/tex} ...... (i)
If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is charge per unit area of sheet and 'a' is the intersecting area,
the charge enclosed by Gaussian surface = σa
According to Gauss’s theorem
Total electric flux {tex}=\oint E . d S=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} q{/tex}
{tex}\oint E . d S=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \sigma a{/tex} ..... (ii)
From (i) and (ii) we have,
{tex}E=\frac\sigma{2\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
Thus electric field strength due to an infinite flat sheet of charge is independent of the distance of the point and is directed normally away from the charge.

question_html: Why is it necessary that the field lines from a point charge placed in the vicinity of a conductor must be normal to the conductor at every point?
question_text: Why is it necessary that the field lines from a point charge placed in the vicinity of a conductor must be normal to the conductor at every point?
explanation: If the field lines are not normal, then the field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} would have a tangential component which will make electrons move along the surface creating surface currents and the conductor will not be in equilibrium.
solution: If the field lines are not normal, then the field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} would have a tangential component which will make electrons move along the surface creating surface currents and the conductor will not be in equilibrium.
solution_html: If the field lines are not normal, then the field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} would have a tangential component which will make electrons move along the surface creating surface currents and the conductor will not be in equilibrium.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. A charge q is enclosed by a spherical surface of radius R. Find the electric flux.
question_text: Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. A charge q is enclosed by a spherical surface of radius R. Find the electric flux.
explanation: The total electric flux linked with a surface is equal to the total number of electric lines of force passing through the surface when surface is held normal to the direction of electric field. The SI unit of electric flux is Nm 2 C -1 . According to Gauss theorem, if charge q is enclosed by closed surface, therefore total electric flux linked with the closed surface {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\oint_S{/tex} E. dS = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
solution: The total electric flux linked with a surface is equal to the total number of electric lines of force passing through the surface when surface is held normal to the direction of electric field. The SI unit of electric flux is Nm 2 C -1 . According to Gauss theorem, if charge q is enclosed by closed surface, therefore total electric flux linked with the closed surface {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\oint_S{/tex} E. dS = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}
solution_html:
The total electric flux linked with a surface is equal to the total number of electric lines of force passing through the surface when surface is held normal to the direction of electric field. The SI unit of electric flux is Nm2 C-1.
According to Gauss theorem, if charge q is enclosed by closed surface, therefore total electric flux linked with the closed surface
{tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\oint_S{/tex} E. dS = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State Gauss theorem in electrostatics.
question_text: State Gauss theorem in electrostatics.
explanation: Gauss's theorem states that the flux of electric field through any closed surface S is {tex}\frac{1}{ \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} times the total charge q enclosed by S. Mathematically, {tex}\phi_{E}=\oint_\limits{S} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{ S}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution: Gauss's theorem states that the flux of electric field through any closed surface S is {tex}\frac{1}{ \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} times the total charge q enclosed by S. Mathematically, {tex}\phi_{E}=\oint_\limits{S} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{ S}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Gauss's theorem states that the flux of electric field through any closed surface S is {tex}\frac{1}{ \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} times the total charge q enclosed by S.
Mathematically,
{tex}\phi_{E}=\oint_\limits{S} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{ S}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Gauss's law in electrostatics. Using this theorem, show mathematically that for any point outside the shell, the field due to uniformly charged thin spherical shell is the same as if entire charge of the shell is concentrated at the centre. Why do you expect the electric field inside the shell to be zero according to this theorem?
  2. Draw the field lines when the charge density of the sphere is
    1. positive,
    2. negative.
question_text: State Gauss's law in electrostatics. Using this theorem, show mathematically that for any point outside the shell, the field due to uniformly charged thin spherical shell is the same as if entire charge of the shell is concentrated at the centre. Why do you expect the electric field inside the shell to be zero according to this theorem? Draw the field lines when the charge density of the sphere is positive, negative.
explanation: Gauss Law states that the total electric flux out of a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed divided by the permittivity. Consider a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius R carrying charge Q If E is electric field outside the shell, then by symmetry electric field strength has same magnitude E 0 on the Gaussian surface and is directed radially outward. Also the directions of normal at each point is radially outward, so angle between E i and dS is zero at each point. Hence, electric flux through Gaussian surface. {tex}\oint{/tex} S = {tex}\vec{E}_0 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}{/tex} . {tex}\oint{/tex} = E 0 dS cos 0 = E 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 Now, Gaussian surface is outside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is Q. Hence, by Gauss’s theorem {tex}\oint{/tex} s = {tex}\vec{E}_0 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} charged enclosed {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times Q \Rightarrow E_0{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{r^2}{/tex} Thus, electric field outside a charged thin spherical shell is the same as if the whole charge Q is concentrated at the centre. If σ is the surface charge density of the spherical shell Q = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} R 2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} coulomb {tex}\therefore{/tex} E 0 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{4 \pi R^2 \sigma}{r^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R^2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}
solution: Gauss Law states that the total electric flux out of a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed divided by the permittivity. Consider a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius R carrying charge Q If E is electric field outside the shell, then by symmetry electric field strength has same magnitude E 0 on the Gaussian surface and is directed radially outward. Also the directions of normal at each point is radially outward, so angle between E i and dS is zero at each point. Hence, electric flux through Gaussian surface. {tex}\oint{/tex} S = {tex}\vec{E}_0 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}{/tex} . {tex}\oint{/tex} = E 0 dS cos 0 = E 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 Now, Gaussian surface is outside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is Q. Hence, by Gauss’s theorem {tex}\oint{/tex} s = {tex}\vec{E}_0 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} charged enclosed {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times Q \Rightarrow E_0{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{r^2}{/tex} Thus, electric field outside a charged thin spherical shell is the same as if the whole charge Q is concentrated at the centre. If σ is the surface charge density of the spherical shell Q = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} R 2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} coulomb {tex}\therefore{/tex} E 0 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{4 \pi R^2 \sigma}{r^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R^2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Gauss Law states that the total electric flux out of a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed divided by the permittivity.

    Consider a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius R carrying charge Q If E is electric field outside the shell, then by symmetry electric field strength has same magnitude E0 on the Gaussian surface and is directed radially outward. Also the directions of normal at each point is radially outward, so angle between Ei and dS is zero at each point.
    Hence, electric flux through Gaussian surface. {tex}\oint{/tex}S = {tex}\vec{E}_0 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}{/tex}. {tex}\oint{/tex} = E0 dS cos 0 = E0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2
    Now, Gaussian surface is outside the given charged shell, so charge enclosed by Gaussian surface is Q.
    Hence, by Gauss’s theorem {tex}\oint{/tex}s = {tex}\vec{E}_0 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} charged enclosed {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \times Q \Rightarrow E_0{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{r^2}{/tex}
    Thus, electric field outside a charged thin spherical shell is the same as if the whole charge Q is concentrated at the centre. If σ is the surface charge density of the spherical shell Q = 4{tex}\pi{/tex}R2{tex}\sigma{/tex} coulomb
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} E0 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{4 \pi R^2 \sigma}{r^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R^2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Which orientation of an electric dipole in a uniform electric field would correspond to stable equilibrium?
question_text: Which orientation of an electric dipole in a uniform electric field would correspond to stable equilibrium?
explanation: The dipole is in stable equilibrium when direction of electric dipole moment of electric dipole is in the direction of electric field.
solution: The dipole is in stable equilibrium when direction of electric dipole moment of electric dipole is in the direction of electric field.
solution_html: The dipole is in stable equilibrium when direction of electric dipole moment of electric dipole is in the direction of electric field.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A uniformly charged conducting sphere of 2.5 m in diameter has a surface charge density of 100 {tex}\mu{/tex}C m-2. Calculate the

  1. charge on the sphere and
  2. total electric flux passing through the sphere.
question_text: A uniformly charged conducting sphere of 2.5 m in diameter has a surface charge density of 100 {tex}\mu{/tex} C m -2 . Calculate the charge on the sphere and total electric flux passing through the sphere.
explanation: Here, {tex}\sigma=100 \mu \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-2}=10^{-4} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-2} ; \mathrm{R}=\frac{2 \cdot 5}{2}=1.25 \mathrm{m}{/tex} Now, q = {tex}4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \sigma{/tex} = {tex}4 \pi \times(1 \cdot 25)^{2} \times 10^{-4}=1 \cdot 96 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1 \cdot 96 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \cdot 854 \times 10^{-12}}=2 \cdot 21 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: Here, {tex}\sigma=100 \mu \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-2}=10^{-4} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-2} ; \mathrm{R}=\frac{2 \cdot 5}{2}=1.25 \mathrm{m}{/tex} Now, q = {tex}4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \sigma{/tex} = {tex}4 \pi \times(1 \cdot 25)^{2} \times 10^{-4}=1 \cdot 96 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1 \cdot 96 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \cdot 854 \times 10^{-12}}=2 \cdot 21 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\sigma=100 \mu \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-2}=10^{-4} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-2} ; \mathrm{R}=\frac{2 \cdot 5}{2}=1.25 \mathrm{m}{/tex}

  1. Now, q = {tex}4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \sigma{/tex} 
    = {tex}4 \pi \times(1 \cdot 25)^{2} \times 10^{-4}=1 \cdot 96 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{C}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1 \cdot 96 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \cdot 854 \times 10^{-12}}=2 \cdot 21 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
In Fig., the electric field is directed along positive X- direction and given by
Ex = 5 A x + 2 B,
where E is in NC-1 and x is in metre. A and B are constants with dimensions.

Taking A = 10 NC-1m-1 and B = 5 NC-1m-1, calculate

  1. the electric flux through the cube.
  2. net charge enclosed within the cube
question_text: In Fig., the electric field is directed along positive X- direction and given by E x = 5 A x + 2 B, where E is in NC -1 and x is in metre. A and B are constants with dimensions. Taking A = 10 NC -1 m -1 and B = 5 NC -1 m -1 , calculate the electric flux through the cube. net charge enclosed within the cube
explanation: Since the electric field has X-component only, the angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between the direction of electric field and the area vectors representing the four faces of the cube (except the faces M and N shown shaded) is {tex}\pi{/tex} /2 in each case. Therefore, electric flux through each of these four faces, {tex}\phi{/tex} = E x S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = E x S cos {tex}\pi{/tex} /2 = 0 The electric field varies along X-axis as given by E x = 5 A x + 2 B Therefore, magnitude of electric field at face M (x = 0), E M = 5 A x + 2 B = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 10 NC -1 and magnitude of electric field at face N (x = 0.1 m), E N = 5 A x + 2B = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 15 NC -1 The electric flux through the face M, {tex}\phi{/tex} M = E M S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} (0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1) cos 0° = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 10 NmC -1 (inwards) The electric flux through the face N, {tex}\phi{/tex} N = E N S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} (0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1) cos 0° = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 15 NmC -1 (outwards) Therefore, net electric flux through the cube, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\phi{/tex} N - {tex}\phi{/tex} M = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 15 - 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 10 = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 05 NmC -1 Now, from Gauss' theorem, we have {tex}q=\varepsilon_{0} \phi{/tex} = 8 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 05 = 4 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 43 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 C
solution: Since the electric field has X-component only, the angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between the direction of electric field and the area vectors representing the four faces of the cube (except the faces M and N shown shaded) is {tex}\pi{/tex} /2 in each case. Therefore, electric flux through each of these four faces, {tex}\phi{/tex} = E x S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = E x S cos {tex}\pi{/tex} /2 = 0 The electric field varies along X-axis as given by E x = 5 A x + 2 B Therefore, magnitude of electric field at face M (x = 0), E M = 5 A x + 2 B = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 10 NC -1 and magnitude of electric field at face N (x = 0.1 m), E N = 5 A x + 2B = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 15 NC -1 The electric flux through the face M, {tex}\phi{/tex} M = E M S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} (0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1) cos 0° = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 10 NmC -1 (inwards) The electric flux through the face N, {tex}\phi{/tex} N = E N S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} (0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1) cos 0° = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 15 NmC -1 (outwards) Therefore, net electric flux through the cube, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\phi{/tex} N - {tex}\phi{/tex} M = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 15 - 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 10 = 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 05 NmC -1 Now, from Gauss' theorem, we have {tex}q=\varepsilon_{0} \phi{/tex} = 8 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 05 = 4 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 43 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 C
solution_html:
1. Since the electric field has X-component only, the angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between the direction of electric field and the area vectors representing the four faces of the cube (except the faces M and N shown shaded) is {tex}\pi{/tex}/2 in each case.

    Therefore, electric flux through each of these four faces,
    {tex}\phi{/tex} = ExS cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = ExS cos {tex}\pi{/tex}/2 = 0
    The electric field varies along X-axis as given by
    Ex = 5 A x + 2 B
    Therefore, magnitude of electric field at face M (x = 0),
    EM = 5 A x + 2 B = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 10 NC-1
    and magnitude of electric field at face N (x = 0.1 m),
    EN = 5 A x + 2B = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}1 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 15 NC-1
    The electric flux through the face M,
    {tex}\phi{/tex}M = EM S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} (0{tex}\cdot{/tex}1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}1) cos 0° = 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}10 NmC-1 (inwards)
    The electric flux through the face N,
    {tex}\phi{/tex}N = EN S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} (0{tex}\cdot{/tex}1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}1) cos 0° = 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}15 NmC-1 (outwards)
    Therefore, net electric flux through the cube,
    {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\phi{/tex}N - {tex}\phi{/tex}M = 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}15 - 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}10 = 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}05 NmC-1
  2. Now, from Gauss' theorem, we have
    {tex}q=\varepsilon_{0} \phi{/tex}
    = 8{tex}\cdot{/tex}854 {tex}\times{/tex}10-12{tex}\times{/tex}0{tex}\cdot{/tex}05 = 4{tex}\cdot{/tex}43 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 C

subject: Physics
question_html: Is electric flux a scalar or a vector? Give the SI unit of electric flux.
question_text: Is electric flux a scalar or a vector? Give the SI unit of electric flux.
explanation: Electric flux is a scalar quantity and its unit is Nm 2 C -1 .
solution: Electric flux is a scalar quantity and its unit is Nm 2 C -1 .
solution_html: Electric flux is a scalar quantity and its unit is Nm2C-1.

subject: Physics
question_html: A uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = Ex {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} N/C for x > 0 and {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = -Ex {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} N/C for x < 0 are given. A right circular cylinder of length/cm and radius r cm has its centre at the origin and its axis along the x-axis. Find out the net outward flux. Using Gauss's law write the expression for the net charge within the cylinder.
question_text: A uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = E x {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} N/C for x > 0 and {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = -E x {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} N/C for x < 0 are given. A right circular cylinder of length/cm and radius r cm has its centre at the origin and its axis along the x-axis. Find out the net outward flux. Using Gauss's law write the expression for the net charge within the cylinder.
explanation: {tex}\varphi_1{/tex} = {tex}\oint_{S_1}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{d S}_1{/tex} = {tex}\oint_{S_1}\left(E_x \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(\mathrm{dS}_1 \hat{i}\right){/tex} = E x S 1 Electric flux through flat surface S 2 {tex}\varphi_1{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_2} \vec{E}_2 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}_2{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_2}\left(-E_x \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(-\mathrm{dS}_2 \hat{i}\right){/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_2} E_x \mathrm{dS}_2{/tex} = E x S 2 Electric flux through curved surface S 3 {tex}\varphi_3{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_3}\left(\vec{E}_3 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}_3\right){/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_3} E_3 \mathrm{dS}_3 {/tex} cos 90 o = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net electric flux, {tex}\varphi{/tex} = {tex}\varphi_1+\varphi_2{/tex} = E x (S 1 + S 2 ) But S 1 = S 2 = {tex}\pi\left(r \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\varphi{/tex} = E x .2 ( {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ) units By Gauss's law, {tex}\varphi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} q q = {tex}\varepsilon_0 \varphi{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_0 E_x\left(2 \pi r^2 \times 10^{-4}\right){/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi \varepsilon_0 E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} = 4 {tex} \pi \varepsilon_0\left(\frac{E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9}\left[\frac{E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}}{2}\right]{/tex} = 5.56 E x r 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 coulomb.
solution: {tex}\varphi_1{/tex} = {tex}\oint_{S_1}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{d S}_1{/tex} = {tex}\oint_{S_1}\left(E_x \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(\mathrm{dS}_1 \hat{i}\right){/tex} = E x S 1 Electric flux through flat surface S 2 {tex}\varphi_1{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_2} \vec{E}_2 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}_2{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_2}\left(-E_x \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(-\mathrm{dS}_2 \hat{i}\right){/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_2} E_x \mathrm{dS}_2{/tex} = E x S 2 Electric flux through curved surface S 3 {tex}\varphi_3{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_3}\left(\vec{E}_3 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}_3\right){/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_3} E_3 \mathrm{dS}_3 {/tex} cos 90 o = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net electric flux, {tex}\varphi{/tex} = {tex}\varphi_1+\varphi_2{/tex} = E x (S 1 + S 2 ) But S 1 = S 2 = {tex}\pi\left(r \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\varphi{/tex} = E x .2 ( {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ) units By Gauss's law, {tex}\varphi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} q q = {tex}\varepsilon_0 \varphi{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_0 E_x\left(2 \pi r^2 \times 10^{-4}\right){/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi \varepsilon_0 E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} = 4 {tex} \pi \varepsilon_0\left(\frac{E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9}\left[\frac{E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}}{2}\right]{/tex} = 5.56 E x r 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 coulomb.
solution_html:
{tex}\varphi_1{/tex} = {tex}\oint_{S_1}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}_1{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{d S}_1{/tex} = {tex}\oint_{S_1}\left(E_x \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(\mathrm{dS}_1 \hat{i}\right){/tex} = ExS1
Electric flux through flat surface S2
{tex}\varphi_1{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_2} \vec{E}_2 \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}_2{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_2}\left(-E_x \hat{i}\right) \cdot\left(-\mathrm{dS}_2 \hat{i}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\int_{S_2} E_x \mathrm{dS}_2{/tex} = ExS2
Electric flux through curved surface S3
{tex}\varphi_3{/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_3}\left(\vec{E}_3 \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}_3\right){/tex} = {tex}\int_{S_3} E_3 \mathrm{dS}_3 {/tex} cos 90o = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Net electric flux, {tex}\varphi{/tex} = {tex}\varphi_1+\varphi_2{/tex} = Ex(S1 + S2)
But S1 = S2 = {tex}\pi\left(r \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2 {tex}\times{/tex}10-4 m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\varphi{/tex} = Ex.2 ({tex}\pi{/tex}r2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) units
By Gauss's law, {tex}\varphi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex}q
q = {tex}\varepsilon_0 \varphi{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_0 E_x\left(2 \pi r^2 \times 10^{-4}\right){/tex}
= 2{tex}\pi \varepsilon_0 E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} = 4{tex} \pi \varepsilon_0\left(\frac{E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}}{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9}\left[\frac{E_x r^2 \times 10^{-4}}{2}\right]{/tex}
= 5.56 Exr2 {tex}\times{/tex}10-11 coulomb.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A spherical Gaussian surface encloses a charge of 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C.

  1. Calculate the electric flux passing through the surface,
  2. If the radius of the Gaussian surface is doubled, how would the flux change?
question_text: A spherical Gaussian surface encloses a charge of 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C. Calculate the electric flux passing through the surface, If the radius of the Gaussian surface is doubled, how would the flux change?
explanation: {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{8.85 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}}{/tex} = 10 4 Nm 2 C -1 {tex}\phi_{E}{/tex} = 10 4 Nm 2 C -1 , because the charge enclosed is the same as in the case (i).
solution: {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{8.85 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}}{/tex} = 10 4 Nm 2 C -1 {tex}\phi_{E}{/tex} = 10 4 Nm 2 C -1 , because the charge enclosed is the same as in the case (i).
solution_html:
1. {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{8.85 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}}{/tex}
    = 104 Nm2 C-1
  2. {tex}\phi_{E}{/tex} = 104 Nm2 C-1, because the charge enclosed is the same as in the case (i).

subject: Physics
question_html:
State Gauss theorem. Show, by using a suitable example, that this theorem is based on Coulomb's inverse square law.

A point charge + 10{tex}\mu{/tex}C is at a distance 5 cm directly above the centre of a square of side 10 cm as shown in Figure.

What is the magnitude of the electric flux through the square?
question_text: State Gauss theorem. Show, by using a suitable example, that this theorem is based on Coulomb's inverse square law. A point charge + 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is at a distance 5 cm directly above the centre of a square of side 10 cm as shown in Figure. What is the magnitude of the electric flux through the square?
explanation: Gauss theorem states that the total electric flux through a closed surface enclosing a charge is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the magnitude of the charge enclosed. Consider that two point charges q 1 and q 2 are placed at points A and B at a distance r apart in a vacuum. Let E be the magnitude of the electric field at the location of point charge q 2 due to the charge q 1 . Then, F = q 2 E ...(i) According to Gauss' theorem, the total electric flux through the spherical Gaussian surface is given by {tex}\oint_{S} \vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{d S}=\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}} \| \vec{d \mathrm{S}}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{d S}=\mathrm{E} d S{/tex} Hence, {tex}\oint \mathrm{E} d \mathrm{S}=\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Now, the value of E (magnitude of the electric field) is the same at all the points on the spherical surface i.e. E is constant. Therefore, {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\mathrm{E} \oint d S{/tex} Now, {tex}\oint d \mathrm{S}{/tex} = surface area of the spherical gaussian surface of radius r = {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\mathrm{E} \times 4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1}}{r^{2}}{/tex} ....(ii) From the equation (i) and (ii) we have {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} It is the mathematical form fo Coulomb's law in electrostatics. Here, q = +10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C = 10 -5 C Consider that the charge q is at a distance of 5 cm from the square ABCD of each side 10 cm as shown in Figure. The square ABCD can be considered as one of the six faces of a cube of each side 10 cm. Then, according to Gauss' theorem, total electric flux through all the six faces of the cube, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Obviously, the flux through the square ABCD will be {tex}\phi'=\frac{1}{6} \times \phi=\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{10^{-5}}{8 \cdot 854 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 N m 2 C -1
solution: Gauss theorem states that the total electric flux through a closed surface enclosing a charge is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the magnitude of the charge enclosed. Consider that two point charges q 1 and q 2 are placed at points A and B at a distance r apart in a vacuum. Let E be the magnitude of the electric field at the location of point charge q 2 due to the charge q 1 . Then, F = q 2 E ...(i) According to Gauss' theorem, the total electric flux through the spherical Gaussian surface is given by {tex}\oint_{S} \vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{d S}=\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}} \| \vec{d \mathrm{S}}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{d S}=\mathrm{E} d S{/tex} Hence, {tex}\oint \mathrm{E} d \mathrm{S}=\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Now, the value of E (magnitude of the electric field) is the same at all the points on the spherical surface i.e. E is constant. Therefore, {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\mathrm{E} \oint d S{/tex} Now, {tex}\oint d \mathrm{S}{/tex} = surface area of the spherical gaussian surface of radius r = {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\mathrm{E} \times 4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1}}{r^{2}}{/tex} ....(ii) From the equation (i) and (ii) we have {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} It is the mathematical form fo Coulomb's law in electrostatics. Here, q = +10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C = 10 -5 C Consider that the charge q is at a distance of 5 cm from the square ABCD of each side 10 cm as shown in Figure. The square ABCD can be considered as one of the six faces of a cube of each side 10 cm. Then, according to Gauss' theorem, total electric flux through all the six faces of the cube, {tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Obviously, the flux through the square ABCD will be {tex}\phi'=\frac{1}{6} \times \phi=\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{10^{-5}}{8 \cdot 854 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 N m 2 C -1
solution_html:
Gauss theorem states that the total electric flux through a closed surface enclosing a charge is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}{/tex} times the magnitude of the charge enclosed.
Consider that two point charges q1 and q2 are placed at points A and B at a distance r apart in a vacuum.

Let E be the magnitude of the electric field at the location of point charge q2 due to the charge q1. Then,
F = q2 E ...(i)
According to Gauss' theorem, the total electric flux through the spherical Gaussian surface is given by 
{tex}\oint_{S} \vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{d S}=\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}} \| \vec{d \mathrm{S}}{/tex},  {tex}\vec{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \vec{d S}=\mathrm{E} d S{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\oint \mathrm{E} d \mathrm{S}=\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
Now, the value of E (magnitude of the electric field) is the same at all the points on the spherical surface i.e. E is constant. Therefore,
{tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\mathrm{E} \oint d S{/tex}
Now, {tex}\oint d \mathrm{S}{/tex} = surface area of the spherical gaussian surface of radius r = {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\mathrm{E} \times 4 \pi r^{2}{/tex}
or {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1}}{r^{2}}{/tex} ....(ii)
From the equation (i) and (ii) we have
{tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
It is the mathematical form fo Coulomb's law in electrostatics.

Here, q = +10 {tex}\mu{/tex}C = 10-5 C
Consider that the charge q is at a distance of 5 cm from the square ABCD of each side 10 cm as shown in Figure.

The square ABCD can be considered as one of the six faces of a cube of each side 10 cm. Then, according to Gauss' theorem, total electric flux through all the six faces of the cube,
{tex}\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
Obviously, the flux through the square ABCD will be
{tex}\phi'=\frac{1}{6} \times \phi=\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{10^{-5}}{8 \cdot 854 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex}
= 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 N m2 C-1

question_html: Define electric line of force and give its two important properties.
question_text: Define electric line of force and give its two important properties.
explanation: An electric line of force is a smooth curve drawn in an electric field. The tangent at any point on the curve indicates the direction of the electric field at that point. Properties: The lines of force start from the positive charge and end at the negative charge. Electric field lines never form closed loops.
solution: An electric line of force is a smooth curve drawn in an electric field. The tangent at any point on the curve indicates the direction of the electric field at that point. Properties: The lines of force start from the positive charge and end at the negative charge. Electric field lines never form closed loops.
solution_html:
An electric line of force is a smooth curve drawn in an electric field. The tangent at any point on the curve indicates the direction of the electric field at that point.
Properties:

  • The lines of force start from the positive charge and end at the negative charge.
  • Electric field lines never form closed loops.

question_html: Two fixed point charges +4e and +e units are separated by a distance a. Where should a third charge q be placed for it to be in equilibrium?
question_text: Two fixed point charges +4e and +e units are separated by a distance a. Where should a third charge q be placed for it to be in equilibrium?
explanation: Suppose the three charges are placed as shown in Fig. Let the charge q be positive. For the equilibrium of charge + q, we must have Force of repulsion F 1 between + 4e and + q = Force of repulsion F 2 between + e and + q or {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{4 e \times q}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e \times q}{(a-x)^{2}}{/tex} or 4 (a - x) 2 = x 2 or 2 (a - x) = {tex}\pm{/tex} x {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2 a}{3}{/tex} or 2a As the charge q is placed between + 4e and + e, so only x = {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} is possible. Hence for equilibrium, the charge q must be placed at a distance {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} from the charge + 4e. We have considered the charge q to be positive. If we displace it slightly towards charge e, from the equilibrium position, then F 1 will decrease and F 2 will increase and a net force (F 2 - F 1 ) will act on q towards left i.e., towards the equilibrium position. Hence the equilibrium of positive q is stable. Now if we take charge q to be negative, the forces F 1 and F 2 will be attractive, as shown in Fig. The charge - q will still be in equilibrium at x = {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} . However, if we displace charge - q slightly towards the right, then F 1 force will decrease as distance increases and F 2 will increase because distance decreases. A net force (F 2 - F 1 ) will act on - q towards the right i.e., away from the equilibrium position. So it will be in in unstable equilibrium position.
solution: Suppose the three charges are placed as shown in Fig. Let the charge q be positive. For the equilibrium of charge + q, we must have Force of repulsion F 1 between + 4e and + q = Force of repulsion F 2 between + e and + q or {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{4 e \times q}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e \times q}{(a-x)^{2}}{/tex} or 4 (a - x) 2 = x 2 or 2 (a - x) = {tex}\pm{/tex} x {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2 a}{3}{/tex} or 2a As the charge q is placed between + 4e and + e, so only x = {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} is possible. Hence for equilibrium, the charge q must be placed at a distance {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} from the charge + 4e. We have considered the charge q to be positive. If we displace it slightly towards charge e, from the equilibrium position, then F 1 will decrease and F 2 will increase and a net force (F 2 - F 1 ) will act on q towards left i.e., towards the equilibrium position. Hence the equilibrium of positive q is stable. Now if we take charge q to be negative, the forces F 1 and F 2 will be attractive, as shown in Fig. The charge - q will still be in equilibrium at x = {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} . However, if we displace charge - q slightly towards the right, then F 1 force will decrease as distance increases and F 2 will increase because distance decreases. A net force (F 2 - F 1 ) will act on - q towards the right i.e., away from the equilibrium position. So it will be in in unstable equilibrium position.
solution_html:
Suppose the three charges are placed as shown in Fig. Let the charge q be positive.

For the equilibrium of charge + q, we must have Force of repulsion F1 between + 4e and + q = Force of repulsion F2 between + e and + q
or {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{4 e \times q}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e \times q}{(a-x)^{2}}{/tex}
or 4 (a - x)2 = x2
or 2 (a - x) = {tex}\pm{/tex}x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2 a}{3}{/tex} or 2a
As the charge q is placed between + 4e and + e, so only x = {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} is possible. Hence for equilibrium, the charge q must be placed at a distance {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} from the charge + 4e.
We have considered the charge q to be positive. If we displace it slightly towards charge e, from the equilibrium position, then F1 will decrease and F2 will increase and a net force (F2 - F1) will act on q towards left i.e., towards the equilibrium position. Hence the equilibrium of positive q is stable.
Now if we take charge q to be negative, the forces F1 and F2 will be attractive, as shown in Fig.

The charge - q will still be in equilibrium at x = {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex}. However, if we displace charge - q slightly towards the right, then F1 force will decrease as distance increases and F2 will increase because distance decreases. A net force (F2 - F1) will act on - q towards the right i.e., away from the equilibrium position. So it will be in in unstable  equilibrium position.
