question_html: State Coulomb's law of force between two electric charges and state its limitations. Also, define the SI unit of electric charge.
question_text: State Coulomb's law of force between two electric charges and state its limitations. Also, define the SI unit of electric charge.
explanation: According to Coulomb's law, the force of attraction or repulsion between two charged bodies is directly proportional to the product of their charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Limitation: Coulomb's law cannot be applied if the shape of the charges is arbitrary because, in irregular shapes, it becomes difficult to determine the distance between the particles. A coulomb is defined as the amount of charge that passes through an electrical conductor carrying one ampere per second.

A coulomb is defined as the amount of charge that passes through an electrical conductor carrying one ampere per second.

A coulomb is defined as the amount of charge that passes through an electrical conductor carrying one ampere per second.

{tex}\phi_{\in}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{1}{\in_0}{/tex}(charged enclosed) = 0
[Since the Gaussian surface is not enclosing any charge]
Thus {tex}\phi_{\in}{/tex} = E.4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = 0 ...(ii)
[Since the Gaussian surface is not enclosing any charge]
Thus {tex}\phi_{\in}{/tex}= E.4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = 0
Hence , no electric field exists inside a hollow charged sphere.

question_text: A charge of 17.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C is distributed uniformly over a large sheet of area 200 m 2 . Calculate the electric field intensity at a distance of 20 cm from it in the air.
explanation: Surface charge density of the sheet, {tex}\sigma=\frac{q}{A}=\frac{17.7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{C}}{200 \mathrm{~m}^{2}}{/tex} = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 Cm -2 The electric field at a distance of 20 cm from it in air, E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{8.85 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 NC -1
solution: Surface charge density of the sheet, {tex}\sigma=\frac{q}{A}=\frac{17.7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{C}}{200 \mathrm{~m}^{2}}{/tex} = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 Cm -2 The electric field at a distance of 20 cm from it in air, E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{8.85 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 NC -1
solution_html:
Surface charge density of the sheet,
{tex}\sigma=\frac{q}{A}=\frac{17.7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{C}}{200 \mathrm{~m}^{2}}{/tex} = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 Cm-2
The electric field at a distance of 20 cm from it in air,
E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{8.85 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 NC-1

subject: Physics
question_html: State Coulomb's law of force between charges at rest. Express the same in SI units.
question_text: State Coulomb's law of force between charges at rest. Express the same in SI units.
explanation: The force of attraction or repulsion between two stationary point charges q 1 and q 2 is directly proportional to the product q 1 q 2 and inversely proportional to the square of the distance r between them. Mathematically, F = {tex}k \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} The proportionality constant k depends on the nature of the medium between the two charges and the system of units chosen to measure F, q 1 , q 2 and r. For free space and in SI units, k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 Nm 2 C -2 , {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} is called the permittivity of free space and its value is 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C 2 N -1 m -2 . Hence Coulomb's law in SI units may be expressed as F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
solution: The force of attraction or repulsion between two stationary point charges q 1 and q 2 is directly proportional to the product q 1 q 2 and inversely proportional to the square of the distance r between them. Mathematically, F = {tex}k \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} The proportionality constant k depends on the nature of the medium between the two charges and the system of units chosen to measure F, q 1 , q 2 and r. For free space and in SI units, k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 Nm 2 C -2 , {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} is called the permittivity of free space and its value is 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C 2 N -1 m -2 . Hence Coulomb's law in SI units may be expressed as F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The force of attraction or repulsion between two stationary point charges q1 and q2 is directly proportional to the product q1q2 and inversely proportional to the square of the distance r between them. Mathematically,
F = {tex}k \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
The proportionality constant k depends on the nature of the medium between the two charges and the system of units chosen to measure F, q1, q2 and r. For free space and in SI units,
k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 Nm2C-2, {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} is called the permittivity of free space and its value is 8.854 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 C2N-1m-2.
Hence Coulomb's law in SI units may be expressed as F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: Two identical metallic spheres, having unequal, opposite charges are placed at a distance 0.90 m apart in air. After bringing them in contact with each other, they are again placed at the same distance apart. Now the force of repulsion between them is 0.025 N. Calculate the final charge on each of them.
question_text: Two identical metallic spheres, having unequal, opposite charges are placed at a distance 0.90 m apart in air. After bringing them in contact with each other, they are again placed at the same distance apart. Now the force of repulsion between them is 0.025 N. Calculate the final charge on each of them.
explanation: The two spheres will share the final charge equally. Let q be the charge on each sphere. {tex}\therefore{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 0.025 N or {tex}\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times q \times q}{(0.90)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.025 or q 2 = {tex}\frac{0.025 \times(0.90)^{2}}{9 \times 10^{9}}{/tex} = 225 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 or q = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C
solution: The two spheres will share the final charge equally. Let q be the charge on each sphere. {tex}\therefore{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 0.025 N or {tex}\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times q \times q}{(0.90)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.025 or q 2 = {tex}\frac{0.025 \times(0.90)^{2}}{9 \times 10^{9}}{/tex} = 225 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 or q = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C
solution_html:
The two spheres will share the final charge equally. Let q be the charge on each sphere.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 0.025 N
or {tex}\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times q \times q}{(0.90)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.025
or q2 = {tex}\frac{0.025 \times(0.90)^{2}}{9 \times 10^{9}}{/tex} = 225 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14
or q = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 C

subject: Physics
question_html: What is meant by quantization of electric charge? What is its cause?
question_text: What is meant by quantization of electric charge? What is its cause?
explanation: The quantization of electric charge is the property due to which all free charges are an integral multiple of the basic unit of charge of an electron (or proton) represented by e. The basic cause of quantization is that only integral no. of electrons can be transferred from one body to another on rubbing.

question_html:
Figure shows tracks of three charged particles in a uniform electrostatic field. Give the signs of the three charges. Which particle has the highest charge to the mass ratio?

  1. Suppose that a particle is attracted towards the positive plate. What must the charge on it be?
  2. Suppose, two particles have identical curved trajectories. Which of the following are necessarily true?
    1. They have same charge;
    2. They have same mass;
    3. The charges have the same sign;
    4. They have the same {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} ratio.
  3. You are given the initial velocity v of a beam particle and the length of the capacitor l. What other measurements would enable one to find {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex}?
question_text: Figure shows tracks of three charged particles in a uniform electrostatic field. Give the signs of the three charges. Which particle has the highest charge to the mass ratio? Suppose that a particle is attracted towards the positive plate. What must the charge on it be? Suppose, two particles have identical curved trajectories. Which of the following are necessarily true? They have same charge; They have same mass; The charges have the same sign; They have the same {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} ratio. You are given the initial velocity v of a beam particle and the length of the capacitor l. What other measurements would enable one to find {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} ?
explanation: Particles 1 and 2 have negative charges because they are being deflected towards the positive plate of the electrostatic field. Particle 3 has a positive charge because it is being deflected towards the negative plate. Acceleration acting on charge q in y-direction, a = {tex}\frac{F}{m}=\frac{q E}{m}{/tex} Therefore, deflection of charged particle in time t in y-direction is h = 0 {tex}\times{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E}{m}{/tex} i.e., h {tex}\propto \frac{q}{m}{/tex} As the particle 3 suffers maximum deflection in y-direction, so it has highest charge to mass {tex}(\frac{q}{m}){/tex} ratio. Negative charge. (iii) and (iv). The particles must have charges of the same sign and same {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} ratio. To find {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} , we measure the vertical displacement h as the particle crosses the capacitor plates. Time taken by a particle to cross the capacitor plates, t = {tex}\frac{l}{v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E}{m}{/tex} t 2 = 0.5 {tex}\frac{e E}{m}\left(\frac{l}{v}\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution: Particles 1 and 2 have negative charges because they are being deflected towards the positive plate of the electrostatic field. Particle 3 has a positive charge because it is being deflected towards the negative plate. Acceleration acting on charge q in y-direction, a = {tex}\frac{F}{m}=\frac{q E}{m}{/tex} Therefore, deflection of charged particle in time t in y-direction is h = 0 {tex}\times{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E}{m}{/tex} i.e., h {tex}\propto \frac{q}{m}{/tex} As the particle 3 suffers maximum deflection in y-direction, so it has highest charge to mass {tex}(\frac{q}{m}){/tex} ratio. Negative charge. (iii) and (iv). The particles must have charges of the same sign and same {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} ratio. To find {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} , we measure the vertical displacement h as the particle crosses the capacitor plates. Time taken by a particle to cross the capacitor plates, t = {tex}\frac{l}{v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E}{m}{/tex} t 2 = 0.5 {tex}\frac{e E}{m}\left(\frac{l}{v}\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Particles 1 and 2 have negative charges because they are being deflected towards the positive plate of the electrostatic field.
Particle 3 has a positive charge because it is being deflected towards the negative plate.
Acceleration acting on charge q in y-direction,
a = {tex}\frac{F}{m}=\frac{q E}{m}{/tex}
Therefore, deflection of charged particle in time t in y-direction is
h = 0 {tex}\times{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E}{m}{/tex}
i.e., h {tex}\propto \frac{q}{m}{/tex}
As the particle 3 suffers maximum deflection in y-direction, so it has highest charge to mass {tex}(\frac{q}{m}){/tex} ratio.

  1. Negative charge.
  2. (iii) and (iv). The particles must have charges of the same sign and same {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex} ratio.
  3. To find {tex}\frac{e}{m}{/tex}, we measure the vertical displacement h as the particle crosses the capacitor plates.

Time taken by a particle to cross the capacitor plates,
t = {tex}\frac{l}{v}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{q E}{m}{/tex} t2 = 0.5 {tex}\frac{e E}{m}\left(\frac{l}{v}\right)^{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Write Coulomb's law in vector form. What is the importance of expressing it in vector form?
question_text: Write Coulomb's law in vector form. What is the importance of expressing it in vector form?
explanation: Coulomb's law in vector form: As shown in fig., consider two positive point charges q 1 and q 2 placed in vacuum at distance r from each other. They repel each other. In vector form, Coulomb's law may be expressed as {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} = Force on charge q 2 due to q 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{12}=\frac{\overrightarrow{r_{12}}}{r}{/tex} , is a unit vector in the direction from q 1 to q 2 Similarly, {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} = Force on charge q 1 due to q 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=\frac{\vec{r}_{21}}{r}{/tex} , is a unit vector in the direction from q 2 to q 1 . The coloumbian forces between unlike charges (q 1 q 2 < 0) are attractive, as shown in Fig. Importance of vector form. The vector form of coulomb's law gives the following additional information : As {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=-\hat{r}_{12}{/tex} , therefore {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=-\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} This means that the two charges exert equal and opposite forces on each other. So Coulombian forces obey Newton's third law of motion. As the Coulombian forces act along {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} , i.e., along the line joining the centres of two charges, so they are central forces.
solution: Coulomb's law in vector form: As shown in fig., consider two positive point charges q 1 and q 2 placed in vacuum at distance r from each other. They repel each other. In vector form, Coulomb's law may be expressed as {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} = Force on charge q 2 due to q 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{12}=\frac{\overrightarrow{r_{12}}}{r}{/tex} , is a unit vector in the direction from q 1 to q 2 Similarly, {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} = Force on charge q 1 due to q 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=\frac{\vec{r}_{21}}{r}{/tex} , is a unit vector in the direction from q 2 to q 1 . The coloumbian forces between unlike charges (q 1 q 2 < 0) are attractive, as shown in Fig. Importance of vector form. The vector form of coulomb's law gives the following additional information : As {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=-\hat{r}_{12}{/tex} , therefore {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=-\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} This means that the two charges exert equal and opposite forces on each other. So Coulombian forces obey Newton's third law of motion. As the Coulombian forces act along {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} , i.e., along the line joining the centres of two charges, so they are central forces.
solution_html:
Coulomb's law in vector form:  As shown in fig., consider two positive point charges q1 and q2 placed in vacuum at distance r from each other. They repel each other.

In vector form, Coulomb's law may be expressed as {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} = Force on charge q2 due to q1
= {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{12}=\frac{\overrightarrow{r_{12}}}{r}{/tex}, is a unit vector in the direction from q1 to q2
Similarly, {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} = Force on charge q1 due to q2
= {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=\frac{\vec{r}_{21}}{r}{/tex}, is a unit vector in the direction from q2 to q1.
The coloumbian forces between unlike charges (q1q2 < 0) are attractive, as shown in Fig.

Importance of vector form. The vector form of coulomb's law gives the following additional information :

  1. As {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=-\hat{r}_{12}{/tex}, therefore {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=-\vec{F}_{12}{/tex}
    This means that the two charges exert equal and opposite forces on each other. So Coulombian forces obey Newton's third law of motion.
  2. As the Coulombian forces act along {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex}, i.e., along the line joining the centres of two charges, so they are central forces.

question_text: A charge q is placed at the center of the line joining two equal charges Q. The system of the three charges will be in equilibrium if q is equal to

options.explanation: A charge q is placed at the center C of the line joining two equal charges Q each, at points A and B. Let AB = 2a. For the system to be equilibrium F=0.Hence , {tex}\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{qQ}{a^2}+\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{Q.Q}{{(2a)}^2}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{qQ}{a^2}=\frac{-1}{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{Q^2}{{(2a)}^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow q=-\frac Q4{/tex}

question_html: Give six properties of electric charges.
question_text: Give six properties of electric charges.
explanation: The properties of electric charges are as follows: Like charges repel and unlike charges attract each other. Electric charges are quantized. Electric charges are additive. Electric charges are conserved. The magnitude of the elementary negative charge is the same as that of the elementary positive charge and is equal to 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C. Unlike mass, the electric charge on a body is not affected by its motion.
solution: The properties of electric charges are as follows: Like charges repel and unlike charges attract each other. Electric charges are quantized. Electric charges are additive. Electric charges are conserved. The magnitude of the elementary negative charge is the same as that of the elementary positive charge and is equal to 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C. Unlike mass, the electric charge on a body is not affected by its motion.
solution_html:
The properties of electric charges are as follows:

  1. Like charges repel and unlike charges attract each other.
  2. Electric charges are quantized.
  3. Electric charges are additive.
  4. Electric charges are conserved.
  5. The magnitude of the elementary negative charge is the same as that of the elementary positive charge and is equal to 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C.
  6. Unlike mass, the electric charge on a body is not affected by its motion.

As F {tex}\propto \frac{1}{d^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{3 F}{F}=\frac{d^{2}}{d^{\prime 2}}{/tex}
or d' = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}d

question_html: If {tex}\vec{E}=6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}, calculate the electric flux through a surface of area 20 units in Y-Z plane.

explanation: Electric field vector, {tex}\vec{E}=6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} As the area vector {tex}\vec{S}{/tex} in the Y-Z plane points along outward drawn normal i.e., along positive X-direction, so {tex}\vec{S}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} Flux, {tex}\phi_{E}=\vec{E} \cdot \vec{S}=(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot 20 \hat{i}{/tex} = 120 units
solution: Electric field vector, {tex}\vec{E}=6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} As the area vector {tex}\vec{S}{/tex} in the Y-Z plane points along outward drawn normal i.e., along positive X-direction, so {tex}\vec{S}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} Flux, {tex}\phi_{E}=\vec{E} \cdot \vec{S}=(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot 20 \hat{i}{/tex} = 120 units
solution_html:

As the area vector {tex}\vec{S}{/tex} in the Y-Z plane points along outward drawn normal i.e., along positive X-direction, so
{tex}\vec{S}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex}
Flux, {tex}\phi_{E}=\vec{E} \cdot \vec{S}=(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot 20 \hat{i}{/tex}
= 120 units

subject: Physics
question_html: An ebonite rod held in hand can be charged by rubbing with flannel but a copper rod cannot be charged like this. Why?
question_text: An ebonite rod held in hand can be charged by rubbing with flannel but a copper rod cannot be charged like this. Why?
explanation: Ebonite rod is insulating. Whatever charge appears on it due to rubbing, stays on it. Copper is a good conductor. Any charge developed on it flows to the earth through our body. So copper rod cannot be charged like this. It can be charged by providing it with a plastic or rubber handle.
solution: Ebonite rod is insulating. Whatever charge appears on it due to rubbing, stays on it. Copper is a good conductor. Any charge developed on it flows to the earth through our body. So copper rod cannot be charged like this. It can be charged by providing it with a plastic or rubber handle.

So copper rod cannot be charged like this. It can be charged by providing it with a plastic or rubber handle.

subject: Physics
question_html: State Coulomb's law in vector form and prove that {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=-\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} where letters have their usual meanings.
question_text: State Coulomb's law in vector form and prove that {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=-\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} where letters have their usual meanings.
explanation: Consider two positive point charges q 1 and q 2 placed in vacuum at distance r from each other. Coulomb's law: Magnitude electrostatic force between two point charges separated by a distance, is directly proportional to product of magnitude of two charges is inversely proportional to square of the distance between them will act along the line joining two charges. F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} {/tex} In vector form, Force on charge q 2 due to q 1 is {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} Where {tex}\hat{r}_{12}{/tex} is a unit vector in the direction from q 1 to q 2 Similarly, Force on charge q 1 due to q 2 is {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} Where {tex}\hat{r}_{21}{/tex} is a unit vector in the direction from q 2 to q 1 .
solution: Consider two positive point charges q 1 and q 2 placed in vacuum at distance r from each other. Coulomb's law: Magnitude electrostatic force between two point charges separated by a distance, is directly proportional to product of magnitude of two charges is inversely proportional to square of the distance between them will act along the line joining two charges. F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} {/tex} In vector form, Force on charge q 2 due to q 1 is {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} Where {tex}\hat{r}_{12}{/tex} is a unit vector in the direction from q 1 to q 2 Similarly, Force on charge q 1 due to q 2 is {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} Where {tex}\hat{r}_{21}{/tex} is a unit vector in the direction from q 2 to q 1 .
solution_html:
Consider two positive point charges q1 and q2 placed in vacuum at distance r from each other.

Coulomb's law: Magnitude electrostatic force between two point charges separated by a distance,

  1. is directly proportional to product of magnitude of two charges
  2. is inversely proportional to square of the distance between them
  3. will act along the line joining two charges.
    F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} {/tex}
    In vector form,
    Force on charge q2 due to q1 is
    {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex}
    Where {tex}\hat{r}_{12}{/tex} is a unit vector in the direction from q1 to q2
    Similarly, Force on charge q1 due to q2 is {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex}
    Where {tex}\hat{r}_{21}{/tex} is a unit vector in the direction from q2 to q1.

question_html: An electric dipole, when held at 30o with respect to a uniform electric field of 104 NC-1 experiences a torque of 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-26 Nm. Calculate the dipole moment of the dipole.
question_text: An electric dipole, when held at 30 o with respect to a uniform electric field of 10 4 NC -1 experiences a torque of 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -26 Nm. Calculate the dipole moment of the dipole.
explanation: Here {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o , E = 10 4 NC -1 , {tex}\tau{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -26 Nm As {tex}\tau{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Dipole moment, {tex}p=\frac{\tau}{E \sin \theta}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-26}}{10^{4} \times \sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-26}}{10^{4} \times 0.5}{/tex} = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -29 Cm
solution: Here {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o , E = 10 4 NC -1 , {tex}\tau{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -26 Nm As {tex}\tau{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Dipole moment, {tex}p=\frac{\tau}{E \sin \theta}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-26}}{10^{4} \times \sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-26}}{10^{4} \times 0.5}{/tex} = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -29 Cm
solution_html:
Here {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o, E = 104 NC-1,
{tex}\tau{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-26 Nm
As {tex}\tau{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Dipole  moment,
{tex}p=\frac{\tau}{E \sin \theta}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-26}}{10^{4} \times \sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-26}}{10^{4} \times 0.5}{/tex}
= 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-29 Cm

subject: Physics
question_html: Four charges +q, +q, -q, -q are placed respectively at the four corners A, B, C and D of a square of side a. Calculate the electric field at the centre of the square.
question_text: Four charges +q, +q, -q, -q are placed respectively at the four corners A, B, C and D of a square of side a. Calculate the electric field at the centre of the square.
explanation: Let E A , E B , E C and E D be the electric fields at the centre O of the square due to the charges at A, B, C and D respectively. Their directions are as shown in fig. (a). Since all the charges are of equal magnitude and at the same distance r from the centre O, so E A = E B = E C = E D = {tex}k \cdot \frac{q}{r^{2}}=\frac{q}{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}}=2 \frac{k q}{a^{2}}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} r 2 + r 2 = a 2 ] Because E A and E C act in the same direction, so their resultant is E 1 = E A + E C = {tex}\frac{2 k q}{a^{2}}+\frac{2 k q}{a^{2}}=\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}{/tex} Similarly, the resultant of E B and E D is E 2 = E B + E D = {tex}\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}{/tex} Now, the resultant of E 1 and E 2 will be E = {tex}\sqrt{E_{1}^{2}+E_{2}^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{2} k \frac{q}{a^{2}}{/tex} , directed parallel to AD or EC, as shown in Fig. (b). cos {tex}\beta=\frac{E_{1}}{E}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = 45 o i.e., the resultant field is inclined at an angle of 45 o with AC.
solution: Let E A , E B , E C and E D be the electric fields at the centre O of the square due to the charges at A, B, C and D respectively. Their directions are as shown in fig. (a). Since all the charges are of equal magnitude and at the same distance r from the centre O, so E A = E B = E C = E D = {tex}k \cdot \frac{q}{r^{2}}=\frac{q}{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}}=2 \frac{k q}{a^{2}}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} r 2 + r 2 = a 2 ] Because E A and E C act in the same direction, so their resultant is E 1 = E A + E C = {tex}\frac{2 k q}{a^{2}}+\frac{2 k q}{a^{2}}=\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}{/tex} Similarly, the resultant of E B and E D is E 2 = E B + E D = {tex}\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}{/tex} Now, the resultant of E 1 and E 2 will be E = {tex}\sqrt{E_{1}^{2}+E_{2}^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{2} k \frac{q}{a^{2}}{/tex} , directed parallel to AD or EC, as shown in Fig. (b). cos {tex}\beta=\frac{E_{1}}{E}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = 45 o i.e., the resultant field is inclined at an angle of 45 o with AC.
solution_html:
Let EA, EB, EC and ED be the electric fields at the centre O of the square due to the charges at A, B, C and D respectively. Their directions are as shown in fig. (a).

Since all the charges are of equal magnitude and at the same distance r from the centre O, so
EA = EB = EC = ED = {tex}k \cdot \frac{q}{r^{2}}=\frac{q}{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}}=2 \frac{k q}{a^{2}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} r2 + r2 = a2]
Because EA and EC act in the same direction, so their resultant is
E1 = EA + EC = {tex}\frac{2 k q}{a^{2}}+\frac{2 k q}{a^{2}}=\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}{/tex}
Similarly, the resultant of EB and ED is
E2 = EB + ED = {tex}\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}{/tex}
Now, the resultant of E1 and E2 will be
E = {tex}\sqrt{E_{1}^{2}+E_{2}^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4 k q}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=4 \sqrt{2} k \frac{q}{a^{2}}{/tex},
directed parallel to AD or EC, as shown in Fig. (b).
cos {tex}\beta=\frac{E_{1}}{E}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = 45o
i.e., the resultant field is inclined at an angle of 45o with AC.

question_html:
An infinite number of charges, each equal to q are placed along X-axis at x = 1, x = 2, x = 4, x = 8, .... and so on.

  1. Find the electric field at the point x = 0 due to this set up of charges.
  2. What will be the electric field, if in the above set up, the consecutive charges have opposite signs.
question_text: An infinite number of charges, each equal to q are placed along X-axis at x = 1, x = 2, x = 4, x = 8, .... and so on. Find the electric field at the point x = 0 due to this set up of charges. What will be the electric field, if in the above set up, the consecutive charges have opposite signs.
explanation: At the point x = 0, the electric fields due to all the charges are in the same x-direction and hence get added up. E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{1^{2}}+\frac{q}{2^{2}}+\frac{q}{4^{2}}+\frac{q}{8^{2}}+\ldots\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[1+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{64}+\ldots\right]=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{q}{3 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Electric field at x = 0 is E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{1^{2}}-\frac{q}{2^{2}}+\frac{q}{4^{2}}-\frac{q}{8^{2}}+\ldots\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[1-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16}-\frac{1}{64}+\ldots\right]=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)}=\frac{q}{5 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution: At the point x = 0, the electric fields due to all the charges are in the same x-direction and hence get added up. E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{1^{2}}+\frac{q}{2^{2}}+\frac{q}{4^{2}}+\frac{q}{8^{2}}+\ldots\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[1+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{64}+\ldots\right]=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{q}{3 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Electric field at x = 0 is E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{1^{2}}-\frac{q}{2^{2}}+\frac{q}{4^{2}}-\frac{q}{8^{2}}+\ldots\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[1-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16}-\frac{1}{64}+\ldots\right]=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)}=\frac{q}{5 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. At the point x = 0, the electric fields due to all the charges are in the same x-direction and hence get added up.
    E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{1^{2}}+\frac{q}{2^{2}}+\frac{q}{4^{2}}+\frac{q}{8^{2}}+\ldots\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[1+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{64}+\ldots\right]=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{q}{3 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
  2. Electric field at x = 0 is
    E = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{1^{2}}-\frac{q}{2^{2}}+\frac{q}{4^{2}}-\frac{q}{8^{2}}+\ldots\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[1-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16}-\frac{1}{64}+\ldots\right]=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)}=\frac{q}{5 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

Weight of proton = Electrical repulsive force
or mg = {tex}\text { k. } \frac{q \times q}{r^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r2 = {tex}\frac{k q^{2}}{m g}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27} \times 9.8}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{23.04}{16.36} \times{/tex} 10-2 = 0.014

question_html: A point charge of 17.7 {tex}\mu{/tex}C is located at the centre of a cube of side 0.03 m. Find the electric flux through each face of the cube.
question_text: A point charge of 17.7 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is located at the centre of a cube of side 0.03 m. Find the electric flux through each face of the cube.
explanation: Flux through each phase of the cube {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \phi_{E}=\frac{1}{6} \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{17.7 \times 10^{-6}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 3.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 Nm 2 C -1

subject: Physics
question_html: How does the speed of an electrically charged particle affect its mass and charge?
question_text: How does the speed of an electrically charged particle affect its mass and charge?
explanation: Effect of speed on mass and electric charge: According to the special theory of relativity, the mass of a body increases with its speed in accordance with the relation: m = {tex}\frac{m_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}}}{/tex} where, m o = rest mass of the body, c = speed of light, and m = mass of the body when moving with speed v. As v < c, therefore, m > m o . In contrast to mass, the charge on a body remains constant and does not change as the speed of the body changes.
solution: Effect of speed on mass and electric charge: According to the special theory of relativity, the mass of a body increases with its speed in accordance with the relation: m = {tex}\frac{m_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}}}{/tex} where, m o = rest mass of the body, c = speed of light, and m = mass of the body when moving with speed v. As v < c, therefore, m > m o . In contrast to mass, the charge on a body remains constant and does not change as the speed of the body changes.
solution_html:
Effect of speed on mass and electric charge:  According to the special theory of relativity, the mass of a body increases with its speed in accordance with the relation:
m = {tex}\frac{m_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}}}{/tex} where, mo = rest mass of the body, c = speed of light, and m = mass of the body when moving with speed v.
As v < c, therefore, m > mo.
In contrast to mass, the charge on a body remains constant and does not change as the speed of the body changes.

question_html: An electric charge q is placed at one of the corners of a cube of side a. The electric flux on one of its faces will be
question_text: An electric charge q is placed at one of the corners of a cube of side a. The electric flux on one of its faces will be

options.explanation: When charge q is placed at one corner, the flux through each of the three faces meeting at this corner will be zero, as {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is parallel to these faces. One-eighth of the flux emerging from charge q passes through the remaining three faces, so the flux through each such face is {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{8} \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{24} \cdot \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Gauss's theorem, obtain an expression for the force between two point charges.
question_text: Using Gauss's theorem, obtain an expression for the force between two point charges.
explanation: Let the gaussian surface be a sphere and the separation between the charge is r and taking one charge inside the sphere and the other be at the surface (r) now firstly we are interested in finding the magnitude of the electric field at a distance r. {tex}\int{/tex} Eds = {tex}\frac{q_{net}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} Eds = {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} (this is an electric field due to charge 1 at a distance r) now we can find force due to this electric field on charge q 2 F = q 2 E = {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} this proves the force of interaction between 2 charged particle.
solution: Let the gaussian surface be a sphere and the separation between the charge is r and taking one charge inside the sphere and the other be at the surface (r) now firstly we are interested in finding the magnitude of the electric field at a distance r. {tex}\int{/tex} Eds = {tex}\frac{q_{net}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} Eds = {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} (this is an electric field due to charge 1 at a distance r) now we can find force due to this electric field on charge q 2 F = q 2 E = {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} this proves the force of interaction between 2 charged particle.
solution_html:
Let the gaussian surface be a sphere and the separation between the charge is r and taking one charge inside the sphere and the other be at the surface (r)
now firstly we are interested in finding the magnitude of the electric field at a distance r.
{tex}\int{/tex}Eds = {tex}\frac{q_{net}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\int{/tex} Eds = {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
E {tex}\times{/tex} 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
E = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} (this is an electric field due to charge 1 at a distance r)
now we can find force due to this electric field on charge q2
F = q2E = {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
this proves the force of interaction between 2 charged particle.

question_text: Define electric field intensity. What is its SI unit? What is relation between electric field and force?
explanation: The intensity of electric field at a point is defined as the electric force experienced by the test charge qo placed at that point. Unit of electric field strength is Newton/Coulomb. The strength and direction of the force depend on the strength and direction of the electric field at the object's location.
solution: The intensity of electric field at a point is defined as the electric force experienced by the test charge qo placed at that point. Unit of electric field strength is Newton/Coulomb. The strength and direction of the force depend on the strength and direction of the electric field at the object's location.

The intensity of electric field at a point is defined as the electric force experienced by the test charge qo placed at that point.
Unit of electric field strength is Newton/Coulomb.
The strength and direction of the force depend on the strength and direction of the electric field at the object's location.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two-point charges placed at a distance r in air exert a force F on each other. At what distance will these charges experience the same force F in a medium of dielectric constant K?
question_text: Two-point charges placed at a distance r in air exert a force F on each other. At what distance will these charges experience the same force F in a medium of dielectric constant K?
explanation: F air = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = F ...(i) F med = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \kappa} \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{\prime 2}}{/tex} = F ...(ii) Dividing (i) by (ii), we get {tex}\frac{\kappa r^{\prime 2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 1 or r' = {tex}\frac{r}{\sqrt{\kappa}}{/tex}
solution: F air = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = F ...(i) F med = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \kappa} \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{\prime 2}}{/tex} = F ...(ii) Dividing (i) by (ii), we get {tex}\frac{\kappa r^{\prime 2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 1 or r' = {tex}\frac{r}{\sqrt{\kappa}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Fair = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = F ...(i)
Fmed = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \kappa} \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{\prime 2}}{/tex} = F ...(ii)
Dividing (i) by (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{\kappa r^{\prime 2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 1
or r' = {tex}\frac{r}{\sqrt{\kappa}}{/tex}
